I have to say that I really wanted to like this game, the idea of it interested me.  However, I find myself disappointed. Playing the game is like controlling a skulking emo kid - you wander around wearing a hoodie jacket wit the sleeves rolled up, trying to sneak up on someone and hold his face near their neck. Yes, that's right - you hold your face near their neck. For a game this new, the blood sucking animation really should at least make it look like you are sucking on the victims neck and not just showing the players head hovering two or three inches away. Now, this is a stealth game, but even so, why can't the main character grab a gun from one of his fallen victims to use if necessary ? The controls are a tad clunky, whether using keyboard / mouse or gamepad. When discovered, you mash the attack buttons / keys, but all that happens is your character stands like sulking wannabe goth whilst he catches the bullets in his chest, from the guard who is standing within arms reach of you. Quite often this results in you pushing the guard backwards whilst you frantically try to attack and then ending with a 'the bullets have broken my back and I have folded over backwards' death cutsccene. Vampire 'powers' are next to useless - don't bother with distraction, you will be annoyed at how you can never seem to aim and use this very often. So much potential, but wasted.

PROS: Gameplay felt like a Hitman game should feel  Graphics were more than good enough for me  No major performance issues that I noticed.  Paris level is a dream come true  Stealth players have a variety of ways to eliminate their targets allowing them to be more creative in their planning.  Like to go in guns blazing? There are so many npcs walking around you could spend hours making sure every witness has been eliminated.  CONS: The 2 hour refund window. 2 hours simply isn't enough time for someone to make an informed decision on a game like Hitman where the true objective is to eliminate your target unnoticed, be a ghost if you will. You could easily spend those 2 hours simply doing recon work at the fashion show.  Players only interested in finishing the missions can burn through all them in an hour (give or take). Note: The mission list includes the 2 tutorial ones on the boat, a final test, and then Paris.  For me this game did not want to connect to the servers. When I was lucky enough to actually connect and get into a mission I would lose connection and get sent back to the main menu. This happened repeatedly for 3 1/2 hours making me ineligible to refund the game. Steam seems to not give any leeway on the 2 hour policy, even if your actual time spent in a loaded level is considerably less.   Conclusion: Your play style will definitely affect how you view this game in its current state.  Probably not worth the risk for most people at the moment but could be worthy of high praise in the future.  Note: Contracts and Escalation were not tested because of the connection issues.

I have played some of the old classic Thief games, never finished them but enough to know they were stealth game, and about stealing things. This new game got a lot of bad/mixed reviews both here on steam and from various other reviewers around. I do not understand the reason for this. Some people might compare it to Dishonored, and sure you could slighty for looks and certain other parts, but Dishonored was based off of Thief. Also some 'fans' were all pissy about this game and I really dont see why, this is a pure thief game, can you fight? Sure, if there is like one guy or maybe two, but in most cases you have to run and hide or you will die. Yeah the AI could use some work, but isnt that a problem with most games anyways? Now asides from that I have to say I really liked this game not only for the fun gameplay that actually made me think instead of just rush into things, but also the story is really good, now if and how it ties into the classic games I really have no idea, as I havent played them enough to know. But if you like stealth games, where stealth is your main objective then here is a really good game for you. I actually won this game for free from a giveaway but it would still be worth about 25-30 some dollars. I know there are a bugs but I really didnt run into many, except right after chapter 6 my whole game lagged but it was fixed by simply restarting my laptop. Plus this is one of those 'Next Gen' games, so it looks pretty good, and might require a little beffy computer. I really liked it, plus the bank heist mission, I think you can buy it, i got a preorder copy, but it was fun. It took me like 17-18 hours to beat the game, but I did I think all the side missions and all the Client Missions, I wish though there were more client missions, but the city is huge and has a lot to explore and find. Also, just in case your wondering, you can still keep playing after you beat the game, allowing you to replay missions, explore the city, or do side missions you havent done yet which is really nice and I was woried they wouldnt have that feature in the game. 4.5/5

WARNING: OPINIONATED SPOILER ALERT! BUY AND PLAY THE GAME, THEN READ THIS AND AGREE WITH ME.   So, it's been a while since I've sunk a good amount of time in to a computer game and, overall I had a blast playing this one! The story is fantastic, the combat system is intuitive and not too mind bogglingly deep, and it looks beautiful (seriously, the art team outdid themselves here!)  'Levelling up' is a nice and simple, and xp is gained by being a badass - something which the game sets you up to be from the get go - as opposed to many. many fetch quests, pointless sideplots, and helping out irritating NPCs.  Everything you do feels like it carries weight in the world, and Talion seems to actually care about making a difference, and not just about avenging his wife and son (a character driver which has been done to death - this game LITERALLY has two different characters driven by the same device!)  This game took me just under 50 hours to 100%, including a very reasonable amount of sidequests, upgrades and discoverables - Outcasts, Weapon quests and Artifacts/Ithilin (sic?) respectively - and I have thoroughly enjoyed the process of getting to the end...Which brings me to my point.  I don't think I have ever been so disappointed with the end fight of a game. After many hours of refining the counter/dodge system, unlocking skills to make me a whirling dervish of death, deciding which Runes are going to benefit me most, and finding effective tactics in different combat scenarios, the final fight was a quick time event!! Seriously?! One of the most action filled and combat driven games I've played this year, and the final fight came down to a digital version of Whack-A-Mole! It wouldn't be THAT bad if the fight was more complex than Dominating a Graug, but 3 QTE actions to complete the game felt like the biggest disappointment since I lost my virginity!  Overall, I'd give this game a solid 8/10 (I don't believe 10/10's actually exist - Not even Ocarina of Time!) purely for the ending! Seriously, the first 98.5% of this game is fantastic, and a joy to play, but the ending is SO tragically disappointing, that it ruins the rest of the experience!  I'd highly recommend you buy this game, kill, find and unlock everything, and get all the way up until the final fight, then leave it there - save yourself the fond memories of how good this game was, and not how ♥♥♥♥♥ the ending is!

What you need to know:  This game may say it's 'Free' but, in a sense, it's more of a trial run if you don't purchase membership.  The reason I say this, is because every DLC is locked behind a paywall, you'll have a currency cap ($2,000), and half the Power options in the game are locked behind said DLC (though they can be purchased individually using real money).  Whereas, if you pay the monthly fee, you'll get all those DLC, all the powers, and unlimited currency cap automatically.  In short, this game is more of a 'Pay to Play' model, since a majority of the content is membership exlusive.  Pros: -The game is very fun, and excellently optimized, you'll always have a solid, high frame rate.  -Many power options (ranging from being a green lantern to having celestial powers) (though to accept the best ones, you'll need membership.  -The community can be helpful, and polite (at least, more often than most MMO's I've played)  -Reaching max level can be done within a couple of days  -Not Pay-To-Win -Plenty of content for both PVE and PVP (ranging from 1 player challenges, 2 player dous, 4 player Alerts, 8 player raids, and PVP ranges from 2 player to 8 player arenas)  -Legends PVP, where if you don't want to PVP with your character, you can play as an iconic character, be it Batman, Dr. Fate, the Flash, Superman, etc. the list is very long.  -Power animations look well done (Especially celestial and lightning in my opinion)  Cons: -Once you've already become geared, and tried out all of the powers the game has to offer, it does become quite boring. -Replay Tokens. Basically that means you can't automatically reset a raid without paying money to do so (unless you want to wait a week for it to reset on its own)  -Character models are a bit outdated and ugly looking. You'll quickly notice their blocky, texture-less faces, that just overall look really bad. The bodies look like they were put together in a day.  -It can take a while to get past T1-T3 since not much players actually do that content (most focus on T4 and above), you can find yourself looking for a raid group for Khandag for hours at a time.  -No Black Lantern rings  -Only 4 movement modes (flight, skimming, super speed, and acrobatics) and all of them are disapointingly slow.

Amazing game. Play it.  More in depth, you don't have to play the first to get into this one but it does help build the universe dramatically. You'll also realize how this one is pretty dumbed down combat and progression wise when compared to the first: there's less weapons, less skills and a much smaller skill tree, and armor differences seem pretty unimportant. The difficulty is also weird, I didn't find beating it on Insanity (or most of it anyway) much harder than playing on easy, while I had many problems in a lot of spots playing Mass Effect 1 on Insanity.  But with those downfalls of being EAified, you get a much more cinematic game with deeper characters, an amazing main quest, and great hype that gets you ready for the third (which I didn't dislike nearly as much as everyone else for some reason). This is definitely one of Bioware's best creations.

Thought, care, and skill has gone into the construction of this beautiful and unique first-person urban exploration and puzzle game. If exploration and unique environments are up your alley, you'll spend countless hours exploring the expansive levels looking for every bit of detail. The game is also exceptionally beautiful, years of craftsmanship and practice have gone into extracting every inch of beauty possible out of the source engine. The game smacks heavily of Portal 2 in terms of quality and scope, however this game is much less linear and provides a heavy emphasis on expansive environments and exploration.  The game is a little rough around the edges sometimes, and the puzzle solutions may get a little obscure from time to time, but I required no external assistance and I'm not exactly a brainiac. The game's developers are also actively correcting and repairing bugs as they are reported on the tracker.  The game, although technically incomplete, has given me over 8 hours of gameplay so far, and I'm still not finished. The value is well worth what you'll receive.

The Vanishing of Ethan Carter is an exploration/mystery type game. The gameplay basically boils down to exploration broken up by puzzles and story set pieces. The plot itself is fairly simple but the manner you unravel it is pretty compelling. The puzzles are good but not particularly amazing, which is fine as they're not really the point of the game.  Ethan Carter looks amazing, the environments are really interesting to explore and painstakingly detailed. Everything from the leaves and grass rustling in the wind to the mist hanging over the lake to the placement of the rocks, grass and trees in relation to each other in the forests is just right. It runs quite well, my PC isn't top of the line or even close and I managed to run all settings on High smoothly by turning down the Anisotropic filtering.  The music and sound complement the game perfectly and has some really great tracks, the music is not too overbearing and always appropriate for the scene. Sounds are excellent, your footsteps over different surfaces, the creeks of old houses and the wind whistling through the trees all sound just right.  This game drips with atmosphere and is well worth your time. I beat it in maybe 4-5 hours, but it's a really unique experience and well worth the money.

I got this game in Groupees 'Build a Greenlight Bundle 2' a little less than a year ago, so the number of hours I've played on Steam is misleading.   Little Racers STREET is a fun top-down racing game. It's simple in it's mechanics, and easy to get into. It has many different difficulty modes, the higher the difficulty, the more money you get, and the lower the difficulty, the less money you get. I found the difficulty settings in this game great. I'm absolutely terrible at racing, sports, and fighting games, but the difficulty settings in this game let me put it to a level that even I could call fair for my skill level.  Basically, the point of the Career mode is to make your way to the top of the ranks. You start out with enough money to buy a E Class car, and perhaps a couple upgrades for it. Paint jobs are also free, which was nice.  Multiplayer is a blast too. Being able to have 12 player races, and even filling slots with AI's is sweet.  It's definitely no for simulation fans, which should have been immediately obvious from the screenshots, but it's a sweet arcade racing game that's definitely worth picking up.

My first thought as I started playing was 'ok, this looks really good', which held true until the last minute of the game. Gameplay essentially consists of jumping/grappling/boosting your way through a few levels while the main character narrates the story. The scenery, for the most part, is comprised either of colorful dreamscapes with floating mountains and cities, or else vast cavernous complexes riddled with bioluminescent life - and it all looks amazing. There's a soothing sort of awe in landing on a floating chunk of rock where a cushion-furnished gazebo happens to have been built. Who built it? Who spends time here? It's all very surreal. In fact, the space through which the player navigates is often littered with floating platforms and objects, odd-looking sculptures, sometimes entire villages, and the whole thing is so architectonically impractical that it could only belong in a dream, or a children's story and, well, the game works well on that level... dream or fable, it manages to endow the experience with a great deal of wonder.   The narrative takes the shape of a bedtime story which you (the grown-up version of the protagonist) tell your daughter about the time when you were a kid and went on an adventure, looking for your missing uncle. The voice acting is flawless, and there's a melancholic tone in it that makes you wonder about the unspoken details of the main character's relationship with his uncle, and the reality which inspired this hyper-imaginative tale.   I was really pleasantly surprised with this game. It's well-executed on almost every level and manages to convey a touching story with the support of simple and extremely fun gameplay. It may not be game-of-the-year material, but you know what? I wish there were more games like this.

I avoided this game for a while, mainly due to it coming off as pretentious. It was a good idea to look at war from another perspective (such an  indie  thing to do lol). But is the game fun? Yes, and in fact it is quite well done. This is essentially a base management simulator, that happens to have awesome crafting and is story-rich.   Pros:  +Graphics are awesome +They really capture the ability for you to drive your own story - you actually do get attached to your characters +Crafting is well done (especially considering that every game now-a-days seems to have mandatory crafting) +There is a real sense of danger in the world, where decisions have real consequences   Cons:  -In this type of game, starting over always sucks - unlike other base/household management sims where you can go on virtually forever -Like titles such as  Don't Starve  you always feel behind-the-ball as far as resources go - you are never really *over-powered* -Price initially turned me off, but its worth it   I got it on a sale and was really surprised at how entertaining it is. If this seems to be your kind of game, I'd go for it.

review_text: MGS V is a good game but I was sorely dissapointed  Settings: Limited. There's no sound options and many keys can't be bound. Graphics options are limited with a 60 fps cap - which can be changed by editing a config file (must be fullscreen)  Graphics: Good and the level of detail is great. The 29 GB file size is surprisingly small compared to the content. Performance is good with 80 fps on my gtx 970. Sound and animations are excellent except for the loud character movement and obnoxious running sounds  Story: Cutscenes are detailed and well done. I can't comment on most of the story since I'm *only* 15 hours in, so I'll give my first impressions on the beginning prologue:  I was baffled. I tabbed out and checked twice(!) that I was playing the right game with such great reviews. The beginning is amateur and makes no sense. There was no sense of urgency or fear since the enemies look directly at you many times. When I was finally confronted with the 'bad' guy I went towards him thinking he was friendly since he'd done nothing remotely threatening to me. I never felt the emotions that were portrayed and it left a bad first impression  Gameplay: The combat is fun, but it's penalized. You're not supposed to kill most enemies. The best course of action is to to tranquilize everyone and suck them up into space using a balloon to recruit them to your base camp...   It's built around being a stealth game but it's not, or it's a bad stealth game. You can crouch right in front of enemies in daylight and they won't see you. You can stand 5 meters in front of an enemy with night vision and they won't see you. The only way they'll detect you is if you're running in front of them or if they're already alerted  Besides the bad stealth the AI are pretty good. I'd recommend forgetting the penalty and just killing most of them. The missions are repetitive but the main negative is the down time. It's not a real open world. Most of the map is impassible except for the long roads. Most of your time in game is spent waiting  There's a lot of content with the main story being about 44 hours. The upgrade and base management system is well made, but most of it is unnecessary and the upgrades useless. There's still some fun to be had with it though  Conclusion: If you like the gameplay then you'll definitely get your money's worth as there's no shortage of content. The reason I don't recommend it is that I feel there's way too much downtime and the combat is only fun if you're lethal. I can't have fun with stealth if I know I don't have to be stealthy to stay hidden. I also don't like the supernatural elements which seem strange, unexplained, and out of place - like the floating girl in a straight jacket

This puzzle-platforming game looks and plays like a dream. If you were a fan of Limbo, you'll love Inside!  As the game begins players take control of a nameless, faceless boy as he runs through a forest, pursued by masked men and attack dogs. That's all you need to know - it's best playing this game with as few spoilers in your head as possible. :)  The gameplay is very similar to Limbo, but manages to improve on it with some impressive set pieces and level design. The story is still ambiguous, but less so than it was in Limbo. It's really impressive how the engaging and intriguing story is told without any voices or dialogue at all!  It's also very creepy at times, with the underwater sections in particular being downright scary.  The PC version of Inside is excellent. Despite a lack of graphical options beyond changing the resolution, the game looks absolutely beautiful. It also works perfectly with Steam Link and the Steam Controller and looked gorgeous on my LG HDTV.  It's certainly a short game but there isn't a single wasted moment and the ending is a knockout.   I received a review copy from the developer to review on my website, Alternative Magazine Online.   Please feel free to check out the full review for further details on why I think this is one of the best games of 2016 so far:   https://alternativemagazineonline.co.uk/2016/07/07/game-review-inside-pc/

This game is an absolute disaster, but let's be fair and address its admirable qualities first. Silence of the Sleep does have some very interesting sound design and visuals (even the pause menu looks impressive), and a decent story too. The concept itself was just brimming with potential. Unfortunately, the developer simply failed to capitalize on much of it, probably on account of their inexperience in making games. While it feels like a very early attempt, if we assume that was the case, it's a really good start, and I'm sure the developer will make great games once they've got some more experience under their belt.  Now, let's get into why it's not worth playing (and certainly not its pricetag!). While the game had some very interesting things going for it, it ultimately just wasn't fun to play. The first thing you'll notice about it is that it's extremely tedious at times, and it only gets worse about this as you progress. It violates a basic rule in horror, particularly in games, which dictates that no matter how terrifying your monsters are, if you're overexposed to them, the whole thing goes from scary to obnoxious very fast. I can't say that its puzzles helped with that either, as most of them are just a lot of running around and very little else (Chapter 3 is particularly guilty of this). And speaking of the weak point that is Chapter 3, all of the game's attempts at humor not only felt out of place, but also fell mostly flat. The whole thing is filled with unentertaining mini-games that completely break imersion and leave you wondering whether you bought a horror game or the Halloween edition of Wario Ware. (In all seriousness though, one of said mini-games is trying to lure a rabbit out from under a bed with a carrot.) These minigames, while not necessarily bad things in and of themselves, don't belong in the bigger picture and are reflective of one of the larger problems with the game, which is a lack of focus. It meanders in a lot of ways, wasting effort that should've been spent on making the gameplay more tolerable and the plot development smoother. Last and possibly worst of all (the thing that made me finally quit, in fact): the puzzles toward the end of the game make very little sense and rely on artificial difficulty that results from enemies that are extremely tough to avoid. Said enemies make the labyrinth environments difficult to memorize or even navigate once you have them down. On top of all this, a new and player-disabling game mechanic is introduced to make it all even more frustrating.  tl;dr: Despite its wonderful aesthetic, atmosphere, and interesting themes, I can't say I enjoyed the game itself due to its many frustrating design flaws.

Alright, so let's start with my first comment: BUY THIS GAME. It is absolutely fantastic and a vital piece of gaming media for a Dragon Ball fan. I didn't think I'd like it, then I played it and now it's one of my favourite online games. I can assure you, you will love it.  Now, let's get down to pros and cons.  PROS:   Easy to pick up gameplay, tricky to master but not exactly 'hard'.  A fun, engaging experience that will have your attention for hours on end.  A steady levelling system that has you work for it, but doesn't grind your face in the dirt or require you put in hours to get a single level.  Gameplay and advancement are well-paced and don't require too much effort, though you're not just handed everything either.  Nice simple graphics that don't tax an average rig too much even at full power; a good offset to the major problem of PC gaming.  Smooth, comfortable integration of USB controllers, although the keyboard controls are just as easy to learn.  ==  CONS:  Some ridiculously difficult Parallel Quest scenarios that will have you seething with rage as if you were playing Street Fighter or KOF.  Server can be a bit laggy and interfere with multiplayer experiences at times; I played with someone only a few miles away and we still had a tricky time with collision detection and movement lag.  Customization. It's another game that presents itself as being highly customizable in terms of character creation, however when you actually get down to it, it's pretty limited. You can only change colours on a very small selection of outfits; the others are based off outfits from the franchise, and as you would expect, they kill the 'character creation' function because the moment the average gamer is given a creativity toy, their first instinct is to make things they already know, so everyone thinks they're Goku, everyone thinks they're Gohan, etc. and very few people actually bother to make an original character in this.  It's a Namco Bandai game. They do this all the time; they bring out a great game that shows so much promise, only give it a little bit of content expansion and then abandon it to work on an inferior title, in this case that new One Piece thing. With SoulCalibur V, they did the same thing to work on TTT2 which was massively inferior. So while they make great products, they're a rather careless and irresponsible company that don't care much for their own products (although they're definitely no Konami or EA).  ==  SUGGESTIONS:  Needs more interactive environments, especially in the hub world.  They should put in at least quite a few more expansions that focus on character creation. Most specifically, let us colour any outfit and let Majin go barefoot; perhaps also some footwear like sandals or ankle guards. What we have is extremely limited despite being presented as bigger than it is.  An actual chatbox so players can talk to each other with text. I appreciate the effort to filter out spam by giving us preset messages, but come on - a private chat window at least.  I hate to sound like a feminazi, but there are SO many great female fighters in the Dragon Ball universe that you completely ignored. There are about a half dozen playable females in this game and several dozen males. Just look at the Dragon Ball Wikia and take your pick; you should put in at least 10 more females.

But what story there is, the characters, the gameplay, they are done decently.  HOWEVER, the game has a terrible ending. If you play it, I can promise you will be asking yourself, 'that's it?'. It ends abruptly. The second half of the game is missing. The game starts off so well...but it's just like they gave up halfway through. Either that cut content is for a DLC or direct sequel, or it was just unfinished.  I would have given a thumbs up before playing the 'ending', but after completing the game, I have to give a thumbs down. The game teased and promised so much - we were to uncover a global conspiracy - but in the end we tasted so little of it. It's still an OK game otherwise, so you can get the game sometime down the line.

app_name: Ori and the Blind Forest: Definitive Edition
review_text: Changes? 1. New area. The first section of this area is darkness gimmick type of place but it's handler pretty well. The second is based around the new abilities you get in the area, and introducing the new mechanics is handled well.  2. New abilities. Dash and Light Orb. The dash makes movement a lot faster and probably pleases the hell out of speedrunners. The light orb is fun because you can use it to break a lot of the jumping puzzles once you get bash, and some secrets were edited to be behind doors you have to open with the light orb, I'm pretty okay with that.  3. Warping between save points. In case the dash didn't already make things fast enough.  4. You can backtrack now. No more replaying the entire game because of one missed collectible.  5. Whoa, 100% isn't based on just map completion and the map completion isn't as broken as in the original. How about that.  Basically, it's an improvement in every way, and there was little downside to Ori to begin with. Nice. Even the new content alone justifies the $5 purchase but if you've never played Ori there's never been a better time to try it out.

After getting this game heavily discounted, I can safely say that it has some of the best value of all the titles found on Steam. You've got tons of nations to choose from, and many play quite differently. Whether you want to expand mercilessly as Prussia, further your empire as the UK, dominate your neighbours as Punjab, or fight to restore Romania, it's up to you. Know that if you want to play a minor, especially an uncivilized nation, that this game is not kind, so be prepared to fight way harder.  Anyways, the game is simple to manage, but frustratingly hard to grasp. The tutorial doesn't help. AI management is bad, especially when it comes to consolidating troops, and the game isn't one for those who expect even a loose adherence to history.  Beyond that, this game really is terrific, and worthy of a purchase, arguably at full price (Though the expansions seem to be a must). With all that said, there's nothing more satisfying after a century of growth then to see your country as one of the great powers, or even supplanting the UK as number one.

After playing Tomb Raider: Anniversary, this part of the Tomb Raider Saga fails to succeed it's sequel, although it is clearly a large improvement over the infamous Tomb Raider: Angel of Darkness.  Even though the game features different locations all over the world it also lacks the actual feeling of being an actual 'tomb raider'; the levels are very often urban oder industrial structures, for example a devastated soviet laboratory in the ice desert of Kazahkstan or a yakuza penthouse in Tokio. Furthermore the leveldesign is not as challenging as it is in other titles of the series. The puzzles are too easy and the focus on combat is too big. The playtime for a walkthroug with about 5 hours for a skilled player is devinately pretty low.  It's not a bad game, you can really see how the developers tried to bring a new refreshed version of a Tomb Raider game and in this point they did a good job, it's still a fun game. But for me, there is plenty of possibilitys to improve.   I would still recommend it because if you can overlook the fact, that this game does not try to bring you the original Tomb Raider feeling but instead it tries to bring you a new expierience, you will have a good time. Even though it's not great.

app_name: Prince of Persia: The Sands of Time
review_text: Just a heads up: if you're going to play any of this trilogy you need to play all three, and in order. Okay. Sands of Time is an absolute gem of a game and it is by far the best of the trilogy overall. The characters are memorable, the story is novel, if not truly unique. The gameplay is elegant and flowing, and the eponymous Sands of Time fits perfectly into it. Among other things the Sands allow you to rewind up to ten seconds to retry a jump you missed or combat you messed up, which is both constantly handy and occasionally hilarious. However, the puzzles can get frustrating, and the other games improve vastly on the combat and platforming.

Tower Wars offers a unique take on the tower defense strategy genre by incorporating the standard set of towers, upgrades, and abilities, with a competetive gaming approach. Here are the Pros and Cons:  Pros: 1. Steampunk-style art offers a new take on the tower defense genre. 2. Gameplay is straightforward and easy to pick up.  3. Different levels and a hex based grid allows for a unique game each time you play.   Cons: 1. Content-wise, the game lacks diversity. There is only one set of units and the only changes in style between opposing players is color.  2. MONUMENTALLY long loading times for a small game will make you wonder if the game has frozen while spinning up a map.  3. Limited replayability as the playerbase is lacking for matchmaking/competetive play.   Summary: At its current 2.00 sale price, Tower wars is a definite buy. Even if you only get about 10 games out of it before moving on to another title, this game does offer some intense competetive play - provided you have the patience to wait through the loading times.

I played Maplestory back in it's early days. It started out really good, but then one patch after another they managed to ruin the game in several ways.  1) They made it more about grinding than going on quests. I can remember sitting at my computer for hours upon hours just going from one side of a small map to the other trying to outkill the rest of the players to claim the map as my own.  2) They put a limit on party quests, which was the most fun way to do said grind because you didn't have to do it alone, and you weren't at constant war with other players over territory.  3) Every new class they released seemed to be stronger than the last, leaving the earlier classes outdated and inadequate.  4) As the game aged, they began pushing the pay-to-win aspect more and more to the point where if you don't spend money on the game, you're going to find yourself far behind the rest of the players, which is really discouraging when you get to the higher levels. All your hard work seems to fall short of the ones that just emptied their wallet into the game.  In addition to the hours I've spent playing this game on Steam, I've spent hundreds more before the game came to Steam. I haven't played this game in a couple years, but I look back every once in a while, praying that at least a couple of these problems would be fixed, but alas, every time I begin looking into it, it's aparent that the problems are still there, and probably getting worse. If you value your time and/or money, I'd suggest you stay away from this game.

Great game, it completly encourages Co-Op, however some missions can be done solo, but this is one of the hardest games i've ever played in Co-Op, and Solo makes it much harder than Solo.  PROS: 1. Gameplay feels alive all the time, you don't know where the enemies are, you got a stamina and health system you don't see. It's fairy realistic most of the time.  2. You actually gotta aim for the head most of the time, and this isn't a typical shooter based zombie game, you will actually need to switch to a melee weapon often due to the scarce of ammo, you will have to manage your ammo otherwise you won't get far.  3. Inventory system that is pretty simple once you get used to, there are 2 inventories, you can drop items for other survivors or just drop them to pick another item up, Holding 1 will show up the items/weapons inventory, Holding 2 will show up the ammo/fuel/etc inventory. Left clicking on an inventory item will consume the item if its a bandage, etc. Right clicking on an inventory item will drop the item.  4. Several kinds of ammo in this game, each kind of ammo needs the correct weapon to work, you don't just pick up ammo and it would work magically with every weapon, this reduces ammo hoarding as people will generally only need to pick up ammo for a weapon they have rather than all the weapon, and encourages Co-Op so you can drop ammo that you don't need for others or can't use.  5. The game soundtrack feels fairly alive, please be aware that the game is much better with friends and i found games where most people had a microphone (I only played the game in public servers with random people) the game did feel more enjoyable with people with microphones, however if you are going solo like i did, it's still one of my favorite zombie games.  6. Free, And made in the source engine which is optimized well, most PC's can run this game.  Cons: 1. When joining a server, there will be an Advertisement sometimes on the top left, this is pretty damn annoying because when you click on Ok, sometimes it opens another Advertisement, so you might have to click Ok several times. NOTE: You don't have to click ok, first click on join game at the bottom right of the screen, if you aren't in the game dead or alive, then you are gonna have to click OK to close the ad's, this only happend to me once however, everytime i clicked Join Game i never had to click Ok and close the AD's.  2. No friend add-system in the game, the only way you can add people is through the players option OUTSIDE the game, it dosen't work INSIDE the game, for some reason it dosen't show up as previously played with Inside the game, you have to exit the game, then click on steam's View > Players > Recently Played With > Then add whoever you want as a friend, or whatever you wanna do with them.  I have wrote this review as honest as possible, i don't think i missed anything, but if i do i'll probably edit this out and fix what i missed.

app_name: Brothers - A Tale of Two Sons
review_text: A very moving game. This is an example of gameplay mechanics blending with the story to create something more. Powerfully moving, I would recommend this game to anyone who things that games are nothing but shooty shooty wartime shooters. The mechanic to move both characters is unique, and also very intuitive. I didn't find it as difficult or uncomfortable as I thought it would be. It's an idea that I had never thought of and haven't seen before.   It creates a feeling of attachment to both of the characters, preventing the dreaded, 'escort quest' feel. Their lives are in my hands as a player and I feel comfortable taking responsibility.   This game does deal with mature themes in a way that doesn't completely digest or resolve them. The setting and atmosphere starts 'safe' and somber. Then, through advancing the story the game gets into some very dark and twisted themes that are real, and also unpleasent.   I am very happy I bought it. I'm very happy I stayed up all night playing it. I'd go through it again for the very first time if I could. Unfortunately I can't. So I'd ask that you do it for me.

app_name: Brothers - A Tale of Two Sons
review_text: I recommend this game for anyone who likes a good story.  The team is Swedish, so I suspect the entire game is in some sort of Swedish dialect (possibly an 'old' Swedish, or one of the tribal languages from way back). It's likely you won't understand a word, but you don't need to (another sign of a good game & story). The characters make fairly obvious gestures as to what you need to do. If you don't figure it out immediately, you can always get Naia (older brother) to point out where you're supposed to go by hitting his 'interact' button.  The game is extremely linear, being only one path throughout almost every map, but: 1) a great, compelling story trumps all, and 2) there are small side-items that you can see in a number of places, which are fun little vignettes or small easter eggs.  I didn't see the warning about the controller, neither in the Launcher (bypassed, since I launched the game directly from Steam), nor the Steam Store page, since I hadn't visited it since I bought the game. I used my keyboard for almost the entire game. I decided to change the resolution during my next session, where I was trying to finish, and found the warning in the Launcher. The trouble with the keyboard is that it isn't intuitive to find out what buttons to hit while trying to turn a crank. It was extremely slow work, but it did it. When I used the controller, what took me a minute before took less than 10 seconds... Get a controller. :-p

Hours don't matter; I beat the game via a  GOG.com  version  Anyway, this game is amazing. Some of the best bosses I've seen in a metroidvania, outstanding music, an amazing final boss and a perfect escape sequence. The story is surprisingly deep with lore everywhere ala Metroid Prime, and the game is very long, easily lasting tens of hours.  With that said, USE A GUIDE.  Don't think you can get away with playing this game blind, unless you're a VERY determined player. You will have to write down nearly everything you scan, explore whatever you can, etc. This game is ridiculous in puzzles and level design and, in my opinion, it's nowhere near to even the best part of the game! It's well worth skipping it using a guide because the rest of the game IS worth it. Items, powerups, etc really help alleviate the difficulty this game has at the beginning.  Also, this game is pretty hard even with a guide. For a good portion of the game you have no defense and very bad platforming; it gets much better later, but if you don't have a guide for powerups, it's understandable how you can get frustrated because the tools to make your life easier aren't available. However, at about the half-way point the game becomes much more tolerable due to the sheer amount of abilities you have gotten. Also, it is worth mentioning that both whip upgrades are permanently missable; however, reloading a save can let you try again. Just keep that in mind. With that said, the game is amazing. In my own experience, it's an 8/10 for the first half and a 10/10 for the second half, but only with a guide. Highly recommended, just, make sure you know what you are getting into.

First of all, let me start by saying I am a HUGE fan of the Holy Build Engine Trinity (Duke3D, Shadow Warrior & Blood). When I heard they were re-imagining Shadow Warrior, I got totally excited at the thought, but that thought soon faded out after I thought about how most things like this turn out. Whether it's movies or video games, re-establishments of classic franchises seldom work out.  To say that Shadow Warrior turned out good would be a massive understatement. It's fantastic.  The game LOOKS phenomenal. It runs very fluidly with little to no slow-down on even the highest settings on my machine. The visuals and environments are clearly extremely inspired by the original game's art style, and couldn't possibly have been nailed harder than Flying Wild Hog nailed them here.  The soundtrack and effects are pretty good, with quality voice acting to back it up. The jokes do get a bit lame at times, and both Hoji and Wang can get a bit annoying after awhile, but hey that's the campy goodness we came to know and love from the masterful Build engine franchises.  The controls are smooth (I'm using keyboard/mouse, as I do with most any FPS) and not frustrating at all.  I really feel that FWH really brought home the gold here with melee fighting and gun combat here. The combo system and vast list of special moves and alternate fire modes are really something special.  The upgrade system is also incredibly deep. You actually get to spec Lo Wang here on multiple levels, and they will directly affect the way you play the game.  I'm not totally finished with the campaign yet, but the story is surprisingly good as well. I'm actually not skipping videos and following along. So it's at least good enough to keep me interested, which is saying much more than I can for a lot of shooters I've played in the past.  Overall, the game is well worth the 40 dollar full price-tag they put on it, but it's a steal if you can get it on sale, ESPECIALLY right now at 10 bucks.  You just can't go wrong with this one. One of the best games I've played all year, and an instant classic right up there with the original.  Well ♥♥♥♥ing done, Flying Wild Hog.

1) Simulated difficulty by stacking enemies. No tactical options. Long tedious setups that result in same AI charge tactics with unlimited movement.  2) Character creation is designed for you to miss things along the way. Stack for combat or get owned every step of the way. Stack for skills and you will have a very rough time of it. Go somewhere in the middle and you are creating a useless character.  3) Party roles are predetermined, cliche' an boring. Sniper, Medic, Thief amd Leader. Anything outside that is bound to fail.  4) Skill system is not fleshed out. Forces you to replay the same story with the same setup. They advertise the playthrough is different everytime...it's not...the loot is different...the story is not.  5) They advertise your choices make a difference and have consequences. Here are your choices: Are you powerful enough to kill whatever it is you ♥♥♥♥ off? If you are you get loot...if not you die. Choice selection list over.  This is a $40 game that should be $20 at best. Wait for a sale on this one. It will be there soon. Same song as most fan funded, greenlight and indie titles. Lots of promises and no delivery.

I was born to a single mother who raised me completely on her own. My father was a singer who traveled all over the country for his concerts and tours, sleeping with whatever women were in the area. My mother told me that what he did was shameful and disgusting and that the only good thing he ever gave her was me. Then one day we got a call saying that he was in the hospital and that he wanted to see us. So we got on a plane and flew to Memphis University Hospital, where he was diagnosed with not only AIDS, but syphilis as well. We entered the room, and he proceeded to tell my mother that of all the women he had been with, she was the only one he loved. He gave her an envelope, then looked me dead in the eyes and said, 'Listen kid, I'm sorry.' His heart monitor flatlined, and he died right there in front of me. Seeing that scared the hell out of me, and I vowed I would never have sex with any woman. Ever. I saw what STD's did to a person's body and I didn't want any of that. But now I regret not ever sleeping with a woman, because I bought this game and it gave me AIDS anyway. Thanks Gareth Bourn.

Got this on sale for $2.49. Gamers who ignore the 'PC Master Race', have no idea what they are missing...  This game is indeed a winner. Tried it out for a quick few min. Turned out to be 1.5hrs  Much has already been said, so, I will say the one thing that stood out to me....  Videos, make this game look Frantic & Chaotic. I was pleasently suprised, to find out it plays at a far more reasonable pace, than it appears. It's not a FREAK OUT AND BOUNCE ALL OVER THE PLACE SLASHING LIKE CRAZY game. You think about which way to go, which key to use, which block to break, which enemy to go after. It's neat, and somewhat difficult to explain.   The whole DIE & START OVER thing, sounds awful, but its not. Once you realize, getting as far as you can, in each run, IS the game itself, you won't mind. If you kept going, it would actually hurt the game. Each time you start, little, progressive possitive changes in your Character, make each run different. You suddenly can wizz past parts that were tough, go after that extra prisoner, snatch that extra key, and.....aaaaaaaaaand.... MAKE IT TO THE NEXT LEVER! Wooot!!  Give it a try. It may be a love it or hate it kinda game, but, looking at the 95% positive rating (out of 12500+ reviews!), Odds swing in your favor.

As platform games go, it's less of an obstacle course with a finish line and more like 'figure out the precise jump/float/slide pattern we intended you to follow to not die on a given obstacle, then do it all again for the next one, oh and sometimes you'll just get hit anyway so SUCK IT UP SCRUB'. It's lot like the latter-day Sonic games (you know, the ♥♥♥♥♥♥ ones) in that regard. The scrolling is pretty janky too, with quite a few points where you have to leap off an edge of the screen to progress and hope it pans to something you can land on. Overall, level design just feels too busy for its own good; you're constantly dodging through one spiky helltrap after another without any relatively simple stretches to give you a breather, and believe me, that will wear on you. I, for one, have been playing video games too long to put up with that ♥♥♥♥ anymore.  One last thing, and I guess this goes out to indie developers in general: if you're not just crowing about it and actually do care about 'balanced difficulty', consider swapping out your testers sometimes, assuming you have any besides yourself (in which case recruit family or friends, just anybody willing to suffer for you); someone who's played through a thumb-breaking, deathtrap-ridden hellfest a thousand times in debug mode is bound to lose perspective of how frustrating the experience really is for everyone else.

Honor and death...or utter humiliation in your classic total war game.   This game sets in the warring states period of Japan. You are allowed to play as many factions (DLC included) with unique traits and bonuses. Each faction has one ultimate goal; obtain the title of shogunate and force other clans to recognize your power.   You are a cowardly tactical commander controlling a vast army of your chosen clan behind your computer screen. In order to succeed, you must be capable of micromanaging units on the field and at sea. Flanking, positioning, and fortifying are all important; terrain is another useful concept to be aware of. You must also manage resources, form alliances, betray friendships, and troll others to obtain your goals outside of battle. Strategy is diverse from negotiations, assassinations, and total warfare; this game is meant for domination victories.   Do you have what is takes to rule Japan? If not, you are a shameful display.

Shadowrun: Hong Kong   is an isometric tactical cyberpunk RPG. The year is 2056, a dark and dystopian world, a world filled with Orc gangers, Elf corporate wadge-slaves and dragon dictators, a world where magic and cybernetics mix. Raymond Black, the man who took you off the street as a teen and raised you, makes a desperate call to you and your step-brother Duncan to meet him Hong Kong, but you arrive to a chaotic conspiracy where you must burn your former life and survive as a Shadowrunner mercenary for the triads.   Pros:  Once again an improvement over the previous games of the series, from the UI to a brand new selection of equipment. The background stills are just as gorgeous as we got used to, but character models are much more defined, especially Orcs and Trolls that are much less stocky-looking. The writing is as good as ever: Your crew but also merchants and crooks have their own story to tell, many of which have funny moments very reminiscent of (and likely inspired by) tabletop RPG player experiences. Your hub, Heoi, feels more dynamic, with NPCs such as other pay-for-hire Shadowrunners that come and go in between missions, talking about their expertise and past experience when you catch them while they're around. Your crew is much more involved in runs: each mission's progression, conversations and resolution will play out differently from one crew combinations to another, adding to the replay value of the game. You even get to chose your own character's past and history through conversations, choices that carry over throughout the game in future interactions and relationships. Music is just as immersive, some are Asian-inspired remixes of familiar tracks, others brand new compositions that really contribute to fleshing out the atmosphere of Hong Kong. The Matrix feels more alive with a sleeker look & feel and reimagined puzzles and behavior patterns for surveillance and defensive software and ICE.   Cons:  The main story is still rather linear, and the ending feels short or rushed, likely in an attempt to leave it opened for the upcoming  Shadows of Hong Kong  mini-sequel DLC. Although there's a wider variety of combat animation, the tactical combat still falls short of other more combat-oriented games, like the   XCOM   or   Shadowrun Chronicles   games. Combat in general feels too optional, and can too often be avoided completely.   Verdict:  ★★★★☆ - Next on your list!  This is the best Shadowrun game to date, with truly excellent writing on par with   Shadowrun: Dragonfall – Director`s Cut  , and that offers a large variety of ways to approach every interaction and mission. Of course, if you found no appeal to those past games, the improvements in this one won't change your mind. But to others, this is a great CRPG that I definitively recommend! I can't wait to see what the new and improved editor will allow the community to create for Steam Workshop!  —————————————————————————   This was just my opinion.   If you found this review helpful, please consider giving it a thumbs up, and feel free to check out more of my (purely opinionated)  reviews .

The story is very short - well, actually there is no real story (except for an intro and an ending clip) - but you have do several quests, earn money for better equipment and skillpoints to beat the game. So first things first, if you are still Dark Souls-virgin I recommend you start off with any of the games prior to this (Dark Souls 1, Dark Souls 2 or Bloodborne).  Dark Souls 3 takes place after the events from Dark Souls 1 ignoring the events from Dark Souls 2 (I'll discuss Dark Souls 2 later on since there's some important points to be made) and takes a more Bloodborne-like combat approach instead of the one we could see in Dark Souls 1 and 2 which was slower and clunkier, in a way this is a good thing in my opnion since anyone who has played earlier installmens has died at least once due to the clunky movements of the character or our camera going crazy when you had a wall behind you, another point worth mentioning is that unlike earlier installments, Dark Souls 3 is actually playable with a mouse and keyboard, the controlls take a little to get used to but after you get the hang of them you should'nt have trouble playing the game.  Now the bad stuff, we all know that Dark Souls 2 was not a great game, in fact is considered the worst of the franchise but at least they got some things right, things that should've stayed yet they were removed, I still cannot explain why they removed the NG+ new enemy placements or the blue eye orb, but the part that annoys me the most, they brought back the most hated thing in souls games but multiplied by 3... autosummon covenants, in previous games we had the bell keepers and the forest hunters which meant only 1 autosummon covenant per game, Well in Dark Souls 3 we have 3 of them, Watchdogs of Farron, Aldrich Faithful and Blades of the Darkmoon, which at least they fixed the blades of the darkmoon but in all my playtime I have never been summoned for Watchdogs or Aldrich  So TL;DR  Pros:  +Faster gameplay +Mouse/Kyeboard combo friendly +Easier to connect with friends compared to other games +More challenging gameplay (Definatelly harder than previous entries) +Surprisingly responsive online connection +No more soul memory  Cons:  -More autosummon covenants -No blue eye orb -Removal of the dual-wielding mechanic Dark Souls 2 had -NG+ stays the same outside from the ability to get stronger rings -Invasions prioritize players who have phantoms summoned -The worst PVP of all games  Would I recommend the game? Hell yes Is it game of the year material? Hell no  Overall rating: 8/10

I'm a veteran souls player that has a giant nerd boner for this game so I was compelled to write a review. Pros -Better starting class choices -Smarter AI, more attack patterns, and faster to act then previous dark souls/demon souls.  -Epic AF boss battles -big dik mystic enviroments to moisten those senses -All around faster paced fighting which instills a more fluid form of combat -running a 970x on max gfx with max fps and no drop. Needless to say the game looks sexy -Better system for playing online with your steam friends -More spells more items more class hybridization.  -Defeat a giant tree boss by punching it in its ♥♥♥♥ -Invasions are still as exciting and malevolent as before. Pvp is the dope thickness Cons -(sort of a con for a more casual player) This game punishes you for any and all mistakes made. This game will not hold your hand and walk you through its gameplay. I say sort of a con because i feel like this actually attracts a lot of gamers, myself included, but to the general masses i gotta say they'd probably not like this as much.  -There's a lot more sunlight in the game.... i mean its not really a con but the games called F@#king dark souls. -This game does not include Bjs or batteries....or bj batteries.... All in all dark souls 3 is nothing but improvements on its predocessors. The gameplays fresh enough to bring new fun to the game but stays true enough to its core to appease the veteran souls community.  10/10 would punch boss dix again

Greetings loyal citizens of the greatest Planet in the Universe. Today, at this very moment, you are contemplating the most difficult decision of your life. You're thinking to yourself: 'Do I want to protect my homeland, earn the privilege to have children, and gain the respect of my peers?' Or 'Am I content with being a coward and most likely an Alien sympathizer?' There's a war out there and Super Earth needs your help!   Fight for Democracy! Join the HELLDIVERS! .... You wanna know something? Starting off this review with such a cheeky intro, probably isn't the greatest idea. What I should have said was 'anything that can go wrong, will go wrong'. HELLDIVERS aka Murphy's Law: the videogame is not to be taken lightly. It can be raucous and exhilirating entertainment, but it can also cause finger-pointing, object-throwing, and enough expletives to block out the Super Sun. Also, while this game is excellent in many ways, it also has a handful of design-decisions that keep it just shy from classic status. Still, if I were to end this review right now, I'd say that this game is very highly recommended.   But the review is far from over, so allow me to continue.  If you've seen Paul Verhoeven's  Starship Troopers , then you already know this game's story. Super Earth, the greatest planet in the Universe, has declared war on three factions: the cyborgs, bugs, and illuminate. At one time or another, one of these factions may or may not have unleashed a devastating attack on Super Earth. This tragedy must not go un-punished. Aside from destroying every last alien you confront, you're also expected to exploit their resources, assist colonization efforts, and broadcast the message that Super Earth's democracy is the greatest.  To really enjoy HELLDIVERS, you're expected to play cooperatively. This is a four-player game with both online and couch support. Aside from the introduction, there isn't really any campaign mode or much of a story. You assist the war effort by completing missions on various planets.   The most intriguing aspect of this game is that friendly-fire is always on and can never be disabled. While this game can be considered an isometric twin-stick shooter, you can't simply point and fire. No, you have to point and  then  fire. If you're not paying attention, your next shot might go through a team-mate's skull instead of into the weak-point of a bug, cyborg, or illuminate. This extends to nearly every action in the game. Your re-supply air-drops aren't going to be welcomed with open arms if they crush your allies. These rules make for a game that's a lot more interesting than most others of its ilk. Over time you'll develop an understanding of what actions your team-mates will take. The best teams will operate as one, while the worst will produce a comedy of errors.  A lot of the entertainment comes from adapting to the myriad of situations that can occur. By using strategems, players can receive unique weapons, call ordnance on stronger foes, or acquire vehicles that makes traversing each planet easier. Some of the heavier equipment (such as the RL-112) benefit more with one player carrying the gun, and the other carrying the ammo. It really drives home just how important good team-work can be, and it's the difference-maker on the hardest difficulties. The enemy is infinite, and as long as their patrols spot the players, they will bring as much force as necessary. At this point, the best response is usually to run away. Almost every enemy can be escaped, and will even despawn if you and your allies get out of the immediate area. In short, stealth is the #1 key to success, but not particularly entertaining.  As you continue to play this game, you'll notice that if you can eliminate patrols, then you won't have to worry about much of anything else. This tends to be the optimal way to play, since dealing with adversity will waste time (and leads to deaths). Aside from missions that require destroying a set number of enemies, or boss-fights, the bulk of the game can feel rather barren. All of the neat strategems and intriguing co-op ideas will fall by the wayside, as long as players are sharp and quick enough to take care of patrols.  This is usually the reason why I play with random players. Jumping into games where I don't have any idea what the other players are doing, and then adapt to their behavior/equipment, helps keep the game fun. I like to see danger, I like to see terrible situations, and hopefully we as a team can turn things around to accomplish our objectives. Videogames tend to 'grow' with the player. As the player becomes more skilled, the game becomes more challenging to compensate (through difficulty settings or other means). HELLDIVERS can only grow so much, and that can be attributed to the design-decisions of stealth. If there were more missions where the enemy has fortified positions, or the players had to invade planets where there more than just patrols wandering around, it would definitely keep the game fresh for veterans.  Some encounters in this game could also use a little tuning. For example, there are three bosses. The boss of the bug faction does a lot of burrowing in and out of the ground. I've noticed that all too often, shots that look like they hit don't do any damage. For such a large and mostly immobile target, this creature has a surprising number of invulnerability frames. As the fight progresses, larger and more dangerous bugs join in. It's not enough that there are threats from every direction, but the probability for actually damaging the boss drops to .01%. Basically what I'm saying is that this isn't a fun battle. My suggestion would be to slightly decrease the damage that some of the enemies do, or maybe not give certain bugs so much armor. At least when entering a boss-battle, players have ample time to attempt it, and change strategies if their attempts fail. A person only has so much patient though, and rage-quits are always a possibility.  As you play this game, you're bound to notice other players with unfamiliar weapons, strategems, and perks. A lot of these are acquired by completing missions, but there are also a lot of them that are straight-up DLC. I'm... well... not terribly fond of this practice. For example, there is a perk known as the all-terrain boots. This allows players to not be slowed down by marshes or snow. There are planets that are nothing but snow. While it can be considered a situationally useful perk, these all-terrain boots are practically forced on the player. If I opt out of buying this DLC, and my team-mates do, then I'm going to be the one holding everybody back. I've also become overly reliant on the LAS-16 Sickle (a DLC gun). I'm not sure how well I'd do if I went back to any of the other guns, and I don't want to think about it.  With stealth being overpowered and the unfortunate DLC situation, I can't quite call HELLDIVERS a perfect game. Usually when reviewing a game, I don't even think of the word 'perfect', but I came awfully close here. The cooperative aspects are absolutely brilliant, the sound-direction is flawless, the mechanics of gun-play and controls are spot-on, and the respect given to friendly-fire is a revelation. It's going to be exceedingly difficult for me to play any other online game that doesn't punish me for shooting my team-mates. This game really does almost everything right, which makes the aspects where it falls just a little short, stand out that much more. Despite this, I wholeheartedly recommend HELLDIVERS. Still a great RTS! Each different army requires a different approach and strategy. The easiest to play are perhaps the Necron (just mass produce troops create a field of corpses then show up with your hero and resurrect the lot whilst producing a giant army). The variety of different armies is quite large (Eldars, Dark Eldars, Orcs, Space Marines, Chaos, Sisters of Battle, Necrons and the Imperial Guard), the graphics are decent (far from gorgeous, but the kind that never really gets old, the game remains playable and enjoyable several years later), the gameplay is interesting (this is a war RTS, so none of that gathering ressources crap: you need energy for your base and to occupy strategic points. The solo mod is fun (a map with planets, you zoom in for each battle), but the scenario is weak. For a really enjoyable scenario - not particularly original, but very entertaining - try the first Dawn of War. I did not play Winter Assault, but I hear that the campaign is good too. Anyway, I'm still playing it, and still lovin' it. You might enjoy it even more if you ever played Warhammer 40 000, if you never have, just take it is a sci-fi strategy game, it's great!

It's a shame the studio was sunk by their failed foray into MMO development. This is a very enjoyable game that does a number of things very well, and I would have loved to see a sequel. If you enjoy RPGs at all, there's a lot to like here (and there's a demo if you just want to test it out). Particularly if you pick it up on sale there's a lot of bang to be had for your buck.   The array of weapons and skills and the mobile combat are quite fun, and I enjoyed playing the various classes, particularly mage/rogue.  I'm going to pick on the flaws, here, however: 1) Dungeon design vs. fast travel - the fast travel system in the open world mostly works well, except for the few places where you can't use it and running back out of a long dungeon or building is quite tedious. To get around this, a lot of dungeons are designed as a one-way loop, and that repeated design eventually wears a bit thin. Though it beats having to run all the way back across a dungeon you just cleared. 2) Combat isn't particularly well balanced. Particularly as the mage, most trash fights were stupid easy after the first 10-15 levels. Likewise, if your Fate is topped out, you can ez-mode almost any fight. But a few of the boss fights seem designed such that they're very hard at low levels if you don't have Fate to unleash, but too easy if you do. The concept of the Fate skill is neat, but it's a bit too powerful. 3) You will reach max level long before you've done all the side quests, and probably before you reach the final boss 4) maxing out the talents (smithing, lockpicking, alchemy) requires knowing where all the trainers are, and carefully leveling and exploration to access them at the right times 5) in that same vein, gold is very tight early in the game compared to what you'd want to spend (when 45,000 to visit a few trainers would be very handy), but you'll likely end the game with millions of gold you have no use for. On my second run through I skipped most looting, particularly chests that needed me to waste time lockpicking/dispelling for what was likely to be vendor trash anyway  Of the DLC, Dead Kel and Naros are both very enjoyable and well designed questing areas and well worth the $3 each if on sale.

Edmund McMillen and Florian Himsl have done it again - made an excellent indie title with excellent gameplay as well as hours of nonstop replayability. The Binding of Isaac is a great game that has a 'origional Legend of Zelda' feel to it - espcially the dungeon aspect of it, which leaves a nostalgic feeling in my heart.   Pros: -Tight and responsive controls -Beautiful artwork, as well as soundtrack -Lots of replayability -A wide array of different characters, with different statistics -A wide array of different items (make every playthrough different) -Randomly generated levels  Cons:  -Difficult (some people might not like this aspect of the game) -Steep learning curve (this game does not screw around)  Verdict: The Binding of Isaac is a difficult game at times, but any sort of frustration that arises from the game is balanced with excellent gameplay, tight controls, and unique music that make for an excellent indie title. If you're picking up Binding of Isaac consider also purchasing the 'Wrath of Lamb' DLC which includes even more hours of content.

Three Words: I AM BATMAN!  Seriously, that's how all three of those games feel. You become the Dark Night and strike fear into the superstitious hearts and minds of criminals. Theres a great variety of all the famous and less famous super villains of the Batman universe and they all are pictured incredibly well. Overkill is, of course, the Joker, who is hilariously terrifying. The game is excellently executed, I love all the secrets you can discover, the buttload of mini-missions and mostly the huge database with info and recordings of the various characters appearing in the games.  The only thing you might argue about is that it can be repetitive at times. Stealth challenges follow mass beat-ups follow stealth challenges and so on. I loved it and found it great fun, but I can understand people who get annoyed by it.  All in all, they are three greatly executed games with great and somewhat intertwined storylines that are just fun to follow and suck you in from beginning to the end.  THUMBS UP!

This is less than a game, and more of a work of art! This beautiful game tells the tale of a starship traveler whom abandoned his or her tribe to explore the vast open universe. With planets that auto-gen to give you a fresh start when looking through this mighty universe. Though its not only just that simple! During exploration the voyager (the player) has to find resources, whether they are rare or not, to advance through the increasing difficulty of higher threat planets. Threat, a word that entitles many great things it. During your exploration in the game the starship voyager will come across many ancient and new styled types of life, and the way that they live. The voyager will come across dungeons, temples, tombs, and many more. These of which contain new weapons, materials, riches, etc. that will help the voyager carry on through his or her journey through the solar system. Tactical gaming and magepunk at its finest. In this game, you can play as any of a variety of classic character types from the Shadowrun universe. You can play a Mage, a Shaman, a Street Samurai, a Rigger, a Hacker, or an Adept. You can interact with the Matrix, control drones summon spirits, use every sort of weapon, and more. Not only that, but after character creation your development is totally free form. You can even pick up magical skills if you didn't start with any. Beware of Essence expenditures, however; as always in Shadowrun canon, losing Essence makes magic harder to practice.  The game is tactical squad based combat. Not much in the way of noncombat ability is present. Even the Matrix missions focus on searching for things while engaging in cybercombat with IC. Noncombat skills that are featured are primarily noncombat applications of the hacking skillls, and the game's social aspect.  The story is full if twists and turns as any good Shadowrun plot is. Fans of the series will realize what the plot really is about halfway through, and your mind just might explode when you do. The plot was established a long time ago, but no one ever actually played it all out that I know of. Enjoy it!  What really makes this game complete, however is the mission editor. You can create your own custom adventures and then play them out and/or share them with others. I have a great deal of missions I could be playing through and simply haven't yet. I also have yet to play Dragonfall, the DLC sequel to this game.  Notable in its absence is the ability to play in Astral space, even though the original game concept included that as an option. This disappoints me greatly.  (Overall 9/10, Story 10/10, Gameplay 8/10, Graphics 7/10, Sound 9/10, Replayability 10/10)
