Holy moly, Freedom Planet. What a game. This game holds true in what its reviewers state: it is not in any way a Sonic copy.  Freedom Planet, at its heart a platformer, is probably one of the best if not the best platformers I have ever played. Having played through the game entirely, I can safely say it is my favorite platformer ever, and it goes right in my top 5 favorite games of all time. The creators of this game really knew what they were doing. The story is so compelling that I found myself impatient through the levels because I wanted to know what happened next. Captivating voicing and absolutely pixel perfect graphics make it that much more amazing to me. To go along with that, the game features probably one of the most amazing soundtracks I've ever heard in a game.  Graphically, it's a throwback to an older generation of games. Having never played a game from the Sonic the Hedgehog franchise (unless anyone at this point counts Sonic the Hedgehog 2006 as a game at all), Freedom Planet did what it could to make me feel that nostalgic connection anyway. Boy did it do that. Galaxy Trail picked a theme that's easy to get wrong: China. WOW is this so GOOD!!! China is prevalent throughout the entirely game, and it is truly beautiful as it stands in this game, visually and culturally. Every color in the game is vibrant and wonderful. Every object pops out in full screen and doesn't bring up the common problem that pixelated games have (when objects that can be interacted with blend into the background). The backgrounds are truly stunning and well put together. This entire game to me is eye candy of the best kind. Graphics bring me to the art style. The art style. Just the art style alone was what pulled me into the game in the first place. I looked at the title card on the Steam Store and instantly fell in love with it. The characters are so well designed and they move smoothly and amazingly. The art style is the kind of style that makes me beg to see more. It looks GREAT.  Next up, characters. The characters. Galaxy Trail couldn't have gotten this aspect more right. Every character in the game is developed well and are given personalities as they are introduced. This is one of the few games I know of that does this, as opposed to introducing a static character and giving them a personality afterward. The establishment of character so quickly leads to an easier time getting into the story, which I will go over later in the review. The characters all have unique and interesting designs, and the voice actors put a lot of work into their jobs. It really shows that they have, as the characters' dialogue are captivating, with full voice-overs for every bit. I felt truly amazed at how easily I could connect to every character (of course with the exception of the antagonist side of characters).  Music. I don't have to say anymore. The music is AWESOOOOOOME. It's so well composed, and it fits the overlying theme of the game perfectly. Even through the music, China is prevalent throughout the entire thing. Truly beautiful, and it fits the gameplay and the environment perfectly as well.  Gameplay. It's tight. It's solid. It's responsive. Every death and every victory you have will make you feel fully responsible because of how good they are. This game's control is comparable to Super Meat Boy. Throughout the game, you have the same set of moves. But while you're playing, it STILL feels like you evolve as the player. I was a fish out of water at the start, and by the end, I knew the characters like the back of my hand. I don't know what else to say, other than that the challenge this games offers is daunting, yet possible, and I mean that in the best way I can (GAME MEANT FOR GAMEPAD).  The story is truly wonderful. Right off the bat, tension is established, and you meet your antagonist, Brevon. This game does well with its immediate establishment of an antagonist. With the accompaniment of a few tertiary characters being introduced, you're immediately presented with the game's protagonists. A dragon(oid)(?) named Sash Lilac and a cat named Carol Tea, later an alien named Torque and later a dog(?) named Milla Basset. All of these unique characters fuel the story so well and progress it in the most sensible way. Freedom Planet presented me with probably the most captivating story of any game I've ever seen. I'm not even going to explain any of it. Anyone reading this who hasn't played the game MUST PLAY IT FOR THE STORY.    Of course, every game has its flaws.  There are a few graphical errors in this game (objects on the wrong side of other objects). As I said, though, there are few, and the truly beautiful environment of the game distracts from any of these errors.  The dialogue is perfect, except for just one little grammatical mistake (the word 'I' in the place of the word 'It'). Of course, any and all games that HAVE dialogue and subtitles coinciding with each other have at least one grammatical error.  An issue I have is that nothing in terms of gameplay changes at all. The Sonic-style speed is the only thing that can be considered to change, and by that, I mean that often times it is unclear what direction Lilac or Carol are meant to go in. In conjuction with that, there is plenty that impedes the forward motion. Despite that, it isn't too much for me that I'm going to bash the game too much. It isn't too much that bashing is warranted, anyway.  This issue I have is a little bit nit-picky, but I feel it's got to be said. The character you play as normally will stick to the ground, meaning that whatever angle the ground they are standing on is at is the same angle at which the sprite is standing. This particular fact isn't what I dislike, but rather it is the self-righting mechanism in the sprites. After running up a wall or something of the sort, the entire sprite rotates from a central point back to being right-side up. To me, this is unsettling and it breaks the contingency of the character as being believable. A simple way for this issue to be fixed is to implement some sort of movement sequence in the sprite that would take the place of the awkward pivotal rotation.  Another problem I had with this game was that it felt lonely. Most levels are lengthy, meaning a lot of time may be spent in each level (unless you're a speedrunner of sorts or get stuck in spots easily, which happens on occasion). The dialogues constantly reference the fact that the protagonists have conquered a certain aspect of a level together. This is strange, considering the only time any of the characters move and fight WITH the character you control is during a boss battle in the middle of the game. This disappointed me, as I find the Donkey Kong Country-esque character switch to be nice.  My only other problem with Freedom Planet is exposition. Whether or not it was intentional is beyond me. But being a minor issue, I still found myself having troble understanding the beginning of the story until I started a second file and experienced the beginning again.    But overall, this game (in my opinion) is near perfect, and a true work of art. It shows how much work, time, effort, and love went into this game's creation. And having played the whole game from start to end in a single sitting, here's what I feel like: THIS GAME IS PROBABLY THE BEST GAME I'VE EVER PLAYED IN THE PLATFORMER GENRE!  Never has any game left me wanting more as much as this one. This game is perfect in my eyes, and it leaves me wanting more from it. I hope that the people at Galaxy Trail are working on a sequel to this, because the ending of this game has set itself up perfectly for that. If it already has a sequel, I NEED TO KNOW AS SOON AS POSSIBLE. The game is short, but it's fantastic, nonetheless.  Plus, this is the first game that has ever made me cry because it ended.  This game is a work of art. Worth every cent. Buy it. Play it. Love it.   - Ian J.

Rocket League offers you a combination of both soccer (or football depending region) and cars style of games. This amazes me from every side since I bought it after being proposed to.  Originally, I didn't enjoy the game, which was before buying it, since I never loved soccer games, nor much about car-style games. After buying this game, I was all wrong. I played enough of this game to even say it's worth your money. The graphics option are outstanding for its price, you can even set it into Performance mode (low quality) or Quality mode (High quality), even toggle many of the featured options such as shadders.  It offers a great taste in gamemodes with 1v1, 2v2, 3v3 and 4v4 with sets of Hoop (basketball) or even replace the ball as a puck (Hockey). This one of the trully amazing high quality games for a cheap price you can play, even competitively.  The customisable part of cars offers a great assets to satisfy your desires in terms of cars, or even just to add a little touch with a hat for fun.  One of the only aspects I slightly disliked was about periods of lag, which sometimes destroy the momentum of your game if it ever happens (but with a decent internet connection you can achieve big). EDIT : The problem is also major in the latest map, Aquadome.  In my opinion, this game is worthy of its price, graphics, gamemodes, cross-platform gameplay and much more.  I rate this with a solid 9/10!  Buy this game and join the Rocket League community now!!!

It's still Beta! Another bare bones game rushed out the door. This one already had a DLC tab on launch. A poor combination of Distant Worlds and Galactic Civilizations. The game tries to limit you as much as possible to still be playable.  I was frustrated with the game, can't do this, can't do that, can only play it a certain way.....  Played it enough to figure this out:  All you do most of the game is survey stars and build mining/research stations. Another game where the AI plays the game for you since you can only directly manage 5 colonies in your empire. Anything beyond 5 has to be AI controlled. And it will replace buildings you don't want replaced with other buildings (it's rather dumb to the point that it won't upgrade any buildings it's built).  It has lots of broken game mechanics. (i.e. click on planet, click colonize = nothing happens, all the unassigned colony ships just ignore it). Broken quests. (A few you can't complete because there are no objectives or are blank).  Popups on top of popups. The popups you need you never get (i.e. research completed, war declared, etc). The popups you do get are repetative, the game will tell you you found something, a short pointless narrative and then you click either fascinating, amazing, or interesting. But none of these actually do anything for you. But they are annoying and they freeze the game. You can't queue your research, and since you don't get a popup for it, you'll often miss that it was completed. What you will get is a small bubble icon at the top of the screen that disappears rather quickly. A leader died? Another small bubble icon at the top of the screen (if you're not watching for it you'll miss it). War was declared? Another small bubble icon at the top of the screen (all the bubble icons have a limited delay that isn't changable).  Shows planets that are colonizable (green circle = desirable), but you can't actually colonize them since you haven't researched that technology yet or your race really doesn't like them. If your race likes (i.e.) tropical and you conquer a primitive race that lives on a turndra, then every tundra planet on the galactic map will have a green (desirable) circle next to it. Sure, makes sense, it's inhospitable, but desirable.  Every AI empire you meet will hate you. Where's the 'Advanced Diplomacy system worthy of a Grand Strategy Game.' Due to border friction, it's impossible to get anywhere with dipomacy. And they'll constantly ask you to turn over border planets. Saying no = war. War = You've just conquered 5 AI planets, but you can't annex them, only cecede one of them to end the war (based on warscore earned and government).  Over-powered enemies. (i.e. Advanced established empires (overwelming caopared to you) that always seem to declare war on you when you're barely getting started). No option to disable them.  Very limited leaders (10, 3 of them must be for research, the other 7 will be on science ships). Why even have the option for governers, generals, or admirals?  Combat is just graphical, you can't control anything. After the first few battles it gets boring to watch.  No option to see all your ship commands, so you end up sending ships on the same missions over and over. All space stations build ships at the same pace regardless of what size it is. The only point of upgrading to a larger size is to build bigger ships. And each station can only build 1 ship at a time. No option to sort ships. Ships, fleets, planets are all on a the same scrollable side bar (lots of scrolling constantly)  Pro's? They added mod support. It probably hurt them to have to do that, but mod support = more sales. You can create your own race.  End game? You're overrun by a new super race that out guns you in every respect.

Intergalactic empires in Star Ruler 2 follow a very chaotic rule of syphoning mass resources away from scores of planets in order to nurture several stronger worlds that all ideally work towards bolstering your core planet. Solar systems feeding planets several solar systems away in order to bulk them up to a point where they’re able to plough gold reserves into your homeworld. Sometimes, you can mine asteroids for valuable substances and bypass that whole colonisation nonsense. Sometimes, if you have more power than love for the planets, you can destroy an entire system and, in the splintered rubbles of worlds that once were, construct a shiny ringworld and insist that people start recognising you as a God.  I could not have been further away from this point when, upon exploring my second cluster in the hopes of finding more food planets to feed my starving empire, I stumbled across a scout ship belonging to the Hoonan, a race of Borg-like computer people who didn’t seem to be doing much harm; just trying to expand their network of planets, much like myself. We both received slight economic bonuses for making contact, and then got on with building up our territory.  Soon, I would burn their worlds to the core. The Witcher 3: Wild Hunt, even for players that have never experienced the Witcher series beforehand (myself included, infact I never had even heard of The Witcher till the hype of Wild Hunt) can be absorbed into the absolute joy that is Wild Hunt, from the beautiful atmosphere, and score, to the deep and engaing story. The Witcher 3: Wild Hunt, is without a doubt one of my favourite games of all time.   For a game that cost a mere £51 million to create (pennies in the AAA industry), Wild Hunt puts many of its much more expensive counterparts to shame. *cough* Fallout 4 *cough* Although the size of its budget does become apparent during gameplay at times (graphical bugs, and general glitches), it doesn't cloud the sheer ammount of love and attention that was put into creating this masterpiece.  I can safely say that Wild Hunt is an easy 10/10 game, I have never played an RPG that has grasped my attention (and taken over my life) as much as this, its a pure joy to experience, and I feel honoured to have done so.  I wish there was a way for me to experience this game for the first time all over again.

Back in the day I played a Magazine Demo of Transport Tycoon (TT) and was hooked. I then went to town and bought the only copy I had remember seeing for sale for a full £30, but it was £30 well spent as it is probably the game I have played the most over the years.  Train Fever is a blatant rip-off of the concepts of TT, but is missing quite a few features that were in the original TT, so might be considered Transport Tycoon Lite with better graphics. It is however also missing gameplay aspects which means this game is not very enjoyable. While TT was quite basic I think Train Fever needs a few more features (and perhaps a few unique ones not copied from TT!) to make it less basic.  The aim of TT was not really about making money, as once you got past a certain point you would be earning enough to do mostly anything, and spend time refining and increase train lines on routes needing more demand and the upgrade them to Monorail and then Maglev and Subsidies at the start which paid more and would help you get the money rolling in.  Subsidies are not included in Train Fever and it only seems to have road vehicles and trains, where TT also had ships and aircraft although this game also does not have any water, so ships will not be possible. Expect to just create a few train lines and then hit high speed and wait until they start making money to be able to create more.  Other things missing from this game is competition where TT had computer opponents, even though their intelligence was not always great. Train Fever seems a bit empty and lonely as you are the only transport company in the game.  The graphics are nice but the playability just ain't there for me. The railines I have created have all been curvy to avoid mountains and speed restrictions of tighter curves and bending\\curving the track 90 degrees seems to be too big\\long. Perhaps I am not doing something right but it does not seem to be possible to raise or lower track to be the same level as the terrain, where terrain will be raised on tracks or cut through's will be created through terrain leaving the track level. When it comes to road vehicles I created a bus route with one horse carriage though a busy town but it just lost money and did not make anything - perhaps the early road vehicles just cant and only later in the game with more demand and bigger vehicles. The houses also look incredibly modern for the 1800's. The Music is partcularly good which many tracks having a guitar feel of 'The Shadows'.  As a test, I also tried playing the game with an erection, but this made the game even less enjoyable.  I don't think in its current form this game is worth £20. Its more like a £10 game at present.

I was a fan of Mechwarrior 'back in the day,' and played a lot of Mechwarrior 2: Mercenaries. Honestly, this game captured a lot of that feeling for me. It's not as deep, but at the same time the learning curve is a little bit easier, too. If you're on the fence about whether or not you should pick this game up, I challenge you to beat the price. Even if you only play it for two hours and put it down, you paid exactly $0.00.  New players have access to trial mechs so you don't have to worry too much about being overmatched right out the gate. Things can get a little grindy after you've played your first 25 matches (and gotten a huge bonus for each one), but it doesn't feel terribly unfair. Even if you're consistently on the losing side and a terrible player, you'll usually make some C-Bills.  There are only three things I don't like about this game, and they're pretty minor:  1. Absolutely no cosmetic customization options are currently available (15December2015) without paying some real money. You can't so much as change your paint job without spending real money. Not a big deal to me, but not something I feel like spending real dough on, you know?  2. The pricing is a little predatory. You don't get a lot of virtual currency for your real cash - it's like Zynga designed their pricing scheme.  3. I don't feel like this is a pay to win game. I've won plenty without spending a dime! However, the people who are willing to dish out real money can get a definite advantage if they want it, loading themselves out with premier mechs and equipment. Skill is still a huge factor, and a good 'free' player will still usually do fine, but it can be a little annoying watching a Hero-level Timber Wolf walking around the map... it's like watching that guy who got into a prestigious college because his dad bought them a new library.

I can still remember the first time I entered the server.   I didn't know that the very first server I entered was actually a pvp server. With my bagpack on my back and a knife on my hand, I was a newbie that dind't know what to do or where to go. It was ironic that my very first spawn point was greenmountains. What did I do? I followed the only road out the village. Actually there was 4 newbies too with me, of course we didn't know each other. I didn't even know that I already left the safe zone, that was until hell broke. 'US' 4 newbies started punching each other. I was the very first one to die...  In my anger, I exited the game....  I returned after one week, and I upgraded myself into a legendary, bwala. I didn't even know what to do, and yet I already bought a legendary upgrade.  I entered the Arizona server, and man, that was one hell of a server, Arizona server is basically 20times larger than the free 2 play servers. With a sense of adventure in my blood boiling, I started to explore the Arizona server.... and man, I didn't know that the server that I enterd was 'AGAIN' a pvp server.   I was killing zombies, but there was this one guy who suddenly appeared out of nowhere and started shooting me. I died 'again' but I actually enjoyed it, why? because after that guy killed me, he didn;t know that I have laid 5 mines around the my dead body, and when he got closer. 'KAbooom'.   That is how I started to enjoy the game.   1'st farm for mines and grenades, then go to pvp server, camp the safe zone, and spam grenades and mines.

Amanita Design has knocked it out of the park again with another amazing adventure experience in the hand-made natural world of Samorost.  What can I say about it? It's moving, enchanting, whimsical... is communicated to you entirely through environments, visuals and sound (no spoken dialog at all)... and you absolutely need to experience it. I definitely recommend checking out Samorost 1 & 2 (and the rest of Amanita's catalog) but no prior experience is necessary.  Many puzzles in the game are completely optional and serve to give you more interactions with the world and characters (as well as achievements and collectible sounds that you can mix together into a big musical mash-up in the collectibles menu).  Highly recommended.

Gonna go ahead and break this review down a bit which also doubles as a minor 'crash course' on some of the more implied aspects of the game.  * The hacking feels very intuitive and although programing is something people should definitely consider however it is absolutely not necessary.   * The 'tutorial' however is perhaps the best I have ever seen in a long time. After you 'prove your sentience' and understand the controls of the game it will start arming you with the basic tools that you will absolutely need outside of the LAN (such as how to acquire money and avoid people that want to take money from you).  * As for difficulty it's kind of hard to pin down. What I mean by this is that it's as hard as you are willing to make it (due to teir levels and also playstyles). You can become as the game dev puts it different 'classes.'   You can become a 'Scavenger' which is something along the lines of a PvE player who focuses on acquiring GC from bots. Much of the tutorial focuses on this playstyle.   An 'Architect' is the kind of person who makes the many programs that you will find outside of the LAN. You don't make any programs yourself for the tutorial but it attempts to make abundantly clear the consequences of running what they make and how to avoid the ones that will force you into making a new user.   An 'Infiltrator' is a PvP player and will focuses on stealing from players rather than bots (kernel.hardline is used a lot here).   Finally, the 'CEO' are the people who organize everyone else into a working machine (Corporations) and the most successful are not easy targets for an Infiltrator though probably a highly desired one.  * All in all a very interesting game with a lot of potential but it definitely is something that you should do a bit of research on before buying. If you are the kind of person to do that anyways, congratulations. Your the kind of person who would probably enjoy the game.

FIXING UPLAY:  UPVOTE THIS SO EVERYONE KNOWS  The reason Uplay crashes when you try to launch from Steam is because Uplay does not install properly when the install is queued/prompted by Steam itself.   INSTEAD, download and install the standalone Uplay launcher.  THEN, run the new standalone Uplay install and log in with your account.   NOW, you can launch the game from steam as usual as long as Uplay is running and you are logged in.

Healing items and weapons/ammo are scattered fairly around the map to give players a chance to survive, not to mention pistols always have unlimited ammo and melee weapons never break so you're never defenseless. Endless add-on campaigns and modifications created by the Steam Community via Steam Workshop throw new challanges all the time at players as well. Even without mods, the game is very addictive, very fun, challenging at first, definitely worth the price, and is great to play with friends.

This game didn't make a good first impression on me. My friends said it was super fun, but I didn't like it very much. I like the aspect of sliding down hills to gain momentum and using a jetpack and such, but the controls are so odd and out of the ordinary, not like any other shooter. I mean, I get that this game wanted to be different, but a lot of people who play games with a common genre are going to expect similar controls, and when you give them very random controls, it isn't very fun trying to learn a new layout. For example, I am used to jumping (or using a jetpack/flying) in other games with the space bar, but you ski using the space bar. I normally expect to sprint with shift, but instead AIMING is on shift. How does that make any sense? Most games use the right mouse button to aim. One more thing, you don't just drop a game that is still very popular just to make or work on another game. Hi-Rez started working on Smite and just dropped this game. They used to try to make the best gaming experience, but now they are working super hard on Smite, which isn't even a fun game to begin with (in my opinion). All-In-All, Hi-Rez has to learn how to manage two games at once, like Activision. They could have two teams, one for Tribes and one for Smite. Activision has Treyarch working on games to be produced every other year, and Activision has Infinity Ward working on games to produce every other year, so that a Call of Duty game is made one year by Treyarch, and then the next year by Infinity Ward. If Hi-Rez had two teams for Tribes and Smite, Tribes might actually still be a good game. Ok, my rant is over.

XCOM: Enemy unknown and the following exapansion, Enemy within, are extremely entertaining to play. It's a rare game that doesn't shy away from difficulty and challenge while able to mixing these elements with fun and frustration. (at the time of writing this revies, I already finished Classic/Ironman on Enemy within and the review will reflect this)  The game put you in charge of XCOM, a paramilitary organization that fight the alien invasion. You have to manage your base, manage panic level of your supporting countries, and train your troops through combat against these extraterrestrial invaders. The game basically divided into two phase, Geoscope and TBS combat.  The Geoscope is rather bland but essential to the progress of your gamr and overall victory. You can't increase monthly funding without building sattelite. You can't build sattelite without building an uplink. You can't build an uplink without energy. Essentially, the game require planning and knowlwdge of previous failure to minimize the loss of time and resource. Moreover, your troops will die without weapon and armor upgrade which directly tied to the Geoscope as well.  The real fun (and frustration) is the TBS gameplay against the alien. Eliminating aliens means more money, more resource, and less panic. Your men recieve EXP point which increase their rank and unlock new perks and statistical increase. However, you will lose some of your men and perhaps some battles. You will see 90% hit chance shot miss three times in a row.  You will learn that you are your own greatest enemy. Hubris means death. It's a part of playing XCOM.   However, Enemy unknown suffered from slow pace combat due to overwatch trapping is the way to minimizre your loss. All player has to do is scout ahead, spot the alien, pull the scout back, put everyone on overwatch. The alien will charge at you (or not) and got shot to death.   The game also suffer from strange pacing. You will see greatest difficulty during the first two or three months but the rest of the game will be a breeze. The game gets easier the longer you play. While I understand that it's a part of the narrative, it's still feel 'not right'.  Enemy within isn't an actual DLC but an integrated expansion into the original game. 2K tackle the combat pacing with addition of MELD, a substance that allow you to turn your troop into cybernetic mechanized warrior (MEC) or genetically modified soldier. MELD urge player to hasten the pace and take some risk since it will self destruct after a while. A great addition to the game. MEC trooper can use flamethrower, puch alien in the face, shoot grenade, etc. But they also unable to take cover due to the gigantic size, cost considerable amount of MELD to create, and require your soldiers to take surgery that irreversibly turn them into a cyborg.  Genetic modification is less graphical but difinitely more creepy. Soldier undergone the gene modification will recieve power to jump to an extreme heigth, regenerate health, camoflague - at the cost of planting alien DNA within their body.   Not that these customizations not good (they are awesome!!) but it is still creepy. Especially considering Dr.Shen's reaction when the XCOM encounter Floater for the first time.   Enemy within also counter the sniper tactic from enemy unknown with Seeker, a matrix-ish octopus robot that cloak and strangle a lone trooper. Making it harder and riskier to leave your sniper alone in the rear and even harder to get MELD.  Alien also has an answer to your MEC trooper, the Mectoid. A sectoid grafted in a collosal exoskeleton that shoot plasma cannon with uncanny accuracy. Sectopod also recieve damage reduction from all source, forcing player to consider some debuff/buff perks instaed of pure damaging.   Enemy within also add another human faction, EXALT. Another paramilitary organization that use alien technology to fight the alien with earth-dominating purpose. They view XCOM as a threat and will sabotage your reaserch, steal your funding, and increase pani level. XCOM can respond by sending a lone agent to perform covert operation and a few days later, you have to send a team to extract your agent. Adding EXALT make the game more complex and a require a bit more juggling to cycle your troop between fighting the alien and fighting the EXALT.  Two sub-campaigns, Operation progeny and Slingshot, are rather hard to play since they add more problem during the first two months of the game. But the rewards are substantial, earlier access to advanced weaponry and guarantee Psionic soldier are definitely worth the endeavor.  Second wave options serve the game in term of replayability. These options can be added during the beginning of the game to add more chaos and difficulty to the game. Enemy within add a few more options. notably the training roulette. However, the greatest option of this game is Ironman mode which autosave the game for you after every move, essentially make all things permanent.   Overall, the game is great for strategy fans and Enemy within DLC is difinitely worth the price tag. A 9/10 for me.

Things I liked about the game   -Weapon customization is cool -Very fast and easy to start out with -Doesn't take long to build a decent gun -The art style and animation is cool -The ability to test out parts for your weapons is a very nice feature -Doesn't feel very pay-to-win at all, which is something they highly stressed -The beginning of the matches where everyone is grouped up in a circle is always a very funny moment -Gun play feel somewhat nice, could use improvements -Decent collection of gamemodes, would perfer more -Maps have different routes which is a fun time to figure out   Things I didn't like about the game. Keep in mind that the positives heavily outweigh the negatives   -First of all, the cosmetic stuff for your character is in my opinion way to expensive -I feel like a dollar should get you at least one day boost of bluetomian points instead you would have to buy 5$ worth of coins to get a 1 day boost. -Healing and buffing weapons, in my opinion, are somewhat useless if you're not using a laucher. What I mean is a laucher can buff and heal instantly and is AOE while other weapons require aiming at the target -Some maps are just too big for the 4v4 format -I felt like blast radius of grenades, rocket lauchers, and most importantly the hammer are just way to wide -Gun parts need more balancing -The competetive game mode is probably the best game mode yet you can't play it in casual -During the pre-game lobby the pull down menues don't do anything -In the store, when you are looking at a page with a scroll bar the scroll doesn't give any feedback on wether or not you are actually scrolling -Main menu is not controller capatiable -Everytime I have to change my resolution I have to quit and exit the game -Matchmaking is sometimes messed up for whatever reason  -Music is kind of boring for an exciting game like this   Things I wish for  -Custom game modes obviously -More gun parts -More maps -Better music -Better support options for healing and buffing -Fully controller compatiable -More balances -Gun skins maybe?  Overall I enjoyed my time with it and I will continue to play it.

Not bad, but didn't fix any of the problems. Let's start with the good things. The story isn't bad, as well as the graphics or city. There are a lot of side quests as well as a lot of enemies, which keeps things interesting. They add a few new items, as well as a few combat tricks which keep things interesting. Now the problem. There wasn't much done new. The combat is basically the same, its in the same city, you fight penguin, joker is the main enemy, killer croc, and bane. Fighting the same enemies every single game gets boring. Take a risk guys. They didn't fix the biggest problem with the last game, the platforming. You will be moving around the city and for some reasons tons of ledges won't be able to be used for grappling. You will even be looking at something you've used before it and won't let you, only to let you once you've looked at it in a tiny different angle. Half the time you grapple something behind you instead. They really screwed up navigating the city like this. Then there is combat/stealth. They make things not intuitive and not easy to use. To use a smoke pellet you can press a button, but only if you've been found does it work. Then later it won't work that same way, and you have to press a button to switch to it, then press 2 buttons at the same time to use it. I would constantly start getting shot but there would be nothing I could quickly do, which is just dumb. Most of the abilities in stealth are like this and require weapon switching and 2 button pressing, which aren't helpful in the moment. Same goes for dropping spray bomb. There is a quick use button, but to detonate you have to switch weapons to use it then hold 2 buttons (why?!). There are times with takedowns that just don't make sense. Like how when grabbing a guy from a gargoyle and doing inverted takedown, you can't cover his mouth, making it a loud takedown, which is dumb. When you try to grapple to ledges it automatically makes you get up and over, which is dumb if you want to do ledge takedowns. Same goes for grate takedowns, as he could easily grab them into the vent, but he jumps out into open for no reason. Multiple times you get stuck on the environment and the game just flat out doesn't work correctly. I would sly down from a stoop and land on a ledge right next to a guy standing there.......but you can't do a jump tackle from that close or do ANYTHING....I would press the attack button and my guy would just stand up on the ledge and try punching the air in front of him, instead of doing something productive like he should do, and I would ultimately get found out. Blade dodging and the shield run-up smash move both are spotty and unnecessarily hard to complete, due to direction and pressing the buttons and doing everything correctly, but them not working.   The camera could not be worse in the game. When you dodge or run in circles, it stays put and looks behind you instead of the way you are facing. You can turn it but it is a pain. Constantly in boss fights or in regular combat I would dodge out of the way and it would turn the camera in a way I couldn't see the people in front of me or the person coming at me, and let to me getting hit very often. It has to be one of the worst camera's in a 3rd person action game that I've seen in a while. It just doesn't move intuitively and always seems to be looking a way that doesn't help you. There are a few bad boss fights (2/2 Bane fights) where the mechanics don't work well by overcrowding with guys, and the advice the game gives you is wrong. Sometimes the game is unclear where you need to go or whether or not you can grapple onto something or use something, leading to you getting lost. This is annoying when you sometimes have to backtrack and other times don't. The game does a lame rip-off of assassins creed by having you do eagle-eye towers (sorry, called comms towers in this). They still love to do missions and things with 100's of collectibles, which should only be for art or something for those tryhards that want to waste time. Also costumes should be able to be used in first game. So due to the combat and stealth mechanics and button problems not being intuitive, or well done, the terrible platforming, and other smaller things, the game is a solid 7/10.

I had never known what a 'Rogue-like' was before I picked up this game; I just loved the idea of being a starship commander. Not just some lone shmuck flying a fighter or a transport ship between systems; an actual captain, with a real ship with compartments, corridors and subsystems and a real crew to obey my orders. As soon as I realized that this game could let me do that, I grabbed it.   FTL is a challenging game, all the more so by the fact that you get only one life - when you die, you're dead - that's what a 'Rogue-like' game is. As in real life, failure means the destruction of your ship and crew. That's ok - the game is designed for multiple play-throughs, and a full game takes only a couple of hours to finish. As you must always stay one step ahead of the onrushing enemy, you can never unlock everything during a single play session, and a large number of unlockable ships provides incentive to keep playing again and again.   Combat is frenetic and nail-biting, as you must manage everything seemingly at once. You must keep an eye on your hull integrity, move your crewmen from room to room in order to repair damage and keep key systems online, even repel boarders who will otherwise wreak havoc on your ship. All the while you have to try to blow the other guy out of space! A wide variety of different enemy ships and armaments means that the same tactics will rarely work twice.   On top of everything, there is a multi-layered RPG system in place. Keep crewmen at one duty post and they will gain skill in that discipline, increasing your effectiveness. Scrap collected after each encounter must be used wisely - do you spend it all to upgrade your ship, or keep some in the hopes of finding a shop and buying new weapons? Keep in mind that you also need it to buy ammo, fuel, and repair your hull! There's never enough resources to upgrade everything to the max - you must choose carefully and decide what to focus on.

A Future, Sci-Fi, Cyberpunk, mystery/thriller. I must say up front, I truely loved the storeline, and found it captivating, well written, and really enjoable to play through.  Pros: -Excellent Story - One of the best of the genre -Great graphics and visuals (when you take the time to look around, or can (more on that later) -decent music, ranging from etherial, and moving, occasional annoying glitch dubstep styles during fight scenes -very stable game - no real play through issues -the memory remix scenes very unique and A LOT of fun, like a little Minority Report in the middle of an action game  Cons: -Platforming! Platforming! Platforming! - A lot of needless, boring, platforming. It too bad because it take your attention aware from how beautiful the city scapes can be (sometimes it does give a great persepetive of height and elevation too). But honestly - I played through the game on standard setting in about 8-10 hours - by no means trying to rush it - but if you take out most of the useless platforming elements, the game would be 3-4 hours tops. -Typical Capcom crappy port from console. Lazy key bindings a lot of 'Start/Enter,' 'Back/ESC,' picture of a mouse with arrows instead of the thumbstick. -As stated above, the controls can leave something to be desired, and feel clumsy and ackward sometimes. A few times I died or loss a fight beacuse of controls and not skill -the camera angles can be ackward at times and make things challenging.  Overall - I really enjoyed the game. The story was beautiful, unique, and well written. I will likely remember the plot line for a long time. I paid less than $7 for this on a steam sale. It was definitely worth it. Is it worth $30.....no, wait for it go back on sale.

She gets half your money, breaks your heart, and did it for no reason other than she heard a rumor through a distant relative's distant acquaintance's 2nd cousin's roomate that you could possibly be cheating (even though you weren't).  The long Answer: Wouldn't work through steam for me, but I still managed to play it. Clocked in several hours (despite my hours on record, which consists of me trying to get it to work through steam) made it to level 35 and tried to look past the problems:  1. Type advantages also seems pretty arbitrary, as I've taken out water type enemies with fire moves fairly readily, sure electric does more, but not super substantially so like in the previous game, and some enemies/dungeons are so difficult you HAVE to over level even accounting for all boss mechanics.  2. Buying way too heavily into the whole fee to play thing. CONSTANTLY reminding you that you can buy these things for crafting or reviving yourself or to look pretty. Also 14 day limits on things? But then, even after ignoring all these glaring signs they decided that wasn't enough. They permanently banned me for absolutely no reason. Early Review-2.2 hours into the game. This is my first review EVER on steam. So keep in mind this game pushed me to write my first review EVER. After trying it with mouse+keyboard I reminded myself I had an old Logitech Controller (the like of a PS4), so I plugged that in my Mac and the experience changed completely: I felt empowered.  I almost forgot I backed this game. It took so long for this to come out.  Then I understood why.  This game is a masterpiece.  The testing and mechanics are refined and you can see the work the developer put into this. It's a Zelda meets Diablo 3 (withouth the loot collection part) meets Super Meat Boy (...and then you die...a lot...but you'll feel like going at it again) meets Dark Souls. The good thing is that it takes all those inspirations and merges them into a new game (and I already hope other games will draw from it, and this deserves a sequel or similar game with different characters).  You have to discover everything for yourselves: where to go, how to find currency to buy an upgrade, how to reach certain areas, how to beat enemies.  The combat is freaking awesome: try pulling incredible sequences without being touched when your life is low, and breathe the satisfaction as soon as you kill the last enemy of a small group. Man I haven't felt like that in a long time. The drift mechanic also reminds me of the Velocity Series teleport mechanic.  The art is stunning and draws from Fez, but has the variety and landscapes you find in a Sword and Sworcery. Animation is incredible and full of detail for a pixel art isometric game: animals living their lives around you, weather effects, and so on... The music in incredibly butiful and dynamic and it's made by Disasterpiece, the guy that made the Fez soundtrack. All in all I can safely say this should be considered as the indie game of the year for now, and I think it has to have a chance in the GOTY discussion. Haven't felt like this in a long time. When I came into this game, I wasn't thinking about A Machine for Pigs. I was imagining The Dark Descent, the game this was supposed to surpass. I can gladly say, I was not disappointed. I don't wish to spoil any moment of the game for anybody wanting to play this game. I would recommend it simply because the atmosphere is completely perfect for this game, and for the developers. I was on the edge of my seat the entire time. They perfectly combine a few well-timed jump-scares with an extremely tense, anxiety-inducing atmosphere and monsters that instill fear in you without the need for overly-grotesque looks. The graphics for the game simply add to the experience, looking like an extremely beautifully built world, through the entire game. I would definitely recommend this game to anybody who likes horror games, or even the horror genre as a whole.  Gameplay: 9/10 Story: 9/10 Music: 8/10 Overall score: 9/10

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Qct9nvz-xD0    Doorways, the Underworld claims to be an adventure horror game. It is split into two parts; Puzzles, which I found more to be of a scavenger quests that send you in endless repetitive loops, and the horror element, which is completely lacking in its ability to invoke psychological  scariness. It is more like a simulation of a coal miner than a horror game. If you are a child, or make it a point to avoid horror games, number one; why are you playing this game, and two; this game might scare you. Maybe.  The bare bones story element will leave you wondering why you are wandering around these caves in the first place.    This game doesn't respect your time. The puzzles are simplistic, and the graphics are basic, and it is most definitely NOT horrific. I cannot,  will not recommend this game.   This is a summary of my review video, which I encourage you to watch if you are looking for a more in-depth review. { Review }  That gameplay is quite nice with all the designing and variety of monsters + simple combat system. I like the whole evolve and fusion concept which makes you wonder how strong your next evolution is. Unfortunetly, I wouldn't recommend this game to those who lack money and patience. You do not need to have money to do well and have fun in this game but you do require lots of patience. This game is literally a pay to win game. The pvp system is unbalanced at all as you might face a relatively weak opponent or an overpowered one.  { Finaly review }  Pros: - Combat system is simple and fun - Variety of different monsters to fight and capture - The content is very simple yet vivid and varied - A good way to spend a few hours if you're bored  cons:  - Energy regain is way too slow - PvP is unbalanced - Gets quite boring after finishing a few levels as the grinding is heck without paying - Easily hackable as I have seen in videos  - Literally pay to win or be on top and finish high end levels and face strong PvP players  Overall : I'd give it a 5/10 and do not recommend this game   Thank you for reading this review but please do not trust me based on my opinion as it is just an opinion of my personal experience. A very fun action/adventure game that doesn't disappoint.  I've read a lot of things about it being similar to the later Legend of Zelda games, and while I find that somewhat true, I'd compare it more to something like Soul Reaver mixed with Devil May Cry because of it's dark atmosphere, unfolding lore, and fighting mechanics.  It's a very neat IP to make into a game and it was well executed I think. It was never too tedious or complicated to progress to your next objective/major story point. The openness of the areas is just enough to let you explore, but not turn the game into hours and hours of hunting for what to do like games of yesterday. Combat is fun but I feel could have been a bit deeper. A number of enemies are essentially recycled as stronger versions, but many hack and slash style games (like DMC) do this already, so I don't complain too much. A few items/weapons add some spice to gameplay but collectibles for the most part are just items for steady strength building. I was really hoping for some 'secret' or amazing things to find, but there really isn't.  Boss fights are fun and actually reminiscent of the later 3D Zelda games where you find a weakness, exploit, attack, repeat -- but the design for each is varied and keeps things fresh.  I received this with the THQ Humble Bundle quite some time ago and was glad I sat down and took the time to play it. Straight playthrough probably took me somwhere around 8-14 hours (I wasn't rushing but wanted to progress the story and find unlocks along the way and the same time) Worth the money I paid 10x over.

Despite having major issues in the begining, crashing, bugs etc, I really enjoyed playing this game. Like the other Total War games it has a mix of a turn based campaign map where you build up your Cities, Towns, Infrastructure and strategicaly move your troops. Real-Time battles from Open Fields and Seiging Forts to Sea Battles with fleets of heavy first rate ships of the line.  Still the game has issues but they can be looked past, because who doesn't love conquering enemy nations with several 'stacks of doom'.  Pros : Game looks great on high graphics settings. Sound is Great. Great Ship battles with a veriety of ships. 3 different Continents to conquer - Americas, Europe and India. Large open fields to fight on with large army's. Historical Battles. Able to save presets for troop selection (Multiplayer and custom battles). Different victory conditions. Multiplayer, though you don't really see any games on it any more (luckily all the achievments are soloable against the AI).  Cons :  Diplomacy needed alot more work, AI can be completely stupid when it comes to the negotiations asking for rediculas things. AI on open field battle needed a bit more work, but its not really noticable here when your having fun slaughtering them all lol. The battle results - Heroic victory etc displays wrong. Poorly Optimized for AMD Graphics cards because its sponsored by Nvidia. Kill 1 million enemies Achievment... ugh this is 1 boring grind, 400+ hours and I haven't hit 200k kills yet.  My advice is to wait for when it's on sale but it's still too good of a game to wait for that :P

Join in on the epic quest for more STUFF.   Similar to Minecraft, but ultimately more concerned with combat, and it has a greater sense of progression.  Specifically, you want to get better stuff, so that you can survive boss fights that you want to win so you can loot their corpses and/or unlock more content so that you can fashion the debris into more stuff you can use for the next boss fight.   Unlike Minecraft, where you have to make your own fun, it does add more structure, so you might enjoy that more if you don't want to build your own palace. However, it also has a definite end point, even if a trickle of content expansions have extended this end point considerably.   This results in a point where you find there's not much left to do, provided you don't want to go on massive terraforming projects, because combat is really the biggest draw, and eventually, there's not much left to kill. Because of constant power-ups and expansions, earlier-game content is a joke unless you strip down gear on purpose to add back challenge, but since the reason to kill bosses in the first place is to get loot you don't have... Likewise, terraforming is probably not for everyone, and it's not quite as rewarding in Terraria as it is in Minecraft, since the 2d isn't quite as fun to explore.  That said, for the price, definitely, it's one of the best gameplay-to-price ratios you can buy, and you'll be playing for a hundred hours before you hit the end.

A nice mix between Minecraft and Castlevania, Terraria is an action-rpg set in a randomly-generated sandbox. After character and world creation, the game gives you an axe, a pickaxe, a sword, and no direction except a confused-looking man who refers to himself as 'The Guide'. From there, you'll dig deep into winding caverns, dive into claustrophobic dungeons, and fight nasty, ridiculous bosses. And if you ever can't progress on your own, bring some friends. app_name: Tom Clancy's Splinter Cell: Chaos Theory
review_text: Chaos Theory is probably the best of the Splinter Cell games when it comes to stealth and espionage. It doesn't have the same 'badass' feel as Conviction, but here you're more about NOT BEING SEEN than looking cool.   The game is mission-based and although each mission has several objectives, only the primary ones are needed to complete the mission. How you complete your mission is very open-ended and there are often multiple paths and ways of doing things. You can be as ruthless as you want, deciding whether or not to kill people (or simply avoid them entirely).  Despite having been released in 2005, the game still looks impressive. The controls are complex and some people might have a learning curve to get over, but please stick with it. Unlike lots of more recent games, Chaos Theory allows you to not only save (both manual and quick saves) whenever and wherever you want, but name them as well.  All in all, I suggest playing it if you like stealth games. If not, don't bother.

Recommended ONLY for Veteran players that wish to revisit Norrath. It will be oddly heart breaking.  Over 5,000 hours played on one character alone. The community has changed substantially over the years and the charm many of us felt for EQ on initial release has long since fizzled. The 'awe' and 'excitement' has waned over the years and the wonder of exploration has died a slow, painful death. PVP is a joke (even moreso than initially). Old players fondly remember the earlier involvement of GMs with events in the Plains of Karana and remember the first time being run over by trains in Karnor's Castle. It's hard to place a finger on it, but this game simply is NOT the same. Having said that, I still put over 300 hours into this game after being away for several years, so there is still an odd draw to the game. Nevertheless, this game is not ideal for the new player and I only recommend this to veterans who wish to jump on from time to time. Your old characters are still there, so that IS something.

I've been on the beta for this game for...I want to say 2 years. Red 5 has had some ups and downs over that time, and the game has gone from relatively 'fine' but buggy to what I now consider the penultimate version. Where PvP was once their (futile) focus, they finally realized nobody gave a damn about their plans to turn Firefall into the next big competitive multiplayer shooter and re-focused the game on PvE, which was the right choice.  As a result, the game is now relatively solid, with a good 50 or 60 hours of content, depending on how fast you play. At the moment there's a level cap of 40, but there's a lot to do to get you to that point. The world they've created for Firefall is compelling, epic and/or far-reaching, and utterly gorgeous. The creature designs range from brilliant to boring, but most of them are scary enough just via appearance to make you want to stay the hell away from them, and that's good!   The major problem I have with the game is how Red 5 has approached world design in relation to their in-game store. This is a BIG world, and it will take you quite a bit of time to simply run/jumpjet from one side of it to the other. As a result, they've implemented methods of transport to get from one side of the world to the other; nothing that can be classified as fast travel, but there are glider pads that let you sprout energy wings so you can cover large distances fairly quickly, and there are motorcycles that can get you across the terrain even faster, providing you don't touch water.   Because in the far future, they have energy barriers that can push back an evil planet-enveloping warp-weapon from another dimension, but making equipment waterproof is ♥♥♥♥ing impossible.   Where was I? Oh yes, traveling. As far as gliders and bikes are concerned, there are several types available in the game. You can craft them all eventually, or you can pay for the coolest stuff up front. Whether you want to do that or not is your call, but the main problem I have with this game is how they seem to purposefully inconvenience players by making mission waypoints ludicrously far apart so as to drive impatient people to just dropping $20 on a fast bike or unlimited glider-wings. That's a terrible idea, and it's ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ basically everyone off. I am not one of those impatient people. It doesn't help, of course, that crafting the best stuff is RIDICULOUSLY expensive in terms of in-game resources.   Anyway. Firefall is fun. It has a lot of character classes to suit your play style, it's sufficiently challenging in places, and they're supposedly dumping a lot new content soon. If you've been curious about the game up until this point, NOW would be a great time to start playing it.

OTHER than that, the CO-OP is fun, the single player is fun, and it's a lot of fun unlocking everything and re-doing missions. Well worth it for less than $30. (check Ebay, you can usually buy a key from there for around $20)

One of the most fun FPS games I've played in a long time. The only gamemode that's played is CTG which stands for Catch the Ghost, it's pretty much capture the flag but with some interesting changes. The player holding the 'flag' (Ghost) gets the ability to see the locations of the enemy players through the walls, but at the same time all players can see the location of the player holding the ghost. The player holding the ghost is pretty much incapable of taking out the enemies solo since it can't hold a main gun or run when holding the ghost so the player has to rely on communcating the enemy location to his/her teammates.  It has quite a high learning curve but well worth putting time into, feels very rewarding when playing good. If I were to compare it to another game it's a bit like CS:GO and Quake had a kid.  Pros + Advanced movement techniques and strategic gameplay + High average skill and skill ceiling (if you're into that) + Extremely dedicated and nice community + Nice A E S T H E T I C S + Balanced weapons and classes (there's 3 of them)  Cons - Gun mechanics are pretty much non-existent, after the first shot the gun just sprays in a cone. - Map layouts are quite hard to memorize (imo) - Outdated graphics (the aesthetics makes up for it) - Very small player base (average somewhere around 15 players online)  This game is definitely not for everyone (see the bad reviews), but you should give it a chance!

So I bought this game during the winter sale and it was for 6 dollars from its usual 30 dollars (a fantastic deal for a fantastic game. I had not heard of anything about this game other than that it's user reviews were extremely high and it's genre tags were what I was interested in. Going in knowing nothing I was VERY overwhelmed and that is an inevitability for someone who equally doesn't know the mechanics about this game. It's a game of graphs, paragraphs, and statistics as you try and run a country of your choosing. You must appeal to many different types of voters of your choosing through the use of implementing policies each turn (each turn is a quarter of a year ingame). You will need to do this if you want to be re-elected for another term. You must try to do this for as long as possible or you can choose to have a limit to the amount of terms you serve (therefore a definite end to your game). It tracks through your actions if your more conservative, liberal, socialist, capitolist, etc. All of this is fascinating. But by far my favorite thing about this game is its interface. It's very sleek, neat, while complex at the same time. The game has a pretty good tutorial which I highly recommend going through rather than skipping it (I did that on my first try and I regretted it). Know that you will never please all of your voter groups and this can lead to a potential assassinations along the line. Even though this game can seem very overwhelming at first glance, once you beat the learning curve, it is highly addicting and an actual fun way to learn more about political systems (even for people like me who hate politics). 10/10.

-Quick Review-   ...aaaaaand today's forecast is; a chance of explosions, with a side dish of pain, a little bit of agony, a whole lot of fun, and a Risk of Rain. Risk of Rain is an platform indie game with many rogue-like elements. You unlock new characters and items, to help you through the game. The game is very fun and challenging, but if you want to make it to the end, you need to play smart and use your time wisely.     -Detailed breakdown review-     Story:  There's not much to say about the story in this game, the description text explains that 'You are the sole survivor of a space train wreck', however that is not the case if you're playing multiplayer; then you can have *four* survivors, but I digress.    Game Play:  There is a lot to say about the game play. Firstly, the controls are pretty simple, you have several attacks (all of which are different based on the character you're playing), you move to the left, right, and jump. There are numerous unlockables throughout the game, new characters and items. There are actually unlockables that you can only get with certain characters, so that keeps you playing and trying out new things. The game advertises itself as 'full of rogue elements' and to a degree it's quite right, but there are some consistent factors: the first level is always the same, time continues to tick... the longer you play = the harder the game gets.    Achievements:  There are 15 achievements for this game. I suspect the reason I haven't written the review for this great game yet, because I... shamefully... haven't been able to unlock the last 3 achievements I need. (at this time: 4/16/15) There are only a few easy achievements that you unlock just by playing it, most achievements you will need to do something very specific. Be it, find a new character to unlock, or use a specific item on another specific item/NPC.    Price:  Risk of Rain goes for $9.99, I personally waited for a sale (much to the frustration of my real life friends who wanted to play it with me)... but in the end that's a fair price for this game, based on how much time I've spent playing it.    Conclusion:  I've mused to myself in the past thinking 'I wonder if these older indie games will age differently then console or ROM games'. It seems like many indie games age well because they have unique-addictive-artistic-theme's to them. I absolutely recommend this game, it isn't one of those 'Rack up to 100 hours' games for most people, but it is a fun change of pace from the other types of games out there. One final note: this game shines when it comes to multiplayer, playing on your own gets old, playing with friends feels like you're playing a classic arcade game with friends.

Not a bad game at all. Game starts off by leaving you stranded on an island with virtually nothing but your wits and clothes, and was quite an enjoyable experience for me. The talent system felt kind of lacking and pointless to me, however, and the game offers only up to about 15 hours of gameplay i'd say. After that it'll probably rot in your library un-touched. Combat is not too bad, controls are pretty solid, graphics are kinda nice, and the game is an ok pc-port, dis-regarding the fact that controller buttons still show up instead of keys. I would say to wait for a %50 sale+ since the game won't last you very long. If the storyline had been longer or had more things to do, i would consider buying it full price (I picked it up on a sale for $3)  UPDATE: (10/3/14) Lately, a lot of other DLC have been added to the game which potentially boost the playtime of it to around, say, 20-30 hours depending on how fast you play. If you do plan on purchasing the DLC along with the game, go for the bundle (If you want the El Diablo Islands DLC you'll have to wait a bit before they add it to the package). Question is, do i recommend the purchase of the DLC? The answer, however, is totally dependent on how you feel about the game and whether you enjoyed it or not. I consider H2S a hidden gem on the steam market of the zombie/survival genre, and I would recommend the DLC if you have the money. Even without DLC, the game is completely playable and should still provide a fun experience. Basically, if you have your doubts about the DLC, don't buy them. If you liked the game, get the DLC. (Try and wait for a sale also, unless you just want the game that badly)  TL;DR DLC add about an extra 10 hours to the game and I do recommend if you enjoyed the game, if you have mixed feelings then i don't recommend them.

After playing this game over a couple times and finally beating it, thanks to a guide on the discussions section, I've come to realize that a lot of your skills don't really matter in this game. There are certain scripted situations of the story that require you to have a certain skill at a certain level, otherwise, you're forced automatically into a bad end.   SPOILERS AHEAD    Near the end of the game, you are rather randomly attacked by another kingdom. Being a person who maxed out the Military branch, I assumed I'd have to pass a few military skill checks and I'd come out victorious. This is not the case. When the enemy king/duke/whatever reaches your castle, you either need to be good with your Lumen abilities (I neglected it entirely and its entirely possible to kill or imprison the person willing to help early in the game, botching your chances at winning) or have a good singing skill and have to pass another skill check afterward. With nearly all choices that are non-lumen, you pretty much end up dying.   This makes your skill choices seem very narrow and selective on other playthroughs of the game. In the end, your skills don't really matter and you don't really get enough time to develop the ones that would help you pass a lot of the skill checks in the game.   Skills aside, the game is pretty fun. I turned my character into a angry warmonger (only for her to die in the spoiler scenario above) that crushed a rebellion. The art design of the characters, save for the main character seem to be a little mediocre and just creepy at times. (The king's mouth reminds me of the 'That makes me moist' old lady meme.) The lore and characters of the game are pretty forgettable, save for a couple recurring characters (notably the main character), as they thrust the names of random kingdoms and people you have yet to meet (if even meet at all) with lengthy titles at you from the very start. Being married or single in the game doesn't really matter, and doesn't seem to really affect the game much, if at all. It has the potential to be a really good game, but in my opion, it doesn't quite make it and ultimately left me feeling unsatisfied.   Bottom Line:  Do I suggest you buy this game? Unfortunately, no. It has a lot of potential, but in the end, it falls flat of my expectations for such a game.   For those of you who want a simple pros and cons list of this game from my personal opinion, here it is:  PROS A lot of skills to develop. Its fun trying to get the main character in the right mood to develop select skills faster. Its satisfying when you DO pass a skill check during the story and it influences it a bit. Unlocking costumes for each skill set is interesting.  The main character is super kawaii desu-desu. The 'morning' classes remind me of a portion the Totino's commercial by Tim and Eric:  http://youtu.be/2lPX5b9m7ro?t=2m18s   CONS A lot of skills to develop and not enough time to fully develop the majority of them. Forgettable characters and kingdom names/lore. Linear story the requires certain skills developed otherwise unavoidable dead end. Choices in skill outside story-related skill checks don't really matter. Somewhat mediocre-feeling character art design.  Lumen is important to story, yet can be easily overlooked due to choices at the start of the game. Doesn't fulfill its potential. Not enough tentacle monsters.

StarDrive 2 impresses me in a lot of ways. Firstly, the music and graphics are outstanding. Secondly, the ship editor is both in-depth and intuitive...though I'm still trying to figure out optimal designs as the default ones don't seem to last very long in combat. The races themselves have unique personalities and I appreciate their quirks and dialogue. The tech tree is different in the sense that you have to pick between 2-3 techs every tier, meaning you'll pass on the others and may never get them. This made me really think about what kind of empire I wanted. Combat was also very fun and satisfying...I liked watching my creations attempt to work with what I gave them (shields and beam weapons, mostly).  As far as what I don't like: the game doesn't tell you when a planet is not producing anything (it does tell you when new research is available) and the AI is still too difficult, even on 'Easy'. I am not ashamed to say that I could be playing better and designing my ships more efficiently, but I'd also like to see 'Easy' made even easier...that is, I'd like to build up my empire in relative peace and then attack when I'm ready. It would be great if there was a way to turn off the AI's ability to attack altogether and instead have it play a defensive game. Either that, or limit their CP in some way. Of course, there are those that think 'Brutal' is too easy and don't want the developer to change anything. That's why there are multiple difficulty levels...I'm sure it's possible to change one difficulty setting without touching the others, making the 'don't nerf the AI for the casuals' complaints irrelevant. Some of us don't want a challenge, just a way to unwind after working 10 hour shifts. I'm just the type that prefers a 'light' challenge...*shrug*, everyone's mileage will differ. If some feel that 'Easy' is just right, then perhaps an 'Easier' difficulty setting could be added.  Long story short, casual 4X players may get frustrated (like I have) at times when the AI decides to come at you with a vengence despite your attempts to be passive. Difficulty aside I'm really enjoying this one and I can easily recommend it to 4X fans.          YouTube™ Video:  DGA Plays: StarDrive 2 (Ep. 1 - Gameplay / Let's Play)

I'm on the fence whether to downvote this game or to upvote. I'll post it as an upvote for now, but I'm teetering on the edge, simply because of a few factors that are pretty noticable once you start playing for a while.  The biggest factor being that it's too easy. Yes, sure, there's a new game plus, but the base game was so easy that I'm pretty sure that a new game plus wouldn't be too much harder to be honest.  Another factor to consider is the length of the game. Had I not experimented with different features of this game and always played stealthily taking no chances, it's probably a three hour game at the very least.  Now the good things about this game are that it has a stealth system, strikingly similar to that of Dishonored's. Dishonored was a great game to me; I replayed it many many times. So to have stealth similar to Dishonored's really made me ecstatic.  I also like roguelikes, and this is definitely a roguelike. Although like I said before, it's a pretty easy roguelike, so it's not a 'true' roguelike, if that makes sense.  All in all, Eldritch IS a good game, but again, as I have said before, this game's VERY short and a little on the easy side. Definitely pick it up when it's on a sale.

Borderlands pre-sequel was designed to fill in the details between BL and BL2. Based on Elpis, the moon of Pandora, and Helios space station you meet Jack and discover how and why he changed into Handsome Jack. I thought the story was pretty good but depressing in some parts. Loved the Aussie accents and their little quirks and sayings which might explain why many people hate those parts of the mission. Loads of references to Aussie culture, like the snicket game, the swearing, the foul mouth gun Bognalla, janey Springs and her three words of wisdom, the swagman , Oz kits, the Dahl respawn station quotes, Red Belly and many more.  The gameplay is a lot easier than BL2 where tens of millions of damage were needed to defeat enemies. Cryo guns, and butt slams are so much better than slag but corrosive and fire less so understandably as there is little oxygen on the moon. Was possible to progress in the game with guns five levels below the mission level and a lot more legendaries from vendors.  However, some of the badass map achievements are mission related so fail to get it means you can not return unless you reset. Others only allow one person to complete the rank per day. At the moment only some of the DLC has been released and thats only been arenas which I am not fond of. And Jacks body double which I loved playing cause he did and said stuff I so wasnt expecting. Some of the boss fights are annoying too like DeadLift and his impossible Dead Weight badass achievement.  Can I recommend this? Definitely, but wait for a special and try and get BL 2 as well because then the pre sequel will make a lot more sense. Already spent over 350 hours here and will spend a few more as more stuff gets released.

I rarely give out reviews as it is very rare to find a game worth commenting on.  However, when I went to the store page to pre-purchase Tempest, I was astonished to see that a bunch of Chinese players, angry that the developers had removed their language mod because the mod maker itself was promoting a pirated version of the game. Honestly, I was both astonished and angry that a bunch of players would throw an outright tantrum over something like that.  The reality is that Endless Legend is a shining gem in the 4x community. It is factions that are very engaging and different from one another and a compelling narrative behind each of them. Honestly, I haven't seen factions made in such a way since Sid Meier's Alpha Centauri, which had very memorable factions and faction leaders. The game is also very interesting with the winter dynamic that comes every now and again to test your faction. The winters will continue to get heavier and longer as the game progresses, which ties into the story a bit.  So where does this game falter? Honestly, the combat leaves a bit to be desired. It's completely automated for the most part and you only really direct your armies on a very minimal level. If in-depth combat is what you're looking for, you're better off looking at Age of Wonders 3. Otherwise, it's a pretty solid addition to the 4x formula and I consider it a must have for anyone who enjoys Civilization or other 4x's. It shouldn't come as any surprise that Civ VI is borrowing a lot of the mechanics from Endless Legend given how well they worked.  But yeah, the game is amazing and is nowhere near deserving of a mixed rating. The Chinese players should suck it up and not throw a tantrum because their language mod got removed for promoting piracy. It's a risk you take when the game doesn't have the native language.

Ryse: Son of Rome would've been one of the best original IPs released in recent years, but unfortunately it is merely 'aight.' Crytek crafted a game which is one of the most beautiful that I have ever seen, and the fact that I took every opportunity to take a screenshot is a testament to that fact.   Ryse, however, suffers from gameplay which many have called repetitive, and going into this game I sort of brushed those criticisms aside, as I have enjoyed Hack 'n slash games such as Spartan: Total Warrior, and of course, the Dynasty Warriors series. The gameplay here though is clunky, and devolves into pressing X-X-Y-X-X-LT almost everytime. This is not the biggest problem though, as pressing LT when prompted takes you into a no fail slow-mo execution attack which slows down the gameplay extremely frequently, as you will be doing it to EVERY enemy.   A further flaw with Ryse is that it's campaign is extremely short, and the story is not as well fleshed out as it deserves to be.   Despite these damning flaws, Ryse is a very interesting title. As stated before, the graphics are AMAZING, the story has a lot interesting facets to it later on, there are many jaw dropping set pieces, the setting is interesting, and the atmosphere is engrossing. Ryse, despite being one of the most repetitive games I have ever played, is worth a look if you want a game with a Roman setting that looks good, and tells an interesting tale. Pick it up on sale like me if you can, however. (I got it for $20)   7/10
