Before you read this review, I'm going to point out that if you have not played '999: 9 hours, 9 persons, 9 doors' and 'Zero Escape: Virtues Last Reward' that you should go do so before you consider purchasing and playing this game. If you can't purchase and play those first two games in this trilogy, then do the next best thing and watch a playthrough on youtube for them both. All three games are excellent and totally worth checking out. And much like you wouldn't start by first reading the third book in a trilogy, you should not play this game until you have experienced the first two. Trust me, you'll thank me later.   Zero Escape: Zero Time Dilemma is the final part of the Zero Escape trilogy. The theme of the 3 games is very similar: 9 trapped individuals are forced to take part in a dark and brutal game which makes these participants question just how badly they want to escape and how much they're willing to sacrifice of themselves and others to do so.   In Zero Time Dilemma, 9 participants are sealed inside a bomb shelter and forced to play what is known as the Decision Game, in which the players are forced to make difficult choices that - in a lot of cases - have severe consequences, including death. Unfortunately, the 9 participants of this game are told that in order to escape, 6 of the 9 participants will need to die in order to open the X-door which leads to freedom.   The game is divided into 3 different parts: Cinematics, escape rooms, and decisions. In the cinematics - which comprise a large part of this story-driven game - the story unfolds as the various characters interact with each other and occasionally reveal some of their past. In the escape rooms, you as the player must check the room for useful items, solve puzzles, and figure a way to get out. And in the decision sequences, you as the player are forced to make an impactful choice that will lead the story in one direction or another.  The beauty of this game is that the story has multiple paths and multiple timelines thanks to all the choices you can make. For example, choosing to kill off 3 of the 9 participants will result in a completely different timeline than the one where all 9 participants are alive. Some of these choices can lead to a prompt GAME OVER screen, but others will lead to very interesting plot developments. Thanks to the structure of the story and the players ability to choose which route to take and which section to play, the games story comes together like a beautiful jigsaw puzzle. Some parts might be quite confusing until a different part of the story is played, but when the pieces snap together in your mind it's certainly a euphoric 'Ohhhh!' feeling.   Without revealing too much, the game stays true to the rest of the Zero Escape series and delivers some great twists along the way. Once the story gets going, it's a tough game to put down. And then even after it's over, it's likely to stick in your mind for a good long while.   Zero Escape: Zero Time Dilemma is an incredibly exciting and compelling game to play, and one that does the series proud. If you love amazing stories, you owe it to yourself to first check out the first two games in this series, and ONLY THEN play Zero Time Dilemma.

Sadly, very sadly I cannot recommend this. I bought this because I thought it would be a nice blend between civilizations and RPG, and it is somewhat in that direction, true, but unfortunately there is SO. LITTLE. CONTENT. I had great fun for the first 10 hours, enjoyed the next 5 hours, but after that I only wanted to win a game and see if there's more to it, but again sadly there isn't.  City: You can build buildings (about 10 different ones) which all either boost a stat of people IN YOUR CITY (so it doesn't benefit your warriors roaming around the map) and/or give a chance for a new citizen to join you. You can craft objects; different weapons, armours and crafting/gathering tools, also different kinds of food. You can assign people to gather nearby resources ONLY in the 6 Hexes directly around your city, which may or may not have anything at all on them (random) and... that's mostly it. There are sometimes random events like storms, demons which might be nice or naughty, or rites and festivals, in these you mostly have different options to choose from, which will give different results, but there are waaay to few of these to be interesting longer than a few hours.  Expedition: Most of your gameplay will be your Expeditions. You form them in your City, you can send however many and whomever you want out. You need to give them food (else they take damage each turn) and fuel (else they cannot heal when they make camp), and preferably weapons and armor, as they will fight all the time. Out on the Map you encounter enemies which you can kill (or defeat through other means, like diplomacy or tactics) and different random events like a lone elven wanderer that may join you after you help them out, or a frosty queen and her escort which will send you on a quest. You can make camp with your expedition, which allows your people to gather ressources from the 6 surrounding Hexes and heals them faster. There IS a main-story, and quite some backstory if you are willing to read a lot of text, however I didn't find it intriguing enough to keep reading after the first 10 hours.  Bonus Hate: Events sometimes let you trade items with travelers, but what you give them is ALWAYS RANDOM. So you might give your best weapon away for something useless, or you may offer someone fuel, and instead of giving them normal firewood you give them extremely rare ancient wood instead.  tl;dr: TOO LITTLE CONTENT if you're fine with 20 hours of fun, I DO recommend this! If you want more, get something else.  Also I have to say that the combat is VERY well made, once you get behind it. It is less random than many people say, the trick is that you have to get the initiative and plan from there ;)

Joseph Stalin or Iosif Vissarionovich Stalin (Russian: Ио́сиф Виссарио́нович Ста́лин, pronounced [ˈjɵsʲɪf vʲɪsɐˈrʲɵnəvʲɪtɕ ˈstalʲɪn]; born Ioseb Besarionis Dze Jugashvili, Georgian: იოსებ ბესარიონის ძე ჯუღაშვილი, pronounced [iɔsɛb bɛsɑriɔnis dzɛ dʒuɣɑʃvili]; 18 December 1878[1] – 5 March 1953) was the leader of the Soviet Union from the mid-1920s until his death in 1953.  Among the Bolshevik revolutionaries who took part in the Russian Revolution of 1917, Stalin was appointed general secretary of the party's Central Committee in 1922. He subsequently managed to consolidate power following the 1924 death of Vladimir Lenin through suppressing Lenin's criticisms (in the postscript of his testament) and expanding the functions of his role, all the while eliminating any opposition. He remained general secretary until the post was abolished in 1952, concurrently serving as the Premier of the Soviet Union from 1941 onward.  Under Stalin's rule, the concept of 'socialism in one country' became a central tenet of Soviet society. He replaced the New Economic Policy introduced by Lenin in the early 1920s with a highly centralised command economy, launching a period of industrialization and collectivization that resulted in the rapid transformation of the USSR from an agrarian society into an industrial power.[2] However, the economic changes coincided with the imprisonment of millions of people in correctional labour camps.[3] The initial upheaval in agriculture disrupted food production and contributed to the catastrophic Soviet famine of 1932–1933, known as the Holodomor in Ukraine. Between 1934 and 1939 he organized and led a massive purge (known as 'Great Purge') of the party, government, armed forces and intelligentsia, in which millions of so-called 'enemies of the Soviet people' were imprisoned, exiled or executed. In a period that lasted from 1936 to 1939, Stalin instituted a campaign against enemies within his regime. Major figures in the Communist Party, such as the old Bolsheviks, Leon Trotsky, and most of the Red Army generals, were killed after being convicted of plotting to overthrow the government and Stalin.[4]  In August 1939, after failed attempts to conclude anti-Hitler pacts with other major European powers, Stalin entered into a non-aggression pact with Nazi Germany that divided their influence and territory within Eastern Europe, resulting in their invasion of Poland in September of that year, but Germany later violated the agreement and launched a massive invasion of the Soviet Union in June 1941. Despite heavy human and territorial losses, Soviet forces managed to halt the Nazi incursion after the decisive Battles of Moscow and Stalingrad. After defeating the Axis powers on the Eastern Front, the Red Army captured Berlin in May 1945, effectively ending the war in Europe for the Allies.[5][6] The Soviet Union subsequently emerged as one of two recognized world superpowers, the other being the United States.[7] The Yalta and Potsdam conferences established communist governments loyal to the Soviet Union in the Eastern Bloc countries as buffer states. He also fostered close relations with Mao Zedong in China and Kim Il-sung in North Korea.  Stalin led the Soviet Union through its post-war reconstruction phase, which saw a significant rise in tension with the Western world that would later be known as the Cold War. During this period, the USSR became the second country in the world to successfully develop a nuclear weapon, as well as launching the Great Plan for the Transformation of Nature in response to another widespread famine and the Great Construction Projects of Communism.  In the years following his death, Stalin and his regime have been condemned on numerous occasions, most notably in 1956 when his successor Nikita Khrushchev denounced his legacy and initiated a process of de-Stalinization. He remains a controversial figure today, with many regarding him as a tyrant.[8] However, popular opinion within the Russian Federation is mixed.[9][10][11] Stalin is attributed to 20,000,000–40,000,000 deaths as a result of his regime's actions.[12][13]

(Please ignore my name and WD2 theme, I'm not a WD fanboy. I review this game based on my experience and compare it to previous WD game.)  Ok, fist of all - let me start off by saying... WOW.  The changes from the previous Watch_Dogs are huge. I will list the changes or things I enjoyed in the old fashioned way so it would be easier to see Pro's/Con's instead of reading a huge romance.   LIKED SO FAR: - Performance, Fast Loading & Graphics (both PC and consoles are very well balanced and has no issues) - Good amount of new gadgets, hacking abilities (like manipulating world, vehicles etc.) - Jumper (RC car) and QuadroCopter (or whatever it was called, anyways a drone) - New characters (they give a lot more life into the game than Aiden did, and the DedSec crew was an amazing way to put more life into the game) - NPC's are like actual people (the world has literally a lot more life than from previous game, it makes you feel that you are in the living world) - New location San Francisco & fast travelling (sure it's not GTA size, but SF is a pretty nice city to cause havok) - Artwork and general graphical design (the way DedSec is represented is very nice and creative, it also makes you really feel that you are a crew of hackers that wants to change the world and save it from 'clans' like Blume) - MP & COOP, side missions and main missions (so far I've enjoyed every mission)   DISLIKED/NEEDS FIXING: - Driving (I felt it was a bit too 'toyish', I mean the driving is off and needs fixing) - Hack Invasion (a preson that is hacking you can easily just climb on the roof and sit there while hacking you, there isn't really a way to get up to the roof as he will just manipulate the machines that could lift you up that high) - basically there isn't many ways you can protect yourself, and when the hacking is done you can't go after the person to kill him like in WD1 - at least I wasn't able to - Sometimes it's very hard to hack the actual thing/person you want as it aims to completly wrong things (not sure how to explain this but it's just there) - Not al store skins are included in Season Pass, which is really stupid - basically you get only some skins, but you would need to buy the Ultimate Skin pack to get all of them

I'm often a fan of experimental and artistic games, and this one certainly has its merits, but I do not think it is worth even the small price of £2.79. The main issue, as many have mentioned, is the length. It is a very short experience indeed, and unlike, say, The Stanley Parable, it has no replay value at all. The player has no control over the narrative; there are some controls you can use to look around, idly fiddle with stuff and such, but the narrative will soldier on whatever you do, or indeed, even if you do nothing. That's a shame; a veneer of 'immersion' with no substance. It's very clear that the game is pretty much made by one person; I'm not sure whether it was a university project, but it does seem like one. The graphics aren't amazing, even on the highest settings I felt like Julian was made of papier mâché, and the story all takes place in one small room, so most of the work clearly went into the script, and most of the budget to the voice acting.  The story itself is poignant and relatable, though the main character occasionally jars the relatability with a racist comment or a crude but oddly coy internal discussion about sex. The voice acting is quite good, but occasionally clashes with the script creating an odd dissonance between the actor and the writer. If only the actor had felt able to paraphrase and improvise a little, it might have come off more naturally.  Play it if it's free; buy it if you're interested and it's at least 50% off in a sale. That isn't rare. I feel depressed after playing it, but that's because it sometimes reminded me of myself and my own internal monologue, and it does this well. If only the character were even more relatable, I could recommend this more heartily.

After the rocky start and issues after the patch this is what I did:  1. Manually removed every trace of the game from my hard drive, not just the steam but any other folders in other directories. 2. Made sure all my drivers and DX were up to date. 3. Re-downloaded and installed the post-patch game.  Since that time the game has worked almost flawlessly for me. Only one crash the whole time. I routinely get 40+ FPS even though I am over-committing my video cards RAM and play on a surround 3X display @5760X1080.  As for the game: I would give it an 80%  Overall I like the game, it is a cool story, and engaging. Looks pretty, voice acting is great.  The gripes I have are minor:  1. Too many of the side quests are dependent on the main quest line so I am almost done with the main quest while side quests are not nearly as far along. I personally don't like this as once the main quest is completed side quests don't seem to matter that much as any benefit to me (levels, gear, etc) are not used on the main quest line.  2. Combat has become complex enough that the key combos to do them are not easy to reach. I have tried many different key re-maps but same thing. Since it is not a controller the reach from the default wsad needs should be the most of the fast-combat keys used, alt+ or ctl+ for combat, meh.  3. Batmobile steers differently between driving and tank modes. This can get confusing.   4. No way to re-map the baterang, and to me, the vertical controls are flipped from what I have been using for decades from flight, and even flipped from batman's glide. So I barely use the batterang as it is frustrating to control. It also seems to ignore the mouse sensitivity so becomes much more sensitive than other modes.  5. Too much reliance on the batmobile. Yes it is cool, but really how is a racing track a 'riddle'?  Because of the above, you can see that the 'open world' is a misnomer, as you are rather lead around a lot by the nose, even if the map is open. They do so by controlling the environment and gear through the main quest.   Overall though, at the end of the day, it is engaging, and fun and I would recommend.

app_name: Nancy Drew: Tomb of the Lost Queen
review_text: ♥ Entrenched in history.  When I was younger my dream was to dedicate my life to being like Indiana Jones. I wanted to uncover tombs and find lost skulls, a dream that guided me to studying ancient Egypt.   I played this game shortly afterwards and fell in absolute love with it. When I saw it in my library yesterday I decided to replay it and was filled with nostalgia at the history it covers! Absolutely wonderful.   ♥ Filled with diversity.   In Tomb of the Lost Queen you have a variety of characters with different outlooks, personalities, and ethnicities. Her Interactive branched out with their character design, a fact much appreciated by the game, and by me. I liked how diverse the characters were though all, aside from Bess and nancy, were united by their sharp snarkiness.   ♥ Great graphics!  Despite my love of older Nancy Drew games, I've been turning into a sucker for pretty graphics. I'm glad to see that they're moving to improving the graphics in the game, though I doubt a day will ever come when we see Nancy, and I'm pleased they're doing so. It provides a trip to Egypt for free....at least it feels that way! ' p '   ✘ Nancy's voice...  and dialogue seemed a bit lacking compared to other games in the series. At times it sounded like Nancy was voiced by an entirely different person (Or is it just me?). Her dialogue fell a bit flat, especially in the emotional department, and paled in comparison to the dialogue provided by other characters.   Nancy shouldn't be outshined in her own game!   ✘ At times the direction is unclear.  It seemed as if at times I was left without any clue which way to go. Whether this was by design, I know not, however I wish that there were more instructions provided. Using the hint hotline proved to be less than helpful, the new system of clicking hints underneath each task in the newer games is a lot more effective.   ✘ Aliens.  Yick. It does tie in well with the history and legends surronding ancient Egypt yet personally I despise the very topic of aliens and wish to play Nancy Drew without encountering tin foil hat wearing characters.   Overall Nancy Drew: Tomb of the Lost Queen is as difficult as it is fascinating. A lovely addition to the Nancy Drew series and despite its flaws, I don't think anyone who plays it will regret doing so.

The concept drew me in, the game turned me off.  -The in game graphics, even at max settings, are about even with late PS2 titles. Yes, that bad.  -The pre-rendered cutscenes have camera problems. -The default control scheme is bad for Keyboard and Mouse, seriously, no reticule unless you're scoping, and the scope is mapped to 'E' but your grav power is mapped to Right Click? Swapped those fast. -The enemies are unimaginative.  I stopped playing before completing the first story mission because it was just that bad.

WARNING: You may experience input lag! You can fix it, but you have to do so manually. See:  http://steamcommunity.com/app/226980/discussions/0/666827315349070543/   It's alright. If you like pinball, maybe check it out.  Menu's are acceptable. The pinball mechanics work and that's about all I'm asking for here. It's acceptable and if you like pinball you'll probably be ok with this game.  I have not seen an already established table appear on this program yet, which has left me disappointed. On the plus side, as this is a virtual program, the tables I've played on have mostly done things I've either never seen before or are not exactly feasible on a real life table, so that's a huge plus.  However, quantifying if this game is good/bad is difficult as that mostly depends on the tables. There are both bad and good tables and, from what I've played so far (5 or 6 tables) the name brand ones (star wars and marvel ones) are not nearly as good as the weird original ones (Tesla is pretty cool). If I recall you can demo the program/tables, so there's that.  Right now there are about 30ish tables available, so there's a lot of variety. On the few I've played so far there hasn't been a lot of skilled play so much as flipper mashing, but that's also true for a lot of boards (I've only played one or two that were actually quite fantastic in real life).  Anyway, if you like pinball to an unhealthy degree this might be worth checking out, although there are also pinball emulators out there that have real tables on them, so you might consider that as well. As a buy? Not terrible, I guess, but I would investigate the dlc (tables) further before you make a purchase of one. I was pretty disappointed with the Marvel ones.

I really can recommend Evoland. It's an amazing, small game that is a lot of fun.  It's a nice throwback to 'old' times when video games were just starting to take off. To see the evolution of them is amazing.  There is a lot to do, you have to solve small puzzles and it's not always super easy to progress, but doable in any way. What irks me a bit about the game is saving. There are dedicated spots to save your game but sometimes, you're too far away from the last spot to just stop playing for a while. So either you lose out on progress or continue playing until you have found one of the save spots.   To sum up, Evoland is a neat little game that's definitely worth playing if you're a fan of video games (and how they came to be)

Papers, Please is a masterpiece. Intuitive, tense, flow-inducing, yet deep and varied gameplay, perfectly contextualized to expose the subtext of its mechanics. Every interaction is both a skill-testing challenge and a moral expression that contributes to a simultaneously emergent and crafted, branching narrative, achieved through precisely modulated procedural generation. This game is meaningful, resonant, and features more cohesive, focused integration of story into gameplay and vice versa than I have ever seen. That it was a low-budget, one-man operation merely makes the state of AAA games all the more shameful by comparison.

I got this game a while ago when it was on sale, expecting a few cheap laughs and a bit of a placebo for my dearly departed 'The Ship' game*. So after a quick download (as it is a rather small file) I was thrown into a pixar-esq world high on the promise of comedic violence...  Does it deliver?... kinda...sorta... not really  There isnt really a dedicated tutorial, you are left to piece together a basic control scheme through a quick 'loading screen button mapping' picture and through occasional tool tip pop ups  But even when you get past the awkward controls you find that most of the budget for the game was put into the weapons (that dont come with instructions). There is a good assortment of weaponry ranging from the several generic melee weapons, all the way to the esoteric 'sleep sheep' (that you will have no idea how to use, or even what it is)  There are 3 maps total, and they feel too big... and at the same time too small. They feel far to big to only have 8 people max playing at any one time, and you will find yourself lost pretty easily. But they feel so small because you keep backtracking to the same spots over and over again to spam a deathtrap or to pick up a weapon that doesnt require you to be right next to your victim  The characters are full of life and their own personal charm, with nobody feeling like a reskin or palate swap of another. Thats a two edged sword as it makes it INCREDIBLY easy to find your prey... but also makes evasion boil down to 'bring bigger weapon against your attacker' or 'run in hopes of losing them'   So between an awkwardly specific cast, confusing weapons and set designs, as well as a totally dead online, I cant in good faith reccomend this game  ```````````````````````````````````````````````````````````````` TL:DR Version  Did you like The Ship?   If yes, you will probly hate this game, because it is only like its older brother in premise alone. It fails to make itself memorable, or even enjoyable most if not al lof the time  If no, then you will still probly hate this game on principal.

Recommend, but it's a qualified one. I really appreciate what this game is doing and I quite enjoyed its nonviolent stealth mechanic and its storyline. The graphics were nice, the voice acting was good, the music was nice.. the whole thing was a little too ..nice. The storyline was about making the tough decisions between career and family, something many of us find resonant, and there were no easy answers to some of the dilemmas presented. Someone else compared it to the first Assassin's Creed and that's not too far off - a promising concept and core mechanic that needs a little more time to mature.  Spoilers follow. The player is a ghost but that entire plotline (via the journals visible only at night) did not resolve - am I K--- ? Or am I an older spirit that inhabits this house and K--- was a person that I drove to suicide? Or redemption? Have I as the ghost been haunting this house and helping the various visitors with their life problems since 1948? Or earlier? It's not made clear. Maybe I missed a journal or two. Considering how the other story beats were hammered home so repeatedly (including The World's Most Obvious Children's Drawings) this was needlessly obscure.   My other problem also involves the storyline. Sorry, it falls back a little too much on cliche for me to really get invested in the characters. A struggling writer with an alcohol problem and an ambitious spouse feeling stymied is the stuff of a million short stories. It wrapped up a little neatly and yes, while I chose the best outcomes I could, I felt the story needed a little more drama and conflict. Maybe I'll play it again but make the wrong choices. Pros - everything is polished and run smoothly when compared with other games in the same genre - quite hard, I have to restart for many times - (mostly) focus on dungeon building and defending, while War for the Overworld feel more like RTS game that you have to play Zerg rush to destroy enemy base - narrator voice can be turn off - graphic is more acceptable than other 2 games  Cons - sadly, its focus on building and researching defense mechanics. I appreciate how hard it is to create a card game with new rules. Well, there are books around here with rules for hundreds of Solitaire game and 2-player games made with regular cards, so you can always get inspiration from those, I guess. This one game uses a custom deck with many more suits, each being associated with a power-up.  The hardest part is balancing it out, I guess. DMD doesn't do it with flying colors, though. You can only unlock tournaments one at a time. If you really suck at a particular set of rules, you're screwed, you just can't finish the game. I'm only halfway through it and with about 12 hours of playtime, I'm guessing there's at least 25 to 30 hours of time to play. It's all very artificial, though... After a while, new tournaments are just variations on the previous ones, with harder AI, etc.  My biggest issue with the game is the complete lack of a story. There are characters; tournaments all have a title that supposedly draws from pirate lore. Great. But no one has anything to say, you just play against them and I guess you have to make up your own story. The soundtrack is very limited, too. There's a quasi rip-off of the Pirates of the Caribbean score used in the main menu (at least it's longer than the very similarly PotC-themed single track from Clicker Heroes), and a more ambient song used during games. Overall, it looks like most of the development time was spent on refining the game rules. To each their own. I'll also note that some of the special rules are self-contradictory, and as such the AI appears to be cheating, when it's just not applying the rules properly. I don't know what to say about that. This game is getting old, I doubt it'll ever be fixed.  If you're into hard casual games, walk the plank and give this one a try. It's well worth its price on sale. Maybe even at full price. I don't know, I'm not used to buying games full price any more... I'm so scared they'll get bundled the following week for a buck. Yes, it has happened before, and it will happen again™.  EDIT: I finished the game after 25 hours. In line with the last of story, there is absolutely no ending. No even a congratulations message for finishing all 60 (?) tournaments. Gosh, I wish it weren't so addictive... At least, now I can move on. Game isn't too hard because it relies so much on luck, and getting XP on losses means you'll eventually unlock everything, including unbeatable combos like Misfire/Parry or Plunderer/Davy Jones' Locker, etc. The fun comes in trying to find the right combo for each duel. Unfortunately, after the first half, all of your adversaries start using the same usual combos, so there's nothing new to see beyond the first dozen of hours. Still, well worth its price.

The last thing you want is to be denounced by Norway for being a warmonger, due to them asking you to go into a joint war with them. Just have to treat the AI a little bit like a fly on the wall at this point.  My biggest gripe is actually the music in this game. The menu music is stunning, but the in-game music is vapid and often doesn't match the leader. Eastern music playing over an England game, etc. It gets very repetitive compared to V. Good fun, of the eldrich horror variety.  Manage resources while seeking fame, wealth & knowledge in the salty spray of a Victorian-era subterranean ocean. The writing is deliciously evocative, its descriptions simultaneously droll and horrific. Though imbued with rogue-like elements such as a randomly generated map & perma-death, each play-through can last several hours (12+ for me, so far) & builds a rich web of stories.  Only $19, with more play-time (& better writing) then a Triple-A game. Definitely worth it.

The main story alone makes this game worth playing but there is also awesome 1v1v1 faction war PvP, dungeons and trials for group PvE, and some DLCs that contain good single player PvE/PvP experiences.  My favorite part of the game is the faction warfare in Cyrodiil. The dynamic keep capturing gameplay makes PvP a consistent battle that is neverending unlike most MMOS using battleground-esque PvP.  There are fps/latency issues that happen when too many characters/effects are close together and it can get realllllly bad, like 5 fps and/or 500+ latency bad depending on your hardware and the amount of stuff going on, so expect to get disconnected occasionally when there are a lot of people playing. You've been warned!!   Overall, I really like this game and would recommend it even though the occasional latency/fps issues are frustrating. I've played at least 400-500 hours of this game with steam offline.

Ive played this game since 2010. The levels that it has now are a new thing as well as additonal tools for the Shaman mouse to use (builder mouse) The way the game plays is simple you get cheese and get to the hole, while a Shaman mouse which can build, builds a way for you to do so. Each round is aproximately 2 min long, so yes its hectic and a fast paced game. Cheese is the currency for the game, all items can be bought with cheese and all are cosmetic, Tribes (Guilds) are purchased with cheese, and you pay with cheese when you submit maps with the level editor too. There is a cash shop currency but everything you can buy with Fraises (the cash shop currency) you can buy with cheese, and everything is cosmetic. Fraises can no buy map making or Tribes however. Its a very fun game and a very balanced one. The levels that were added recently give Shamans more tools than they used to have in the early days of the game. There are various game modes, the normal maps are get cheese get to hole, there are survivor- dodgeball type maps, soulmate tied to a partner, racing maps, racing maps with coins, bootcamp ( hard jumping and no Shamans), Vanilla - easy maps, and various other kinds. In addion to the normal game things such as marriages and government, and roleplay type things have been added as well. The commuity used to be really bad but i do think its improved quite a bit , its pretty easy going compared to how it used to be.

EDIT: The game has been pached to address issues that people had, and it has made the game a LOT more playable! The framerate is still 30fps, but it has been optimized and smoothed to a point where it honestly doesn't even feel 30fps. The game also looks a lot better after this update, pretty much as good as you can get a 12 or so year old game to look.  Tales of Symphonia is still a game I recommend to people to get them started in the Tales series. It's one of the series' defining games. It's incredibly fun to play, and the story is engaging right from the start.  That being said, it's by no means perfect. It lacks a lot of features that make later games even better. Despite the battles taking place on a 3D plane, there's no free movement, as you're restricted to an X axis between you and the enemy you're targeting. The story, while it's pretty good, isn't as polished as a lot of the other series' games. It's also packed with plot twists, almost to a fault. The game in general just really shows its age. It's a dated game. But it's still an essential part of the Tales series.  tl;dr if you can't stand games made over a decade ago then don't play

It might just be THE definitive Souls game. A true return to what made the series great in the first place: the level design. It's not to say, however, they haven't also touched upon other aspects. The combat feels more refined, with both character and npc animations looking as smooth as ever.  The haunting gothic atmosphere returns again to chill us with the deathless laughter that had us replaying the original over and over... and this one is no different. If you already love the Souls series, there's nothing to stop you from loving Dark Souls III.  It's a shame there are those here just to complain about day one blues. Game works perfect for me. Have yet to experience a single crash, with streaming on, and through SteamLink. Some people may just need to learn to either a) wait for the day 1 or 2 patch that always happens or b) clear some hard drive space from all that porn.

Like the eponymous bar the game takes place in, this is a game that's flying under most people's radars, but is highly deserving of a look, if you're a certain kind of person.  Make no mistake, by and large, this game resembles a visual novel; it emphasizes writing and aesthetics, and goes so far as to do away with dialogue choices, long seen as a staple of interactivity in an otherwise linear genre. But don't take that to mean that this game has no element of non-linearity or choice - merely that it resides in mechanics which aren't usually seen that way. The gameplay is not meant to be challenging, but the writing is stimulating and compelling in ways rarely seen in this medium. Anime fans will savor the references, which can get mind numbingly obscure and are often gut-bustingly funny. Fans of story will find characters with an uncanny balance of quirks, flaws, hopes and stand-out moments that stick with you. An incredible amount of care was put into the environments and people that you meet here.   There are people who won't enjoy this game - anyone who's turned off my the anime aesthetic, or anyone looking for more cyberpunk action than cyberpunk bartending. But for those looking for a story they hadn't heard before, this is a diamond in the rough. It's about 10 hours, it has replay value and a good amount of secrets, and most importantly, it's got a personality all its own. Recommend.

Impressive tech combined with an interesting story and solid gameplay.  Its similiar to the point and click police games of the past but in real time instead. The main thing here is the story where action segments are really just a way to break up the investigations and keep the game from being monotanous. The shooting, driving, and open world are really more of a secondary aspect where the investigations are the main thing you do in the game. The action isn't anything new or hugely interesting but the shooting and driving are solid enough and fun enough to not become annoying especially when broken up in between investigating.   What you do alot of is talk to people and figure out if they are lying or telling the truth essentially. This can be very hard and in some contexts I found that the game didn't make logical sense in what I was allowed to use as evidence of a lie in a conversation. Usually though it is logical and there is alot of help mechanics in the game ranging from intuition points that literally remove wrong answers or show you what the community has choosen statistically, to suspects using questions to tell the player what evidence would be applicable in a given situation. Its really fun to actually do but can be extremely frustrating at points.  The story is also very good. Very gritty and unfortunately realistic. I don't want to spoil anything but just know that the story and cases are very intertwined and can be hard to connect the dots at first. The story goes places and is diffrent and not always predictable. I really like it... even if I don't always like what happens.  The setting is lovely. This is when the USA was at its epitome in most ways. Its lovely to see all the classic cars (which are actual Ford or Chevy vehicles) and buildings aswell as clothes and everything in this game fits in the time period as far as I can tell.  The big downside is that this PC version is very messed up. Its very glitchy at times and loads much slower on the fly than I remember the PS3 version. Having things pop in very close to you is pretty common aswell as having sound cues not work right or animations of characters faces wig the heck out in scary ways. I encountered nothing that broke my game but it was very hard to know if I found all the clues in a given area because I felt the majority of the time the audio cue that tells you that you found the cues, it just wouldn't play and when it did it seemed incorrect to me. Rockstar gave up on the PC very long ago but this game is still solid enough to enjoy on PC.  The facial animation tech is amazing, its a real shame they didn't go full GTA on this game and expand it as much as a GTA game. You can't fly planes or shoot people out of a combat sequence but there are some street crimes for you to answer the call to. Outside the story though unless you want to do street crime missions or collect useless items/find historic buildings theres nothing really to do. I don't have as much an issue with this as some would because I enjoy experiencing a place or era that would be impossible otherwise. One of the biggest complains I have had is that they didn't adopt the physics engine of GTA4 into this game, so you have much more in terms of canned animations and boring physics compared to GTA4. Looking at the Rockstar Games releases after GTA4 it seems they gave up on Euphoria physics which really does make the games worse as that physics model is still fun to toy with in GTA4 to this day.   This is one of my favorite games, there just isn't alot of real time police games especially ones with this level of writing and actual care put into them. The ambient chatter of people is at times hilarous and at others extremely helpfull. Not often do I find a game story that I actually care about. The only complaints could be the PC port being not great and the actual real time action combat are a pretty baseline but are solid

I must admit, I thought I was going to HATE this game straight from the intro. So much so in fact, that I had tried it before and quit almost immediately! But with a little bit of motivation from my friend Monstar, I whent passed the horrid introduction, and was pushed into an open world environment full of adventure and intricacy!  Fallen Earth is a highly community based, adventurous, and well-balanced post-apoc MMO. If you are a fan of the Fallout series, you will most definately love Fallen Earth, as it is very similar in appearance mood. The community is very tight and helpful, boosted by the finely balanced economy and trading system. The community is also enriched by the placement of 6 factions that hold alliances as well as enemies.  The only caviot to playing fallen earth is it's lack of satisfying combat as well as it's difficulty when it comes to leveling (seriosly it takes AGES) But as long as you keep an open mind and are willing to spill quite some time into this game, I can assure you that you will have a good time. Just as long as you can get through the intro.

This thing came out in spring 2004 few months before Doom 3 and i guess that was the reason of D3 missed some portion of popularity... Of Course not everyone wants to deal with ugly demons somewhere in the a$$ of the hell when there is possibility to ride a motor boat/jeep around green and shiny tropical islands and be pleased by such beautiful water rendered by CryEngine. I can bet everything i have this game was the one who set the rulles for next gen 1st person shooters for at least next 3-4 years in 2004. And the only few things that this game missed we could saw in Project IGI such as gameplay balance/moments. Today i can't imagine what does 'next gen' means b'cos every freaken 2nd B+ game called next gen... But back to 2004 FarCry 1 was true next gen game. With many attempts since 2004 I was walking through this game and at last 'happy-ended' it one or two years ago on highest difficulty like I'm doing sometimes to get maximum satisfaction after finishing game to the end and after this stand up and say something like 'Yes! I Did it!'. This game was the 1st game where i've saw how enemy's IA working with some kind of virieties... how smooth ingame 3D graphics can be .... how to search and find the only 1 possible way to walk through a certain moments(highest difficulty) and how to learn to save game every freaken minute or less. Farcry 1 have common but aint bad story line in canons of action/thriller genre. Game is linear but still have some freedom/roam of movement b'cos it's big maps/levels. Multiplayer was also very fun and satisfiyng. I still remember those crazy jumps and kills on boats/jeeps across tropical islands that we can see now in modern Battlefield series... As year 2004 project 10/10. Sound/Voice/Music - 8/10. Graphics/Visuals - 10/10. Storyline - 8/10. Gameplay - 9/10. I would reccomend this game as common 1st shooter but one of the best 1st person shooters ever were released... to everyone. This game is strong part of history in PC Game industry. By the way... single player gameplay is not so dynamic as people can see in CoD series. So DO NOT expect nonstop action. -W-

Having this game for awhile and played hundreds of hours into it, Starbound is the definitive 'Terraria successor' that we've been wanting. It has sandbox, combat, mystery, adventure, and even a story, coupled with your own Outpost that, through story development and quest completion, more shops become available.  The movement, however, is garbage, and has been since day one. You remember how some games like Minecraft and Super Mario Bros has momentum movement that slows down and stops? Well, in this game, it doesn't matter how much speed you're running, you will always have a set momentum that is akin to walking on ice. Be sure to carry plenty of healing items, because the slippery momentum will injure you repeatedly, time and time again... possibly even leading to death. That's my biggest complaint I have with this game.

If there's one thing I can say about The Last Revelation, it's that it surprised me greatly. I'd be lying if I said that my expectations weren't low for the fourth entry into the immortal Tomb Raider series. No Croft Manor? No Judith Gibbins as the voice actress for Lara? No inventory ring? No musical score by the awesome Nathan McCree? No locking Winston in the freezer? All these charming little things from the previous games that up and left with The Last Revelation had me thinking that I would be quite disappointed with Core Design's next foray into the adventures of Indiana Jane and the Breasts of Enormity. It's not often that I like being proven wrong, but I can happily say that The Last Revelation surpassed my expectations and proved why I can't keep myself away from this excellent series.  While it is sad to see the previously mentioned elements missing from this installment, the gameplay itself is still the stellar, straight-forward and damn fun formula players have grown accustomed to in the last three games. Lara returns with all her previous manouvres, and a few new ones to boot, including rope-swinging, shimmying around corners and pole-climbing. Nothing groundbreaking, but hey, if it ain't broke, don't fix it. The Last Revelation has seemingly taken a few pages from Resident Evil's book. There's a largely increased emphasis on puzzles and riddles, some of which are quite complex. This is a well thought out departure from Tomb Raiders 1-3, in which pretty much every puzzle boiled down to 'find keys to open this door so you can open that door to get that key to pull this switch'. You can also now combine certain items and vision-based items such as binoculars and scopes are frequently used. Some weapons also have multiple ammo types which is a lovely added touch.  The Last Revelation is also the most accessible game in the series since the first. I loved the slow but steady learning curve of the original release, and I found it irritating that in the second and third games, Core went kind of ballistic with the difficulty, throwing all kinds of cheap deaths here and there and ruining the pace accordingly. The Last Revelation is quite easy compared to its predecessors, but thankfully the challenge once again builds up as you get used to controlling Lara once again. Later on in the game, you gain access to hub-like levels and backtracking between areas adding some welcome open-world and exploration elements. Fans may be disappointed with the reduced difficulty though, and I find this understandable. Secrets are a cinch to find, and there's so many items jam-packed into Lara's tiny backback, I'm surprised she can even stand up, let alone monkey swing, drop-hang onto a crevice, crawl, 180, backflip mid-air, catch a rope, swing back and forth, jump pistols akimbo slaying infernal bats, sprint and dive-roll inbetween rotating circular blades.  The game is also fantastically long. The earlier entires opted for a smaller amount of levels, but these levels were insanely large and sometimes overly complex. This time, due to their reduced size and the addition of backtracking, combined with the ever-changing environments leads to a far less boring and cumbersome experience.  Apart from the first two levels in Cambodia, which serve as a tutorial, the entire game takes place in Egypt. The Tomb Raider series is well known for its varied locations, with Lara often globetrotting to retrieve her beloved artifacts, but even with only one major set-piece, the game never gets boring. The choice to have a single primary location for the setting helps keep the game more cohesive, and it makes the plot flow together much easier. Yes, the story is still not Pulitzer Prize worthy, but it's entertaining and far more involving than the previous games. It's great to have an antagonist who has some sort of depth and character.  Peter Connelly is no Nathan McCree, but he does a pretty swell job on the soundtrack. As long as we still have some form of the original theme, I'm content. Jonell Elliott also provides a great voice for Lara, although I did prefer her more mature tone that Judith Gibbins provided in previous installments. The Tomb Raider engine is showing its age at this point, but Core continues with its stellar level design and intelligent workarounds to ensure that levels remain unblocky, despite the engine's limitations. FMV sequences are heavily improved from past entires, but the animations are still kind of robotic.  What else can I say? I was so pleasantly surprised with The Last Revelation, and I think you will be too. Tomb Raider 2 and 3 felt more like expansion packs, but The Last Revelation is truly the sequel to the original masterpiece. Core has basically perfected their formula at this point, and I can only hope that Tomb Raider: Chronicles follows suit. It's definitely the best since the original, and I would even go as far to say that it surpasses it.  4.5/5

Decent game let down by awkward dialogue and repetitive gameplay.  The basic story is you're a woman named Nilin. You've lost your memory in a world that can alter memory. So you go and kick ♥♥♥ without really knowing why.  The gameplay is actually quite solid. Think a less polished batman fighting system. The combo idea is good, but the fights get a bit dull towards the end, with the constant kickkickkickpunchpunchpunchpunchkick. This is what most of the game ends up being.  It's unfortunate, because one of the best parts of Remember Me are these bits called memory remixes. They are where you watch a memory, then go back and alter it. Sounds simple, but it's executed very well. Thoroughly enjoyed every remix. Shame there's only about 4...  The story is interesting, but I felt it wasn't executed as well as it could have been. Some conversations with your bro Edge either feel awkward, or unfinished. For example, Edge will say something that warrants an answer, but Nilin will just ignore him...this resulted in a lack of interest in the characters and I didn't feel anything for them.  Graphics and artistic design are really good. The french vibe, the little electronic tags that hover over things, these are nice little touches. The music was pretty cool, especially the whole glitchy sound that occurs when your health gets low enough. Nice orchestral, nuff said.  So overall, I reckon its definitely worth a look at, but it falters here and there.

Outlast is a game where you play as a reporter, who gets contacted by an unknown user who tells him about the horrors that is happening in an asylum. So far that seems pretty normal for a horror game, but as you progres you find out that it was a retakaning on the MKULTRA experiments of sleep depravation and experiments of the sort made on patients of the asylum, the plot thickens as you go on and discover what happens.  There are great unforgetable moments on the game, the story is not too expansive besides the notes that you find, there are little 'cheap' jumpscares, but most rely on thick ambiance and paranoia of the player.  The graphics and production values are nothing but amazing for an indie developer, they are top noch and could be easly compared to a triple A title with water physics, SSAO, great lightning effects, great sound (pherhaps one of the best I have seen), and while the game is a bit short it is a scary expirience throughout the entirity of its gameplay (3-5 hours) keeping you on your toes most of the time.  There are just a few complains. There arent any real puzzles and the game does get a bit repetitive , but this are minor and is definetly worth the value of admitance.  Great game, cant wait for the sequel to come along! :D

This game has its flaws, and it's not going to appeal to everyone - or even most people, probably. It has a certain PS1-era charm to it that may resonate more with 'older' folks that don't necessarily care if every single texture isn't 4K and animations are bemusingly clunky. When it comes to content, the actual experience, there's a lot here to love. The asymmetrical approach to coop works very well, as long as the hacker player doesn't need to be engaged in high-adrenaline activity every second - there are periods of time where the hacker will be doing little other than observing their partner, and that's ok. So far, I've been playing the field agent while my friend plays the hacker, and I tend to be slow and patient, preferring maximum stealth and to avoid contact whenever possible. This very much feels like the way the game is intended to be played; getting spotted is almost always a quick death.  My biggest and really only complaint is the seemingly inconsistent checkpoint system. There have been a handful of occasions where I've spent 30+ real-world minutes completing several objectives, only to be spotted by a guard, restart, and find myself having to redo all of it. But then, on my second or third attempt, a checkpoint will trigger where one never did before. It's little more than a mild frustration if you're patient, in which case it doesn't happen very often, but it is something that needs to be addressed.  The tutorial is also a little bit lacking, as there are certain features that aren't explained very well and need to be figured out by accident or through trial and error, but once you know what you're doing, everything actually works and works well.  Overall, highly recommended. Certainly worth a purchase if it's on sale, and even at full price - $25 is more than fair for what you get.

That sums up the basic gameplay features of Xenoverse, i hope other's will explore the game until they liked it as much as i did. app_name: Game of Thrones - A Telltale Games Series
review_text: Exciting, thrilling, heart-breaking and amazing game by Telltale Games Company with a great storyline. 'When you play the Game of Thrones, you can only win or die.' Telltale Games strongly accentuates this famous quote from the original series.   I wish you all the luck you can have as House Forrester.

Gameplay: It's very similar to Team Fortress 2 (TF2) with elements of COD. Two teams, one pushes the cart (in this case a tank) vs th other team trying to stop you, there are other things you got to do but well save that for when your in game.   If you think this is all about kills, you will be in for disapointment. This is not a KILL DEATH RATIO (KDR) type game. It's very objective based, complete the objectives or you lose!   Upgrades: You upgrade your mercs with cards, these are simply 'load out cards' with guns and perks as specified on the card. For the most part all guns of the same type will have the same damage value. The higher tier cards will have small perks like reload while in sprint, or a slightly faster fix the tank feature. The fancier cards are not enough to give you an edge in a gun fight! Anyone who tells you diffrent is simply mis-informed.  Misconceptions: There's been a lot of complaints that this game is Pay 2 Win... mostly from ignorant five year olds who really dont play this game. I've got near 90 hours at the time of this review, and I can honestly say... I have never lost a gunfight because the other guy had a fancier cards (load out card) than I did. I lost because he was on target faster than I was or other guy was a dirty b*tch and shot me from behind!  Besides your earn credits (in game points) and free cards (free load out cards) just for playing, and you can use the credits to upgrade your cards, so if you just play enough you will the same cards as pretty much everyone else. There is litterally nothing to pay for if you so choose.  If your getting started in this game this biggest tip I can give a rookie is memorize the map layouts, and save your free credits and cards. Credits upgrade cards, bu also buy new merc (new characters), and after a bit your gonna want to try out proxy, nader, spark... just whatever you do DONT BUY THE SNIPER... snipers arnt worth a damn in this game.   Final Thoughts: This game is beyond fantastic! I seriously can't believe they gave this game away for free! Download it now.. and if your on my friends list hit me up for noob tips to get you started. =P
Best Game perfect 5/7

Oceanhorn is a mixed bag; it's definitely more good than bad, but it's clear that there is a lot of unutilized potential in it's design.  The most notable quality of the game is that it's derivative; of The Legend of Zelda to be specific, though that comparison is drawn quickly and immediately by anyone who has a passing familiarity with the Zelda franchise. Unlike many similar indie designs, Oceanhorn doesn't feel like an homage or a tribute (much in the way that Ittle Dew or Binding of Isaac do) -- instead it feels like a somewhat randomly curated collection of Zelda artifacts, patched together in to... something, without really knowing what all those pieces were meant to do in the first place. While it is an enjoyable playthrough, the game shows its most promise in the rare moments where it isn't trying to shoehorn another Zelda trope in to it's structure.  The story hurts the most of the trouble; many of the characters are direct references to Zelda staples, but they lack any of the history or context of that series, so instead of feeling like they contribute, they just end of feeling hollow or forced; the villain, for example, reads as a Gannon cliche, not as an evil peer. And in an effort to create gravity, many ridiculous plotholes are developed -- we're told that the world is in terrible shape, but we never see it; the Gillmen have been waiting for help for centuries, but it's not clear why they haven't sought help; the Sky Islanders watch everything, but are completely in the dark about anything meaningful (not to mention that they have advanced technology, but couldn't figure out a safe way to get to the ground). Heck, the game even tells you that you're liked in town, and drops a romance in your lap, which is crazy because when it happens you're little more than a pot-smashing drifter in town.  And with all the mirroring between Oceanhorn and the Zelda games, there is an unfavorable light cast on all its rough edges. The camera doesn't really work; there's mismatch between the overwrought dialog and its subtitles on several occasions; there's numerous small glitches and inconsistances that can ruin whatever it is that you're doing. And if Oceanhorn stood on its own, I'd probably be comfortable ignoring all of that; it's just how firmly planted in the shadow of another series the game chose to be that makes these issues pop to the fore.  That all said, it's a reasonable length for a good adventure; it's most tedious parts are either optional or far apart; and the game oozes with a respectible amount of polish. I think the dev team put some serious elbow grease in to brewing this game up, and they realized the concept admirably. They just need to get much further away from their inspiration before they decide to build.

I already wrote a review under the last game, Kurenai, but i feel i should put it there as well, so maybe someone will try this game.   Finally played through the last one and now i can review them all together!  I grew to absolutely love East Tower series. When i started out with Akio, i was pretty 'okay sure' about it, not really negative, not really positive. It had its nice moments, it had its annoying moments, but mostly it felt casual. A game to play and never think about it again, i suppose. Then i played second game. And third. And fourth. And i changed my mind.  East Tower Takashi is much better than Akio, and i enjoyed it way more. It felt more natural and balanced drama-humour better than Akio, and i grew to love Takashi as a character. But still, the first two, they're only the beginning.  I fell in love with East Tower during Kuon's story. In my opinion, it's much better written than the first two, and balances out everything surprisingly well, even though plot gets more complicated than in previous games. It completely sucked me in and made me enjoy every second i spent playing.  And then, finally, Kurenai. Now that one is good. I shouldn't be surprised, since there are signs like, you know, order of routes or increasing price, and yet. I still can't believe how Kurenai managed to meet all my expectations and then exceed them.  Kurenai tells you everything you wanted to know and more. It uncovers the whole story, and doesn't leave anybody out. The amount of choices combinations is just stunning, and it took me about half of my play time to hunt everything down. Which is both a good and bad thing (i can now recite some choices order from memory after clicking them repeatedly a couple of times), depending if you like that kind of thing or not. The whole story is actually really interesting and even though some things are predictable i still found myself thinking 'but... maybe it's actually not true?'. East Tower manages to make you wonder and teases you all the time. I, for one, couldn't resists and beat both Kuon and Kurenai in one sitting.  The best thing in all of the routes is heroine. She isn't your typical shoujo/otoge heroine, oh no, she is a character in her own right and it's impossible to overlook her. I think i loved her the most in Kuon's route - her comments and thoughts were just so relatable! She's absolutely irreplacable and that is a thing many games with female protagonists fail to deliver well.  The other characters are just as good. I guess with Akio and Takashi you just don't spend as much time as you do with Kuon or Kurenai, so naturally they don't feel as developed and complex, but they have their good moments. Kuon is really interesting, and i personally like that sort of character with that sort of plot (avoiding spoilers is so hard!). Same goes for Kurenai, who managed to capture my heart almost instantly. Even the side characters are great and i liked them all.  I know some people make fun of/dislike East Tower's graphics, but i genuinely like them. Although sometimes they look weird/anatomy is wrong, i absolutely love colouring, textures, and how diverse positions/expressions are. I think only in Akio i was frowning at art, but East Tower captures you with its plot and completely overshadows its graphics. It should also be noted that there's a lot of cgs, the winner being Kurenai (117 cgs, although a lot of them are different versions depicting one scene; still, that's impressive!).  The music is also really good, some of the tracks are still stuck in my mind. Music also completes the picture; when i turned it off for a while i felt like something was lacking and playing was less enjoyable.  All the voice actors are good, and i'm so glad we can enjoy japanese voice acting in these games! It feels so right.  So, to sum up my review: East Tower series is really good. I enjoyed every second while playing it and felt it was so worth my money. I recommend it to anyone, whether they like visual novels or not, because East Tower is a good, solid story, that definitely deserves more praise.

I'm not really good at writing these types of reviews but I felt the current mixed rating this game has is, in my opinion, undeserving. So I'm hoping that this review might help people look past the current rating.   So what is Orcs Must Die!: Unchained? It's a MOBA combined with turret defense type gameplay (or trap defense in this case). In most MOBAs (say DOTA 2) minions spawn from 3 lanes from a barracks type structure and head towards a main structure in the enemy's base to attack and destroy between them are Heros (players) and turrets.  In OMD: U it's slightly different. For starters theres only 2 lanes and the minions spawn from portals. Which must then travel from their portal from your base in to the enemy's rift. There's nothing for them to destroy once there, they simply must get in to the rift to score points. Once the enemy has lost all of their points your team wins. The minions must overcome traps (laid out by the enemy team heros) and guardians (which is a big dude in armor with powerful aoe attacks) which stand in front of gates.   Now heres the cool thing: you are free to customise what gets sent down the lane! There are 3 slots for front, middle and back positions. You can send everything from orcs which are numerous but not very tough and only worth 1 point each to giants which are big, tough but slow and I believe worth like 3-5 point each. You can also get dwarfs that cast protective shields or crossbow infantry that perforate heros and guardians from a distance. To get different minions to need to unlock thier cards which are crafted from materirals you get as you play the game or from chests (which can be bought with real money).   Your can also unlock different traps in this way and traits which are minor boosts to your hero. From bonus starting gold to slight increase in health or mana.   But thats for a 5v5 match Siege mode. If you're not feeling competitive theres a Survival co-op mode where you and up to 4 other people must endure several waves of minions.   Most people will complain about micro-transactions and whatnot but well thats how a free to game works I'm afraid. Do they expect that the poeple who work on this game work completely for free? Not to mention they are completely optional. Maybe if people focused more on the actual FUN of the game instead of grind this game might get more favourable reviews but I don't really want to get more in to that. If that's their opinion then so be it.   In short this game is really fun and even though it's in beta it's very well polished. And well it's completely free if you wish to at least try it out.

MULTIPLAYER DOESN'T WORK  If the multiplayer in a game doesn't work then I am going to feel angry about that. Quake 2 online still works, Civ4 online still works, Heroes of Might and Magic 2 online still works and these games are over 20 years old!   I understand that a small dev team might not have the expertise to bring decent online to a game, its complicated and a million little things can bugger it up. Their is no easy solution or fix and most PC users will just download Hamachi to fix these issues but Hamachi is no longer free.   So now I have to pay to bandaid the problem with the game I also paid for? How about no. Risk of Rain had this exact same problem. Direct IP connections are just not going to work for the majority of people.   Multiplayer aside, how is the game? Well its a twin stick shooter with a giant object in the middle that i/we need to protect but can also accidently shoot due to friendly f-- no.  I don't want to believe it, twin stick shooters are the simplist form of enjoyable gameplay around. You don't even need to go out of your way to make a twin stick shooter game enjoyable, by its very nature its enjoyable so I am truly amazed that they ♥♥♥♥ed it up.  Friendly fire is always a pain in the ♥♥♥ in any game but it  can  enhance the gameplay when done right. Not doing it right however is a thing you are doing when you have us on a escort mission and we can kill the person we are escorting! In a frantic, monsters from all sides type of game, we are going to suicide, a lot!  I dunno, the graphics, voice work, music and presentation really sell the game but the actual gameplay and the non working online make this game a tragedy.  I had no expectations going in except that of course the game will be great. Twin stick shooters by their very nature are great and also featuring online? Sign me the ♥♥♥♥ up! But the online part is a lie and the gameplay is ♥♥♥♥ed up in a way that could be easily fixed and so this game comes so close, so close to being great but the first wave of buyers will be like me. Burned by the whole experience and leaving negative reviews and unless the game is patched, fast, it will be a huge shame because genuine care and love seemingly went into this game only for no one to check if the handbrake was on before parking the car of the top of the hill that is steam front page. TLDR; Game is awesome fun and very physical. 100% immersion, bow position in left hand could be a little better, but is good enough. I recommend purchasing it, $15USD isn't too much for such a neat experience.   Bought this game yesterday, and I've only played it about 45 minutes so far. This number *WILL* increase.   Firstly this game is rather awesome. I've played maybe 15 VR games, and this one is the first to make me squeal like a little girl. The archery is almost spot on (more on that later), the waves of enemies build up nicely, and there's more variety in oppoents than Space Pirate Trainer for example.  The Enemies: First off you start with some harmless looking cubes, which shoot things at you. These aren't too bad, and if you just dodge they seem to go away even if you miss. Then all of the sudden there's so many cubes, all you can think of is 'F*** I hope there's not one behind me!!' Things calm down for a second when the next enemies are introduced, a swordsman. He doesn't shoot thigns at you and just comes at you with a raised sword. Relentlessly. And before you know it you've now gotta pay attention to where the sword guys are while the cubes fire more missiles at you. It's pandemonium. There are more enemies, and at least one boss, but I'll let you find them.   Movement: Takes advantage of roomscale. Enough said really, although I have a roughly 2x2.5m space and I wouldnt want to be playing in any smaller, because have I mentioned that you move. Like a lot, jumping, dodging, weaving (falling over...) I'd having a guess that this is probabaly the most active game on VR currently, and defintely the most active I've played.  Combat: The archery is good, one handed mode is sort of like The Lab, and two handed mode is more how real life is like. Sadly, I wasn't fully happy with the way the controller was mapped to the bow, it feels like you're holding it too low. About 5-10cm (2-4' for you Americans) too low. You can adjust to this, but I just feel that the archery would be perfect if it was changed a little. Devs, maybe an option to change the grip height on the bow?   Overall, this is an awesome game. I love it. If you like the archery in The Lab and games like Space Pirate Trainer, I recommend picking it up.   Performance: On my system this game works really well, and is fairly easy on hardware. My GPU's don't run VR terribly well, but at no point did I experience any desire for more framerate. Note: I haven't tried in single GPU mode, but in every other VR game that I've had performance issues with, one card is better than two.  i5 2500k @ 4.6GHz 16GB DDR3 RAM @ 1333MHz AMD 7970 GHz edition x2 (Crossfire)

When I first seen this game, I wanted it and added it to my wishlist. Just looked like a fun, hop around, kill, and completion game.  However I did not enjoy it nearly to the point I thought I would. This is actually more like Super Meat Boy with brooms. Only not as fun, not as funny, and controls not nearly as tight.  Not a bad game, but I felt myself being bugged by the controls and there is no real combat: only appears that way.  The basic idea is that you pick a zone, then pick an area within the zone to clean dust/leaves/goo by jumping and sliding along the walls. At the end you kill a few monsters who cannot hurt you and the level is over. You get points to unlock keys to open more areas to do the same thing.  Each of the four janitors plays a little different, but all of them feel a bit awkward or delayed. More of a muscle memory on some of the levels and not precise contrtols. Just not as good as what I wanted.

Having not been a gigantic in-depth Civ player in the past I'm not too sure what the hate toward this game is. It does seem more dumbed down than Civ5 but Civ5 was more dumbed down than 3 and 4, and thats just a trend games see to be unfortunately following these days (Fallout 3 to 4, Elder Scrolls 3 to 4 to 5, even the new Star Wars Battlefront is ridiculously basic).  But as far as a space strategy game goes, this is a decent basic one that can still steal away lots of time. If you'd rather spend your time on some grand space game you might want a true 4X one, but if you want Civ with aliens and astronauts then Beyond Earth is, truly, a lot of fun.

I like this game. It'll take about 25 hours to finish the campaign on Normal provided that you appreciate everything as opposed to rushing. With these types of games, my advice is to just stop when you start to get annoyed. Play it only when you crave to visit the wasteland again.  I didn’t like the races even though I saw that the purpose was to make use of the open world. I suggest completing the races on first attempt. If you fail, then take a break and continue with the mission.  I found it disturbing how they can twist real life culture into an apocalyptic scenario. For example, UK guys, Russians and Red Indians as badass clans. Or even a Nazi portrayed as a prestigious figure.  I was expecting some 60ft big boss towards the end as there was word of it from one of the subway townsmen. But it turned out to be some really intense tight space combat from a horde of mutants. I walked in unprepared and without bandages. I loved it! To be quite frank I’m getting annoyed with big end bosses.

This is something like Dead Space (you 're an engineer and wander all over a ship fetching things and offing bad guys) and something like Mirror's Edge (female PC, lots of climbing, red pipes). But the best part is the water. The ship is flooding, and the ship has some amazing fluid physics, and makes use of water in interesting ways: swimming, flooding an area on purpose, drowning enemies, a wacky water attack.  It's short, and there are some story issues (you don't get the water power till just before the end, and the enemies are repetitive), but it's a fun idea and the price is right.
Much fun was had in this game.

What can I say that hasn't been said below(or above) nothing really... this isn't what I'd call a game, it's more like a medieval soap opera out your dreams/perfect kind of eskapisism without the actual 'must' to be a nerd to 'get' - if 'the dark ages' of Europe is of any interest to you, that is.  Anyway... it's highly priced, so either wait for a sale or get it with extreme caution (I got it before the two latest dlc's Charlemagne and the Way of Life came out - so I got it simply because I could live my dream of being a viking: having a great berd, sacrificing to the Old Gods while having a few horns of mead AND raiding the Brittish Isles times and times again (all these things at the same time), then loot some neighbors (like the finno-ugric people, slavs and balts) take over more land form the kingdom of Sweden and, if the Gods will, the Empire of Scandinavia. Take over more land just because I hopefully can. or die trying - but becomming a great Viking dynasty.   On negative aspects the learning curve, THE learning curve and all these dlc's comming out all the time (making it hard to decide whether to buy them or not) but Steam workshop's here for most situations.  The good thing is that you can basically be anyone you want, at any point of the timespan of the game (depending on the dlc's you have bought)   Summa summarum: a fairly great game. about 9+/10, not perfect, but there are quite a few ♥♥♥♥♥♥ games out there, trust me.

Crusader Kings 2 i found a little confronting to begin with, however as i learned to manage my vassels, lands, armies and the endless troubles that come with them i found it a rather addictive experience. I know this game won't be for everyone but to me theres something in the slow taking of land and the constant threat of it being ununited by rebels, other kingdoms and even your own family that keeps me coming back and playing this for hundreds of hours. For anyone willing to put a bit of time into the game, it will evenutally come to you how to play and react to certin events and a large empire will become yours. DLC like the Old Gods, Sword of Islam and Horse Lords introduce different playtypes into the game that make their cultures different and have new elements that will make or break your realm. Religious tensions, holy wars, revolts, claiment wars, raiding, unhappy family members, taxation, assassination plots, building projects, diplomatic relations, technology research, laws, successions, imprisonment building armies and more, all another day on the throne.  The combat has changed a little for Crusader Kings 2 which makes wars longer and more expensive along with an alliance system that stops huge realms from conquering everyone around them, which i don't really mind and makes you think twice before attempting war with another nation. I like the history behind the game and your ability to create your own history of your dynasty. Like Tywin Lannister said in Game of Thrones it's about 'The future of our family' and it couldn't be more true for Crusader Kings 2 becuase how you win is by increasing your family's prestige above all others in the world, based on every action, every holding and event that takes place. You must forge generation by generation to make your name respected above all others.  I will admit however that the DLCs are possibly a little overpriced and could be argued should have been in the game for free, but there are a few that change things up and is worth getting if its on sale. I would higly suggest getting: Old Gods Sword of Islam  and if you get into the game enough The Republic Horse Lords Legacy of Rome Sons of Abraham Raja's of India Charlemagne  Much of the other DLC isn't really worth it unless you find it nice in which case i would still wait for them to be on sale.  Crusader Kings 2 is a game that requires time to be put into it and if you play online there will be times when everything feels like its falling apart but it delivers an unquie experience and i would recommand it to anyone who likes a bit of strategy in their war games.
