- WARNING! Review may contain spoilers -  Rocksteady, their bussiness model is shoddy and their PC port and release of their final installment of the Batman Arkham series was a disaster, and they knew it! But still, Rocksteady manages to grab my attention with their brilliant ability to convey a story and keep the audience interested and engaged with the characters.  Firstly, I can't review this game without giving a mention to Mark Hamill's utterly superb Joker! The reason this game was so good for me was entirely down to the character of the Joker. Yes, the Joker is dead, but throughout Arkham Knight a wild, hilarious and intriguing hallucination of the Joker, trapped in the Batman's mind appears. Taunting and showing the sub-conciounce of Batman, Mark Hamill's joker is a truly amazing character!  That's pretty much it though. Apart from the gorgeous visuals, which truly are gorgeous, the game is rather repetitive. The side missions don't really change and the game is rather dumbed down compared to the previous games. Implementing a 'fear takedown' which should have been implemented differently as an award for scaring enemies but in fact is a one-button kill 5 enemies to make the battle easy way of doing things.   Also, there is too much focus on the ridiculuous tank! At the end of the game the Arkham Knight steps down from command and Deathstoke, a character who is alike to Batman's physical and mental abilities, takes over. In anticipation of the final fight beyween two great characters, the developers force another ridiculuous tank battle in your face. A battle that neither Deathstroke or Batman would take part in!  Overall, while the underlying issues of their DLC infested game and forced tank battles may be of hinderence, the game has still managed to be one of my favourites and I would still recommend it. The main point and success of this game, I truly believe, is mark Hamill's Joker and Rocksteady's ability to tell a story without forcing the player to lose control by naturally grabbing your attention.

Basically, if you like survival horror, play this game. If you like any of the Alien movies, play this game. If you like both those things...how have you not already played this game!?  Alien: Isolation perfectly captures the atmosphere of the original Alien movie with its claustrophobic design, gorgeous style and very well done audio design. The gameplay only reinforces this by making you constantly feel helpless even when it throws a multitude of craftable items and weapons your way to assist. The encounters with humans can turn into frantic shootouts, the 'Working Joe' androids who patrol the station will bash you to pieces and, oh, then there's the xenomorph!  Having the actions of the lone alien persuing you through the station comletely dictated by an A.I, instead of relying on scripted segments, adds a constant tension to every second of the game. You never know when the xenomorph will show up, what actions will attract it, or where it will go once it begins the hunt. Moments where humans enemies and the xenomorph both converge on you are also a thing of horrifying beauty ;D  Only downsides to Alien: Isolation are a disgustingly long run time (my first playthrough clocked almost 50 hours of me faffing about and dying repeatedly), some wonky bugs in the physics engine and a hard save system without checkpoints. None of these really get in the way of the enjoyment, though and I would say the weak, self conscious plot is the only downside. It doesn't add a whole lot to the canon or building its cardboard characters up.

Far Cry 3: Blood Dragon is a kick-♥♥♥ 80's Sci-Fi style shooter set in a post-apocalyptic vision of the year 2007, VHS style. It has a ton of references to Sci-Fi cinema and popculture from the 80's including: Predator, Terminator, TMNT, Robo-Cop and much, much more. But just like the title says; it's still Far Cry 3, you don't have amy new moves or skills to unlock, unfortunately. Though there is some new weaponry including a lever-action shotgun called the 'Galleria 1991'(in reference to the shotgun used by Arnold Schwarzenegger in Terminator 2), the 'A.J.M 9'(which is the iconic pistol Robo-Cop used), a sniper rifle called the 'Kobracon' and an assault rifle called the 'Fazertron'. All of them have unique attatchments including explosive rounds, lazer rounds, fire rounds and several others.  Blood Dragon has clever and very enjoyable humor if your a fan of the 80's Sci-Fi genre, if not you might wanna give this one a pass since its strongest point, the over the top and corny humer, might not be your cup of tea. Otherwise, give it a go! Trust me when I say you get bang for the buck(quite literally) with collectibles, fun Far Cry 3 gameplay and a kick ♥♥♥ ending. Far Cry 3: Blood Dragon recives an 8/10

This game had a lot of promise but ultimately felt like a parody of itself. This game was loaded with recycled content including characters that shouldn't have been there - and game mechanics that were random and only served to draw the game out.   The load screens were my favorite where Gabriel/Dracula stared thoughtfully into some candlelight while his inner dialogue summed up the most immediate objective. Soft, introspective piano music would play in the background. Then you the accept button and a slashing sound was followed by Gabriel glaring suddenly into the camera... It was over-the-top.  The return of his wife Marie was a surprise and a happy one but they failed to tie her presence in to the over-arching arch. The ending was like watch an okay movie where they awkwardly avoid bring closure for the sake of making sequels that you already know are going to be bad.   Its a little sad given how well this reboot started.

I love boardgames. I really do. I've never played Talisman, though. And this might be one of the reasons I absolutely hated playing this game. Being a rulesmonger, I usually read all rules before playing a game. Twice. Maybe even 3 times. And this might very well be the 2nd reason I hated playing this game.  The game does an extremely poor job of telling you the rules and give you any sort of insight as to what might happen on the different tiles on the board. I was left with playing blind and ended up just walking away, since the computer controlled characters decided they all wanted to bash on me, so I kept loosing turn after turn, effectively turning the 'game' into a slideshow.  I trust the game will work out to be a lot more fun if you already know what it's all about, but I have no way to tell. My recommendation is therefor this: If you don't know the game, stay away from it. I just doesn't work as a stand-alone product.

This game, for the low price of $7.49 that I picked up during the winter sales has proven to be worth every cent. The challenges that my friends and I faced has caused us the necessary frustration but the possibility of completing it has kept us at it. We are 70% past the game and honestly, my only wish were that it could be longer as we do love the game.   The upside are the stages which are initially simple but you can opt to do the challenges which are much harder and have forced our team to use special set-ups in our attempt to conquer them. A fond way to think of it would be like a 10-25 man dungeon raid you would experience in some of the MMO's but that your team was reduced to 4, including you. Although you can play it single player, it is a much better game to play with friends and I would say your enjoyment rises exponentially with each friend you play with.   The downside is that the game is relatively short, if you play without the challenges, you can easily complete in maximum of 1-2 days?  This is one of the most challenging games and its still worth every cent.

From what little I experienced, it seems like Void Mode is the meat of the game, expected to take a lot longer than the first part. This game is freaking terrible! If you were a fan of the last reboot, and are wondering about whether or not to get this game, read this review!  You start most missions in concealed mode, which allows you to creep around the maps and set up an ambush. In this mode, Overwatch no longer has an aim penalty, so you could set all of your soldiers but one on Overwatch, and have the last one attack, or run out in the open to trigger a 'pod'. (Group of enemies that are just stood there) Once triggered, they will most likely run for cover, and your soldiers will shoot them.   Concealed mode is an interesting addition, but during the timed missions, where you are given 8 turns to complete the objective, or you fail the mission, you cant really be messing around slowly setting up an ambush to deal with the enemies. In this way, the game comes accross as having multiple personality disorder. Because of the new abilities the aliens have, you have to be very careful, running away and looking for cover. But because of the round timers that the developers have, you have to be very fast, making sure to get there on time. The developers also saw fit to remove the ability to 'dash' to a door or objective and activate it in the same turn. That means that you have to move there, and use your second action point to activate the objective, or dash there, and activate it on the next turn. Nearly all of the 8 turn missions I have attempted have been failed with one soldier standing right next to them, ready to activate it! FOR GOODNESS SAKES! I failed 8 missions in the campain, and completed 4, (some of them tutorial missions) on the easy difficulty, even though I have played the other game on impossible.  The aliens themselves have also been given much more power. Sectoids can now mind control, and appear just as early in the game as the last one. In the last game, only Sectoid Commanders could mind control, and you didn't encounter them until much later, when the research your scientists had done allowed you to manufacture advanced armours and weapons that leveled the playing field. Not only that, but Sectoids can also now raise the dead. So if one of your soldiers is unlucky (Literally, since the game is based around pseudo-random hit chances more than ever before...) not only does it hurt because you can attack the enemy once less per turn, but also it hurts because they can attack you once more per turn! Killing the offending Sectoid will kill all the zombies as well though, but unfortunatly, it is rare that you encounter a Sectoid all on it's own. It is often accompanied by a pair of 'stun' troopers. These aliens are also encountered early in the game, and can dash AND melee attack your troops in the same move! Not only that, but they have a high chance to stun your troops, robbing them of their next move. It is hard to run away from these troops AND reach the objectives in time. Not only that, but while you run away for more cover, which is the only strategy in these Xcom reboots (everything else is random, positioning is the only certainty (and suppresion /explosives )...) you will invariably trigger another pod of deadly aliens, which will surely slaughter your entire team in the next go.   What is also poorly explained, and completely ruined, is the geoscape. In the last game, you were given clear goals of what to do next, or what decisions you could afford to make. In this game, Everything is random. There are 11 continental perks, but there are only 6 continents. The game randomly picks your starting position, randomly picks 6 perks out of the 11 in the game, and randomly gives each continent one of those perks. I started in New India, in Asia.  In the last game, you had a scan button, which would advance time. In this game, there are Scan buttons located all over the map. These buttons are essentially like bars, that get filled up as you choose to scan at that location, instead of another. once you have scanned it empty, you get the reward from it, what ever that was. Now, instead of monthly funding, every month, the funds you would have got are put down on the map somewhere, and need to be scanned for three days. You also need to build communications centers in your base to be able to contact more regions, and the regions are contacted in linear order. Research and base management works the same, with a list of upcomming events that get completed as you scan in the map. Manufacturing works similarly, but now once you have manufactured certain items (Like armour or weapons) You only need to manufacture one, and every troop can have one.  There are certain missions in the game which advance the 'Advent' bar until completed, which when full, ends the game. These missions don't disapear after a day like the others, and can be attacked at anytime. But only if you have contact with the continent that it is located in. So if you dont have access to that mission, you will have to build a communications center. But if you don't have enough power, you will need to build a power plant. But if you don't have enough money to do that, you will need to scan around to get some. While scanning, there is a risk a pick two, or pick three mission will pop up. You can choose to ignore it if you want, but there are 2 or 3 negatives associated with each of those missions, such as all enemies will have armour for the next month, or your funding will be cut in half next month. You can only pick one of those missions, and the other two negatives will happen anyway. But it will be difficult to beat those missions, with the new timer mechanics, and unfair enemy powers. If you dont have enough time to wait for all the building either way, then you will lose the game.  There is no way to play the game like the last one in my opinion. Now, instead of being able to start a new playthrough and put on Ironman/Impossible, and follow through with a certain strategy you have been working on, the game does everything randomly, making it so you go in blind everytime. The old rules still apply to a deggree, elevation is good, blow up their cover, run away if you can to a more secure location, focus fire etc etc.  But all of those rules go out of the window randomly. You rely on cover? Well what about if one of the humans running around on the map randomly turns into a giant monster, that can walk up to your soldier on a balcony, and swipe it's claws, and completely destroy the building your team is stood on, in one move? You always know you can run away from the aliens to secure a much better environmental advantage, like higher cover or a vantage point? Aww, well, too bad, you got a mission that expects you to kill 10 aliens and hack the box in 8 turns, with four rookies, and devastating consequences for failure.    There are some positives though:  They fixed the mouse lag, making for a much more enjoyable user experience. The game also runs well for me on my old rig, so I don't know why some users are reporting performance issues, but it may be a driver issue or something. The game looks pretty good as well. The game features well done randomly generated maps. The designs, the music, the sound are all great! One great moment I recall was a grenade knocking a soldier into a train, only to have a 'BANG' play, as his body hit the metal train! xD The game is also much more snappier than before, it's very responsive and less time-wastie to give orders and pan the camera. The game world and designs ooze atmosphere also.   The tactical randomness lets the game down imo. You shouldn't have to mod the game to give you one or two more turns, that's something the developers should have anticipated.

As someone who loves strategy games in general, and who also enjoys space hulk, i was on the fence about buying what appeared to be a 'lesser clone'. Well i decided to try this out and you know what? This game is a perfect compliment to your strategy collection. This is really the opposite of space hulk (powerful military might in small number against an endless hoard of lesser intelligence beasts). The concept in this game instead of dissimilar opponents, is much more based on two more equal enemies in terms of intelligence and weaponry. The difference between the games is very much worth your money because this title really has its very own feel and in a very good way. I did encounter a bug where the enemy turn would keep running forever so i had to restart. Apart from that very minor annoyance, the game is absolutely fun and challenging. If you like turn based tactical strategy, this is your ticket for a great ride due to the combination of luck/skill making each game a little different and therefore very replayable. You can play a nice quick round anytime you like which is always a good thing thanks to the skirmish mode with the same maps from the campaign as well as a few new ones. Bottom line, its worth the asking price and worth your time. Good luck in your struggle against the machines!

app_name: Command: Modern Air / Naval Operations WOTY
review_text: This is very heavily based on Harpoon 2/3. I still have the original Harpoon 2 command cards, and all the keys in-game are even bound to the same values. I've played a great deal of Harpoon 2 and 3, and this is Harpoon 2/3 with nothing removed, but with a lot of extra detail added. The multimonitor support is very nice too.  In a nutshell, it's Harpoon 2/3, with a graphical and UI over-haul, better performance, improved sensor modelling, mobile ground units, and with rates of turn and acceleration added for aircraft and vessels. This game is the ultimate in its genre, truly fantastic.

The concept itself is very unique, I've never played a game quite like this. However, there isn't much else going on here. The presentation is very minimalistic and the story tries too hard to be clever. You really need to love the core gameplay or you'll get bored within ten minutes.  It is one of the most innovative shooters I've played in recent years, I'll give it that. Just don't expect anything more. Definitely pick up on sale if you're at all interested.

I'm pretty new to this video game, only logging about 8 hours of gameplay so far. I haven't played a DBZ game since Budokai 3, so I thought it was a good time to step out of retirement and see what all the hype was about. Instead of making more 'TL;DR' material, I'll list what I view as pros and cons into my early Xenoverse career.  + Plenty of choice in terms of game play, in terms of following the story, doing side quests, learning moves from your 'master', etc. + Lots of customization. You can learn some of your favorite moves, mix and match, and become the type of fighter you want to. + The graphics are pretty. The moves look crisp (usually) and the environment is fun to play in. I haven't done this so far, but I miss kicking my enemy through mountains, like I could in Budokai!  - The voice actors don't have me convinced. I wish the original voices were used (from the Anime).  - Every now and then, during cutscenes, the mouths of the characters won't move but the audio will play. It takes away from the scene, in my opinion. - I assume this is my issue and not the game, but the controls on a keyboard/mouse feel clunky. This is the first fighting game I've played on my PC, and I find it hard to keep track of what I need to press. I ordered a controller to see if that'll help my cause. - It can be hard to find your enemy at times, if they are much higher or much lower than you, the camera angle seems to get messy.  All in all, a very fun game and one I can find myself playing a lot more. I hope I'll actually be able to improve my playing ability with the new controller, too. :)

I really do have fun with this game, but I would not recommend this game to anybody unless you are a hardcore baseball fan and really want to play a baseball game on the computer. When you buy the game, you know that it isn't going to be an MLB The Show quality baseball game, but a fun arcade style baseball game.  I do really enjoy a baseball game on the computer to play, but for how much the game costs, it is not worth it. It needs to polished a bit more, the controls need to be fixed and there needs to be a something that tells you the controls( if there is something besides the loading screen which lasts not long enough to read it, than it needs to be more apparent), and it also needs more content for how much it costs.  I would recommend this if it was $5 or even $10 dollars but it is a pricey $20 dollars and there are many other things you could get for that money. I do enjoy playing the game when I am bored but that is just me. I really do appreciate how baseball is being brought back to the computer and xbox, but this just isnt good enough. I have spent probably over 600 hours in MLB: The Show 14 and am still playing it, so I would love if the PC got it's own quality baseball game.  If the game goes on sale for anything $10 dollars or under I recommend picking it up, but for now you should just wait until it goes on sale.

So let me get something straight: I do not agree with the many things that are currently in the game. I am not white knighting IMC nor am I giving them a pass for this being Early Access.  I am, however, willing to stick by a game that is fun. Tree of Savior IS a fun game if we're putting aside all the glaring issues that you see in the many reviews here. I would just like to state that many MMORPGs have had rough launches. One game that reminds me of this launch is Diablo 3 if anyone remembers that. Launch was horrendous, riddled with login errors, had terrible end-game and leveling and the actual gear was a mess. Don't even get me started on the ridiculous Auction House. It's essentially the same mistake that Tree of Savior made, they crippled the concept of trading. However, Blizzard addressed the concerns of their playerbase, removed the Auction House, started making slow and steady steps towards a better game. I have been monitoring the official Tree of Savior forums and IMC's responses to the playerbase and I have to say that although there can be a lack of communication, THEY ARE TRYING. They have been fixing issues and working around the clock to deliver a good experience and have definitely been on the right track. It will take time, please be patient with them, but I have to say that all these negative reviews ARE JUSTIFIED. IMC has done this to themselves. They deserve all the negative backlash for their poor design choices, lack of proper foresight and just terrible management of the launch overall. However, I can understand that not all good games start off as such and I am willing to be patient with IMC to see where this game goes.   TL;DR IMC messed up big time and deserve all the negativity, but they are absolutely taking the right steps in communicating, addressing and fixing issues slowly. Step by step, they ARE working towards a better game. Patience is key with this game.   PROS -------- + Breath-taking Art + Excellent music + Highly intuitive and complex class system that really gives you endless customization + They rebalanced the leveling process and made it smoother + Controller support, mouse support and keyboard controls are smooth + IMC is taking proper measures to ensure a stable game + Very good fansites that provide plenty of information about classes, items, monsters, etc, including Skill/Stat simulators + All the CONS listed below are things that CAN (and most likely will due to the pressure from the playerbase) be fixed  CONS -------- - Extremely limited Auction House - Overpriced Token (Premium Subscription) - No F2P Trading whatsoever, P2P trading limited to 30/month (This is the biggest con by far) - Stat system is incredibly linear and the game is almost entirely gear dependent - Progression can be confusing and frustrating if you have not done extensive research - Dungeon entry limit is too strict; Two entries per day for F2P users is too harsh

I think the fact that I have unlocked over half the achievements in less than a week already says quite a bit. Even despite the resolution issues I had with this game since June, after a little while, I kind of just got used to it. Taking that aside, however, I really do like what the game has to offer; a good chunk of it is fanservice, and I feel that there are hits (  Ex: Soul of Cinder phase 2  ) and misses (  Ex: Intro of Siegward and to an extent Anor Londo  ) in this regard.   Taking this into account, however, it almost seems as if DS3 expects you to have played the first game to understand some of its lore. This isn't necessarily a bad thing, per se, but I suspect that it can oftentimes lose you if this is the Dark Souls game you start out with (EDIT: this seems to apply moreso to the main game; the first DLC is much less reliant on its predecessors    though it feels a bit similar to the Painted World from DS1 in terms of atmosphere; they even brought back the structure that you fight Priscilla in  ).  Other than that, some of the positives I've encountered are that a lot of the bosses I've enjoyed (plus their respective OST's, I'm pretty sure I've added about 10 tracks to my Soulsborne YouTube album in just this past week) and level design is pretty good. The world doesn't intertwine in itself like DS1 or Bloodborne, but each area has its own shortcuts to sort of compensate for that.  Some negatives I have with it, however, are that I feel that it is a bit too linear compared to the previous 2 games. Not that the entire game is set on a 100% strict path, but the absence of branching pathways like in the first two games (looking at how you got the Lord Souls in DS1 and the Great Souls in DS2) would likely diminish some of its replayability. Like I said before, the quality/resolution settings require a high end PC, so it can diminish the experience quite a bit if you have a laptop more optimized for anything up to console generation 7 (so Xbox 360/PS3 era). I do know about some balancing issues regarding PvP, but I'm not one to say on this too much since I rarely get invaded (also because I suck horrendously at PvP).  Also, (yes, I'm going to be that guy), the devs really should fix poise; too often I've gotten staggered with a mid build about as easily as someone that would have a light build, and it is annoying especially when I want to try to perform a strong attack with my Exiled Greatsword that takes a couple seconds to charge up.  (EDIT: Poise has finally been adjusted prior to the first DLC, so the complaints on this do not feel as relevant.)  Overall, though, I can't really say much else. As of writing this review, I just got into NG+, and since I don't think I'll have much time in the near future to go through it, I can't really say too much about DS3's replayability. The various classes are still there to experiment with (knight, sorcerer, assassin, etc.), so the game can still that degree of replayability as its predecessors (EDIT: As an example, some weapons from the first DLC like the throwing spear can be used for some neat challenge runs.). I still recommend playing this, especially if you want to see how the game itself plays out in the overall Dark Souls narrative.

### Executive summary statement: The trailers are misleading and in some cases blatantly lie. If you are looking for a casual lobby shooter themed in the 40k universe and you play without expectations, this may just be the game for you. Who knows, by the time they release on console it might even play like a finished game. Alternatively, just wait until Spacehulk:Deathwing comes out in November to get your 40k fix or for your massive multiplayer shooter needs, BattleField One launches later this month (Oct. 21st?).    ### Executive highlights: - Not an MMO! (trailer lies) this is a traditional 'lobby shooter' with unimplemented strategic map that doesn't do anything. Currently, there are less than a dozen maps in rotation. - Typical character/weapon grind with power curve that will leave new players at strong disadvantage. - Pretty graphics and sounds, except when they glitch. - Suboptimal weapon/faction balancing (broken in some cases, but with intent to be patched).    ### How long have I been playing? Backed this game almost from the beginning; Captain's package cost me about $120 back in the day? I was willing to spend this money on the 'hope' that this game would deliver on half of what it was setting out to accomplish, but in the end, I can't say with confidence that they've made it even close to that far.    ### Marketing deceptions, buyer beware: As it stands, the state of the game right now is not living up to its marketing or trailers: There is no persistent world or even a dynamic world map to be affected by your battles... these are false claims. This is not an MMO by any stretch of sanity.    ### Game modes currently available: There are less than a dozen arenas where four [poorly balanced] factions compete in a 'skirmish battle' (traditional capture point style conflict) or 'grand battle' (sequential capture point style conflict with an extra siege vehicle type and limited lives for the defenders). There is also a PvE game type where a squad of friends can clear an installation of AI controlled tyranid, but in my experiences playing it, the animations were a little buggy and the enemies showed a severe lack in variety or intelligence, which just made the game mode boring at best and downright frustrating if you had any expectations for it to begin with. There is also a garrison mode, which is really just a small lobby to try out weapons against static targets and not a real game mode at all IMO.   ### Balancing issues: There are countless posts on the Eternal Crusade forums bemoaning and highlighting the balance issues present between the four playable factions, but underneath all of this the real demon is the way the game implements mixed melee/ranged combat. The melee system itself is a nice try at a rock/paper/scissors type combat, but with buggy netcode or server latency being combined with poor balance decisions in animation/movement speed differences between factions, this results in some unusual and even abusable game experiences.    ### Character grind and power curve: Starting out, new players will have a completely different level of challenge and handicap depending on the faction they choose. Some factions get a wider range of options for starting loadout, such as an anti-vehicle kit, whereas other factions will not have this same option for a new player. You must spend time in game to gain achievement points (this number is based on such factors as your kills and objectives) to unlock certain weapon types and perks as well as requisition points, which are used to purchase basic variants of these new weapon kits or random loot boxes, which may include better weapons or even just visually different weapons with the same stats. One limitation with the current unlock system is that it's character specific... if you make a Space Wolves character, for example, their unlocks will not carry over to a Blood Angels character even though they are the same faction... your initial character creation will lock you in visually to whichever chapter or subfaction you started with. Also worth noting, while there are plans to allow for payment to increase the number of character slots you have, currently there are four character slots to match the four playable factions, so choose carefully what you do with these slots.    ### User interface oversights: While the user interface is themed well and visually appealing, it is not very intuitive in places and really makes grouping up with your friends to play a match quite a bear. For example, once you manage to get your invites out to form a 'war party' with your friends, if at any point your party backs out of a queue the whole party is disbanded and you must re-invite all over again. It's also not clear at times where you need to go to perform certain actions. For example, every character has a limited 'inventory' for the gear they unlock and acquire (most of which, you will find is redundant or unuseful to you), but there is no single place you can go to manage this inventory and 'relinquish' these unhelpful items you've acquired... instead, you must build out a loadout that can equip that item, then open up the weapon (which you must purchase) for the item in question before being able to select it and relinquish it for more inventory space.    ### Immersion and visual effects: Right now, the best immersion is to be had with the 'grand battle' game mode, where you can be a part of a 60-player match fighting to defend or assault one of several fortress maps. The sound effects are awesome, except for the moments when they glitch... and the visual effects for the maps, character models, and weapons are beautiful. You will believe you are in the 40k universe right up until the moment you can't drive a vehicle through some invisible barrier that was implemented for game balance or the time you try to shoot across the map with a long range weapon only to find there's some invisible wall preventing you from hitting your target.

Oregon Trail meets a 4x game. Or maybe it's better to describe it as a tile-based King of Dragon Pass. Your little people are all individually named and kitted out; you can make swords for them or shirts or knives or whatever it is you feel they need. They go and have CYOA adventures or fight with cards. It's rad as hell. Do not expect an expansionist strategy game, though -- you get one city, and that is it. You can establish temporary camps elsewhere, and they can easily become self-sufficient if they're in the right spot, but they will never have any population growth and you can't build structures there either. But you WILL want camps, because there's resources that you're going to want which are not near your city, and the only way to get them is to explore or mine. It's very medieval and very good and you should buy it, alright?  Just buy it.

It's not the worst I've played... But to call it a game is a stretch. Heres my breakdown.  The Good: Lightweight. Despite being pretty to look at, it's not that intencive. This is Unity at it's most streamlined, I think.  Uses external audio, so you can literally use anything. Go for it. Literally any website, format of audio/video, or anything like that is usable.  The Bad: Movement controls. What made Audiosurf so playable was the quick, mouse-based controls. It made moving left to right easy, sharp, and accurate. This is simply clunky in comparison. Even with a gamepad, the analogs just don't feel 'good'.  Uses external audio. While it can use anything, it's also not user friendly. Being lightweight isn't an excuse to force the player to use another program to play music/media.  The Ugly: Firing controls. Firing is music-based. And the special secondary weapon is time-based. Everything is automatic. Which means theres no chance to actuallly use skill, because the only thing you can do is go left or right. Sniping blocks or even destroying all of one block becomes a chore as you pray that this riff causes enough shots, and hoping that you don't 'run out' when you have a strong on 10c blocks.  Zero consiquence for missing blocks. This makes some sense as firing is automatic, random and skill-less, but theres literally no insentive to play this. Just fire up a random playlist and let it play itself on repeat while you're at work and sleeping. I bet you'd have all the upgrades in a few days or so. Idle games can be fun, but this could be so much more...  Uses external audio. Yes. It's greatest flexibility is it's greatest weakness. Being able to read off the audio output is a nice feature, but it shouldn't be the ONLY way to play. It ruins immersion, and really makes it feel more like what it is; Not a game, but a semi-interactive visualizer.  I'd recomend it simply because it is pretty, and makes music fun to watch... But as a game, it's kind of a flop.  Visuals: 11/10 Gameplay: 1/10

This game nails most of the zombie action FPS conventions right: the action is frantic, the gore is fantastic, the weapons have real kick and the levels are inspired and appropiately apocalyptic. The characters are represented with style, along with interesting indirect narrative gimmicks that lend a good amount of atmosphere to the game.  If there are gripes to be had, is the fact that, once you play through the campaigns enough times, the game turns really stale. The layouts are still linear enough, despite the AI Director's best efforts, to rush them through with little effort once you have experience. However, tackling the hardest difficulty settings with a group of friends can prove enormously entertaining.  In all, a great game, worthy successor to the original, with a bit less grit.

Quite simply one of the greatest strategy games ever made.  From the outset - Crusader Kings is not for everyone.   It will be best enjoyed by people who are interested in deep systems and the interplay between them. Those seeking a way to crack the 'optimal' way to play, and those seeking to 'win' will be disappointed, whereas those seeking emergent systems and effects will enjoy hundreds of hours of discovery.   It is therein that lies CK2's greatest strength - creating a narrative through emergent systems.  Crusader Kings II relies largely on the player setting their own goals and then working towards them. Be they immediate goals, medium term aspirations, or long term dreams, a large part of CK2's appeal lies in pondering how best to 'exploit the code' to reach your ends. In fact, the best way to play CK2 is not to play to win, but to let the game 'happen'; to let your dynasty's fortune ebb and flow with the passage of time, creating a narrative of a family streching across the centuries.    Crusader Kings 2 is a game that you can sink literally hundreds of hours into and still find out new and exciting things to do. CK2 isn't a perfect game; the learning curve can be steep, particularly for those new to the genre, and the presentation is a little short of the cutting edge. But, with a fantastic level of developer support, continual free and paid content additions, hundreds of mods, and a lively, friendly and supportive community, it is fair to say that CK2 is one of the greatest strategy games of all time and, for strategy fans, possibly one of the greatest games of all time. For this writer with over 30 years of gaming behind him and hundreds of hours on the game (mostly on the Gamersgate version), it's certainly cracked the Top Ten!

*sigh* Of Orcs And Men..where do I start ~   http://www.oforcsandmen.com/index.php?lang=en&rub=game  (their website might give you more impressions)   To be honest, I probably would have never played this game if Frosty hadn't gifted it to me.  But I am pleased that he did! I was interested a long time in this game, but never felt to buy it - which would have been a huge mistake.   When I got thrown in the world of Orcs And Men I felt a little lost.. the controls are very clunky though the tutorials were helping a lot. Anyway the game is basically about a war between Orcs, Goblins, Wizards, Humans and some other races, and you get to play as the main protagonist Arkhail who's a member of the Bloodjaws after leaving his old clan / family (I wont spoil anything important here). He later on meets the unique Goblin Styx (a lone wolf) who's told to show Arkhail the way to accomplish his mission to make an end to this War.   But everything gets out of control and the two find themselves in a ~10 hour long Journey full of twists and mixed up game play: Styx -rather small and fast - is a master of stealth and you got the option to get 1-3 enemies out of the way quietly before entering a fight. Arkhail on the other hand is big and slow but with powerful attacks when things get real.  So the game play styles of both together were the best possible combination to me in a game like that... though i preferred to play Styx :3   The characters of both could not be more different.. Styx is more grumpy and pessimistic / afraid due big challenges while Arkhail is willing to die for his cause / clan in Honor. This gets especially clear during several of the amazing cutscenes and dialogues where you got sometimes the option to chose whether Styx or Arkhail answer / choose their own way (for example: Trust someone or Kill stuff -Arkhail / not trust someone and flee - Styx )  I loved this game...it lacks in a lot of things because its very very and I mean VERY linear..not much to explore on 1 path besides the main each mission but it fits the game somehow.. I cant even tell why I like it so much - but what I know, is that the music is AWESOME! it changes during calm situations and fights and its just amazing in my opinion x) the setting is beautiful if you don't care too much about graphics - they are nice and enough for a game like that - the atmosphere created is of course subjective but I can recommend most of the time.   To sum this up.. game play might not be the best but the game plays well, graphics are neither too low nor to high and RPG fans shouldn't bother anyway, Story was interesting though the ending could have been implemented better :D   If you are a fan of linear RPG's with a rather short but Interesting Story + setting this game might be worth your time if you grab it on a sale for ~ 7 bucks   'Styx: Master of the Shadows' is the Prequel to this game and I'll play it now with a Joy! Hope you didn't have a hard time reading and the review was helpful :)  Have a good day folks!

A fun romp through side-scrolling shooty action. The game plays very similarly to Metal Slug and games of that nature but with a bit more forgiveness. Gameplay's pretty standard and fun without too much innovation in terms of controls and whatnot but what keeps it interesting is the customizable weapons. You completely customize your loadout with 6 different weapon parts at any given time that have different stats that affect how your character plays. Will you take a handgun with only 2 parts and be super fast and agile but with limited firepower? Will you take a huge minigun with tons of ammo that pierces walls and completely destroys things before it gets on screen with the side-effects of moving slower than a slug in molasses? The entire game revolves around finding loot to craft new weapons and mods that change how you play your character. It works extremely well because of the focus that it has on variety and uniqueness to each weapon. It's not perfectly balanced yet but it's still in development so it's not an issue. An excellent game that really gets the visceral fun of just raining bullets down pat.

It fits the mad max universe well. * The upgrades are many, but customizations are very limited. Your car is everything, it's basicly maxs soul. So I found it surprising when there were so many upgrades but no customization. After all customizations are what makes your car yours. * Controlling the car was often challangeing and sometimes frustrating, the devs are not car fanatics that's for sure. You will find the controlls sloppy and a lot of wiered mechanics. For example, adding weight to the car creates a delay from stearing input and upgrading the suspension gives you more turning radius. I really wanted to like this game. I love the TV show and the original game so when I saw this on the steam sale I was really excited, but the game just wasn't that great.   Let's start with the cut scenes, there are way too many of them. I'm sure it is a common complaint but it is very much a problem with this game. To make matters worse, the default settings don't make it very clear on how to skip them. It took me way too long to realize I had to press esc and go the menu and select the option to skip the scenes. Though, it is good that they at least give you the option to skip especially on the first play through.   The controls were okay, but they weren't perfect. There were a few times where I wish the game gave me a little more control over what was going on by making the controls a little more sensitive, and I'm not a huge fan of the drop down jumping that occurs when you are on a rope or a chain. I wish the character could have done a little more other than just kind of let go of the rope and fall in a general direction. Though, this particular complaint might just be a personal preference from me being somewhat spoiled by modern platformers with really great controls.   The other thing I really disliked was the way the falling platforms worked. When I jumped onto a collapsing platform it would start to shake while the character was in the air. This didn't make a lot of sense to me. The platform shouldn't begin collapsing because you are in the air above it. The mechanic should wait until your character lands on the object in question. Not only does this make more sense logically but also I can't recall many games that do this in general.  Other than that, I noticed a few graphical glitches when I played the game. Occasionally a random enemy would blink out of existence and back into view. It didn't happen all that often, but it did happen a couple of times. I'm not overly concerned with it though as it didn't really affect the gameplay much and was pretty rare.  The game did have some positives. The voice acting was great and I liked the artwork. The levels were pretty much what I would expect from a remastered version of a game. I do think they could have done a better job of things. While I cannot recommend this particular game, I would like to see them remaster a few other older games and work on getting better at it before maybe considering trying to make new versions of some of these old games. I think there is room for improvement, and I would hate to see this stop because of a subpar attempt.

If you had the chance to play Telltale's The Walking Dead you pretty much know what to expect from this game: a very well crafted narrative experience, maybe a little poor gameplay-wise but very compelling, rich of moral dilemmas and interesting views on delicate subjects.   Compared to The Walking Dead this The Wolf Among Us is probably a little less dramatic, you won't form with Bigby the kind of emotional bond you formed with Clementine, but it is much more mature in its main themes. The Big Bad Wolf is a very well written character, and you get to explore the way the others connect to him, who he really is, and what he has (wants?) to do to protect Fabletown from a strange series of murders with a very hard-boiled vibe. Secondary characters are a lot and very well diversed, interesting backstories, accents, generally well detailed (even from an aesthetic point of view). Just like in The Walking Dead series your choices hardly change anything in the long run, but the little details counts, because they affect the way YOU feel about all the characters, their motivation and their reasons to support or hate Bigby.   As long as you have very clear in your mind the gameplay (no puzzles, some QTE, heavily focused on dialogues) The Wolf Among Us is a game that I'll absolutely recommend. I loved it maybe more than The Walking Dead, and I really hope there will be a season 2. Thumbs up.

This game is a gem, a masterpiece. Eugen has fine-tuned every title in this serie, and in this the latest one put themselves in the absolute frontline of realistic RTS games. This is a deep game, with a massive amount of real world unit types which allows you to build your own combined force. The game mechanics are quite easy to learn but hard to master, but the pure amount of statistics of every single unit means you have to put in a great amount of time to learn how to use each unit the best way possible. You have to concentrate on your strategy and mastering the use of different unit combinations for the given situation and be prepared of your opponents units and how to counter them. The campaigns are not the strenght here, but the multiplayer are, and it is excellent. Eugen is continously and often updating the game and tweaking/correcting units, game balance etc to fine-tune the game according to the players response. DLC´s with new countries are continously beeing released. Prepare to loose sleep. The more you put into this game, the more it gives you back. It is timeless in many aspects.

I tried, but in the end i didn't really like the game. tl;dr verdict: Not fun enough!  Easy mode is waaay to easy and normal & hard are very punishing. But more importantly, it just isn't very fun to play. Nothing feels 'cool' or exciting except a few bosses that do manage to stand out. Gameplay works but just feels kinda uninspired. The later decks introduce better enemies and the pace picks up a bit, sadly most of my time was spend running around all too similar layouts and combat situations.  It's not a bad game, so you won't regret a purchase, it just isn't very good. Compared to Bionic Dues it manages to bungle the UI in a different way and it's overall simpler to get going, but there's no strategic mode other than advancing through 5-level sets.  The worst fault of the game is probably that i never felt invested in my squad or cared about the characters. Despite some random banter and unique abilities and upgrade paths, there wasn't any connection.  Also, for a game that's fairly straightforward and easy to get into, somehow the pacing felt very very slow. Even with AI movement sped up, it takes too long to finish a level and it doesn't feel rewarding.  The pixel art doesn't work for this game. Some assets look nice and clear, while others look downright ugly.  Steam Marines is not a bad game, and it's not even dissapointing. I can't bash the developers because very little here actually stinks. Unfortunately nothing is above average either making me wonder how i spent around 7 hrs playing it.

Ok, so... There are a lot of starbound, minecraft, terraria clones out and about these days. They involve hours of endless grinding for materials and 'exploring'. Some of them have interesting quirks like an advanced crafting systems for gizmos and gadgets or a semi-sophisticated battle system. Both of those things exist in this game which create depth and playability, but the game lacks soul. What I mean by that is the world is rather repetative, enemies are boring and forgettable, gathering materials drags out for hours and there's limited to no explanation for how to accomplish certain things. I mean I get that the idea is to explore and discover how to construct and craft different things, but it feels more like a drawn out scavenger hunt without clues to the locations of the things you need. The game is in early access, and we all remember how terraria and starbound were at this stage, so we'll see how the devs do with creating more interesting content. For now, I'd recommend holding off on purchasing.

Too Far, Too Fast, and In What Direction?  Hype is a terrible thing. Especially when it’s directed by Ubisoft’s incredible marketing team. Seriously, they should be showered with praise for their ability to spin the titles of the Assassin’s Creed franchise into something that their “consumer base” will want to consume.  For everyone thinking that this was just a continuation of Ubisoft’s money spinning of this franchise, you’re simply looking for a simple excuse for, let’s face it, a game that didn’t make par because the developer threw the hype train into high gear on the day of reveal. That’s not to say that I’m disagreeing with you.   Yes, the game is riddled with bugs. Yes, the software runs like a sloth on hallucinogens. Yes, the service supposedly handling the load of multiplayer isn’t doing its job.   But people are forgetting that a tonne of people at Ubisoft put their heart and soul into making this game BE the next-gen title that we and they were hoping for. The artists, especially; the texture, level and environment designers; the guys behind mo-cap, animation and voice capture; hell, even the actors behind the mo-cap did a decent job.   The people that should be getting an earful are, firstly, the optimisation team. Either that or they should be getting bereavement for being harassed because they had a deadline out of their control. Secondly, the people that are in charge of the online services, connectivity and servers; OR those in charge who FORCED the game out with the online services not being fully ready. 30% of the issues with the game would be and hopefully WILL BE sorted with connectivity fixes, another 30% with the fixes to game optimization.   The last 40% are simply arguable issues with the game itself. It seems like Ubisoft pushed too far too fast, wanting to claim this dream of a “next-gen” gaming experience. The combat feels organic, but is too interrupted by sometimes irritating camera angles and a lack of warning when you’re about to be shot-fodda, or smacked in the head with an axe. The immersive Paris with randomly generated side-events is nice, but far too samey to be enjoyable for the length of a full play through, especially considering the size of the city. The open-ended assassinations would be a nice change, if you weren’t funnelled down a predictable path determined by “optional” opportunities that help you get the mission done.   The Helix interludes are nice little morsels of distraction from the main game. But even they are let down by again, being too samey, and secondly, reminding all the fans of the series that there is a world OUTSIDE the simulation that seemingly, plays little to no part in what happens INSIDE it.   All in all- the game deserves a 7.5/10. Maybe an 8. But as it stands, until these fixes get fixed, it can only stand at a 5.   Get it. Give the massive team that worked on this the recognition they deserve. But only do it if things get fixed (stay posted), and or when the game drops in price. Just don't be an ♥♥♥ and post a mindless negative review because you're too selfish to see the work that actually went into the game.  Specs: (The game runs decently at 1600/900 with most of everything on high/ultra apart from AA)  i5-4570 3.2Ghz GTX760 8GBs GRAM   PS: To Ubisoft  So much of your player base are actual fans of the franchise. Get the simple stuff done first. Please. The things the best games in the franchise were famed for- the story and protagonist, the mechanics, the integration into history (a woven tale that actually wants you to care about the history and its important figures), and for goodness’ sake- give us fans back our modern day content - you nearly had it perfect with Black Flag (exploration if you wish- but no major or compulsory ties to story/achievements).   If you really want to make any more money off this franchise, stop thinking you know what’s best, and start listening to your fans and player base rather than your bottom line. And anyone at the top of the Ubi-ladder- respect your artists more, and stop pushing their work out under unfinished circumstances.

This is an interesting little game that combines a choose-your-own-adventure type of story with RPG elements. The idea is pretty freaking cool, as well as the setting and atmosphere. However, I have a few problems with this game.  1) The RPG elements are not explained at all. The stats are self explanatory for the most part, but it still would have have been nice to know what each stat did for you specifically.   2) The class and morality system, while cool in concept, don't feel too distinguished. Outside of a few choices that offer different flavor text and some different stats, it is entirely possible to build your stats in such a way as to transform yourself into a different class completely. The classes just didn't feel too different in the end. I also didn't understand what the consequences were to being entirely good or evil.   3) I kept running into a specific bug that froze my game on multiple occasions. This only occurred when I was quickly moving through the exposition, but it made it annoying because I wanted to move through text I had already read as fast as possible and this bug prevented that. Also, it seemed some words were missing or misplaced in certain parts of the game.  The saving grace of this game was seeing all the different consequences of the actions you take. Trying to complete this game gave me a newfound determination. I would love to see this style of gameplay more refined and fleshed out. There is a good concept in here. I was gifted this and it would totally worth being free. It might be worth 5 bucks, more worth it on sale.

Basically Sid Meier's Gettysburg remade with modern UI and graphics. Nothing revolutionary or very ambitious, just a nice simple quick addictively fun game. Scenarios are like short segments of the battle, usually only like 1/4 of the army is in any one mission, and the full battle is played as a branching campaign of these short scenarios. Unit losses are preserved (and even positions, sometimes) between scenarios, which is a nice effect that gives a sese that you really are using the same brigades in each engagement.  The AI isn't horrible, but I don't see much evidence of it's awesomeness or intelligence... it seems to understand 'attack' and 'don't attack', and maybe its positioning troops defensively (or maybe that's just the default destination reinforcements are sent to?) but it doesn't seem to know how to flank or anything more complicated than a mass rush attack. The gameplay is basic enough that there isn't really much it can do wrong, though, so it's decently competitive anyway.

This new installment in the Deus Ex saga is a worthy successor to Deus Ex: Human Revolution with refined gameplay, graphics and map design. The freedom of Choosing your own way to solve a problem has been futher expanded here. There is no one situation (that I can think of) where you have only one way to do something. Most times, there are 4-5 ways to approach a solution to a problem. To all those who hated the boss fights in Deus Ex Human Revolution, you will be happy to know that these are absent from this game. It's all around a more polished and better designed product and I enjoyed every moment of my 30 hours playing the game.  The only things I will say is inferior to Human Revolution is the story depth and some bugs and mouse issues that plagued me at launch. The story is fine, it's not amazing but it does it's job. The way it is being told is clear that the writers are thinking long term now that the franchise is a sure thing for Eidos. Most of the technical issues and mouse problems have been resolved at this review post but I am finding the load times to be brutal.  There are a large number of bad reviews for this game but I am seeing a lot that are focusing too much on stuff that really doesnt matter imo. Many negative reviews seem to be centered around the microtransactions (which I didnt even notice in my playthrough) and the day one DLC being only usuable once on a single save file. I need to say this....it's time we treat day 1 DLC as what it is....trash. You can argue about this all day long, but in the end what are you paying for? To play the game asap!, or to sit and look at Jensen in a different coat. Let's be real here guys. I didnt preorder my game for the ♥♥♥♥♥♥ extra gimmicks. I preordered so when I got home from work on launch day, I could dive right in.  I am a true fan of Deus Ex. I have played and completed Deus Ex, Infinite War, Human Revolution and now Mankind Divided and I hope that this review helps fans like myself make an informed decision before a purchase. Well worth the buy - 8/10

What can I say about this game…?  First of all let’s get something on that table, I am an extreme casual player, I don’t raid and I don’t PVP. I have nothing against people that do, it just doesn’t interest me. However I DO play MMO’s. Started with Dark Age of Camelot, probably hit every one since then, played WOW for nearly a decade. I am a crap player, I like WOW because even for a sub-standard player like myself general questing and exploration is as easy as 1, 2…face roll.   Yet as much as I love such games I get all fantasied out, I get sick of the same world, the same ideas, it’s like GOT is good but come on sometimes you need a change, you can even get bored of chocolate. To this end I sometimes cheat on what fantasy MMO I am playing with liberal applications of EVE, SWTOR, and STO….I tried this because, well I was on GW2 and people were talking about it, really it doesn’t get as much hype as it rightly deserves.  That said there is very little here that hasn’t been done before, but come on, MMO’s are scraping the bottom of the barrel now, it’s a tried and tested formula the only way forwards is to offer more options to growing army of players looking for different things. Maybe stop them logging off your world to go to other games like Mine Craft and the new No Mans Sky. That said though what is here is frankly as engaging as all get out.  The cartoon graphics and irreverent tongue in cheek humour is deliciously juxtaposed with some of the darker and grittier plot elements I have come across in a game. Even in starting areas such contrast in my opinion heightens the shock element, because with such humour and harmless cartoon graphics you are unequipped for the bite.   I personally love the ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ization and melding of the concepts, Wild West, Firefly-esque ideas collide with Star Wars, Trek and Galaxy Quest almost a parody driven world like Terry Pratchett’s disc world that feels familiar to the player…that familiarity for me makes the world warmer, comforting, and allows me to draw parallels with pop culture that delights the geeky girl I am. Nirvana….  Don’t get too comfortable though, because this is no face roll game, even questing presents a challenge!, one of those players who can’t get your ♥♥♥♥ out the fire in WOW…That mind-set is going to be a distinct disadvantage here. Moving and interrupt is key, and from what I know from guild chat about raids and dungeons, throwing everything into DPS is also going to be your undoing. I can fully empathize with players coming here from WOW thinking they’re the “Bidness” and the “Muts Nuts” then getting their arses royally handed to them, won’t like this game. However if you like a challenge and some peril for your buck it’s definitely worth a try.  Another thing to consider this is the most F2P game I have come across, the only one so far to allow free players to acquire via playing currency to spend in their cash shop. I mean what the hell people…free players don’t have to subscribe, you are going to find that nothing really vital is locked behind a pay wall, and the things that you may want, a few cash shop purchases gives a currency that even opens those….account wide.   However the best I leave to last. Housing in this game is EPIC…to be honest I would subscribe to this game just for that. Although people do say the population suffers, maybe not as many people for raids and dungeons however I don’t believe that, I just think guilds are more discerning and this game is not conducive to pugs, that content is a tad too challenging. I do believe that many of the people who would be otherwise raiding are on their housing plots creating. I haven’t come across a game that does it so well, I really think more work on this aspect of the game and it could sustain its self indefinitely. There is nothing you can’t build, and what’s more the components are used throughout the game its self so your housing looks seamless. This gives an immersion and vitality to role play sets that is unique and frankly awe inspiring. There are a lot of builders, don’t believe me, check em out on You Tube….I recommend the time lapse of the throne room in Game of Thrones…  This game hasn’t the population of WOW but it’s healthy compared to the low level hitters we have out there, it’s fun and dark in turns, with humour and sadness in equal measure. It’s graphics never seem to date and are aesthetic eye candy. It won’t be to everyone’s taste and if you like to primarily raid or dungeon in pugs or aren’t skilled enough to hold your own in a guild, I could see that it’s as frustrating as hell. If you are like me, a casual, likes to explore, quest, role play even, and you have any creative tendencies….just try it, it’s so worth it.  :Disclaimer:  Since this my first review I have had a lot of friend requests, some people even have been so good as to seek me out in game. You can always hang out with me on Jabbit: Exiles in my guild 'Sunny Disposition'. A very active, friendly social guild catering to mainly housing and Rp, PM either Maeve Morrigan or join my recruitment net discord  https://discord.gg/w6nYTdE  and I'll pick you up asap.   Yours Sarah

Let's make this as short and simple as I can.    It's a good game, with tons of replayability.  It involves 3 different killers, and 4 different survivors, each with their own abilities. Gameplay as the survivors consists of turning on 5 generators, that, when powered, will allow you to escape the map by turning a lever to let a metal door slide open. All while running away from the killer. As the killer, your job is to prevent the survivors from leaving the map, by hacking them to pieces and sacrificing them on hooks to your deity. 3 themed maps, with 4 variants each. Randomly placed objectives and structures (except for one type of building exclusive to each variant of a map). A never ending progression system that let's you unlock items forever. Pretty exciting gameplay moments, whether it is hooking a survivor or succesfully escaping the map with the killer on your toes.    Now, does it have problems? Yes. It does. It lacks a party method, so no playing with your friends. (Devs said they will patch this into the game as soon as they can). Ranking is a little bugged, and people leaving matches will derank you. Connection is P2P, so expect some laggy matches. No rebinding keys. Graphic options are pretty lackluster, with a single button for it ranging from Low all the way to Ultra. Local save files, which means cheaters.  I'll also say this game will have microtransactions (Devs confirmed it, but are not sure on what model to follow. They did clarify no P2W, though), just so you know.  Now the thing is, I really like this game. The issue? Well, the game is in a state where it shows it could've used a bit more time to iron out the details. Because of this, I can't recommend it at this time. As soon as the Devs fix these problems, I'll be the first to edit this review.

I haven't been glued on a chair to play a game in a while...  I like the idea colonizing other worlds and I like strategy/micro management games. Planetbase gives that and more. Unlike other games that toss immense data and spreadsheets at player, Planetbase forces player to observe and be aware what is going on. Player must be aware of risks and issues that can struck his colony and to figure it - where is and what is the problem. Even when player achieve 'stable colony' that still doesn't mean player is free to go wild.   Player do not have direct control over colonists as in most games. This feel strange at first but once you get used to it, it is better that way - controlling 300 colonist individually would be really too much. Player must understand the needs of colonists, face the issues, solve problems and prosper above all.  This is one of rare challenging games when some simple thing (i.e. selling too much unneeded resource) can kill your entire colony. Also this game has overwhelming feel 'Just to make this one thing...' and you keep saying that for the last 20 made things. I really like that game loads quickly.  There is not all so nice and well. The bad things about Planetbase are:  - AI is strange (especially in crucial moments like engineer builds connector to the Solar Panel but then he ignores Solar Panel even though all needed materials are there) - many features should be extended:   * visitors can be more gamy mechanic not just intruders or tourists * helping ships in distress also offers huge potential * new home made tech research could be a thing and to be specialized per planet * possibility to have some colony speciality that can affect gameplay * ABOVE ALL WE NEED NEW PLANETS WITH THEIR SPECIFIC CHALLENGES (lava planet? liquid methane planet? planet without solar power possibilities but almost constant wind and slowed movement outside?- can only enhance specialization idea)  - better control over prioritization  - better control over saving games - better airlock model (new airlock that can let in or out more people) - windowed mode  I hope game will evolve and offer new content. Even as a base game, I recommend it to everyone.

I completed this game on PSP ages ago and just started replaying it for the second time after getting a new copy. Gotta say, I'm still enjoying it despite knowing what goes down. Really looking forward to unlocking the end mode to waste away my time on chibi micromanaging endeavors that don't feel related to the story at all, in all earnest.   Is this game a detective game? Yes and no. There's clue gathering, some puzzle solving in regards to how the pieces fit together to form the transpired events, but it's not in league with other more thought provoking detective games like the Ace Attorney series. That said, if you like Ace Attorney like myself, this won't kill you.  The difficulty is pretty much all in your own endurance. There's no real penalty for guessing something wrong when it comes to class trials other than needing to start the trial over. Trials are so quick though that it's hardly worth calling it a penalty. The most gamey part is in the trials with the Trigger Time events. Wish they just passed on that as it just makes the experience frustrating.  Aside from murder and some pretty dark humor, there's some good ol' sex jokes (that are surprisingly funny), completing the circle of a good time and adding some good character to the script. Still, I could do for less panty shots, but what's to be expected from a Japanese game? Japan can't help itself.  Let's go with one more question: why wouldn't you want this game if you like the above? If you're expecting Nine Hours, Nine Person, Nine Doors or Virtue's Last Reward, you need to lower your expectations. This game is not complex. There are times it will make you think, but it won't keep you on your toes. You want to play this if you're more for finding passive interaction with a quota of gaming elements to pass the grade.  That's about it. Why the ♥♥♥♥ did I write this again? You know if you want this, person. Act on your urge.

Up front, if you are at all interested in the game, look at the Rebirth remake. It's not based in Flash, it should have far less bugs, and the difficulty shouldn't change with the updates. It SHOULD be the definitive version, look for negative reviews there to see if new bugs have appeared.  I'm glad to be finished with this game, as the gameplay is just mediocre and tends to drag on with massive repetition. You are tasked with navigating (often with no map) floors full of enemies to find few items and the boss to get to the next floor. Bosses are constructed on around 12 concepts, using reskinned designs to pad out to the 40+ advertised. Several items have similar or identical effects. Rooms are always the same size, with no real visual variety. I understand that these limitations are largely due to being based on Flash, but WHO decided Flash was the way to go? Being programmed for Flash caused performance issues, achievement bugs, limited updates, and didn't provide many benefits. A mod is necessary just to allow  resizeable windows  and the option to backup save data, so thank the community for actually making this game functional. Speaking of save data, it's slightly important to back it up since clearing Flash cookies will wipe your progress. Cloud sync seems inconsistent, and problems are known to occur by using CCleaner. Flash should be reserved for free online games, not this.  Even in the Rebirth remake, the game is somewhat limited and dated. The visuals will appeal to some, along with the old memes and references. Gameplay is a light bullet-hell roguelike, with no twin-stick freedom to shoot. Animations and physics are awkward. Most of the gameplay is just brute-force trials, hoping the random items are decent. You are expected to beat the game at least 20 times to unlock most of the content, but not much variety is added. It gets tiring, and the only real challenge comes from the final 2 copy-pasted bosses. Those bosses require at least 16 completions to unlock, and they are only difficult because they break so many of the rules you are taught about the game. Enemies shoot projectiles in arcs, and you need to watch the shadows to know if you will be hit. These bosses fill the rooms with projectiles, and have blue shadows instead of black. The floor being blue, along with the projectiles, makes it artificially challenging. Just make the damage invisible at that point.  Luckily, I had limited deaths in normal play, and didn't really struggle with the game. The Candle item just breaks the game, and most runs have a few decent items. Most damage is avoidable, and Spirit Hearts make the game a breeze. However, getting most of these items and progress means playing slots, grinding through many rooms, and spending a lot of time seeing the same gameplay and bosses. I mean, the not-quite-final bosses that just constantly spawn small enemies, those are SO interesting to grind down slowly as half the time they are invulnerable. After playing this and Gish (a sleeper mediocre game that didn't get a raving fanbase) I really don't feel either has good game design or ideas.  I like shooters and roguelikes.  Tower of Guns  has amazing gameplay, far more variety, and progression not requiring beating the game 20 times.  Not The Robots  has strong gameplay, great variety, and progression that never requires beating the game. If you want a RoboTron game,  Ultratron  is one of many that actually supports twin-sticks natively. I really can't recommend the game concept, and I feel Rebirth will still have the same bad game design and issues. There's hundreds of hours potentially, but it's all the same rooms and bosses. But if you must get it, go for Rebirth. This edition has definitely passed its prime.

Mass Effect is pretty cool, I guess.  You play as Shepard, who can be either male or female, but I played him as a man, so I will be addressing him as a he. Sorry Anita.   The game takes place in the future where I somehow haven't conquered the world. Humanity, in its efforts to into space, exluding Poland, find a ancient alien relic called the Mass Relay at the edge of the solar system. They activate it, which is apparently a big no-no, and starts a interstellar war with a alien race called the Turians, it's okay though because you can bang one later. All greviances lost.   Depending on the backstory you picked for Shepard, there is a chance he can compete with Batman on the world's deadest parents competition. As part of the Earth military, Shepard is sent off to a planet called Eden Prime where he must find out what happened to the country bumpkins and their space farms. He finds corpses, oh no! After the death of a Spectre, this games version of the hall monitors, Shepard is brought to the forefront of the galactic community as the first human hall monitor. Gonna monitor those halls so good. Through his Star Wars journey, Shepard discovers new friends, bitter enemies, and discovers his infatuation for blue buttcheeks, you go Shepard, get that blue berry.   The combat is a mix between RPG elements and cover based shooting. You hide behind chest-high walls while popping out occasionally to shoot some fool or hit them with your Jedi mind powers. Like all older Bioware games, Mass Effect likes to tell you what's going on. There is a codex that covers aliens, places, alien places, weapons, alien weapons, goblin darts, and historical events, and alien historical events... Did I mention the aliens? They're kind of important. I appreciate the time they took into making the game feel like it has it's own world instead of just being a series of events.   The story isn't the greatest thing ever, but it does enough to get you lost in it and forgetting that you live on a raft going down the Mississippi because you won one too many hobo fights without throwing the match. Let's see you catch me now, Booker.   Should you play Mass Effect?   The characters are fun to be around and the story and gameplay is engaging enough to keep you interested. Your journey for blue butt cheeks should last you a few hours and it will probably keep you interested enough to play the sequel. Not the second sequel though, don't do that to yourself, you're beautiful and don't deserve that.   I give Mass Effect two butt cheeks out of ten.
