I was really astonished by the sheer amount of quality this game delivers for the amount of money that I put down. It reminded me of the good old times, that I would reach out for a game in the heavily discounted bin and discover a gem. So what kind of game is Faster Than Light?  Faster than light is a management game set in space. The game starts you off with your own spaceship and crew. You will unlock more spaceships and layouts of those spaceships while completing challenges and winning the game. When you start the game you are immediately informed about your mission. You are part of the federation fleet and you have crucial information which needs to be delivered to the main fleet. The problem is, rebels are chasing you down to make sure you are not able to relay your crucial information to your fleet. Thus begins the challenge, you need to explore as much as you can without falling in the hands of the rebels. The places you can visit are marked on your map. Every point has lines attached to them which indicate where you can access that area from. It is very important that you strategically choose you destinations in order to explore as much as possible without falling in the hands of the rebels. Every time you travel the rebels will come closer to you.   The amount of things that you can do and find in this game is astonishing. While exploring you will encounter shops, people in need, enemies and other kinds of encounters. If you complete them, you can gain resources like: scrap metal, drone parts, fuel, crew members, missiles and sometimes even upgrades for your spaceship. These resources are vitally important because you are going to need them in order to defeat the final boss.   You will be managing your ship in a fight by: placing crew members in rooms that you want to see operated, dividing your energy over multiple spaceship systems and closing and opening doors. The rooms that are present in your spaceship are the: medical bay, shield generator, engine, pilot room, camera’s, co2 room, weapons room and more. Some of these rooms need power where others do not. You can increase the effectiveness of these rooms by: upgrading the rooms with scrap metal, allocating more power to them and placing crew members in them.   Your crew members will gain experience by working in these rooms which will make the rooms more effective. For example an upgraded crew member in the weapons room will give you an increased reload speed. Each crew member has its own merits, for example humans will learn faster but some alien races will give power to a room or fight more effectively. You can find these alien crew members in shops or in encounters( some spaceships start with different alien races). Besides the rooms that you can upgrade, you can also invest in weapons, drones, systems and augmentations which will help you.  Weapons have different kinds of effects ranging from setting rooms on fire to temporarily disabling systems and of course dealing damage to the hull of the ship. Most weapons will only deplete energy but some use missiles which you only have a limited amount of. Drones will also deplete a resource named, drone parts. Drones come in different versions such as defensive drones, attacking drones and hacking drones. In order to destroy your opponent you must deplete your enemies hull integrity to zero. In some instances you can avoid an fight by giving up resources or making a deal with the enemy. If you feel outgunned you can always warp away with your spaceship after a set time has expired. Enemies can also try to warp away if they are damaged enough. you can however stop the warp by damaging systems that are needed for the warp.  Combat in this game is further enhanced by the addition of environmental effects. Some places you visit have ION fields or sun flares which will disable space ship systems or effect the maximum amount of energy you can use.   Systems grant additional options such as hacking, mind control and cloaking which makes this game more interesting and engaging. Passive boosts can also be found and are called augmentations.   The visuals suite the atmosphere of the game quite well. What really stands out though is the amazing soundtrack! The music is extremely mesmerizing and really fits the theme of the game. I cannot stress you enough to at least check it out on Youtube or even better, buy it from steam.  If I have to discuss a negative aspect of the game, it would be the RNG. The game randomly generates enemies and random encounters which can screw you over big time. Some of these situations can be avoided however by knowing what the possible negative effects are of the responses you give. This does not change the fact however, that there are situations where it’s better to start over instead of trying to reach the end.  In conclusion I really recommend checking this game out.    + a lot of content/options and strategies + graphics fit the theme well  + beautiful soundtrack!  + game play does not get stale( for me at least) + system requirements are low  - RNG can be aggravating at times.

I've only played for a little bit but I can already tell this is a fun game. There are three multiplayer modes and I've only played Team Deathmatch so far, but it's very addictive. The pace is very fast, like Quake, Unreal Tournament, etc. One nice thing I like about it is that you get your 'upgrades' from drops from killed enemies. One of the frustrating things of Quake, UT and similar games is that there are power ups at specific places, and it's possible to steam roll once your team sets up in certain places. Since killed enemies(and allies) drop goodies in Minimum, that's not a problem. You keep fighting, you keep moving, and you keep upgrading. Armor upgrades are persistent for the round, and weapons upgrade automatically when picking up power ups(one drops per dead body).   Titan looks like a fun mode. Haven't played it, but the concept is straight forward. The titan map I did see is really small(smaller than the TDM maps I saw!) so I imagine it's a blood bath.  There's a crafting system, which, like everything else, is straightforward and minimalistic. There's a variety of options for weapons, armors, and weapon skins. You slowly but surely accumulate materials as you play rounds and the cost to craft new things isn't absurd(as is true with games like Warframe, where you spend *tons* of time getting the crap needed to craft even one thing of value)  Small learning curve, but tons of fun. The 'Minimum' theme is all throughout the game, from menus to the gameplay, and I definitely think it's done right. It makes everything feel sleek and responsive. I much rather prefer games like this than made-for-console games that are sluggish in almost every way imaginable.   I bought this on sale, but I would recommend it even at full price. This is a great throwback to old FPS games with fast-paced arena-style action and a novel aesthetic.

A tremendously well done art style coupled with excellent gameplay make this one of the better games I have played in recent memory. A $10 price of admission is good considering it does not have multiplayer.

Tried this on the free-bee weekend and best I can say is okish.   A direct L4D2 port (literally), so you'd think that if you just copy the best MP game out there, you wouldn't be able to go wrong. Well, I think this is a lazy attempt, atleast in its current state.  L4D2 excels at MP, even though the solo wasn't terrible. Vermintide's solo is really poor, whereas the MP is ok. It looks to me like FS just gave it the bare bones and then rolled out dlc. The class balancing is abysmal, the spawn areas can be really dodgy and the action in general is just sloppy.  Probably the worst aspect of this, after dragging L4D2 brilliance through the mud, is that there's no versus mode and anyone who's played L4D2 knows that versus is its best aspect. Who wouldn't want to take control of the Rat Ogre right?  Not a fan and I may be harsh in saying this is a lazy rip-off but when you make a direct copy of another game, don't balance the characters (which is a basic fundamental) and neglect to include the ripped off IP's best feature before churning out dlc it then it's just lazy and smells of a smash and grab.  If the above were addressed then this might be good but as they weren't even addressed from the start then I'm not sure this is worth it even in a sale. Doom is only a few £ more in the current sale so...........

This game was clearly designed with the intent of creating a single-player analog of the insanely popular (and complicated) board game 'Arkham Horror' -- and it does so very well. Those familiar with the game will recognize the old ones and investigators re-envisioned here, and it really does capture much of the fun of playing Arkham Horror without the crippling setup time, all the fiddly bits, or the need to find 3+ other people to play with you. Game is relatively easy to learn (even though the tutorial is substantial I was through it and playing quickly) but as the saying goes, difficult to master. 'Adventures' are resolved by drawing a certain number of 'glyphs' which can be manipulated by items, abilities, and other things obtained in game. If you are looking for twitch action or a complex RPG, look elsewhere -- if you are looking for an occult horror boardgame-esque puzzle and stategic challenge, this may scratch that itch for you. I am thoroughly enjoying it. May not be the best thing for folks who aren't already fans of Arkham Horror though.

More clearly, half of guns are underpowered, hatchet is underpowered, while that one ♥♥♥♥ing gun which everyone use is terribly overpowered.  So the only way to play this game is with two revolvers which make up for a fast 2 hit kill, while for example the hatchet (axe) takes 3 hits to kill and does that slow since the time between swings is like 3 seconds. This is a HEAVILY chopped down review, if you want my full thoughts, click here:  https://www.reddit.com/r/GrandTheftAutoV/comments/3cqffc/an_in_depth_grand_theft_auto_v_reviewrant/   After over 300 hours of gameplay. I think I can give this beast a proper review.   Since IV is my beloved in the franchise and the previous major installment, a lot of my comparisons will be between the gameplay of GTA IV and GTA V. Is that biased? Very. So take this with a grain of salt from a IV fanboy.  GTA V is a great sandbox game, but when you compare it to the previous iterations of the GTA franchise, it feels as if something was left behind. There’s so much to cover in this game, I think I’ll go bullet point by bullet point in order to better organise my ramblings. I will be covering the three core gameplay mechanics of every GTA game. Driving, combat, and story. I will also be talking about the multiplayer, since that was where a majority of my time was spent.  (If you want a quick summery, skip to the bottom of this review)  -DRIVING- At the core of every GTA game is, surprise, driving. And man does GTA V really improve on driving. Cars have much better handling compared to IV, Vice City and SA. GTA IV always had slippery cars that took quite a while to learn. GTA V improves upon this with better breaking, tighter controls, and more realistic handling. There’s even some nice attention to detail when it comes to driving. Car statistics, double clutch acceleration, working internal gauges, fuel that drains if the gas can is breached, and more. The driving has improved so much, there are racing crews and communities within the game. So hats off to you Rockstar.  However, a lot of their statistics are hidden in the game’s code. The game records four vague stats about every vehicle in the game. Top speed, Acceleration, Braking, and Handling. These stats are completely not indicative of the real performance of cars, since there’s so much more going on under the hood, and some stats flat out lie. For instance, the Bravado Gauntlet muscle car completely beats the Vapid Dominator in terms of stats, having significantly better Speed and Acceleration. Yet the Vapid Dominator is one of the best muscle cars in the game. Why? Because the stats don’t take into consideration torque, wheel traction, weight, durability, oversteer and understeer, horsepower and so much more.   -COMBAT- Combat in GTA V sucks, plain and simple. So if you don’t want to hear me whine about it, feel free to skip ahead to the story and mark that as my opinion.   GTA has always had clumsy melee systems, but at least IV’s system was functional, albeit awkward. This, however, is a joke. If you go bare knuckle against someone, you’re in for one hell of a time. When you fight, you spastically flail your fists and legs at the other person like you’ve just taken muscle relaxers or suffer from spinal paralysis. You will brawl like none of the characters has ever clenched a fist before until someone falls down dead. For the controls, there’s a button specifically for your kicks, however, pressing the attack button (which there are two of that do the same attacks) will randomly intermix some of these weak shoves into your “combo”. Blocking is only really effective for using counters, which is the only consistent way to look like you know what you’re doing while knocking someone down.   Guns, are disappointing to say the least. Rockstar games, aside from Max Payne 3, have always had good, but not fantastic gunplay. The guns sound awful, one even sounds less threatening than my airsoft gun. Fire rates on pistols and automatic weapons are also terrible. Pistols fire once every half second, and the AK fires about as fast as a Civil War gatling gun. I must give props to the detailed animation work that went into reloading and firing, but that cannot forgive the horrible feel of the weapons. Not only the the guns limp to fire, but shooting someone is equally disappointing. In GTA V, when you shoot almost anyone, they immediately flop to the ground like a dead fish, sporting almost no animation. In actuality, they have a lot more health left, so half of the gunplay is you shooting at bodies already on the ground trying to finish them off.  Health is another major issue that GTA V has. GTA V switches all of this up for the worst. Your health regenerates slowly if you are standing still, in cover, or driving up to half of your health bar. Let’s just take a moment to absorb how annoying that is. I can understand regenerating fully, or regenerating a little so you don’t die an embarrassing death to stubbing your toe, but half? On top of this, you die. Fast. And to make matters worse, enemies are deadly accurate. All enemies with all weapons in all situation. I cannot tell you how many times I have been hit from an untrained, two bit thug, blind firing a pistol with one hand from several meters away. If you thought random shotgun cops in GTA IV was bad, imagine that as the entire combat experience.   -STORY- GTA V takes a bold step in a new direction by introducing three unique characters at the same time that you can freely switch between. This is quite a cool idea, but unfortunately, none of them are quite as memorable because of it.   Michael is bitter, Franklin is fed up, and Trevor is crazy. I want to love these characters so much more than I do, since the actors give such great performances and the writing is spot on. But splitting the story among three characters while keeping the game length the same means they’re much more shallow. The connections between the main and side characters are less significant, and as a whole our protagonists feel like the one dimensional pieces of a better character. Michael is the most relatable character among the three, having a family you interact with frequently. His backstory is fleshed out the most, having history and revealing that he did things to get out of his life of crime for his family. Franklin might as well not exist in the story. He’s a pawn throughout the entire thing, following the orders of someone else and never taking things into his own hands. He really could have been replaced by anyone. Hell, Lamar is a significantly more interesting character because he did dumb things on his own. Not to mention having hilarious writing. I guess Franklin is supposed to be the player, more of a blank slate that you can identify with. Trevor, good god Trevor. Trevor got the best writing, the best performance, the zaniest missions, the most unique characteristics and does everything for himself. But he’s a bit too much, he doesn’t really have a side you can identify with as human. In fact, the game goes out of the way by making him feel entirely inhuman. When we’re introduced to him, he confronts and kills a character that you played as in a previous GTA game. I was this character, I learned about him, fought through his struggles, and came to love him. Seeing Trevor kill him without any sort of ceremony sucked. It hampered my ability to love Trevor.   -CONCLUSION- I’ve enjoyed my time in GTA V. Don’t get me wrong, despite my griping, it’s a fun, open world sandbox, and it’s done well. But after dealing with it’s major to minor issues for 300+ hours, I’ve had enough. This is where GTA V and I depart. Overall, I cannot recommend this game. I felt like I’ve had more of a negative experience coming out of this game than coming in. There’s so much more I could talk about, but I’ve already outstayed my welcome, and probably ♥♥♥♥♥♥ off a few people along the way. This rambling wasn’t to complain about a product or ♥♥♥♥ on people who enjoy it, but rather to show people that it isn’t a groundbreaking, flawless game like the Metacritic score may suggest. It has its faults, some rather large ones, and GTA V doesn’t do anything better than previous installments can do. Which is why this gets my thumbs down.

So I bought no man's sky 2 days after its release, and I can honestly say I do not regret getting this game. I do regret paying 60 dollars for this game it is simply not worth it due to the limited capabilities of the game itself. That being said having played this game for a few weeks and experience tbhe gist of what this game has to offer so far I see a lot of potential in what could develop with this game. The open world nature of this game offers a lot for those whom enjoy a slower pace of game, I've noticed a lot of negative reviews and I really believe that this was due to alot of expectations that were created by the players whom were unfortunate enough to have bought the game for 60 dollars under misconception that this game would be a large open world MMO with the aspect of space exploration but in defense of the developers of No Man's sky watching the pre trailers for the game itself, in no way do they mention direct interaction between players, there were no indications that this game was anything more then a exploration space sandbox game at least to my knowledge. In regards to many peoples complaints about the pricing and the developers going back on their word about paid DLC, if the developers actually read their reviews which is simply a hope of mine in all honesty I know the developers are egotists and will not read most reviews due to the simple hubris of their nature, but i digress my originally point is that those whom have purchased this game at 60 dollars should recieve something like a legacy status where in which they would recieve the first maybe 5 or even 3 dlcs at no cost for having over paid for a game that had Top notch marketing with a niche game that would really only appeal to a select market, doing something like this i believe would help salvage what little repulation the developers have left. It saddens me that this game's repuation is a result of good marketing, unclear concepts, and overly imaginated expectations from consumers whom wish to interject their own expectations as promised made by the developers. At the end of the day this a good game, great for those whom enjoy the smaller aspects, but the developers should seriously consider lowering the price to 30 dollars and giving those whom purchased the game first boons for having helped fund the developers and whatever lavish tastes they squandered their profits on. lastly What should be done for this game is over time more diversity should be added in model design, aliens, so on and so forth, physics mechanics could use an overhaul as well as navigation controls for taking off and leaving planet could use some tweaks as well.

A simple idea made great with awesome hand-rendered charcoal/paint/ink art. Looks easy at first but very quickly becomes a highly precise battle against the wind and gravity. Despite only running in a 4:3 black bordered box or window, The Howlers representation of a steam-punk Lithuanian capital Vilnius remains hugely atmospheric. Excellent.

What primarily motivated me to get The Howler was the really unique art style; what can I say, my love for indie games is strong. And even though it looks easy enough, the gameplay can be a (frustrating) challenge. I never thought that wind currents could ever possibly be such a nuisance to me.  With exception of one or two rage-quits the game is otherwise chill and calming, the fine soundtrack definitely contributes greatly to that.   All in all The Howler is an original and relaxing game, make sure to pick it up!

A lot of unpolished elements. As is would not recommend at all. Some examples:  UI: -- UI is laggy. When leaving a menu you have to wait several seconds before you can actually click anything. -- When popups with choices come up, like those for Desecration Demon, the selection screen blocks the play area, and there is no way to move it aside or peek at your board. Once you make your choice there's no going back. -- No voice acting or sound in the cutscenes. Very awkward. -- Clicking the text for an option does not work. It has been standard in games for years now, that clicking the text also clicks the box. You don't realize how annoying not having this is, until you play a game like this one where it's gone. -- You can't separate the opponent's land to see which ones they have.  Gameplay: -- The AI is bad. It will do stuff like Languish it's own board when you have no creatures. -- They want to charge for cards, but the deck restrictions aren't the same as magic. -- There's no way to see what's in your deck when you start a story mission. Very annoying. -- The reaction interface is very bad. Most games let you skip to next turn, skip to next main phase, etc. This one has a 5 second timer and then it plows ahead. It's not clear whether or not you are reacting or if you're in one of the main phases. The order battles are resolved is arbitrary instead of standard magic rules. -- Large AI slowdowns for seemingly simple play states. Not sure why sometimes it takes the computer 30s to decide what to play on turn 2.  Graphics: -- Very ugly presentation. Small cards on a large bland playmat. No colors or customization options. -- Minimal interface, but no unifying theme or artistic direction.  Overall a very poor implementaiton of Magic. I'd recommend the official MTG client over this. Or, better yet, any of the alternate clients like XMAGE.  Compuer specs: Intel i5 4690k @ 4.2 GHz 16GB DDR3 RAM 240GB SSD NVIDIA GTX 680

app_name: STAR WARS™: Knights of the Old Republic™
review_text: SAVE OFTEN. Not like before major events often. I mean save every 30 seconds. Save before every room. Save before every converstation. The problem with this game is eveything can and will kill you. Without warning even. Its got a great story but the battle system is wonky at best and boss fights are incredibly broken. You will die to every boss at least once. Most will one hit you. Sometimes there is a trick to beating them. Usually its just spam medpacks and hope they dont change targets. Oh and use lots of grenades. Sadly you'll kill your own people in the blast and the controls are broken such that theres no helping that. It was fun on Xbox years ago when I didn't know better. Now I realize its kind of garbage. Pass this up, go play Mass Effect. This game will take years off your life in anger alone, not to mention the time wasted playing it.

It is one of the most immersive games that I have played, and that is exactly what it is trying to be - an immersive hacking puzzle experience. When I picked this game up on sale, I was instantly hooked and I spent the rest of my night playing this game and it was totally worth it. I felt like I was actually accomplishing something, and learning something when I had to find out how to play the game. I felt the stress and intensity of running out of time to get acess to someones IP and I feel like I have learned a lot about game design and hacking in real life. The most immersive part of the game is when you have to use your computer in terminal mode, with only commands and no external help to fix your computer. Overall, I give this game a 8.5/10 for a great immersive learning experience that offers fantastic puzzles.

Fantastic Game. A very tricky blend of RTS/RPG/FPS. If you've played the first two, then you'll most likely have some high expectations.  I find that the game returns to the origins of the first game without a lot of the additional features of the second (Demons). They basically took the first game and analyzed how it could be better. They've added BOT support so that your team is never completely outnumbered or ridiculously stacked against.  The Human Commander was really the make/break point for a match in the original. They took this concept and added an AI commander that is reasonably balanced and performs fairly well and at an even pace. This allows for much better matches that aren't won/loss within the starting minutes of the game. They still have Human Commanders, but this is in the 'Advanced Mode.' The AI Commander can be found in the 'Standard Mode.'  They also updated the theme from the original as it was somewhat silly to have a Nomad with a machine gun (repeater). The humans now have more of a Sci-Fi feel and the beasts are as good as ever. I also feel that there have been some improvements in the control and hit boxes of the characters overall.  Top of all that off with much better graphics, and you have Savage: Resurrection. All in all, a fantastic game that can certainly hold your attention. I highly recommend this game.

How this game isn't more popular is beyond me. No it's not perfect, in fact its cringe worthy story and terrible voice acting is probably why it isn't. But if you can get pass its flaws, Dragon's Dogma is a seriously fun action rpg. Borrowing many elements from other games such as the open world of skyrim, the bosses/monsters of monster hunter and the combat of dark souls/shadow of the collossus, along with its own unique elements such as the pawn system allowing you to create a party with 3 pawns, 2 of which made by other players. This makes fighting an enemy like a cyclops a really unique experience, as mages heal, provide buffs and rain thunder while striders climb its body to target its weak spots to a warrior charging its greatsword for a fatal blow. Yes the game has fatal flaws but that shouldn't stop you from trying one really unique open world rpg, all the more better through this great pc port that runs like a dream with mods and excellent controller support.

What I have started to discover more and more lately, is that I am a sucker for a great trailer; A great trailer and a great thumbnail. As such, I had very high hopes for Firewatch, maybe a little too high? I can't tell just yet. It certainly didn't blow my mind like The Cat Lady, or Sword & Sworcery, but there are definitely aspects of Firewatch that make it stand out from the crowd. The critic in me mourns for opportunities missed, but then the optimist in me is left with a series of images and feelings that have really stuck with me from playing Firewatch.  Cissy Jones' voice acting, to me, is the backbone of this entire game. That may cause cringes for some people, but playing Firewatch reinforced for me that the quality of the voice acting plays a MASSIVE role in how immersed I can get in a game. Throw in a good, original story, as well as plenty of opportunities for screenshots, and you basically have all the reasons I play story-driven games. The quality of the protagonists voice acting doesn't even come close to that of the lead female character unfortunately, which left me feeling like I wasn't the star of the show, rather just some Dev's Brother who does commercials for men's incontinence pads, and would do this job for $37 and a case of beer. But to be fair, there are VERY few male protagonists voices that I haven't found off-putting, so play on I guess.  Firewatch is short. Normally I'm okay with that, but in this case I was left with a little bit of a bitter taste in my mouth, and for once it wasn't because I'm a tight ♥♥♥♥ and expect AAA games for $15. No. In this case it's because the story itself is absolutely full to the brim with potential to flesh out the experience further, and provide a positively exceptional psychological thriller title. All the plot hooks are there, 90% of the items and locations are there, but unfortunately underutilised. Capo Santo have all the bones for such an excellent story in Firewatch, and I was totally sucked in to be sure, but I always had this niggling feeling like more should be happening. I don't know, maybe I'm subliminally comparing it to stabby-stabby-murder games and I've missed the point?  The beginning of the game uses a very interesting 'choose your past' kind of mechanic, and includes some pretty weighty emotional themes that set the tone for the entire game, and immediately had me sitting up to pay attention, and I really would have liked to have seen that mechanic used more often throughout the game. Not only would it have given the game a better sense of coherence (The film style in the 2004 version of Dawn of the Dead comes to mind), but it could have been used to subvert some of the uneventful walking throughout the game.  What Firewatch has managed to do is get me very interested in Campo Santo. It's very obvious from this game that they are extremely capable writers, able to deliver original concepts. The 'Girls at the Lake' scenario absolutely enthralled me, and was beautiful in its simplicity. In the future though, I think I will definitely be expecting more from the gameplay in their games: How they make me go from one excellent plot thread to the next. Simply because with Firewatch they have shown a world of promise for future releases.

Midsummer Night is unlike any game I have played before. The way the story is told and the unique playstyle offers hours of fun. Right off the bat you find yourself thinking through challenging but fun puzzles and figuring out every path you can take. One of the best pixel-art games out there.  Pros: Amazing artwork and gameplay Excellent and fun story Music and sounds are very well done Controls are easy to use Smooth transitions No bugs Great replayability  Challenging puzzles that make you think  Cons: No way to adjust sound, just to turn it all off Some graphical errors  Overall, Midsummer Night is a phenomenal game. The storyline really keeps you interested with the game going on around you. The way the story is told is very interesting and quite a good concept. The sounds and the music really add to the immersion and makes Midsummer Night feel like one of the best, award winning adventure games out there. The puzzles are actually quite challenging and keep you on your feet on what you can do to get to the next path or escape an enemy. Any fans of puzzle, adventure, great looking, pixel-art games should definitely take part in downloading Midsummer Night. I would give this game a 9/10 for its amazing art, story, music, adventure, and challenges. This game is so well done and out together that I could keep playing all day.

Paid price: $15 USD  Mix Saints Row 3 with Prototype (wall running, air dashes, gliding) and numerous cultural references (movies such as Armageddon and Hitch, games Metroid and Metal Gear Solid, too many WWE references to list), and you have Saints Row 4.    Story and length   It started off strong, but be warned,  there is a LOT of filler in this game . The main story line, loyalty missions and DLC will only take you only 6-8 hours. I completed 80% of the game in 21 hours, but as I said earlier, most of that time was spent gathering collectables.  The soundtrack is small, but enjoyable. There is sound levelling issues between voice volume and background music during cutscenes, but it's a small gripe.    Gameplay   It's refreshing to be able to roam through an open world environment at high speeds. I didn't use any vehicles, except when forced. Each side quest is  ONLY  filler missions (hacking, wave defences, car collecting, target hunting) and quickly turns into a grind. Frustratingly, there are also co-op only missions that are locked away from solo players.    Price and value   $40 is far overpriced for what essentially is a 'large' expansion pack of SR3. you can get it on special, it is most definitely worth your time, even if it's only for the refreshing freedom. Just play through SR3 first.  Base Game:   7/10 - if you're a fan of open world games, and a fan of the series.   DLC: Enter the Dominatrix:   2/10 - Extremely weak. Not worth your time.   DLC: How the Saints Save Christmas:   6/10 - Enjoyable. Generic objectives, but interesting dialog.    TL;DR - Buy SR4 if it's below $20, and play only through the main story line. Side quests are only filler missions, there's no content to be found there, so don't bother.

FOR ANYONE WITH PROBLEMS READ THIS!  -I see a lot of bad reviews for this game and the majority is for the sole fact that this game was a console port to pc, which makes no sense to me. I dont get why this generation of gaming is now centered around the ps4 and xbone and major games are being released broken on pc.  -If you have an Nvidia card like me, then this game should run okay for you after some quick fixes.  -To get it to run better: What I did was update to the newest driver(which is specifically for this game), also what I did was enable 60fps in the .ini file and remove the intro movies.(Look it up online, theres a good article already). Im using a ps4 controller and the great runs pretty well. The fps does dip down a bit sometimes, but I never really see it go under 30, but its still noticeable and can annoy you.   -For AMD: I believe they released an update to the drivers and thats why most people were complaining, so try that I guess. Also, enable the 60fps and remove the intro movies for time saving.  -Now for the game itself:  -I have played a little over an hour and I gotta say its pretty enjoyable. The intro scene both looked and felt great and the combat is just like it was before, but feels a bit easier is what I would say. The batmobile...man is that thing fun. I saw a review saying the handling was bad, but im guessing they are using a mouse and keyboard and I dont get who wouldnt use a controller for these games. Its pretty fun just to drive it through the streets and watching it transform into combat mode. The graphics seem pretty good to me, but when you go to graphics setting you can tell this is a console port by how little you can actually do with the graphics.  -Overall, if you liked the previous 2 in the series, then I would definately recommend this one. Ive only played around an hour but can already tell it may be the best in the series.  -PS, buy it on the g_2_a website(only used underscores because you cant type in the website), i only paid $27 for my copy, didnt think that site was actually legit, but use paypal and its actually legit.

Complexity: this game has it in spades. The learning curve is brutal, and the included tutorials are more or less useless. It's Oregon Trail + Final Fantasy Tactics lite. The combat seems pretty simple on the surface, but they way they use Armor and Strength is neat. A unit's Strength is effectively their HP, and the damage a unit can do is Strength - Armor. If a unit's Strength < opponent's Armor, they have a (Armor - Strength)*10% chance to miss completely. If you ignore armor and go for strength attacks, you run the risk of missing with your weaker characters, but if you only focus on armor, they can do more damage to you on their turn.  Between fights, you're on an oregon trail style adventure after the invasion of your village by Dredge, a race of heavily armored people from the north. They're the main antagonists of the game, forcing the main characters to flee from city to city looking for refuge. The number of supplies you have determine how many days you can travel before your clansmen and warriors begin to die. In addition, the longer you travel, the worse your clan's morale gets, meaning you have to balance supplies with camping and resting. Various dialog and story events play a part in the number of clansmen you lose or gain if they decide to join your caravan.  The story mainly revolves around a hunter named Rook, who falls into the leadership role in a caravan of refugees. You and the other characters are attempting to find refuge between two separate apocalyptic events and a horde of invading warriors. Similar to Game of Thrones, the story is barely ever not dark, but it fits the tone of the world. You're not on a quest to save the planet, you're just trying to get your people to safety.  Only downside is that it lacks closure on some of the bigger story elements. Looking forward to the sequel.

As a massive fan of the original Dead Island I was surprised at my lack of hype of Dying Light which may have something to do with me not clicking with Riptide. After spending 8 hours in Dying Light I can say this should have been Dead Island 2. so far I've only completed 15% of the main story because I keep losing myself in the games sidequests which, after the first 3 hours of painstaking tutorial missions, come thick and fast and each has been interesting enough to make me do them first. Mixing the combat of Dead Island with a parkour mechanic for getting to higher ground faster, Dying Light feels more dynamic in every way, especially during the night missions (night isn't scripted but an actual cycle) when the machanics change stealth style as you face the volatiles, Zombies that can run as fast as you and can take you down in seconds. The visuals are gorgeous at max settings although the best settings come at a very high performance cost so your going to need something beefy to run it at those settings. Dying Light jis easily one the best zombie games available and in my opinion beats my previous favourite; Dead Island.  EDIT: 23 hours in and still in the first map. This game has a shedload of interesting content to do away from the main missions.  The ollowing Update: Just a small note on the Paid DLC that was released as part of the Enhanced Edition update. This DLC is massive, adding in new quests over a whole new map that is easily as good as the content in the main game. You are given a buggy ala Half Life 2 to upgrade and repair along with new weapons. If you liked the main game then this is unmissable.

Sprawl Simulator  After several hours and 6 false starts, I think it's fair for me to say: this game is lousy. If you're looking for a deep sandbox city builder, you will be deeply disappointed. If you're looking for a railroaded monument to suburbia, I guess this is your game. If you want to make your own city and experiment and risk disaster, look elsewhere. If you can't bear the thought of failing and need the game to tell you how to make it 'correctly' and compeltely disallow you from doing the 'wrong' design, this is your game. Right from the get-go, this 'game' allows very little wiggle room to make YOUR city. You MUST make cul-de-sacs, you MUST zone in the blocs the game designates AND NEVER SHALL YOU DEVIATE from what the devs see as an ideal city.  I reintall Sim City 4 periodically and try new mods. I expect a much higher degree of freedom than this game allows. It's a railroaded city sim. It's pretty, sure. But good luck trying to make a gridded, or even mildly organized city. Add in the fact that 10 months after its initial release and there's STILL no ctrl-z function, it fails to be little more than a sprawl simulator and seems to revel in the disgusting, wasteful hedonistic design that accompanies that kind of urban design.

Magic Duels is virtual Magic the Gathering on the cheap. If you're hardcore into MtG, you probably already play MTGO, so Duels won't have much to offer you. But if you're a relatively casual MtG player and don't feel like dropping huge amounts of money on virtual cards, Duels is freaking great. It's the only official place where you're going to get to use Jace, Vryn's Prodigy without spending $90+.   My only complaints are that there are some strange rules changes, like how rarity is tied to how many copies of a card are allowed in your deck. So you can only have one of any given mythic, two of any given rare, or three of any given uncommon. You also get one free mulligan, but no mulligan scry if you go down to six or less. And more than that, each set in Duels are missing some pretty big chunks of cards, presumably because they were considered too complicated. That's the biggest bummer to me, because I feel like even casual MtG players appreciate the game for the complexity that it allows, but maybe I'm off base on that.  Either way, it's free, so if you dig MtG, give it a try.

'Deadnaut' refers to the unfortunate astronauts you command in this real-time squad tactics game, so named because your mission is to hunt through derelict spacecraft full of dead things, and also because their odds of survival are about that of a 2016 GOP presidential hopeful. Each randomly-generated campaign is four missions long, each mission being a crawl through a randomized ship with randomized enemies, layouts, and corpses to loot. Most missions either have you nabbing the ship's log from the bridge or clearing out all hostiles, and scanning the bodies you find earns you Knowledge which you can use to buy new gear between missions or clone dead 'nauts. What sets this particular roguelike tactics game apart, however, is the presentation. You command your squad from the safety of your ship through some 1960s Soviet submarine-looking monitors. The actual action is all wireframe and icons on a fuzzy radar screen, with rough readouts on health and sanity and radio chatter marred by glare. The real draw of this game is basically roleplaying Gorman from Aliens, commanding your troops on lovingly-rendered low-fi hardware and despairing helplessly as they get picked off one by one.  And they will get picked off, because there are very few limits on what the RNG will throw at you. For some reason, your Deadnauts and their alien foes have a ridiculous number of stats, including combat skills, sanity modifiers, specific weapon resistances, and more. I say 'for some reason' because despite the enormous depth to the characters, you cannot affect these stats and skills in any way. You have the option of customizing Deadnauts before you start a campaign, but once you begin everything is locked in. There's no training or buffing. Worse than this, however, is the enemy generation. Enemies can be anything from ghosts to mutants to cyborgs (all depicted as plain red dots, mind you) meaning their health, attack patterns, and resistances can be all over the place. But you don't know this going into a mission and you can't re-gear once deployed, so if you picked beam weapons and end up fighting things that resist beams, game over, man, game over. Diversifying your team between different weapon types and shields and such could mitigate this, except that (A) you won't get nearly enough Knowledge to effectively diversify and (B) you won't have the focused firepower to take down super beefy enemies.  The strangest limitation by far, however, is the lack of combat orders you can give your team. You can move your Deadnauts around as you want, order them to investigate points of interest. You can give hacking specialists orders to open doors or commandeer automated security systems, and you can have defensive units shield others or debuff enemies. But when it comes to actual fighting, and it ALWAYS comes to actual fighting, all you can really do is stand and shoot. You can't even effectively order 'nauts to attack a specific enemy because the game seems to factor in their sight lines, attention span, and sanity to decide if they're going to listen. There are no cover mechanics, no flanking, nothing. I wasn't kidding when I called it a Gorman simulator, because your orders will end up being as effective as his were.  It's such a strange and ultimately disappointing game, because the foundation for something amazing is all there. The presentation and atmosphere is spot on, and there's a huge variety of situations and systems to plan strategies around. But the game never actually gives you the tools to realize those strategies. Stats are meaningless if you can't do anything with them, and tons of weapon and damage types are actually a liability if you don't know what you'll be fighting next and can't afford to diversify. Even the systems that are really fleshed out have severe drawbacks. Your connection to your Deadnauts can crap out, forcing you to boost audio or video connections to stay in contact. It's a neat touch that does a lot for immersion, but it can also send your Deadnauts into a depressurized room to die en masse because the bad audio connection makes them mishear the order (yes, this happened). I've beaten the campaign once, and I can honestly say it was because I got a lucky combination of gear and easy enemies to use it on. In the final analysis, it's a strategy game where your strategy isn't going to determine whether you win or lose. With so many other great offerings like Invisible, Inc. and Darkest Dungeon, it's impossible to recommend.

I really enjoyed the time I've spent playing this game so far. The characters feel like the embodiment of the classic tower defense tower archetypes, but the mobility of the characters and the special power up ground runes adds a strategy to the game that makes it more interesting than static towers placed along a path.   Characters both have different ways of dealing damage and also different strengths. For example, the vampire hunter guy with his crossbow is strong against vampires but weak against most other types of monsters. The priest guy is good against undead with his holy/frozen water damage-wise but can do little more than slow down other monster types. The third character you get is the typical aoe character to stop large, fast streams of week ghoul-like creatures in their tracks. The fourth character seems to me like a heavy-hitting melee type character who fights the larger ghouls and I haven't unlocked the fifth character yet. Each character feels fleshed out and unique.  There are ways to upgrade the weapons of the characters with runes that you can either buy with in-game currency or find for completing missions. Then, as the characters kill waves of monsters, they collect points which allow them to use their weapon powerups in times of need. Each character also has a pretty robust and interesting skill tree that keeps your characters getting progressively stronger as the game progresses.  Villagers play an important role as an 'oh crap' button. A wave of villagers can come streaming out of the town hall every so often to crash into a wave of particularly troublesome enemies. They're best used as a last ditch effort to protect your 'base.'  Overall, having played around 1.4 hours so far at the time of this review, I really enjoy this game so far and look forward to completing it and then going back to complete the levels on harder difficulties.

I would suggest either major renovations to the gameplay and technical issues or reframe this as a more passive electronic entertainment with, possibly, some branching dialogue choices instead. With these aesthetics and the atmospheric sounds, making it into more of an interactive movie would increase the use of its strengths and minimize its weaknesses. In its current state, it barely constitutes a game.

app_name: Cats are Liquid - A Light in the Shadows
review_text: Cats are Liquid is a minimalist platformer. You play as a cat (who, as the title suggests, can turn into liquid), attempting to escape from a maze of rooms. The cat gains multiple abilities as you go on and a 'friend', in the form of a small gray cube.   It took me around two and a half hours to complete the main game and under one hour to complete the companion levels.   A semi-story-oriented game, the plot revolves around a cat who has been locked in a set of rooms. Starting out oddly charming and slightly whimsical, it does end up getting (quite a bit) darker as you progress. I'm assuming the ending I got is the only ending, though it was abrupt and wasn't really what I had hoped it would be. Though it was interesting, the ending and the entirety of the last few worlds was a sudden change from the rest of the game.  Be warned -- the last world also has a sudden and often frustrating difficulty spike, out of nowhere.  The abilities you receive are, as follows, the ability to turn into a gaseous form, the ability to put out a small gray cube to press switches, and bombs. My only gripe with these abilities is that having the gaseous form so early on cheapens a majority of the levels that want you to actually platform -- it simply allows you to fly over dangerous obstacles like fires.  If you're looking for a fun little game/time-killer, Cats are Liquid is just that.
Despite there only being 5 campaigns (plus The Sacrifice), the game is certainly replayable. It's not too difficult to play either, and very well balanced.

Don't let Amnesia's reputation fool you, this isn't just another jump-scary survival horror game! It has some of that, but the real scares are pretty varied and unpredictable, and the bulk of the game is actually progressed with puzzles. Overall I'd rate it a solid 4.5/5. There are a couple of puzzles that are pretty unintuitive, while others can feel a bit *too* easy, and the occasional encounters you're supposed to avoid being noticed by are more irritating than scary, but there's still a *lot* to recommend this game over others. The graphics are lovely, the audio and controls are immersive, a lot of the concepts and monsters are honestly unsettling, and the story is well-presented and enjoyable. Plus, even though there's basically no inherent replayability, the game is longer than you'd expect!  Quick Pro: Dark, immersive atmosphere. Quick Con: Stealth gameplay segments are unsatisfying.
Short version: 87%

It is a very interesting game to say the least. Many games have tried to create an environment where the world reacts to the music playing, and Symphony kinda does that. It analyzes your music saved to your machine (For which I give them props. I like how we're not limited to the music already within the game) and supposedly coordinates enemy amount, aggression, and type to the music. From what I can tell, it only analyzes volume changes which seems a bit lacking because bass-heavy songs will yield easier enemies than the song's beat will imply.  I wish it had more variety to the difficulty modes whilst playing (The color changes indicating enemy's aggresion). Three difficulties just doesn't seem like enough. I don't know about later stages in the game, because I've only gotten to the Mezzo-Piano difficulty, which leads me into my next point.  How do you save this game? There's no save button in the menus, and there's no autosave function after every completed song, so how am I supposed to save progress? I signed up for that ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥t service they have in-game which I thought would allow you to save your game. but so far I can't tell if it worked. 'Continue' on the main screen is still grayed out so I'm convinced there just isn't a save funciton in this game which is really, really bad. Playing for an hour only to accidentally go back to the home menu and losing all your progress because you clicked RMB makes this game feel like a waste of time, even though the actual game part of it is awesome. And yes, who in their right mind makes RMB a previous menu key? At least it's not active during gameplay, just in the menus.  But as for the actual game, Symphony is pretty cool. The colors are bright and vivid, and the explosions are just the right size. The glow effect from some weapons can prove to be distracting (Level 3 Scatter Cannon cough cough) but it is manageable after you get to recognize enemy ships. I experienced my mouse sensitivity suddenly skyrocketing a couple times but I believe that is a problem on my end as I've had it happen to me in other games as well.  Customisability is lacking in the aesthetics department, but all weapons look and feel diverse excluding the rare versions of the same weapon. I wish we could customize the look of our actual ship, instead of having the same falcon-like body throughout the game.  Final Verdict: 7/10 - While I thoroughly enjoy this game, the lack of an obvious save feature really breaks the balance between having an awesome game and a pile of crap. The game part of Symphony is very good and fun to play, and the diversity of weapons is great, and the art style is just hands-down beautiful, but god damn, they just couldn't get the menu system right! No obvious save button turned this gem of an Indie game into a pile of crap. HOWEVER, it is a very pretty and well polished piece of crap. If this game goes on sale, get it. You'll have fun for a couple minutes or hours before you lose your save game.  Afterthought: Locating your music files is weird. You have to use an in-game explorer to find your music folder which is weird. I think they should've just let it open Windows Explorer or whatnot instead.

Pay 2 Winday's basically just crossed a line.  I picked this up months back, sunk about 100 hours into it so far, loved it all. Bought all the little DLC packs, even stuff like Western Weapons and the Overkill pack which have no PRACTICAL use in game except for looks. I've been having a ton of fun.  Unfortunately, the mediocre recent DLC packs, the innumerable cosmetic character changes, and the ton of power creep just aren't keeping me confident that this game's worth buying. OVERKILL seem to be going for an MMO style model with this title. Buy expansions for more gear, use gear on the higher level heists that you purchase. The $20 price tag is an ENTRY FEE, not a complete game in itself anymore.   Don't get me wrong, I love the core gameplay. However, the company's business practices are seriously putting me off, and I doubt I'd be in a hurry to give them my money any time soon. Don't buy this game.   EDIT: The new microtransactions system actually REPLACES part of the old gear unlock system, and gives you no control over what gear you get. Combine this with the power creep and weird balancing, and it's possible to drop real money on a weapon skin, have to buy DLC to use said skin, and then have the weapon be strictly and numerically inferior to another DLC weapon even with the microtransaction buff.  EDIT: As of 31/12/15 I haven't played this in over a month, despite having played it practically everyday before then since I bought it. I'd get a refund if I could.

Eugen Systems has found a winning formula with Wargame. Airland Battle adds to European Escolation, adding fixed wing aircraft to the player's arsenal and even more ground units. Visually appealing (albeit not spectacular) with solid gameplay, Airland Battle is a strong addition to a declining genre.  What is most distinct about Airland Battle is the scale of the maps. These are full fledged warzones. Weapon ranges aren't compressed for the most part. Tanks can and will engage targets at 2000 meters. You will find situations where a 15000m range artillery piece won't have full map coverage. It allows players to be unpredictable and creative with their unit maneuvers and increases the importance of logistics and recon.  Some won't like the scale- players will spend a lot of time watching their vehicles roll toward the enemy and the battles can end very quickly and decisively when they occur- but for me, the only glaring flaw in this otherwise good game is the audio- there is not much variety in sound effects.

app_name: Brothers - A Tale of Two Sons
review_text: If the characters can be made loveable, rememberable and relateable without any words, something has been definately done right.  Brothers - A Tale of Two Sons is an adventure with great visuals and beautiful sceneries. The benches that have been placed around the routes you travel give you a nice little pause and another angle to look at the world around you. Technically the game does not amaze you, but how the gameplay immerses you into the story is quite an achievement. Play with a controller. Once in the first chapter I ran into a glitch where the younger brother fell through the sand on the river shore. Nothing game breaking, but little funny atleast.  I played the game through in a little less than 3 hours. But those were really, really good 3 hours. Video games are an art. And this is a fine example what it can be at it's best.

Ground combat is no animation even, just a textpopup telling you whether you have won. No espionage or blackops in the game. Diplomacy is sort of alright and so is the galaxy overview. The research tree is needlessly overblown, most likely to hide the fact there is no real content in the game.  Everyone who loved MoO2 will hate this. It is much worse in every aspect, especially combat. The dominating problem is that you don't feel like you are in any sort of direct control. Second worst problem is the digging through redundant stuff, such as 10 times almost identical research or 100 ship designs where 10 would suffice.

This game is awesome. Not in that 'so-bad-it's-good' way or even 'I'm a fan of the franchise so I'll say I like it's good even though it's eats schlanger' way. No. It is actually awesome.  It's set in the rebooted Mad Max: Fury Road universe but is far from a licensed movie title. No bad thing. The plot it is tight and compelling and the presentation is beautiful at best and brutal at worst. The titular character gives one of old Immortan Joe's sons a chansaw lobotomy within minutes of hitting the start button. Beyond that you are free to roam and cause mayhem with story missions rewarding completion with juicy car upgrades and new environments to explore.  Visually it's very impressive. Doing away with the muted colours of other post armageddon titles (I'm looking at you here Fallout 4) and instead mimicking the saturated pallet used in Fury Road. Again, no bad thing. While set in a desert wasteland you will find yourself pausing (or taking advantage of the smoothly implemented capture mode) to marvel at masterfully crafted settings at sunset and sunrise, the way shadows are cast at high noon or just how terrifying the storms can be. On my 8350 / 290X setup it has never once dipped below 60fps either.  Gamplay is Dinki Di as well. Developed by Avalanche Studios of Just Cause fame, the driving may not be 100% realistic, but it's fun and the game gives you a load of cool weapons to play with while charging around in your V8 deathmobile.  Out of the car, combat is very similar to Arkham or Sleeping Dogs. Smash the punch button and ocassionally tap the counter button to keep the increasingly bloody violence flowing. Gun play takes a little getting used to but works perfectly once you do.  When you're not smashing faces or blowing the balls of War Boys with your trusty shotgun, there are plenty of rock-scrambling exploration sections which channel Uncharted or recent Tomb Raider games which reaveal the true brutality of life in the wasteland, all aptly punctuated with Max tossing in his 2 cents about how humanity lost everything and it all went to hell in a hand basket.  If you are a fan of the franchise or feel let down by recent open wolrd titles then this is a no-brainer. Just buy it.

Banished, a colonial-era city building game with a survival twist, should be in the library of every self-respecting city-builder addict ;)  City-builder? More like village-to-town builder, set in the days before the industrial revolution, when european-derived economies were still craft-and-trade oriented, and had a strong focus on local agricultural production and primitive resource extraction.  What do you do in Banished? Simply, you build a village, and ensure your villagers survive and thrive.   The city-builder part of the game is a classic, top down build-your-own economy sim, in the style of Sim City. Of course, as you're not building a city per se, you don't have to worry about zoning, or budgets, or urban problems. Instead, you get your town started on effective resource extraction, adjusted to your population size. Build homes for your villagers, and basic town services that allow you to make tools, extract fuel, make clothes, and distribute goods in relation to the scale of your village. You can also build a trading post, where you can trade goods produced in your village at nominal value against goods produced 'elsewhere' (i.e. outside the map boundaries).  As you try out different terrain and village formats, you can experiment with optimal resource extraction, work distribution and town production - which generally happens in a fairly organic way, even if you try to plan things out in advance. Banished thus has a seamless feel to its building and allocation functions - which is part of the beauty of this game, alongside the pretty graphics and relaxing music.  The survival part of the game is tied to how well you attend to all your villager's basic needs such as food, housing, and energy requirements. In my view, this is what makes Banished unique, because the simulation is focused on a basic truism of all settled human societies : the critical importance of surplus production. Basically, you need to make sure there is always a surplus of food, fuel, and tools, otherwise the game will punish you with a downward spiral of negative effects. If you're low on tools, for example, soon your labourers won't be producing food, or cutting logs, or whatnot, as per your village requirements. That means no new fuel to warm homes, or new food in storage. As cold or hunger start to have their toll, your villagers will begin to die off. Too many deaths in a row, either of natural old age or because of negative resource spirals, will result in a moribund village.  It can be difficult to recover from a spike in deaths, so be careful ! Always tend to your surplus...   Another remarkable thing about Banished is that it is a one-man project, a true labor of love. Released in February 2014, Banished was designed and programmed by Luke Hodorowicz of Shining Rock Software. Banished has since release attracted a lively community of modders, who have extended the basic game with great mods. I recommend you try the mod packages, such as the Colonial Charter mod. Adds many additional hours of village experimenting. And kudos to Luke for making his game moddable !   Anyhow, that's the gist of my review. If you've enjoyed reading this, and would like a more in-depth analysis of how Banished simulates an early-modern town economy, I'd like to toot my own horn here and recommend an article I wrote about Banished for Play the Past in the fall of 2016. The article goes a little bit more in depth into the mechanics of surplus production in the game, as well as issues surrounding the design of historically-themed economic sims.  The article is called Banished : it Takes a Village to Raise a Surplus, and you can read it here:  http://www.playthepast.org/?p=5695   In the final analysis, I recommend Banished without hesitation. If you're a seasoned city-builder player, Banished will satisfy your micro-management itch with its tightly-bound economic engine, and plenty of customizable scaling challenges with the Colonial Charter mod.  If you're new to city-builders as a genre, and unsure as to which title to start with, Banished is also a good place to dive in, because of how it allows you to fully control the scale and complexity of your town - whether with the basic game or with additional mods, to boot.  Can't go wrong with Banished !

The sequel to the semi-popular flash game Soundodger on Adult Swim Games, Soundodger+ offers much more in terms of difficulty and replayability.  If you haven't already, check out the original on Adult Swim, here (it has half of the levels, and the same gameplay):  http://games.adultswim.com/soundodger-puzzle-online-game.html   Though it may be easy to jump right to the level editor or auto-gen mode, Soundodger+ is mostly about its single-player experience. There are many edge-of-your-seat moments where focus and concentration are key. This may not be apparent from the first few levels or trailer; the game starts off slow, easing you into the later levels with simplified versions of the patterns; it feels a little too slow moving in, and you need to beat ~10 of these levels before the advanced levels can be played. This is my only real gripe though; the level of polish and love put into the later levels is evident in spades. (Look for advanced levels indicated by a black background.)  The quality of music is also exceptional. If you peruse the Indie section of Steam often, there's a good chance that you'll recognize some of the artists; Disasterpeace, Sonic (also known as Jasper Byrne, who did all of Lone Survivor [music too]), Austin Wintory, Danny Baranowsky, Lifeformed, and Bill Kiley. That's not to say that the others not mentioned are bad; far from it. You may recognize Ghost Kollective, but other names such as Mike Forst, D. WIGHT, Anoctave and Bean proved to be exceptional with the tracks they provided.  Next, the editor. Soundodger+ has plenty of very interesting mechanics; you can slow down and invert time, fill the screen with bullets, and control the movement of the outside 'enemies.' All of this can be accessed and used in the editor. Though initially appearing dense and convoluted, the editor makes a ton more sense after viewing the available online tutorials, and it can be a blast not only to play finished levels, but also to watch said levels as well. Be warned, Soundodger+ only accepts tracks encoded at 44.1 kHz; I ran into the problem of not being able to use some tracks I wanted to because of this.  Finally, the Auto-Gen mode. I put this at the bottom because it's easily the weakest part of Soundodger+. Levels are typically created on a scale of how loud the music is, similar to Audiosurf. If you don't have loud tracks, you'll end up with a level that's too easy, on the scale of the early campaign levels. The levels generated often don't make sense either; the game creates a pattern only when there's a spike in the noise, causing bullets to fire off at seemingly random times. And again, the Auto-Gen mode only works for songs encoded at 44.1 kHz, same as the editor.  TL;DR: Soundodger+ is highly recommended, especially if you're looking for something in the vein of Bit.Trip, Audiosurf, Sound Shapes or 140. Do not buy for the auto-gen; rather, buy for the single player experience.

In a time of so many dissapointing game launches, hyped-to-fail pre-orders and unrealized expectations, Obduction is a breath of fresh air. It's exactly what was promised and what we've come to expect from Cyan, the makers of Myst. It's also so much more.   With every step, I'm reliving fond memories of playing the Myst series while building brand new favorite experiences in a completly new story and universe to explore. The same quality is there - from the puzzles to the story to the 'oh crap, I'm stuck' moments that I sleep on, to the 'Ah! So that's how that works!' moments that make you smile and do a little fist pump.   The game invites you to explore, giving you just enough info to start piecing things together while asking you to poke around and think about how things are constructed in order to figure out how it's all connected. As soon as you think you might be nearing the end, you stumble onto another door, open it and stand in awe at what lies beyond. I'm not even close to finishing Obduction, and I'm absolutely in love.   Thank you, Cyan, for your Kickstarter, for your excellent communication, for showing how much you love what you create, for delaying the launch until you were sure the game was ready, and for producing one of the best games I've ever had the pleasure of playing.  10/10 - thank you, thank you, thank you!

I picked this game up onsale really cheap and for the price it was more than worth it. I see a lot of reviews complaining about problems that aren't really in the game. It has about 3 hours of gameplay if you put it on advanced mode from the start probably less if you play it on normal.   Pros: -Very few objects are repeated, Many hidden object games use the same objects over and over, this one didn't suffer from that. - Easy to follow story. - Fast Travel, some complained about spending too much time moving around the map but the game has a fast travel system in the journel that highlights what scenes you can currently do things in and lets you click to go there immediatly. More Hidden Object games could use that. - Even on advanced mode the hint system rechearges at a more than fair rate, and if you can't figure out where to go even with the help of the journel it wont even consume a hint and will keep pointing you in the right direction. I only needed to use it a few times and Didn't need to consult a guide at all. - Decent voice acting - Steam Acheivments and Trading Cards - Price  Cons:  - No replayability, You can get 100% of the acheivments in about 3 hours, I guess that's better than being repetitive though.  - The story while easy to follow is a little lacking and doesn't serve any other purpose than to move you around and give you objectives. But lets face it you aren't playing a game like this for the story.  Overall: 9/10 This score is based on what the game is, comparing it only to other hidden object games I personally have played.

Having completed NEKOPARA Vol. 1, I ask myself three questions: 1. What is the purpose of a visual novel game? 2. Are visual novel games for me? 3. What the flying ♥♥♥♥ am I doing with my life?  Playing a visual novel is essentially reading a book with beautifully drawn and animated pictures but with voices to offer that extra bit of immersion. Nekopara is a story about leaving home, running a patisserie and looking after catgirls (I was especially interested in Nekopara because of my unnatural weakness to animal girls.) What I did find especially interesting is that if you were to collate all the information you find out about catgirls in the world, and try to understand it, it doesn't make too much sense, let alone give a reason for the existence of these scientific 'abominations'.  On the other hand, they're SO CUTE, and I totally want one. Nekopara does a good job of telling a rather sweet story (or erotic story, if you buy the uncensored version) that is in actuality quite mediocre as I can summarise it in about two sentences.  The 'player', if you can call them that, is told the story from their perspective, which is as far as the game goes to involve you. Honestly, I was a bit bored, having just finished 'Hotline Miami' before playing Nekopara I felt a bit psychotic and was surprised to find myself a little disappointed that I didn't get to kill anything in the visual novel (anybody else feel like that after playing video games - perhaps you can recommend a good psychiatrist?) For people who need a dose of 'kawaii', who are too restless to watch anime and too lazy to play a 'proper' game.  Ending on a positive note, you can speed run the game, with only one hand, in about 5 minutes or 20 minutes for the uncensored version ;)

app_name: DARK SOULS™ II: Scholar of the First Sin
review_text: I bought the game during the autumn sale, and I must say, in a way the game is good, in a way it's ♥♥♥♥ing terrible. You get punished for trying to learn a boss, for example, because your maximum health gets reduced by 5% every time you die in hollow form. Why? Because why not, I guess. It's literally just an unnecessary mechanic that punishes you for trying to learn the game.

This is one of those games that makes me wish Steam had a 'Meh' option. Yes, I've only played it for 45 minutes, but that was enough time for me to get the 'regular' ending, along with taking a ten minute bathroom break during play time. Don't read too much into that. It's a (mostly) solid platformer, comparable (sort of) to the first Super Mario Bros., except the jumping gets a bit awkward at times. My level of irritation at some of the level design, though, often ended up outweighing my level of intrigue, which is unfortunate, because it doesn't propel me to find all the collectables. So, in that respect, as a platformer, it's pretty 'meh,' maybe on the 'no' side of the review spectrum. Same with sound. It irritated me. It's the rest of the game, however, that pushes me to suggest this game with a 'yes' review. Every review of this game that you've probably read that says it's scarier than it looks is (mostly) right. It's definitely an offputting, nervous game, and, as the developer states, not for those of 'nervous disposition' or something. And he's right. This game'll make you think twice about playing it at night. But, all told, I didn't find it THAT bad. While it did catch me off guard multiple times, I don't think it's the kind of game that's going to give you nightmares. Unless you're a prepubescent, then it very well might.  So, it's a short, okay platformer with a twist that's worth seeing (though maybe only on sale, or the free version online, of which I hear there is one). Oh, and some final notes: the graphics, while being pixelated, are pretty tight and often unsettling, as the game promises, but the gameplay is standard platformer with interspersed segments of dimension shifting, which may have been original in 2010, but is no longer terribly novel. Let's sum it up: Buy it on sale. It's worth your time, but maybe not worth a whole five bucks.

(Full Release Log) Current Game has reached my books as a 9/10.  Characters: Development has grown to show more of a natural VN setting even with prior knowledge to this game from S:MOA  Specifics:   Sola I love what you did but wished more CG  Chigara: Incredible making me rejudge her character a bit  Asaga: Okay you got me good on this one  Maray: Like the helped wish for later Imouto Route unlocked sometime  Cg: Definitely worth the wait, there is a patch for the Uncensored Version  Gameplay: Typical sense, able to buy items and give them to girls also increase stats. The day system was unique and considerate. Using the Calendar and Stats bring a familiar pace from other games. Kinda reminds me of the unique old dating sims we had in newgrounds. However the game had a nice objective and way to explain how it works. As you help and grow with the team, so do the feelings.   Story: Well seeing from S:MOA I believe that this was a nice change of pace. Wish the other characters got their roles in more like an expansion. The development introducing you in the beginning helped show lines connecting EVA and Maray and what she was kinda like. However there are priceless moments as you go along. I feel that in the game there was a definite amount of good time and revision brought to this.  Personal Thoughts: Well the 9 was due to the other game and hoping for some involvement however as you play through this game you kind of see a nice pull around from them. I truly enjoyed the idea of running as Vice President and Reliving a life style with an end date to score or be a bore. Maray as a BL fan was hilarious in my opinion. Sola's nature was truly nice. Chigara's story line truly made me think about her a bit more and developed a bit more affection. Asage well I'M THOROUGHLY Surprised. More so that I've grown to accept her Wild Side to be more acceptable. However only thing I kind of wished was like it would go past the whole after you get the girl and in the end you get more CGs and by the graduation year, by all you did, it shows a certain extra bonus CG or events. Nonetheless playing the game over during early access and back now has made me appreciate the characters even more. I truly found this game entertaining and would highly recommend it to many other VN and Dating Sim lovers as well as anime fans. You'll find a nice appeal and to be honest, they did a great job.   ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------- Beta Early Access (First Log) Thoughts so far 1/20/15  Sola: I ABSOLUTELY LOVE HER, silent swim club member (Reminds me of Nanasaki Ai and Mana Kawamura). I really do love what you did with her, please continue to do so.  Asaga: I have grown to see a Haruhi appeal but has her finer moments. Needs more moments of fun as she is now and some more plot involvement.  Chigara: Typical but there was some drama I was not expecting on the route. Need more build up loving the story you created.  Maray: PLEASE UNLOCK the Imouto route SOON  Ava: Okay I admit she has her finer moments here, needs more opening though.  Other thoughts: Include the others from Sunrider. I saw a mention of Claude. Please let Kryska, Icari, and the others be there too. Maybe even as a rival school? Chance of that or transfer student.  Nonetheless enjoyable work so far and I find it truly great this came to be. Simply put, I found myself repeatedly giggling and yelling out with 'Wheeeeeeee!' at 2am. Take that as you will!  Both the story and collectibles feel like an unnecessary additions that stuttered the game's sense of flow, but there are a surprisingly large number of intrinsically fun moments to be had, especially surrounding the joy of movement itself.   Greatly helping with this is that the majority of the game uses an expert sense of lighting-cues to indicate how the player should progress, which is both efficient for the player and preserves immersion. The efficacy of such lighting makes the game's few confusing/ambiguous areas all the more obvious since then the (typical of modern games) faulty waypoint system becomes the primary navigation tool.  The experience offered is notably short (~3 hours), so use your own judgement on whether or not you think you'd enjoy it at the current price.  For me personally, I thought it was a joyful experience that was worth the price paid.
