This is one of the most enjoyable games I've played on the Vive. The neon enviroments are vibrant without being too bright or overbearing; combined with it's killer sound track this game immediately drew me in. I think they nailed the atmoshere, everything from the menus to your first interaction with your opponent are smooth and thematic. No stupid tutorial or long cutscenes you'll be right into this game in seconds.   You really have to move around your play space during matches, the AI loves hitting into the corners! I really enjoy how hitting the ball feels, your moves really affect how the ball spins/travels and once you get the hang of it smashing aces into that smug robot square is immensely satisfying. This is one game you definitly have to wear the wrist straps and be aware of your enviroment! Even keeping this in mind I managed to send things flying off my desk in the first match when I reached for a hit; be careful! It's not the games fault, it clearly shows you the boundaries. It's just very easy to become immersed in a match and want to pull off a dive or jump shot forgetting you're actually a few ft away from your wall.  For this price you can't go wrong, if you have a vive with some room space you should get this game.

So it took me 54 minutes to uninstall this game and refund it, mainly due to design issues.  -First off, the game doesn't let you manually crouch, your character automatically crouches when he gets to suitable cover.  -No actual cover system. (also can't blindfire)  -No sprinting.  -Gunplay is stiff and feels just bad overall. You can't run and gun, you have to aim before shooting, which slows you down a lot and completely ruins the flow of the gameplay.  -Enemies are extremely spongy from the very beginning, I'm talking 1 or 2 magazines with a glock to take down one enemy. This just isn't fun at all.  -Optimization is laughable, I had to put this game to medium settings on a GTX 1070 to keep a solid 60 fps  The only cool thing about this game is the whole time bending thing, but it completely fails on all the basics of an enjoyable third person shooter.

Bestest game ever.  No other game captivates you like this one, sadly it is underplayed for the potential it has and generally people only buy it when it is either on sale, or their PC is not good enough to handle other games.  I challenge someone to name another game where at 200 hours you are considered a noob and a scrub by most other players. You are able to learn sword fighting as you learn to write. To start with you force the moves and they are often rough and not very good compared to someone with more experience, yet with time it becomes instinct. You are able to execute complicated feints without even thinking about it.  In Multiplayer the game has two real competitive scenes. Dueling and Battle.  Battle tournaments such as: WNL and NC bring hundreds of amazing players together to compete for the title of either, for NC, winning country or, for WNL, winning team. WNL mixes players from various countries together and creates a great competition. The NC allows players to feel pride for their country as they represent it on a global stage. Dueling tournaments allow players to fight as they would never be able to in a battle; some of the unique and complicated feints that people develop can leave you amazed and attempting to figure out how they do it and create your own version. Duels between top players can last minutes, although not often as trying to perform fluent feints whilst also watching for spam and then trying to block instantly after often wears you down quickly.  People tell me that Counter Strike requires more skill, and is more difficult to play. I have never played it so I cannot give a definitive opinion but I struggle to believe it. In Mount and Blade you can always get better, and everyone has their own fighting style. Everytime you fight someone they will have their own style that you will have to learn and work around in order to defeat them. This is less true in Battles, as sword and shield fighting at a high level requires that you do certain things in order to win, and far more involves angles and A LOT faster reactions. In a sword and shield fight (If both players are top standard and have the right angles) swings can be many times faster than they would otherwise be and you have to be able to react in less than a second in order to block the swings. However in dueling, EVERY single person has their own different fighting style. Some people you will find have similar styles, but never ever the same. Different people have different patterns of feinting and swinging, you learn these as you fight them. When you first start being able to compete against duelists of a higher standard, you will find you have to consciously (whilst fighting) note how they are fighting and how much they feint/chamber/spam/kick (being the four main techniques). Once you start matching top duelists you will sub-consciously start absorbing this information.  You can get an enormous sense of achievement from this game. When you join a clan (You kinda have to in order to get good faster, as you are able to fight regularly with other players whose styles you are familiar with) you will often either be one of the worst or perhaps average in comparison to the other members. When you defeat the best fighter, then start regularly beating him until becoming the best, that is when you get the sense of achievement. (Bare in mind this will probably have taken months of tryng).  This game has so much to offer, yet has so few players for the potential it has. You genuinally learn to sword fight in the game, as you would in real life. At the start you can do nothing, you find yourself constantly being killed by superior players, but then when you revisit that server in a months time and find yourself far better than those players? That is when you truly start enjoying the game.  100% recommended for players looking for an experience that will suck them in and keep them hooked for years, but only if you have the time and commitment to put 1000s of hours in, learning to block, different feints, to chamber, to kick, when to spam and all the other angles/tricks there are in the game.

Above ground, in a tomb. Ugggg.  Also WTF happened to the ending. The whole time the story builds towards some alter ego/jeykll hyde type reveal but then ends up going no where. No where at all. There was no ending. Blah!!!     Yet, aside from the above two complaints I still really enjoyed this game. I had fun and I'm glad I played it. Totally worth whatever cheapie sale price for which I'm sure it was bought.

This game is something very special. As far as I am aware there is not, nor has there ever been, a game quite like this.  The conventional grand strategy we have come to expect from Paradox is all well and good, but the true heart of this game is in its roleplaying. Whether you're arranging marriages for your children or murdering someone else's, the interaction between characters is always at the forefront.  While it may seem an intimidating game at first, I find the emphasis on characters make it a lot more accessible than the other titles from Paradox. In simple terms, players are more easily able to make sense of people than numbers, and CKII is a game which caters to that excellently.

I played this game long, long ago, on the suggestion of Tycho from Penny Arcade. I played through all the demo levels in one sitting. When the finished game became available, I bought it.  And then, like so many rereleases on steam, I bought it again.  Auditorium is one of the best chill out games. It's a puzzler that can tax you intellectually, but still manage to feel fair and grounded in a consistent set of rules.   Ever played Orbient? It's a game in Nintendo's Art Style series, featuring gameplay that revolves around(excuse the pun) planetary orbits and gravity wells, singularities and the resulting trajectories of moving from one gravitational influence to another.   Auditorium quickly becomes the cousin to Orbient; At its best, ontrolling the path of the flowing lines across the screen becomes an entrancing kind of interstellar dance, with objects that attract or repel the flowing trajectories of sound. FTL gives you hours and hours of fun for very little. It's a gem. You might think 'how good can it be? screenshots seem kind of lame to me' well you'll totally change your mind after you spend some hours with it, I did.   It's hard at the beginning, very hard, so don't feel bad about how you can't finsh this little tiny indie game. I used to think it was a game about good/bad luck, but not at all. You'll have to try over and over again, read some guides or just general advices (there are tons on the community) but you'll feel very satisfied once you discover that luck plays just a little part on the game and finish it for the first time. You'll learn to drive your spaceship with what you come across and make the most of it. It's really rewarding every time you finish it, and you'll be attempting to finish it again and again for some reason. Every run is different than the previous one. It has a lot of replayability for such a small game.  I'm not saying it's for everyone, if you're more into action/shooting/slashing stuff and nothing else then you might not like it. However, given the critics reception it had you should give it a try, I'm so glad I did.

It's got kinks, it's got some bugs (still!!!), and it's got a bit of a learning curve. But if you find a group that works for your playstyle, this game is like no other. Luckily, the in-game voice is farily advanced in functionality to allow you to accomplish this, so once you sign in, do join a squad, say hello and look to follow your team mates. It has completely changed my point of view about videogames and their stories. It doesnt matter what your kind of games is, if you are a kid or an old man, you play Undertale or Call of Duty, or even Train Simulator, if you are open minded enough this game will just break it (your mind). It is not a complex plot, indeed there is no other plot rather than your personal history and self evolution, maybe this was not the intention of the creator (maybe yes, indeed), but, at some point, antichamber changes your life. My only recomendation here is to play this game alone, with no hurry at all (with this I mean to play it when you plan to have a 2/3 hours gaming sesion), reading everything carefully and enjoying the visual experience. I haven't said anything about the game itself because I feel like I would be spoiling it. It is completely worth the money, and as I often say this is not even a game, it's a masterpiece.

7/10  Pros:  -Intense car combat that has you run around the map picking up powerups, weapons and repair kits then blasting your opponents to bits ala arena shooters like Unreal Tournament.  -Satisfying and accessible arcade handling model that still requires finesse to master.  -Many tracks with reverse variant across a large variety of environments.  -Maps have good layouts and powerups are placed well.  -Good amount of cars modeled after their real life counterparts.  -Many unique weapons to choose from.  -Good postfx (e.g. when hit with a heavy weapon, the screen turns grayscale and depth of field is applied).  -Good damage modeling.  -Challenging especially when set on higher difficulty settings,  Cons:  -Campaign is quite shallow.  -AI rubberbands too much.  -Graphics are a little outdated.  -Online is (mostly) dead.  Overall, it is a good old-school style car combat/racing game but it lacks depth to have lasting appeal.

This game is a two-edged sword. Let me list the reasons why I both like and hate Nether:  Pros: Massive open world with buildings and areas to explore.  They give you a reason not to kill people: You get their blood on you and it attracts more beasts. Which, in my opinion, should be used more in these types of games.  The PvE is great! It feels way better than some games I played in the same genera (Rust)  Gunplay felt great! Same with melee weapons. It feels tight and worth the time to get the guns and ammo.   Cons: This game has a premium currency. IN A PAYED GAME. This is my biggest annoyance in games, but the developers let you be able get keys from other players. Keys are used to open locked containers that have better loot.  The community is both good and bad, as in most survival mmos, they're people that want to help, want to give you food, water, and new weapons that they don't need, while on the other hand, they're people that will lure you out of safezones just to kill you and take your loot. By far, one of the most mixed communities ive seen. At least in Rust, you could know if somebody wanted your head.  The world is too big! Even though the world is vast and can be explored, there is no reason to, since the buildings are mostly empty.    Should you get it? I would say, as long as you have a friend, go on ahead. The price tag is worth it. Its way cheaper, and way more complete than other survival games.

I just love Telltale's interactive stories, I could even forgive the illusion of choice they give you. The stories are always great and the interaction makes you feel part of it. First, I want to start this review by mentioning that this game does not seem to properly communicate with Steam. It doesn't tell Steam about your achievements properly, and when I quit out of the game, Steam doesn't get the message. I have to kill the process in order to use Steam in other places.  There are so many ways to enjoy this game. Where to begin?  If you like Dwarf Fortress, but wish it were even bigger and more complicated for some reason? This is your game. Go nuts. You can do everything from micromanage every aspect of your empire to let it run itself, only occasionally offering up your input. Automate the stuff you don't like doing, and manually control everything you do like. The AI is competent, if not great, and it will often do a much better job than you by sheer virtue of the fact that it knows the rules, and you likely do not.  If you like 4X games, it really doesn't get much more in-depth than this and the game, while still expensive, is vastly cheaper now than it was before the Universe package was released.  The user interface is atrociously bad, but I honestly can't think of any other way to get across the vast amount of information needed to play this game to the player. That said, finding buttons requires a freaking map and compass. Keep Google handy if you want to do something the UI doesn't think you'll need to do.  EDIT: After going further into a game, I've noticed a major flaw in the UI that cannot be overstated. The UI starts to look like a Jackson Pollack painting that moves, which is neat and all, but almost completely impossible to understand. You need to pause pretty frequently to understand what's happening, or automate more and more of your empire as you go on in order to keep pace with what's going on.

I got this game as it was remembering me Frozen Synapse/gunpoint, games I enjoyed a lot, with stealth elements. I was really looking into it. I read some reviews which let me down at first but I jumped in to check for myself.  Pros: -Art: I do like the blueprintish 2D view from above. -Controls: Controls respond well. -FPS: The game is running well without any problem on my Nvidia 580. -Stealth: Stealth is efficient, you need to stay out of the vision of the guardc and cctv.This is rather easy most of the time (if you get a guard outfit the vision of all the enemy gets reduced by 2, it is then very easy to get out of sight of everybody) -Punishment: If you get caught, depending where you are of course but there is a high probability that the guards will find you and one shoot you. This is good as its avoid the raise alarm run all over the place and everybody gets reset asap. As the level aren't that big it is ok to die. -Level design: The level aren't very big, which is good when you die and have to restart at the beginning.  Cons: -Art: It takes time and trial to understand some of the object (like windows took me 10 run to see the difference and why that guard was seeing me despite being inside a room). -Doors: As soon as you get too close from a door it opens automaticaly, it is annoying if you want to peek inside and see the most of the room. -Kills: All the time, the only option is killing the guard and 'innocent people' ? Despite the description of the game saying 'Stash unconscious guards in cupboards', if I kill them, they will be dead and not unconscious. -Story: The story will be forgot as soon as you close the game, even the mission objectives lack excitment sometimes and it's a little all over the place. -Hacking: The prologue was not clear on one point. When you hack a terminal, you can access the item unlocked at anytime and you don't have to stay near the terminal you hacked.  I quite enjoyed the game but it lacks on several points. Specialy when it comes to time. It took me an hour and a half to go through all the game, I have seen reviews which indicate they closed the game in under an hour) The story isn't memorable, I was reading it but just to be sure I wasn't missing anything. You won't play for the story (better for you to play gunpoint if you want something similar with a bit more of story and being able to choose your path in it).  I do like to compare game to movie when it comes to price as this is, for me, interactive entertainements. For about a bit more than an hour I would expect the price of the game to be similar to a movie theatre ticket (around 7/8euros in France), I got the game on sale for 6euros which suits me well.  I do recommend the game despite being so short. It was fun to play it. This game is probably for people who love stealth game and want a game that can be finish in the evening.  Luuna.

This game is an excellent blend of stealth, resource management, base building and decision making. You play as a group of civilians trapped in a city beseiged by an ongoing war. Scavenge, trade, craft and even lie and kill your way to survival. The question is, how far would you go to survive? This War of Mine asks this question constantly, and it means it.  One highlight is how good the game is in connecting you with your survivors. I have felt despair, anguish, sadness and hope in my playthroughs. It's remarkable how the game accomplishes this although each playthough can be very differerent. The game sets you up with a different set of survivors each time you play, and many events and locations are randomly generated to a certain extent. You may see the same location in a subsequent playthrough but the events and circumstances might be different, prompting you to use a different strategy whist visiting. A market square bustled with survivors eagerly engaging in black market trade might become a dangerous, deserted spot preyed upon by hostile snipers in your next game.  I thoroughly enjoyed my time with This War of Mine. Everything feels fresh and new, and I feel I'm a kid again eagerly discovering everything for the first time. This is the sleeper hit of the year.

We'd been at zee for a long time, running dark to conserve fuel and avoid the attention of the pirates and great beasts. Despite my best efforts -- spinning tales of comfort for my zailors, brazenly defying Nightmares, even initiating an affair with my engineer -- I'd managed to let my Terror get all the way to 100%. The achievement pops up; 'Lose Your Mind'. This doesn't bode well, but I'm a mere ship's length away from home port, and docking will reduce terror to 50%; a much more manageable level. Surely I can hold out just a little--   I'm instantly presented with a choice between murdering my entire crew (with 12% chance of success), or attempting to win them over (9%). Failing either one, I was told, would end my game. I did the only thing I could do, and somehow managed to kill everyone aboard, save two. We made harbor and, with a fresh batch of new faces, set out to zee once more.  Time passes.  An adventurer whom we've been running jobs for finally announces that he has all of the supplies he needs to make his final journey and I, having proven my capability, am his first choice of transport. There's just one problem...my trusty ship lacks the capacity to hold his cargo with room for food and fuel. We're going to need a bigger boat.  I buy a bigger boat and hire on twice again as many zailors as I already have. We load up and set sail, but not before I accept just one more smuggling job from my 'friend' with the bad eye and the concealed knife. Corsair's Forest? I've been there a dozen times; consider it done.  It's not until we're two days out that I realize my new ship has half the speed of my old one, and my triple-size crew are a hungry bunch. We're running low on supplies before we've even reached our initial destination, but we make the drop and start to head home.  Then the realization strikes. If we dock in London now, before finishing that smuggling job, I'll have to cough up the fee plus a hefty chunk of interest, not to mention lose a valuable connection...but the nearest trading port is in the opposite direction.   It took every scrap of food we had, but we managed to reach Mount Palmerston without resorting to cannibalism...again. We traded with the devils for supplies and loaded up on coke freshly dug from Hell. On our way home we made port at Pigmote isle, where a civilization of guinea pigs threw a banquet in our honor -- their returning saviors, though this time we were the ones in need of saving.  After a harrowing encounter with a manic Jillyfleur brought hull integrity to 1%, and a misbehaving Clay Man took five lives before we subdued him, we were desperate for rest and repair. An ill-fortuned shore leave on Gaidur's Mourn pushed Terror to worrying levels, but we had our package. As we pulled out of the port, a straggling pirate ship fired one last shot across our bow -- but the shell missed by an arm's length and we were homeward bound. Two days later, limping into port with a skeleton crew, I breathed a sigh of relief that I'd narrowly escaped losing my mind for the second time in the space of a week. My sweetheart and child welcome me home. I say nothing of the suicides -- surely they were suicides? -- that only I had been privy to. I pass on an uncanny artifact to my son; someday he, too, will be a zee captain, with enough cares of his own. No need to trouble him with details.  My friend has another job for me, and the Admiralty has need of news from the outlying colonies. I take a loss on a purchase of a smaller ship, name her after the one I'd traded to begin with. Eighth of my house and 211 days at zee, I sail on.

review_text: Not terrible, it's still CoD, still shooty shooty bang bang but it's lacking something, doesn't feel like it has much of an impact.  Characters are forgettable, storyline isn't too enthralling and I don't really enjoy the futuristic crap that seems to be the trend these days, when I played this I felt myself thinking back to incredible memories during Modern Warfare with Captain Price, Soap, Gaz and other memorable awesome heroes from the past.  It's got the mechanics and gameplay, it just doesn't feel like you are a soldier anymore, you're a bloody walking death zone and for me that killed this game's reputation.  Unless the next Call of Duty is a remake of Modern Warfare (remastered and perfected) I don't see myself playing them anymore, otherwise it's not a bad game and can be entertaining, the multiplayer especially but it's not the CoD I remember. I recommend it so that you can appreciate MW1/MW2/MW3

A good ammount of the time. However this game was missing one of two things to be a ringer; my preferred desire would be a deep and desirable world with an ending that pays off. Yeah the premise kept my attention and the art was superb (I can't stress that enough, it is this games strong suit) but... nothing really happened at all with that entire experience. I can sum up the game for you very simply, right here, right now.  Minor event>pin up>minor event>pin up>minor event>pin up  Sure the characters were nice to look at, and they started with interesting stories and had interesting personalities; but the ending screwed it all up. There was no pay off for being teased by these characters for hours, you don't really get to 'choose' anyone to be with at the end. The only choice in the entire experience isn't even worth replaying for as it only changes the festival scene for sure and MAYBE the end (I'm not replaying this so I can not gurantee even that's true). I really don't want to play through another afternoon waster just to get the pay off that should have been in this game. If you had taken the time just do four fair endings this would have bumped up from a disappointing experience to at least an acceptable one. Because like I said if this is to build up to sequels... with the same characters... just to get the pay off there, you are not getting my money for that. Heck to the naw. I know you probably will get some money off them so long as you feed upon the simplistic weeaboos who don't just find much less tame stuff FOR FREE.  But if you respect yourself as artists; give the reader a little prize for making it through your story. Give them choices, give them pay off, give them freaking depth over 'hey look at this cute girl talking to you and her BEWBS' give them something to go off of. Otherwise you're just wasting everyones time. And you know if you're going to feed to the lowest common denominator anyway, to people who really don't give two turds and a corncob about plot and development and depth. At least give them full on porn. Like I said, you can find way better than mostly naked boob for totally free.  So yeah; more depth and intrigue or a lot more nudity one way or the other... or both, can't complain to both I suppose.  I will give you the benefit of the doubt, maybe it's an early attempt (which kudos if-so) or if there's a time limit I dunno. To prospective buyers; if you want an easy time waster and nothing too complex for your young minds. This is an okay buy. If you're looking for something mind blowing that you can read and wank to whenever you want; you can find better stuff for free. And frankly what feels like a dating-sim that doesn't let you, you know, get with a girl at the end; romantically or sexually. That is a huge glaring error that should not have been a thing. I originally backed Catlateral Damage on Kickstarter and helped Greenlight it for Steam and it has not disappointed me. I remember some kinks in the early portion of the beta but it has come quite a bit of way now that we're getting closer to release (beta until Sept. 1st as of the latest email from creator Chris Chung!). I periodically play in to see new development on the game and it has smoother swatting motions, fun challenges, and a lot more ease of movement than before. In the beginning, the motion aspect was the biggest target and then later came in meowing, additional achievements, mini challenges, litterbox mode, and the Kickstarter stretch goal levels such as the museum and grocery store.   Thus far, I have played Objective mode, Litterbox mode, and the bonus museum level. I don't have any complaints about the game because it is certainly satisfying to swat objects onto the ground, watching some of them shatter (like dishes & vases!), getting upgrades, and the disappointed feeling that comes when you jump and fail epicly. Objective mode features a timer and a goal to destroy a number of items within a timeframe while Litterbox mode allows you to destroy items at your leisure. After each level is completed whether through timer in Objective mode or casually through Litterbox mode, you can simply click continue to be taken to a whole new house/level. I really like how each house has a different design to keep things fresh.   As I've gotten older, I have become more of a casual gamer so I mostly play in Litterbox mode. I'd rather take my time destroying my owners' precious items than being rushed to do the job. I'm a cat, right? I'm gonna take my sassy-♥♥♥ time then. The only problem I could see other people having, is the graphics, but what you see above is what you're getting. It is a simple, sometimes slightly stressful (Objective mode) game. Catlateral Damage is a fun distraction simulator essentially and I love it. And yes, you can chase a red dot when the challenge comes up.

Disclaimer : Deus Ex is my favorite cyberpunk universe, I played all parts of the series and read J. Swallow's novels.     The reason why I admire the Deus Ex franchise is because these games manage to accomplish two things that are particularly important and rare in video games - they inspire and provoke thoughts. In my opinion, this is much more than a flashy sci-fi story. DE series manages to picture the core of the conflict between authority and liberty, it provokes asking important and extremely timely questions, such as 'Is the world we're living in ready for the challenges that science may bring to us in the near future?', 'Is our moral evolution lagging behind our technological advancement and progress?'. Deus Ex: Mankind Divided does have all these features and same good old touch of conspiracy and transhumanism..   So, no matter how hard the publisher tries to ruin the game with micro-transactions and horrible customer support (Submitted a ticket about missing items from the DLC bundle 5 days ago, finished the game already - still no response) - I simply cannot rate it down...  P.S.:  - No complains about ending..    A lack of closure is not only a prerequisite for a good continuation of the story that (hopefully) awaits us soon, but, in a way, it's a central protagonist's conflict

An old little gem from Apogee, this is a fun and challenging 2D platformer. When I was kid, I had the shareware version (back when shareware was still a thing), which included only the first chapter, and I had a lot of fun with it. Having the chance to finally complete it was great.  As far as story goes, like most games from its era, it doesn't have a lot. You play as a secret agent (hence the name) tasked to infiltrate a series of island controlled by an evil organization in order to recover the blueprints to a superweapon.  When it comes to gameplay, each chapter (there's three of them) consists of a large island filled with enemy bases you need to take down before gaining access to the island's fortress. Each level (or base) requires you to find an explosive to blow open the exit, although you might also have to find keys to unlock doors, or floppy disks to deactivate laser fields that may be in your way. There's also several enemies and different hazards on each level. As I mentioned above, it can be quite challenging, as some of the levels include sections that are basically trial and error, and you may end trapping yourself with no other option but to restart the level, which can be a bit frustrating. Even then, losing your three lives only forces you to restart the level, and all of them can be completed in a few minutes. Another good thing is that you can play the levels in any order you like, so if you find one too challenging, you can always go back to the island map and try another one, in case you're missing ammo, for example.  In conclusion, I recommend this game. Whether you lived through the 90s (or earlier), and have some nostalgia for the games of your childhood, or if you never experienced that era, and want to see some of the early days of gaming, you should give this game a shot.

I was a bit apprehensive about purchasing this release. Having grown up in the nes era, and having played countless hours of the original series releases, I was worried that this release would be another '3D', modestly edited port similar to the FF3 and FF4 releases. I'm no super fan by any means, and I enjoy the new ports, but I prefer the older style.  This port has really delivered and remained true to the original (well, mostly). The story is all there, the characters and general mechanics are all there, and the graphics are a great balance between the old and the modern styles. I'm a bit turned off by a few character portraits, but that is a matter of preference. Also, the UI will likely feel like a hot mess to players looking for the original interface, but with a little time it's just a mild annoyance and fully playable.  Could this port have been made better? Yes. I'm more than satisfied though, and I highly recommend this game to new and old fans of the series.

Firewatch is pretty brilliant, pretty smart, and pretty in general. From the begininning, youll be quickly led on to the fact that this is a 'feels' game, And damn does it 'feel' good. You play as henry, a man trying to escape an incredibly complex and unfortunate past, but luckily none of the game is really about your past. No typical cliche story thumbtacks involving a man with a shady past, It's simply there to get you out at your new job.  You've taken a job watching over a large section of forest, reporting any fires that start, and dealing with rowdy kids disturbing the laws of the trail. It's a secluded job, but from the first day you reach your tower, you will be in near constant communication with one of the most pleasant people you will ever come across in a video game. This person is your sanity, your drive to keep going, and with a system in place that you can talk to her about just about anything you find in the forest, it feels more like a person rather than an extensive dialogue tree.  I can't say the game is perfect, but it does a lot of things perfectly, and I have no doubt that someone looking for a nice game to enjoy their alone time, would be nothing but satisfied with this 2-6 hour journey (depending on your pace).  The only part I don't like (semi-spoiler):    For such a well thought out and well executed single player experience, the ending really isn't what you'll be hoping for, it's not a sad ending, or a terrible ending at all, its just not the satisfaction you will be entitled to.

It's the good old world of Dishonred, with its unque setting, stunning visual design, lots of crawlspaces and highly corrupt but silly guards. I love it. And the soundtrack is pretty good. However, the plot and writing aren't particularly impressive. I've dropped the game for a week mid-playthrough and now I don't even remember what was happening there or why I should care. I kinda hoped D2 would be an improvement over D1 in this regard. Oh well. Some of the NPCs' lines and Emily's high-chaos random comments are truly ridiculous and cheesy. The whole morality/'chaos' thing still feels artificial and disturbing at times given the choices you have to make and their consequences. Anyway, I weren't looking to play D2 for the story or the acting (but hearing guards declaring their urgent need to pee over and over again can get annoying). Regarding the performance and visual quality, things didn't go well after the release. There are still rampant technical issues. Looks like the latest patch has sacrificed texture and shadow detail, now the shadows are all glitchy, triangular and flickering, and textures pop in visibly when you move closer. Antialiasing produces noticeable artifacts, like banding and flashing pixels. I've noticed numerous holes between meshes, ffs. I still enjoy the game, but these problems do ruin the immersion at times. I'll recommend against buying D2. If you liked D1, you're still going to buy this game anyway (although waiting for a sale is a reasonable option), and if you didn't, the game isn't going to offer much more for you. P.S. Shooting guards with sleep darts causes them to launch into walls or into air with great force for some reason, killing them instantly.

At first I despised the whole 'group controlling' aspect, because I was looking for another RPG like TES. But after spending 1-2 hours, I started feeling oddly attracted to the game. I literally fell in love. The story is amazing, the graphs are still pretty decent after 4 years, the gameplay is fantastic. Amazing 'tactics' system that inspired me to actually strategize my attacks. All hands down, probably the best RPG I played in a long while.   That being said, a few things must be said. Firstly, SAVE A LOT. Some quests can be BUG nests, so be extra cautious.  Secondly, before you even CONSIDER trying to build a GENERAL character, know that you will encounter party members of different classes, so just pick one path and master it. Otherwise, you will end up creating a weakling-deadweight character whom you will always have to carry in to battle. Long story short, you won't have enough time to master every single skill, so just master one or two if you are lucky.

Dragon Age Origins is another notch in the sword that is BioWare's vessel of masterful storytelling, fantastic world building, and meaningful character development.   I instantly fell in love with with the rich cast of characters (well most of them) and I genuinely cared for their well being - if you are familiar with KotoR, expect the same level of detail and character development. The story is epic and harrowing, the decisions are morally exhausting. I loved traveling and exploring the world, talking to every npc, buying and finding the best possible gear for my party that I could, and completing every quest and side-mission.  If you are the type that likes playing these games to completion and doing every quest, expect 60-65 hours on your first playthrough - though keep in mind there are many MANY more hours of gameplay to be had following completion. The included DLC and added missions add dozens of hours of additional gameplay.  This is the same combat system (for the most part) used in KotoR: fast-paces turn-based combat with tactical control over your party with the ability to lay traps if your character has the required skills. Did you like Mass Effect? Did you enjoy the tactical aspects of it? Were you ♥♥♥♥♥♥ off beyond belief when they started easing out the tactical features for straight up combat in the sequels? Were you also intimidated by the WALLS OF TEXT within Mass Effect lore? Do you desire something a little more... generic? Familiar? Bland? If you said yes to these then welcome to The Bureau: XCOM Declassified. It's a good game but it pales in comparison to a large number of cover based shooters, espcially the Mass Effect series that a LOT of this game is influenced from.  And like I said, it's a good game. It's slow to start and if you don't spend time emphasizing tactical commands you ARE going to die. A lot. Your teammates will make sure to tell you this, constantly. Whether it's jumping to other pieces of cover INTO enemy fire, whining after taking a few shots to damage and asking you if you're alright when you take similar damage, or freaking out when enemies start slowly approaching right into your line of fire. The AI is actually somewhat sharp, though pathfinding is pathetic so you may never actually notice. The story's alright as well, but characters are pretty damn dull and uninteresting. Plus while some games relish in giving you choice and letting you watch the consequences this game goes 'here's an important choice, WHOOPS, WE'RE GOING TO DO THIS INSTEAD' while still vaguely referencing the choice you made in the background. There's a bit of a reason for this later in the plot (which was okay but used in a REALLY hamfisted way) but it still feels lazy.  So ultimately that's what I can say about this game: from the Mass Effect styled menus, and faithful work to the source material, it's built to be an alright game, but it never goes further than that. It tries some interesting things, but you can feel that not a lot of love went into making sure the game stood on its own two feet.  I grabbed it for about five bucks, so if you're on the edge about it, it doesn't hurt to wait for a sale. I mean, it's still XCOM for all that's worth.

But regarding the gameplay as a narrative medium, not only it is the most solid, well balanced and well crafted design in all the series, but it also has some of the most cunning and innovative uses of the camera and the gameplay as means to tell a story of all games in this generation.  For instance, the scenes in which Batman interacts with   his Joker hallucination   uses the player's camera movement in order to change the environment, in a boldness that only the interactive art of videogames can afford.  Highly recommended!

Amazingly, F.3.A.R. does the impossible and manages to be even worse than its predecessor. The horror elements were pretty much reduced to quick-time events in F.E.A.R. 2 and have been binned altogether by the third game; the gameplay is as uninspiring as ever with most enemies taking all of one headshot to kill; the story is completely nonsensical even if you ignore the first game and worse still if you do take it into account; and instead of the perplexing abortion of an ending that we got in F.E.A.R. 2, we now have TWO of the bloody things. Happily, the third game is even shorter than the second, taking only about three and a half agonizing hours to trawl through.  Avoid like a crotch rot.

Ah yes, Warframe. The game that did beat my favorite game in that one MMOBomb tournament. So it is only natural that I try the game to see how it works. For the record, what happened with that MMOBomb tourney is all said and done, and what was said will be the last time I mention it in this review.   Looking at it for the first time the game doesn't really stand out. It's a third person shooter set in space, and while space games are a viable genre, it's a known genre. You take control of a Warframe, out to defeat a faction hell bent on taking complete control of the Solar System or a massive weapons manufacturing company wanting to exploit secrets of a past life being, one of which is your Frame, or tasked with wiping out an infestation of mutated beings from the first two factions. Sounds pretty run of the mill, but it works.   Graphics are solid; the Frames themselves seem realistic (or as realistic as they could be for a sci-fi shooter) and one can definitely sense the textures used on their suits. The environments are nice looking as well, ranging from industrial grit to high-tech polish to overgrown biomass strewn all over the maps. I can't say that I was too immersed in the game, but with a game like this, immersion is almost impossible so what the game does it does well. I do feel that a few of the newer Frames do seem out of place - they're over designed and have too much going on with them, leading to messy textures to gaudy coloring - but for the most part everything feels solid. The weapons are nicely modeled and textured as well; weapons that are from a particular faction look the part though some do stick out a bit, namely the Infested type weapons, which don't seem to fit in the game as well as the others. Sounds are good as well, though I do feel that the enemies - namely the Corpus - do get annoying with their gibberish. Turning the sound off is easy enough however.   The UI is also good in this game; it's clean, easy to read, and unobtruisve, which is what every UI should be. I have seen screenshots of the game in its infancy and I am glad they changed the UI to what it is now, which makes the game look a lot more professional looking than before.   The gameplay is where the game does shine though. As stated, this is a third person shooter, so the game isn't too groundbreaking. However, what it does it does well. Everything seems to fit seamlessly together, with intuitive (for the most part) controls and fluid motions. Using melee is a bit awkward at first; to use it quickly one presses E and one has to bind a key to switch to melee altogether, but in the end it makes sense as E is readily accessible. There are a few more problems though; wallrunning is a bit akward, especially if one is trying to run up into a vent. It does not always work and often leaves me annoyed. Another is switching weapons, which is done using the F key (rather than the middle mouse wheel like other shooters) but I am assuming that can be rebound.   Crafting is straightforward enough. In order to make something, you need a blueprint and materials. Blueprints are often bought from the marketplace (the rest farmed from missions are bought from your clan dojo) for in game currency while the player farms for the materials. Simple enough, and once that is all done, you hit build and wait about 12 hours. I do find that the time is a bit silly; 12 hours for a gun? On top of that, there are few weapons available that can be bought with credits, which is the in game currency. The majority of them are bought with Platinum, which is the Cash shop currency, though Platinum can be traded between players. It feels cash grabby and arbitrary to have those timers, as one can rush build them for Platinum.   Now for the drawbacks of this game. One is the mastery system, which requires players wait a full 24 hours before each attempt, especially the first test where new players are screwed because of the starting gun. This could be solved in many ways - one, attempt the test as many times as you want if you fail. This is a video game; why should you have to wait? Two, give new players a better gun, or three, make the test somewhat easier. I know many are thinking that I suck because of the gun being used, but for the record, I have switched to a better gun and as expected, I passed. Normally I would say that it depends on player skill for these tests, but with the damage system in the game, it's the gun that makes the difference moreso than the player. In short, remove the 24 hour restriction; it is pointless and all it does is add frustration, or if DE wants to keep it, give new players a better gun or make the test easier.  The next drawback is the elevators in the game. They seem simple enough; call for the elevator, get in, wait for your team, and trigger it. Except almost every team I ran with forgot the third part; wait for the team. I know that this is more to do with the players, but DE could put in a way for everyone to tag the elevator BEFORE it can be triggered. Otherwise - and this is what happens usually - you have one person who shows up too late and misses the elevator. If it's a short ride, this is no problem, but in some cases waiting can be a real pain and all it does is thin out the party. DE has a checkpoint system with the dual locked doors, but something in the elevators that forces everybody in the party to tag it before triggering would work wonders.   Third, and probably the biggest drawback of the game, is the fact it runs out of steam VERY quickly. What I mean by this is simple; one can reach the end of the game pretty quickly, even with lower levelled items. This could be for many reasons; the build timers could be one, or the fact that certain materials that are required for some weapons are far off and by the time you get the materials you either have a better weapon than the one you want to build OR that you have your weapon modded enough that the one you want to build is actually worse. It feels awkward, and makes me want to play the game less. Why would I want to repeat the process with a Frame or weapon that is worse than the one I have? Maybe it's just me, but I feel the game just runs out of steam.   In all, Warframe is a great game. It's not anything groundbreaking, but what it does it does well and makes for a fun hack-and-slash game that can keep you entertained. I do hope that it is expaned on in the future though, and not just with events but things that acutally expand the gameplay. Aside from that, it's definitely a game to check out.

Free to play space ninjas game. Give it a try, at least.  Great graphics (and very well optimized!), combat is very satisfying both for the experienced and novice players. The game can be rather grindy but there's always a challenge to be had, so the grind isn't boring like it is with most other games. New updates all the time, which are especially nice for folks like me who only play off and on. Whenever I come back it's like a whole new and improved game.  I do strongly encourage you to play with friends, or try to find a group to join. The game can be rather dull when played alone. Group up with friends and set your sights on specific objectives (such as unlocking characters, leveling up, or collecting rare items) and the game starts to realize its potential.  What's especially nice is that everything in the game can be gained without use of real world currency, to the best of my knowledge. Of course, it can be difficult to get some things without spending cash, especially newly released Prime characters and equipment (basically upgraded versions of regular stuff that has a unique skin and better/tweaked stats), but it is far from impossible.

Let's get the negatives out of the way first. The puzzles are extremely easy. They seem more like they're there to just move the story along rather than to challenge the player. If you are looking for the crazy puzzles of ye olde LucasArts, you won't find them here. There's also no fail state whatsoever, for those looking for any sense of risk or danger. For wallet-conscious players, I squeezed almost 5 hours out of Act 1. While there's definitely a reason to do one replay through Act 1 to get the most out of the ending, it might not be the best bang for your buck right now. I also had some weird performance issues whenever I tried to use something from my inventory.  Okay, got that out of the way. This was my first PC point-n-click game since Myst. I never liked the genre, but Double Fine was a studio I was completely in love with. I know a lot of their history is in point-n-click and I was fully prepared to make cat hair mustaches and to deal with nonstop Princess Bride references because that's still a thing. What I got was a masterpiece.   The art is incredible. I want to print out and frame every scene. Every once in a while a neck will pop in front of a head or someone will climb behind a ladder; those kinks will get worked out. The characters are signature Double Fine. While I don't think any of them reach Raz level, every character is very well realized and drowning in charm. The puzzles are extremely easy. I'm including that in the positives because I'm a dumb baby and 'classic' point-n-click puzzles are ridiculous. I had to use a guide once, but for a silly reason. This is perfect for those who want to use common sense so they can continue to bask in the glory of Broken Age, rather than trawl some message board looking for an answer. Then there's the story. I was quite meh on Velle's (The female protagonist) storyline. The characters in it are great. The artwork is beautiful. Everything inside of it is wonderful. The story itself is just a straightforward retelling of The Hunger Games until you wrap up Shay's (The male protagonist) storyline. Shay's story is radically different to the point where I couldn't understand why these were part of the same game until the conclusion of Act 1. Shay's history creates a deliciously weird world for him to exist in that is much creepier than it lets on. The end of Act 1 is insane. Maybe some people will see parts of it coming, but I think Double Fine did a good job of not treating the player like an idiot in that regard. It definitely requires a second playthrough to see how the puzzle pieces match up.

Spelunky is a modern masterpiece. It's as close to perfect as it is possible for a game to be. Every element is deliberately designed to fit together into an intricate system in which each failure teaches you something and prepares you that little bit better for the next game.  Its genius is in applying the traditional 'roguelike' elements - randomly generated areas, high cost of player death, and learnable systems - into the approachable form of the platformer. No single element of the game is terribly challenging. Each monster, trap, item, and weapon has a very simple behaviour and is easily mastered. Once you see how a bat moves, you know how to deal with the next one. However Spelunky rarely gives you a series of single challenges, but a mix of several all at once: so while a single bat is no problem, a bat, a spike trap and a spider is something else.  This is the heart of what makes Spelunky so great. Because the levels are randomly generated, you can't memorise them, and instead have to learn the underlying systems. You're constantly having to quickly eye up a situation, apply what you know about the world, and work out a way to get through it. Even though you can get very skilled at the first few levels you never cruise through on autopilot as the arrangements of obstacles always offers a challenge.  And no matter how good you get, there is always some way to test yourself even more. I recently defeated the game the easy way, so the next challenge is to find the secret areas and hidden levels and defeat them too. I know what is involved and some of it seems eye-wateringly difficult. But I'd have it no other way!

[ Introduction ]: Blockland is a mix between Roblox (non-steam) and a bit of Garry's Mod, therefore looking and playing like a 'Roblox done right'. The idea of the game is great: allowing players to build things with LEGO blocks and host their own servers with different game types.  For further customization and setting of servers, Blockland also presents a simple enough Eventing System (simplified scripting) allowing the user to affect the entities, game client or the game running in the server.  [ Positive Points ]: - Freedom of creation: For those who like to create and host servers, there is a great deal of freedom in the game, allowing anyone to create and host an unique server. - Easy to learn and get started. - DIfferent gametypes, that varies from Freebuild to Kart Races (with or without guns), Team Deathmatchs, CityRPGs, Empire (Civilization-like mini-games) and more.  [ Negative Points ]: - As expected, the worst part of the game is it's community, probably composed by many children (or brain-damaged young adults and adults). Some very annoying and immature/offensive/pornographic stuff is said on the chat in any server. Even some servers Admins/Moderators are stupid and may get into the way of the players (derping in the middle of a firefight, standing in the middle of a road during a race, etc).  - Also as expected due to the large amount of stupid members, many people don't know how to read, don't bother or are simply trolls, and dedicate themselves in annoying others. Stupid players also ruin RP games, like 'Super Murder Mystery' (Blocklands' Trouble in Terrorist Town counterpart), turning most RP games into TDMs with lots of RDMing. Many mods and admins do their best to keep their servers clean, but any player will see this happening many times. - Buggy and laggy collision physics on vehicles, make some game modes very annoying or take much fun away. - Build mode can be very boring to work with when using a laptop without a numpad. - Little number of players and servers (from my experience).  [ Conclusion ]: If you can get past the annoying community, there's fun to be had in Blockland. Switching between game modes and the shared simplicity of the games is very fun. Forming up a group of friends to play on a self-made server is also a great idea.  I'm pretty sure that Garry's Mod would be a better option, but still, Blockland has it's own vibe to it to make it worth a try, so try the Demo from the official site before buying.

Okay, here's how I see it. Many people are peeved that this games is either 'too hard for them' or they are simply not happy with the mechanics of the game or they claim that it doesn't have enough cards. I see it like this. TRY LOOKING AT WHAT HAS BEEN RELEASED FOR THE ACTUAL 2015 TCG AND THEN MAKE YOUR JUDGEMENT!!! The game has almost every card for the 2015 pack. It does take a little bit to get started, I admit. I actually liked the challenge presented to me for creating new and diverse decks. The game play could use a little tweaking, as I have found a few deviations from the rules that I find only mildly annoying. As far as an over-all review goes: I love the game. My suggestion is this: Make your own deck! Figure out what style works best for you and go with that. Expand on it. Optimize it. And when you get to the point that you get tired of your current deck, build another one and start cleaning house with that. This is a TCG-based game, it's not Borderlands, Mass Effect, or ES: Skyrim. It's not made to be a vast adventure that you embark on to rid the world of all evil in a 3-D setting with a diverse character set, customizable characters, and graphics that will blow your mind. It's A TCG-based game. Simple, with a basic story line in which the fate of the multiverse relies on your cards. Simple, fun, and concise.

I've only played a few space - or futuristic - RTS games. Those being Earth 2160 and a bit of Starcraft II. Me being a huge Command and Conquer fan, I thought I'd add another space RTS to my collection, that being this game.  I have to admit, I'm a little disappointed in the game since I thought you would be base building but you're not. It reminds me of Command and Conquer 4: Tiberian Twilight more than anything but that's alright. I love the concept of this game and I love the gameplay. The maps are absolutely beautiful at max (and my video card is a cheap 2GB one) and the game is challenging. So my disappointment in the lack of base building is irrelevant.  I recommend this to anyone who likes RTS games and doesn't like micromanagement (base building) very much. In this game, you command a carrier which deploys units and where you research upgrades and units to build from your carrier. I've only played Skirmish so far so I can learn the systems and whatnot, but plan on trying the campaign very soon. I will update my review then.  In the mean time, I recommend this game. From my experience, I don't recommend the two Fleet DLCs as they are basically two retextured fleets of the base ones. I played about 5-10 minutes as Soban and I don't think I noticed anything different from the Coalition. If someone can explain the DLC more in-depth to me, please do so. I will update my review.

app_name: You Have to Win the Game
review_text: You Have to Win the Game is simple, short and sweet. The developers took much care in making sure the presentation and gameplay are genuine to the mid 1980s PC style of platformers. The controls are easy to use and the artwork is themed well. As a totally free title, this game is a welcome addition to the genre even though there isn't too much content nor a storyline to speak of. There is fun to be had here and you won't feel like you wasted your time. Unless... the ending bothers you as it did me. Without mentioning any spoilers, I feel it's necessary to at least say that the end-game 'puzzle' feels out of place and I personally didn't like it at all. It really didn't have to be included in the game. I understand the developers felt like the finale had to be brought together somehow but the puzzle they decided on really feels like it doesn't belong in this game at all. Besides this final 'puzzle', the game was enjoyable, if only a bit shallow and without any music. It's free, though!  You Have to Win the Game is okay.

My biggest complaint is the server issues and the long loading screens that eventually time out forcing me to re-log. However, the development team is well aware of the problems and are working diligently to solve them.   One thing I can say about the development team is that they interact with players on a daily basis. They read the forums, they respond back to players and often times you'll see them log in to chat. They take into consideration every suggestion on the forums and are open to constructive criticism. Throw them a learning curve too as it's a small team and the very first game for some of them. PhurieDae and Anakiro are awesome gamer girls themselves, so I love that they're part of the dev team, offering up help and support whenever needed. This is a weird one. As an experience, I found The Beginner's Guide intriguing, and genuinely wanted to see what was happening next in the story.  However, I paid for a game, walking simulator or not. Something with options and outcomes, like The Stanley Parable, that I could pick up at a later date and get a different experience from. You won't find that here; it's a completely linear experience where the options you're given are things like 'do you want to read this unimportant note?'. Nothing you do matters, this is story time, and I completely understand why.  I ummed and ahhed over getting a refund on this game, and eventually did so (my first ever). I didn't hate the experience, far from it, but I don't think it justified the price, and you could get the same experience from watching someone play it for an hour on YouTube. Had it been just half the price, I would have let it stay in my library as I feel the developer deserves something for his work.  Very interesting game, but I just can't recommend it as a purchase. And I feel kind of bad about that.

This game. Before buying it, I knew I would feel bad doing it.   ... I was right.  As you can guess, there's no story. No help, not even an explication ingame. At least you can get almost all of it on the steam store page, hehe.  It's the king of game that made you wonder how it got approved. $5 for this could almost be called 'stealing'.  I bought it for $1, and it feel way too much for this.   I played 1 hour to get the gist of it, to finish this game at 100%. Yet, I only enjoyed the first 5 minutes or so.  This game is freaking repetitive, the music is generic as hell, nothing outstanding for the graphics, and there's this really bothering lag every time you go on the menu. First time I launched the game, it always showed '0/10 achievements', even when Steam was saying I got some. It even showed '5/20 achievements' from time to time (wtf?) I got really bored of it, and I continued later. The achievement counter was fixed when I launched it a second time, and it was the star counter.   Like, some basic features like that are not even correctly done.   This game wants to be 'hard', 'frustrating', 'giving the chance to improve', and yet, after every death, there's this a bit too long for my taste, wait to finally starts. It frustrates me more than the game itself.  There's also the fact that when you finish a level, you see for some seconds the screen saying you failed, then the right screen is shown. What is wrong with this game?  I think it would be way more apt for this game to be on a website like Kongregate, somewhere where it would be free to play, since it's definitely not worth paying for.

Before I begin this review, I'd like to say I almost didn't reccomend this game. Why? The game has a lack of content. Its often the same map every few games, and theres only 3 game modes. There is offline games, but they're not much at all. Often times servers are done and you can't play the game, and it takes a while before Ubisoft fixes bugs in the game.   The upside however is it's a unique game. Not very tactical, but it's quite fun. As you play the game you get in-game currency that you use to buy officers & different upgrades for that specific officers gun. Each officer has their own unique abillity. One of the officers in the game Sledge, has a sledge hammer used to destroying things in one hit such as a wall or barricaded window.. Think of this game as Call of Duty with a tactical twist to it.  Worth the money? No.  Is it fun? Yes.

The Division. The long awaited 3rd person covershooter/RPG/MMO that we've all heard about for so long.  First let me say that I personally enjoy this game and it is an absolute blast with friends and isn't too bad by yourself either.  Pros:  Great Shooting mechanics Good Cover system Enemy Variety Good teamplay  Great abilities  Pretty Good sounds; indoor echos, reverb off objects, etc. Fun story; that although Clancy himself didn't write you could see him doing so if he were still around. an overall great experience  Cons:  Servers broken on Day 1 (but hey its Ubisoft) Dark zone rogue system for PvP not perfect IMO, but functional I suppose A bit unstable FPS wise. But i always stay above 45. (i7 4970k, 16gb RAM, GTX 760 4gb)  This game received some undue hatred because of a rocky launch, quite typical of Ubisoft.  BUT!! Like many Ubisoft games, once released adn the bugs ironed out they become extremely well made and enjoyable games. Some of my favorites: Ghost Recon, Rainbow Six, Splinter Cell, and Far Cry 2,3,4 all are Ubisoft.  Overall this game provides a great experience for Coop and even solo play, a real diamond in the rough. With a ton more content to be released this year alone this game will be a well polished game that will last for 2 years or more.   Worth my money.

Like Automobilista, this is a great simulation. The physics are stunning, as well as the force feedback. There are also great mods available. You can set your races / seasons, having complete race weekends. Set these as you desire. Drive at day / night, or have transitions between these (you could also multiplier time changes). It's also possible to drive in rain. It's dynamic, so it's possible the rain start and stop. The driving itself feels great, like iRacing. The A.I. is really great, and you can have great races and improve your skills by using rFactor 2. To me, I like driving in Automobilista and rFactor 2 much more, then Assetto Corsa. This is like iRacing, but offline to me. But having more cars / tracks, and I'm not sure yet, but the physics could be better. And off course, you could drive in night more often, and have rain (iRacing doesn't support rain - yet)

DOOM (2016) is a forgettable modern FPS priced out of a recommendation. DOOM has decent gameplay that makes heavy use of the 'press E to execute' QTE design that other ♥♥♥♥♥♥ games also use, where every encounter is just a room where enemies spawn until you kill them all, and completely atrocious modern boss encounters that remove all tension and excitement and turn the already mediocre gameplay into a gross chore. I found myself wishing the game had ended hours before the end, alt+tabbing to watch cartoons. I absolutley love it and if I was a rich gal' I' dbuy a copy for all my friends. Best game in years.

This can actually be a very fun indie game if and ONLY IF you own a steam controller (I've created a configuration that allows me to attack enemies using turbo - a steam controller activator featurer). Other than that, I cannot recommend this game to everyone else. Why? This game relies heavily on clicking on the left mouse buttton, and it would ruin your hand  and become a chore if you had to play that way, as another reviewer has stated here.  So, if you get past that hardware limitation (having a steam controller), you'll actually have fun playing this game.   Pros  - Simple and fun gameplay - Nice rpg elements (you find eggs that can be used to get random upgrade items) - short and sweet   Cons  - A steam controller is required if you don't want to get a sore hand (and even so holding the same button can be tiresome) - Annoying music (play anything else instead)  Grade:  - With a steam controller: 7/10 - Without it: 5/10

Not quite sure why there hasn't been a whole lot said about this game, but after playing it I can say is is pretty damn good. This isn't your typical fist pumping, oh ♥♥♥♥ i'm a badass storke my ego military shooter. While the controls and combat are pretty decent for a 3rd person shooter, its the story that really makes this game. You get REAL decisions to make at REAL moments, and you see the real effects of things like friendly fire and killings of civilians. Its not a game you are gonna walk away from feeling all happy feely, but in my opinion that's a good thing because It touches on a lot of stuff other games won't and doesn't do it in a gimmicky way for shock value. I think you can take away a commentary on war if you want, but if not its still a pretty fun game to play and it has been really cheap so i'd recommend buying it if you want a decent 3rd person shooter with an interesting story.

One of those 'almost good' games. Here's what i liked and didn't like.  Liked: Graphics Train variety ...... ummm... building's in towns will move to let you place track pretty much anywhere, so that makes things easy  Disliked: okay, lets say you are going to build your own miniature railroad but only have a 2 ft x 2ft area to build in. You could make everything very small scale and fit alot in or, like this game, make everything oversized to rediculous proportions. even the biggest cities are hardly as wide as 4 RR tracks, and one full sized 6 car train can be in 3 towns at once. As you might imagine, this creates some major issues when you have more than one train running. If they had either made the maps larger or the trains and tracks smaller this would have been a much better game.   I bought the 3 pack of Sid Meier's Railroads, RR tycoon 2 & 3. I can't say I'd recommend any of them, and I REALLY like trains.

A well thought-out, fun, sharp feeling and well paced FPS that brings a unique and authentic feel to the old wild west theme. Good artwork and a decent story acted out by convincing actors makes for an enjoyable experience. The audio is also spot on with some great music to get you engrossed in the environment and pumped for action. It's not Crysis69 in terms of visuals but it does have its own aesthetic and it wears that well.  It's definitely worth getting, especially if it's on sale.

I have a habit of writing way too much, so here's a simple list of pros and cons:  Pros: +Really good gameplay, both the stealth and combat +The addition of magical powers, especially blink, manage to spice up the experience +An interesting and pretty original world, personally I just love the setting +Cool looking aesthetic that reminds of an oil painting +Some pretty interesting and well voice-acted characters +A lot of choices tied to the way you play, the world changes accordingly to your actions +A focus on exploration, especially vertical exploration +Requires several playthroughs to experience all the content +On higher difficulty levels it can be quite the challenge for people who want that +If you want to, you can dig into the lore and learn more about the world and the characters  Cons: -The story is ok, but if you play a game for the story, you might end up disappointed, especially by the 'good' ending -You play as a mute with no personality -The game can be pretty short depending on difficulty level and playstyle -There really isn't an amazing variety of enemies  Overall it's an amazing game, and to me the gameplay is what makes this game one of the best stealth games and makes it surprisingly replayable. The DLCs improve on the main game by giving you a cool protagonist, a pretty good story, some amazing areas and new combat and stealth options, allowing you to be even more creative.

Imagine if someone took Prototype, made the plot and narrative tone consistent with the gameplay (which is to say dumb as hell but great because of it) and also made it not control like an enraged cow and you've got Saints Row IV. When you play a sandbox game you want satisfying and precise movement controls, side missions that are actually fun and rewarding, and main missions that are moreso. This game does a pretty good job of delivering all three, though getting Gold ranks on some diversions can be a bit of a slog. It also ran with all graphical options set to medium on my fossil of a desktop without any issues.

If you haven't already noticed cry of fear is a psychological horror FPS based off of the Half-life 1 engine. It has quite impressive visuals for a game running on the Gold Source engine. Its single player has a rich immersive storyline, nothing to new, but it will still draw you in none the less. Its voice acting is pretty decent and the ambiance creates a good setting. Beyond the Dodging and stamina features cry of fear plays like your average shooter; point your gun at enemy, shoot said enemy until dead, repeat on most enemies. The recoil on most guns seem realistic for a young man in the city however certain weapons are much more stable to fire from the hip which kind of makes me question who in Team Psykskallar thought a shotgun would recoil less than your standard handgun? The difficulties offers nothing more than an increase in enemy damage and enemy health respective to the difficulty chosen, meaning that after one play through, the game offers little change to the higher difficulties. The jump scares, whilst scary the first few times, lose their horror factor as you become more conditioned however there are enough jump scares in the game to keep your pants brown. The additional custom campaigns allow you to extend your solo experience however only some are worth a try.  When it comes to the multiplayer Team Psykskallar have yet to fix it, and most likely aren’t. Seems that the use of Hamachi or Point forwarding is the only way you and your friends can Work and survive together. The co-op is fantastic, it’s always fun to leave your friend to fight the enemies as you laugh at their cries for help. Also the Suicide maps provide a fun but short lived extension to the multiplayer modes as they allow you to survive against waves of enemies until the timer runs out. The Initial and Manhunt co-op campaigns are both entertaining but beyond them there are virtually no additional maps either by Team Psykskallar or the community itself.  Overall 7/10. Good horror fps, great for mucking about with a few mates.

A very cool and unique co-op game. More like a sleeper hit. Probably didn't do so well since Gamespy shut down and that is what this game used for multiplayer connection.  You can however get around this since games with LAN support can use a program like Tunngle to continue playing co-op games no longer supported by Gamespy (again, they need to support Local Area Network [LAN]). It may be a little frustrating for users who are not very familiar with networking but once you get the hang of it, it's very easy.  The game is not completely without faults as it will sometimes crash near the end of the level (or the start of a new one) randomly. This seems to happen mostly if you're playing the game for long periods of time.   Since the game can be bought for cheap (right now on a steam sale for $1.64) you really can't go wrong.  The graphics are good, the gameplay plays like a 3rd person hack/slash/shooter. The controls can feel a little wonky (I used keyboard/mouse) and the user interface could be MUCH better but all in all you get used to it and it's fine.

This chick keeps her shirt on the whole time and the guys don’t pressure her to put out. Not only does this take away from the realism (‘cause the libido of anime dudes IS real) but one potential love interest is a shy guy with glasses. His long hair glistened in the sun and this was accentuated by his inability to shower because his hair retained its natural oils. When his baggy bloodshot eyes met mine I looked away of course. app_name: War of the Human Tanks - Limited Operations
review_text: This is only a review around the beginning of the game so I may add more later. Note that I haven't played the second game in this series due to various reasons that I won't even try to explain.  If you've played the first game before, then you know that it's a real(ish) time strategy game with cute human tank chibi's fighting other cute human tank chibi's in the fictional land of Japon. One difference though is the lack of the ability to buy new human tanks (as far as I know) and the use of a limited amount of human tanks you can use in your arsenal.  This kind of makes you want to switch somewhere from being aggressive (which usually causes waste which was fine in the first game since you can always buy more human tanks, but not here) to being a heavily defended turtle of your most valuable units (like Mihari the scout tank as a great example as she can be the key to winning if you know where that long range enemy unit/commander is) but you're also pressed for time to achieve victory so you always have to calculate for risk constantly whenever your units can move. You can always fire blindly into the fog of war the game has and chances are you'll get lucky in scoring a hit. Note that the enemy can also blindfire and get lucky as well.  Graphics: Who cares? I don't for this game.  Music/Sound: Personally, I think it's likely a rehash from the previous games for the majority of what I've played so far, but that's fine.  One thing I currently find annoying is that it seems to be doing the opening animation and credit scene on each beginning and ending of a chapter. I have no idea is there's any changes in each chapter as each five to ten seconds of both of these seem the same.  Now excuse me as I bite my thumb in worry and excitement of how to protect my human tanks as I try to beat this game.

review_text: One quick thing, buy this if you do not own Dark Souls 2. But if you do the upgrade isnt really worth it.  Anyways...
