I've been playing MWO since closed beta, Summer 2012. Four years is a loooong time to be playing any one game, but it's kept me and my crew together. If you want heavy firepower wielding mechs, this is pretty much the best game in town. Less twitchy than other FPS titles so better for older players.   The Good:  Art and Design of the mechs. By far this is the best that battlemechs have ever looked in a video game. Just go look at MW4 and cringe. While a few of the redesigns were a bit of a miss (looking at you WarHammer), the major are unbelievably good (Atlas, Catapult, Cataphract, Summoner) etc. Even o es that never looked that good to being with (Jager mech, Stalker).   Gameplay is solid. The arm+torso movement for aiming is simple brilliant and this is the best mechs have ever handled. Assaults (except Kodiak...) feel heavy and methodical. While something as small as the Locust feels insanely fast and nimble.   Mech customization is damn near infinite. Not very chassis can do what you want, but there's a mech or variant somewhere that CAN. Build variety and the near endless way to build a mech is solid.   Negatives:  Harsh to new players. Much like Eve, opening this game up the first time is less of a learning curve and more of a vertical WALL. There is finally a new player tutorial for driving a mech, but still absolutely nothing for the mechlab. That said, the community is very helpful, and there are many community run Team speak servers avail with people glad and willing to show players the ropes.   Tiny community. MechWarrior just isn't that big anymore. While huge during the 90s, it's very much a niche product today. After 11 or so Pacific, you'll start seeing familiar faces every drop or two. Just not a big enough pool to build matches.   Big fish in little ponds. This game has a 'Pilot Skill Rating.'. (PSR) Basically it's an XP bar that fills to move you up in tiers. Play really poor early on and you'll probably start at Tier 5, and over time you'll move up. Big issue is it is HEAVILY biased to move players up, so you'll hit tier 3 pretty quick. After that you're just food for the Tier 1 elite crowd. They tend to be the players that support the game the most financially buying the pre-order mech packs, so to keep them entertained the game is geared to throw easy targets at them. Unless you run with a crew that WANT to be the best and focus fire and build to the meta, you're going to be rolled a lot at higher tiers.   My crew is a very casual group that play because we love big stompy robots and want to play something that supports 2 to 12 of us at a time. But because the PSR is biased so heavily, most of us are tier 1 or 2, but we're NOT that caliber of players. We've just played enough it shoves us against MS, 228, KCOM, etc. So we get rolled. Small community though so MM is designed to put as many people into the same port as quickly as possible.   Limited game modes. You've got Assault (kill everything or capture their base), Conquest (kill everything and capture enough bases), and Skirmish (kill everything). There's none of the real objective based gameplay that MechWarrior is known for. There was an attempt with community warfare for two years, but it's largely died off because of poor design and developer miss steps.   Glacial development. The last real issue is development is at a snail's pace. The core gameplay really hasn't changed since Alpha. They do have mech creation down to a science, but actual Ballance changes or adjustments are once yearly, at best, with huge imbalances receiving no attention for months at a time.   Overall what you see is what you get with MechWarrior Online. I don't see it changing much in the next two years, but for solid Team Death Match with Robots, it's pretty much unbeatable. Just don't go in expecting the Battletech or MechWarrior heritage for objective based gameplay or more. It's not here

Figure I should get a short review out before Half Genie Hero comes releases since this game rightfully deserves every bit of praise it gets. I could elaborate further, but in short, this game is a must-have for anyone who enjoys Metroidvania games and any fan who loves Shantae.  This is one of those instances where the sequel to a game improves upon every aspect. The story is in my opinion the best story of all Shantae games, involving you to team up with your arch nemesis to defeat an even darker power. You’ll still find the same funny light-hearted dialogue combined with music by legend Jake Kaufman the series is known for. Gameplay is smooth and very fair with an excellent balance of combat, exploration, and puzzles.  I’ve clocked in a little over 6 hours for my first playthrough with 100% completion. Playing the game in the New Game+ option is also very satisfying, because it gives you all your movement abilities to start with, letting you breeze through many segements. I have a few more game hours due to getting the speedrun achievements which aren’t very difficult and I urge others to give it a try. I also own this game on the 3DS and I can pop this game in any day and still have a great time. Even with the release of Half Genie Hero, which I've already completed, I still put Pirate's Curse as the #1 Shantae game out there, but it is extremely close. There is still content to come for Half Genie Hero so this opinion could change at any time.

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Buaw-s-GzfM   This is a summary of my review video, which I encourage you to watch if you are looking for a more in-depth review.   Party Hard is a 2d pixel top down Slasher game, where you use stealth and guile to complete your mission. The music is decent, but a lot of the songs sounded similar.   Gameplay;  There is a sense of progression, as new characters and options open up to you as you forge through the game. The game is fairly simplistic, and uses a point system. This would have been ideal to have a leader-board, but unfortunately you can only compete with yourself. Your character attempts to navigate a map and kill all of the party-goers without being seen. You are able to sneak around and hide bodies, but the stealth aspect is hampered by a lack of places for the character to hide, such as in the shadows or in a closet. Killings are done on a line of sight basis, which can be interesting, as you are able to pin killings on others if they are the first seen by the body. Stealth becomes more of a puzzle strategy, in figuring out how to kill everyone without getting caught.  Story;  The story is told in a third person manner, listening to a cop talk about the killings. Overall I enjoyed the story, and wanted to find out what was going to happen. The story gives the game a cops and robbers feel, which makes the conquest of completing levels even more sweet.   Summary;  The game is not perfect, it does have its' suspension of disbelief issues, such as the zombies that run around and need to be killed, but the cops don't attack them. It is a basic hide and seek game, with a line of sight kill mechanic and a puzzle feel, rather than a pure stealth game or slasher. The gameplay was decent, however, and though it could be rather repetitive, I enjoyed it well enough. I can recommend this game, though just barely... The Score:  Gameplay: 9/10 (improves a lot, adds more fluidity) Story: 7/10 (Good but short) Graphics: 7/10 (The white gradient prevents from being 9) Setting: 7/10 (Rome is realistic but too much of the same, several cities is better) Innovation: 8/10 (several new modes are added to the game)  Overall: 7.6   If you loved AC2 buy this one but expect more of an expansion than a really fresh game.

How to make stir fry;   Ingredients:  -2 tablespoons of vegetable oil -1 red bell pepper, matchstick style -2 carrots, sliced thin af -1 1/2 cups of fresh broccoli -1 pound of beef sirloin cut into 2' strips -1 green onion, chop that thing -1 teaspoon on minced garlic -2 tablespoons of sesame seeds, toasted -2 tablespoons of soy sauce  Directions:  Heat vegetable oil in a large skillet over med-high heat; cook and stir the beeg until browned - that takes like 3-4 minutes usually. Move the beef to the side and add the broccoli, bell pepper, carrots, green onion, and garlic into the center of the work station. Cook the vegetables for 2 minutes, then stir the beef into the vegetables and season it with soy sauce and sesame seeds. Cook and stir until the vegetables are tender, should take around 2 minutes.

app_name: STAR WARS™ Jedi Knight II: Jedi Outcast™
review_text: I haven't played Jedi Academy yet, but I can safely say that Jedi Outcast is superior to Dark Forces and Jedi Knight (and far, far superior to Mysteries of the Sith). It has the same fun combat (with a lightsaber, many other Star Wars weapons, and Force powers) that made Jedi Knight such a good game, and it this time it has 21st century graphics, legitimately good voice acting, and a stronger story. It's in the period when Luke's Jedi were headquartered on Yavin 4, and it pits Kyle Katarn against a really great set of new villains while sending him to a lot of iconic Star Wars locations. Unfortunately, the platforming which has been my bane throughout the series is still here. If you love platforming, that will be a plus, but for me, it's the one thing I don't like about this game. (Of course, you can save at any time, so at least it's manageable). I'd say this is the third-best Star Wars shooter ever made (right after Battlefront II and Republic Commando). If you are a Star Wars fan (especially an Expanded Universe lover like me), and/or enjoyed previous games in the Dark Forces/ Jedi Knight series, I recommend this game. If you're interested in the Jedi Knight games but don't want to play something with extremely dated graphics, then this is my recommended jumping-in point.

I don't really know how to describe this game, other than to say it's an interactive story with some light gameplay in it. However, I don't mean that in a bad way, as it's a very good format to tell a story.  The story follows two doctors working in a company that grants its clients (who are all people on their deathbeds) a last wish of sorts before they die: they rewrite their memories so they can accomplish whatever they dreamed of; in the case of the game, the client is a man who always dreamed of going to the Moon, hence the title of the game. The story is very good, and emotional; and I'd say the game it's worth it for the story alone. Still, I'd make a disservice to the devs, as they managed to craft a very good game not only storywise, but in aesthethics and overall tone. The traditional RPG style fits the game pretty well, and the soundtrack is very good as well.  As far as gameplay goes, as I said, it has light gameplay; there won't be a lot of fighting or anything like that; only going around interacting with certain objects, and having dialogue with different NPCs. Even then, it's entertaining enough, and the gameplay works to enhance the story.  After the main story, there's two extra DLC chapters (which can be downloaded for free), which show a bit more of the company the doctors work for; and it's set during Christmas, after the events of the main game. The tone remains somewhat similar to that of the main game, although there's a few interesting twists (particularly in the second chapter).  Well, I don't think I can say much more about this game, other than I recommend it.

A review for older gamers: IF YOU LOVED URKEL, THIS GAME MIGHT BE FOR YOU!  Remember Urkel from TV? His strange voice always sounded forced and unnatural. Like it was 'supposed' to be funny. That is what much of the voice acting is like in this game: Urkel.  Auto-save is highly flawed. Nuff said. Also don't choose the food-effect-doubling trait; that is broken (but still in the game for you to waste your precious points on). You can't pause the game, even with some quests and effects being timed. On the other hand, if you fail a timed quest because your phone rang, at least there is Auto-save. Oh, wait....  There are just enough mobs at each level to get you to the next level, so any replayability comes from using different classes or tactics next time you take on exactly the same mobs & quests in exactly the same order. This makes the game outrageously priced at $40 imho, especially when the soundtrack gets 4/10. I hope you like drab classical piano, or flute-&-lute music from a Renaissance Festival! Very little of it is up-beat outside of the tavern.   My male toon is a ranger, but looks more like a former pro wrestler who took radioactive steroids for many, many years. His arms and chest are so over-the-top that even his elbow is wider than my female's waist, and there is no option to adjust either. If Urkel had a dream where he was a huge hulking gladiator champion, this is what my *ranger* looks like! You think Arnold is big? Pffft! That's nothing, this is MUTANT URKEL,THE DESTROYER!!!!, light on his feet, and quick with a bow. :-/  Divinity gives you LOTS of text to read from NPCs. The writing is totally meh. Long paragraphs of text would be cool if they came from rich, engaging characters, but half of Divinity's writing is just redundant over-bloated schlock which I can't skip past because I might miss that one important tip. So I read it all. Meh. Meh. Meh. ...and yet more redundant over-bloated meh.  At level 2, I am offered the chance to join an organization and work my way up. I pass on that quest. Doesn't sound like the path I want to take. Turning down quests in this linear game (even just one) isn't a good idea, especially if you don't take the Talk With Animals trait, because apparently the animals offer quests in this game, but only to those who speak 'Animal.'   Still level 2, I'm given a mission (main storyline) to prevent    Time from ending. The Weaver of Time (who hasn't spoken to anyone in all of eternity) has a nice little chat with me, of course. All worlds will end    unless I    restore the cosmic balance of the universe,    because, you know, that's just the kind of thing that only a level 2 hero could possibly set straight... just please pardon the water bucket I'm wearing on my head, it's just that I haven't been able to find a real helmet you see, but yeah, I'm The Chosen One, I guess. Why not? Even if that other organization wanted to start me out on the bottom rung just five minutes ago, I'm clearly the best choice to solve the biggest problem in the history of ever. Urkel (weaing an upside-down water bucket on his head) would be right at home in this travesty. Truly. Now imagine this painful scene overlayed with a somber classical piano score endlessly slogging into oblivion, and you start to see why I give this game a thumbs down rating. It desperately tries to be both silly and compellingly serious at the same time, but inspires only a certain longing to be in a dentist's chair, huffing nitrous oxide, forcing myself to think about Katy Perry while the sound of a drill drones on endlessly, and I stoically try to convince myself that my sweat is from the hot sun as Katy and I stroll down the beach, hand-in-hand, talking about penguins. I'd pay money to experience only one of these two ordeals again. I'll let you guess which one.  Crafting: are you really supposed to discover all on your own, that you can split a stack of hair and craft something by putting the stack back together? Hair + hair = thread. How are you supposed to ever figure that out on your own when the game automatically stacks duplicate items for you? I like games that don't hold my hand, but come on!! Why isn't that in the tutorial? Also, the crafting isn't rich and rewarding. Cook iron ore in the *food oven* (!) once, and it is an iron bar, twice and it becomes a steel bar?!! Steel shouldn't just be twice-baked iron. You should at least have to add a little lemon zest or something! And while I'm at it, in a game where hair is actually used in crafting, and where pie is craftable, why can't you craft hair pie? Come on! I guess we don't even give an indirect nod to adult humor in Urkel's House of Good Clean Fun.  Inventory: when you mouseover an item in your bag, a box pops up showing details. An *opaque* box. A big black unadjustable opaque box that blocks out the other items in your bag. When I'm looking for one specific item among 70, I like to use my cursor and let my eyes follow. Don't try that in this game; it makes you want to stick knitting needles through your nipples, twist them 12 times, then let go and run around the room pretending to be a turboprop airplane! Oh wait, you HAVE to do that in game, cuz that's how you craft: by dragging one item in your inventory on top of another item in your inventory. Hold it! Where's the second item? I can't see it cuz of that opaque info box!! ARRRGGHHHH!!!! Where are my knitting needles?! (This WILL happen to you if you play this game, as surely as my name is Bullwinkle!)  At level 5, I entered The_Level_5_Dungeon (it's what you do). To progress to the second level, I needed to find a secret. After about 10 minutes, I figured that I needed to do X. And in fact doing X does reveal the secret for most people, but either I had my camera pointing in the wrong direction, or too far zoomed in, or maybe I looked away from the monitor during the critical 0.8 seconds, because I never saw the secret get revealed. So for me, doing X seemed to have no effect, and you can't do X twice. Its not like pulling a lever that has 2 interchangeable positions; No, you can only do X once. So I just kept searching for the secret that allows you to progress down to the next level. No idea what it is, but I'll just keep searching for ... something, somehwere. It is so well hidden, it blends in so well, that I'm embarassed to tell you how long I kept searching before breaking down and going to the wiki. 2 HOURS! Unacceptable! A better computer game would be prepared for what an idiot I can be!  This game is too linear, has unsatisfying crafting and weak inventory management. The writing contains as much sincerity and insight as one might get from a junior high school girl raised in the Mojave Desert attempting to weave a psychological thriller about a crew of old fishermen in the Baltic who decide to take up rapping, calling themselves The Home Buoys. And many of the voice actors, just like Urkel, try wayyy too hard, winding up smack dab in downtown Phonytown, corner of Sponge & Bob. I have never enjoyed badly-written novels, Urkel and neither plodding somber classical piano music nor flute-&-lute Renaissance music, but when fused into one ensemble, I must admit that they come together like a lengthy Shakespearian play performed underwater by a troupe of snooty gay French mimes (sorry if that last bit was redundant). That is to say: in too many ways, the over all experience is just painful, slow, insincere and over-acted. There should be more to RPGs than enjoyable combat and pretty graphics (which it does have, btw). Amazing potential unrealized. 6/10.  p.s. If you're under 21 (or live in Texas), kindly disregard this review, 'cuz the game has enjoyable combat and pretty graphics! Besides ... who's 'Urkel?' 8.5/10

First of all, this game is depressing.  It puts you in the boots of a poor paper pusher at a border-checkpoint of a dictatorship(ed?) country .   While you are struggeling to keep your family fed and warm, you are deciding over human lives in a always shorter timeframe. Why this? The faster you check the immigration papers of people seeking refuge, the more money you earn each day. But if you make mistakes you can loose money as well. This gets harder every day as the administration releases more and more rules and admittances for you to check. At the same time the game throws you into some moral decisions, where there is no right or wrong. Do I turn down the human-trafficer, although his papers are ok? This could lead to a fine and I could not afford the medicine for my son. Do I take the bribe from the nice man claiming to smuggle medicin for his sick wife?  As I said... this game is depressing. Would I recommend you to buy and play it?  Yes!   Because this game manages something only few games manage to do. To get you involved and question your own morality.  And even if you do not get involved in this depressing atmosphere. The game is still a very good reaction-puzzle-like game that needs you to figure out where the small mistake in the forged immigration documents in front of you is.

I picked this game up because I was craving for some old-fashioned 3D platformer, and I approached it without any knowledge about the associated show.  On its own, the game is pretty decent and surprisingly well-made, but with certain drawbacks that take away some fun from the experience.   Pros:  * The environments are a joy to look at. They are crafted with love and care, and I found myself being impressed by how much of the classic Pac-Man's imagery they managed to cram into the visuals.  * The controls are good, for the most part. They flow nicely and work just as they should.  * The game provides a variety of different powerups that nicely spice things up.  * Chomping down on all those ghosts is pretty damn fun.  * The overall design of levels (gameplay-wise) is decent.   Cons:  * Very often the game can get a sudden spike in difficulty just because of unfortunately placed bottomless pits that kill you instantly. As a result, the very first boss fight is shockingly difficult, but all the later ones are laughably easy. It's made all the worse by the fact that if you do die, you tend to go back a considerable distance. On the flip side, when you're not under the danger of falling into bottomless pits, you usually breeze through the game with little trouble.  * The camera, while usually not an issue, can be a little annoying in that, for whatever reason, it moves a little jumpily and doesn't let you look up.  * The voices are so annoying that I turned them off completely after just the first level. Couldn't they have just made them talk in Simlish? Okay, I get that it's probably because of being based on the show, but still...  * The last two worlds are just lazy rehashes of the previously visited worlds.  * The ending is rather disappointing.  * Many of the powerups seem under-utilized. Some of them appear only for a handful of levels, only to be forgotten entirely for the duration of whole worlds, and only briefly reappear at the very end.  * The story is a really lazy plot of the 'villain XYZ is trying to take over the world, stop him' kind.  The conclusion is that the game is a commendable effort with various things to like, with really lovely looking environments and many good ideas for gameplay, but hampered by certain shortcomings (most notably the difficulty, voices and uninteresting story) that prevent it from becoming a truly unmissable gem. As it is, it's a nice game that's worth checking out, but nothing magnificent.  If you are going to get it, however, then do it only if it's on at least 50% discount. The full price is an outrage.

Set during the tail end of the Great Depression, White Night is a stylistic thriller where the contrasts of light and dark are more than just window dressing. Playing very much like Amnesia: The Dark Descent, but with the camera and controls of the early Resident Evil games, White Night begins when you crash your car after swerving to miss a woman in the road while coming home from the bar one night. You follow the woman into a nearby mansion in search help, only to become sucked into a mystery whose secrets are as dark as the night itself.  Say what you will about the Resident Evil style controls, while the've always had their flaws, White Night seems to recognize this and does a decent job of making sure that changing camera angles don't hamper movement too much. Most of the game involves your searching for keys and other items needed to progress through the mansion while reading various diaries and newspaper articles that advance the narrative along the way. The puzzles for the most part are simple enough given either a little bit of thought or a little trial and error and the mechanics of light and shadow that often accompany them never feel gimicky or too out of place. The enemies are usually just another puzzle themselves, living obstacles that can be vanquished by the light. Visually the game's high contrast style is executed perfectly, though I felt the character models were lower poly than they could have been. The anti-aliasing could have been stronger also, as the high contrast really highlights the effect.  The sound is excellent, from the music to the foley, creating a consistent mood thoughout. The voice acting as well is excellent, though the dialog and portions of the writing suffer a bit. This may just be due to English not being the developer's native language however. In addition to the narrative of the protagonist, the woman, and the mansion's owners, there also seems to be some attempt at subtext or metaphor regarding capitalism and the downfall of the American dream, but it gets lost in the mix and regardless of its Gatsby-esque intentions, feels a little half-formed.  If you're a fan of narrative adventure games like Amnesia, Gone Home, or Among the Sleep and you're looking for something similar with lots of style and a noir setting, then I definitely recommend White Night. It has a few flaws here and there, but its nothing that detracted from my enjoyment of the game.

(Side Mission) My instructions were clear. Stop the army of maids from uprising. They wouldn't go down without a fight. Several dented serving trays and torn frilly dresses later I come out bruised, topless and with a maids headress in one hand and a serving tray with a head shaped dent in it in the other.   But in all seriousness. Akiba's Trip ,if you can look past the flashing lights advertising the fan service, has some great story to tell with various character routes. The combat is straight forward, engaging and as fan servicey as it gets. Customisation is very abundant, featuring editing systems for most of the games environment. On game envrionment, the game does a great job creating a lively and (as sources would tell me) realistic virtual Akihabara filled with anime culture. This game radiates all that is modern anime culture. The replayability is high with much content to keep playing on to achieve.  I'm glad Akiba's trip came to Steam otherwise I would have missed out this great game.  Unfortunately being a port from console there have been plently of technical issues and as of now random crashing is still about and that is for the people that could get the game to start and run on its odd system requirements.   Akiba(s)trip was very pleasing and I would recommend it especially to anime fans. It's worth saying that you will get funny looks and concerned Steam messages upon buying/playing this game as if you have bought hentai so be weary of your Steam reputation. (though there is no nudity in the game).

I don't know which one has been the worst game i played through so far in my life but this is pretty damn bad, even on my standards.  First of all the game liked to crash while launching every single time approx 4-7 times before launching. When i first actually launched the game i saw message stating follow another characted called Diaz and Diaz was a floating gun in midair and nothing more, enemies were invisible as well which (i don't think i need to even say this) was pretty damn difficult to get the game actually started... I checked some forums and found out that the game needs to run as DX11 so i started the game once more and checked the optionmenu and the game had DX9 as default. Well this fixed the graphical problems luckily for now. If DX9 causes so much problems, maybe the game should run a DX11 as default, or after several complaints they could've fixed the problems with DX9.  Anyhow.... I played through Act 1 and thought i'd give it a rest for the day and get some sleep. Next day i started up the game once more and continue on Act 2, only to notice the complete lack on audio, except on the cutscenes. (Also apparently a very common problem which you might be able to fix or then you can't... People have asked for patch to fix this from the beginning, no answer or patch/fix from the developers so far.)  For some reason the game itself likes to swap between windowed and fullscreen mode whenever it wants and throw the ingame menu-cursor in the game making the game unplayable (Only way to get rid of this problem is to reboot the game, and if you're in a place where you haven't yet reached the next checkpoint and you're playing on expert, you're going to hate these situations.  One last gamebreaking/pantsripping/ragequitting situation in the game itself was on Act 3 when you were supposed to snipe out the helicopter pilot. The game itself ran perfectly on maxed out graphics (Was also on default) before this point. When you were supposed to take out the copter the FPS drops to unbearable levels. Well then it's onto options, changing the settings, reboot and take out the helicopter pilot.  Finally i think i can talk about the game itself. The graphics look quite nice actually, eventhough you are looking everything through the sniperscore mostly. Audio.. well i can't say too much about it since they didn't work after first act. In Act 1 they we're quite pleasant eventhought the guns were far from realistic.  Story is pretty generic for a FPS and especially for the sniper series. You have a baddie, and your job is to end his life.   I was playing the game on expert and i have to say one other thing i actually liked about the game is the 'realistic' sniping system where you have to take notice to the wind and distance if you want to hit anything. What i wasn't extremely keen on though was the A.I, (If you're in a ghillie suit 3 km/2 mile away from the enemy, lying in a bushes and take a shot towards an enemy who is facing his back towards you and can't even see any gunflash or whatever its called, you happen to make a miss, he will absolutely screw you over by turning around, kill you in oneshot with a pistol or any gun he's having. It's just ridiculously difficult from time to time to continue in this game especially with some maps and huge distances between checkpoints.  That's all said and done, I really can't suggest this particular game to anyone before it has been patched and fixed.

There are many who after a generous amount of hours have still downvoted the game, I suppose to each his own. However I have played this game since the day the alpha came out and this game has seen a drastic amount of changes, largely based on what the players said they wanted. They are still willing to work with the customer. Though like most alpha's, this game had to go thorugh a vigorous 'test and see' phase where the devolpers had to see what worked and what didn't along with balencing and all that jazz. That led to a myriad of bugs, glitches, and of course lag. Which also led to very angry people and in the game community, endless whining.  That being said what they are working now is seems to be more of an arena based shooter that does reward teamwork and communication, then it does a MMO. I still think that it does the warhammer universe a decent amount of justice and it sure is fun as hell to play. Of course I have been angry at moments but overall it has been a very satisfying to play as any of the 4 factions and rip the other to shreads. Obviously there are still elements that need to be addressed like OP weapons and certain classes, but if you play smart, sneaky, or ambush or ANYTHING besides runing in like an idiot headfirst... then you can do pretty well. Teamwork is still the best option though and there are plenty of options in game whether it is a mix of classes or a cordinated assault with tanks and rhino's all at the same time, working together will help you all. To either crush foul xenos or sate your bloodlust in honor of the dark gods, or even slaughter Tyranids by the thousands... the choice, is yours.

This port is not as bad as people say. Yes, you can't change the controls, but they are sufficient controls as is. You can use a mouse since you are on PC, therefore, you will have a much easier time aiming. Especially useful if you play a Scout (can start with a sniper rifle.)  The graphics are better on PC. This is a bit of a dated game, but the textures are really all that need some improvement.  The story is very tailored to 3-person co-op, but is okay for a Transformers game...let's be realistic here, Transformers is not about a good story. I love the tone, though.  Co-op is extremely fun, btw, but...  ***MULTIPLAYER! In no other game can I do the kind of stuff that I do in War for Cybertron!   Play a car, sharp turn around a corner, kill someone with your car guns, transform, still moving forward from the momentum, shoot people while transforming, jump, smack someone in the air, come back down, probably still sliding, switch weapons and snipe someone, turn invisible, pick up someones dropped minigun -- you can steal other classes weapons -- use a minigun while cloaked.   All in the span of about 15 to 40 seconds if you're good!!! There are few other games that I can think of where you can so fluidly combine what you're doing. Super Mario Brothers 64 sorta does, but that's single-player on a console.  ***No*** other game I have ever played has multiplayer as frenetic as this. I can't keep up 50% to 70% of the time. UT2004 wishes it came close.  True, the matchmaking could be a bit better, there isn't the best player base in the world, you need to make sure people have a decent connection, not many people play anymore, but people still do play. It's worth the gamble. The multiplayer is just ***that*** good.  I vote that we get a strong player-base going for this game. The multiplayer gameplay is seriously amazing.  I would almost buy this game full-price *just* for the multiplayer.

Quake was the first game I was ever really addicted to and focused a big portion of my time on for months or years at a time. Counter-Strike was the second - and easily more addicting. There's a reason that I still compare every multi-player shooter experience back to original Counter-Strike, to this day. There's a reason I still compare every server community to this (Counter-Strike servers each had their own community and personality and people truly got to know each other back in the day, compared to these days, where a server is just something you're automatically dumped into and feels like every other server).  Despite many iterations, nothing quite plays like the original. It's not hard to see why there are still 10,000 to 60,000 people playing this  at any one time on any day , fifteen years after launch and why it is always in the top ten games currently being played on all of Steam.  This is where it all started. Without this, there's an entire genre of shooter and multi-player that just vanishes. The original days of Counter-Strike remain the most enjoyable gaming experiences of my entire life. It's unlikely that will ever be trumped.

Sequel to one of my personal favorite horror games and while it's not as spooky, it's definitely just as fun as the original F.E.A.R. and it gives way more backstory to Alma and Pointman than the original. This game is absolutely worth the purchase if you enjoyed the first FEAR and have yet to play this.  The story starts about a half hour before the ending of the first F.E.A.R. and you play a different soldier in the First Encounter Assault Recon taskforce. When the large scale explosion happens, your character is nearly dead and has lost a lot of blood. You wind up in a hospital, having been given Pointman's blood, and now you have his special skill of enhanced combat reflexes and can essentially slow down time to a crawl, to get the upper hand on your enemies. Pretty quickly, things get spooky, when you wake up in a hospital and no one is around. Things begin to go bump -- presumably in the night -- and it's time to arm yourself with all sorts of awesome firearms. The shooting is more intense than its predecessor and with way more fun weapons. The story is just as captivating, only this one has a way better ending that will leave your jaw on the floor and your eyes agape.  + Graphics still hold strong today + Intense action + Great horror experience + Lots of subtle homages to horror + Alma returns + Fantastic story + More lore + Rocking soundtrack + Legit story reasons for a new character to have Pointman's abilities + Mech suits + More awesome weapons + Enemy AI actually seem intelligent, for the most part  - Invisible enemies - Ghosts - Creepy school - Creepy hospital - Multiplayer has been removed - 'Horror needs a cliffhanger ending'cliche

Hotline Miami is one of the coolest IPs to be developed in a long time. The game is absolutely worth buying at any price and features one of the best soundtracks I've ever heard. The game is crazy psychotic and as you play you kind of feel the same way after a long sitting. It is a fast paced tactical action game that almost feels like a puzzle game. A lot of trial and error and boy do you feel it when you screw up. The brutality isn't actually softened by the pixel graphics, it is amplified. My only tips for playing the game are play it with the mouse and keyboard for precision but most importantly, play it loud. Really loud.

Hotline Miami is a brutal game when it comes to violence and gore, but you cant take the violence seriously and thats a good thing because Hotline Miami is a game than doesn't take itself to seriously. You start out getting a phone call that tells you that something needs to be done, it may be babysitting or picking up your cake from the cakestore but its just a front for 'Time to kill.' And you find your way out of your appartment to go to another location where you need to kill everyone in a brutal fasion. The graphics is pleasant to view and the music is really really good, capturing a 80's vibe all around. The difficulty vary from its challenging but fun to how am I supposed to do this? But when you manage to get through a level with chainkills and get rewarded with a good grade its totally worth it. Recommend it for everyone that want something a little different.

A game that starts out pretty fun, but I ran into several irreconcilable problems very early on.  The first is that the game is extremely unauthentic as far as how game development really works, which is a big problem for a game whose sole purpose is to replicate the feel of game development. In a world where companies like EA or Ubisoft crank out a game with a similar engine nearly every year and receive excellent ratings and earn money hand-over-fist, it makes no sense for this game to suggest that such a strategy doesn't work. Instead, you will be forced to create several completely unrelated games in between each iteration of a particular franchise.  For example, when I created my first 'franchise' (Ancient Astronaut Theories), it received somewhat positive acclaim and did marginally well in sales. A report analysis of the game showed that I had discovered a 'great combination' of genre and setting. When I attempted to release a second game with the same genre and setting, the game gave me artificially lower critical reviews and lower sales because I 'released them too close to one another', even though I'd staggered their releases by nearly a year. Five Nights at Freddy can release games within months of each other and make tons of cash, but I couldn't do the same in this game without making several completely unrelated titles in completely different genres and settings first? That's idiotic. It's artificial difficulty, meant to force you to create lots of games with lots of different settings so that you can't spam one good combination straight to victory. Yet in the real industry, that's exactly how it works, so why shouldn't it work here too?  The second core problem is that many aspects of game design that were fairly basic even back in the day this is meant to take place are locked behind a 'leveling system' and 'research' mechanics. In order to improve your games, you have to make more games, which levels you up to a point where other options for games become available. This feels particularly silly and exists mainly to stall out the process of developing better games until you obtain a particular level. It's more artificial difficulty, and it feels ridiculous. It gets worse when you consider that some upgrades, including some very basic tenets of game development, are unlocked ONLY by surviving until an arbitrary point in the game. For example, you can't create formal 'sequels' to previous games until midway through Year 8 in the game. Are you telling me that nobody in the industry came up with the idea of sequels until 8 years into the game market? What absolute tripe! Similarly, you can't make a game that covers multiple genres until Year 12, and you can't go multi-platform until Year 17. This all stinks of more padding to artificially inflate the difficulty of the game and extend its play length.  I played for a bit over an hour before just getting tired of it. My company was doing well and I probably could have continued on and 'beaten' the game, but it all feels so artificial that I had no immersion whatsoever. I didn't care any more. It became less about fun and more a matter of how long I wanted to deal with the tedium. And when I'm running into that wall before I even hit double digits in the number of hours played, that's not a good sign.  All in all, a neat idea that's been inflated far too much with artificial 'difficulty' that utterly spoils any sense of realism that the game might have clung to. You have very limited control of your company and what direction you want to take, and everything interesting is locked behind a play wall or a level wall, inaccessible until you slog through the early game. It's just too much tedium to get to the 'good stuff'.

Alright, serious review:  You may think this game is dumb, but dammit, its fun for what it is. The devs are always adding more content, and there has been 2 major updates since release that were given for free which nearly doubled the in-game content each time. As for the paid DLC, that's worth it if you enjoy playing this.  This game is great for achievement hunting, finding secrets hidden throughout the levels, having a laugh, etc. It even has split-screen multiplayer to cause even more chaos with your friends!  Once you find everything, the replayability is meh, but the devs may have another update for it by the time you finish what is here already, plus Steam workshop support for player-made levels, which somewhat lack in quality, but are really fun if you find good ones.  So give this game a try, its a great physics sandbox, probably the second best on Steam (after GMod of course)

So what do you do on an universe that is infinite and uncaring, where your presence does not really matter? Whatever you do with your life right now, that is life.  I have think about this and I figure the reason why the game falls short is because there is no antagonist. Sure some enemies will attack you but it does not feel personal at all. If you carry valuables space pirates may attack you, but you never get to see who or what they are, and again falls short of a personal conflict.   You don't get to be a hero   cause there is no conflict, and no drive to action, and your actions are ultimately meaningless. Your existence has no impact for any other being in the whole galaxy... just like real life.   Another place where it falls short: should you die; you just respawn. This takes all the stakes away, as death is merely a detour or an inconvenience. A basic bad design desicion that really comes down like a hammer on this game.  Well 'dude' clearly you hated the game. No, surprisingly enough I like it well enough and would recomend it to  CERTAIN  people:   'Where the game got it right':  The game does not hold your hand, in a way this is very advanced gameplay and places a lot of faith in the user. Your journey is what you make of it, you can not 'win'/'end' the game and narrative has to be earned through exploration, this is maybe the only game where the play time advances the narrative and not the other way around. to put it simply; It is conceptually the oposite of a 'noir' and as far detached of an RPG as something can possibly be.   I enjoy the sense of emptyness and uselessness (but I am effed in the head, so take that with a grain of salt) i know, in this day and age where we are used to be connected 24/7 and where every game has multiplayer there is this game that puts you squarely in a place of solitude, and forces you to examine what is the meaning of its own existence. also the calm atmosphere of the planets does a lot to relax me.   you can stop reading here, I wanted to address some common complaints i've heard.    I heard people complain about storage space, in 15 hours I had 25-30 slots on my backpack (exo suit) and my ship was not too great, but I made the choice to update primarily my backpack and use my ship as more of a secondary tool.  I heard about lack of variety 'is the same planet with different color' not true many things come into play some planets have a vibrant subterranean life but dull exterior, weather, and yes sentinel force all come into play to shape planets differently. I heard about people giving up because the 3 alien races. it makes sense after you read about 100 monolits for each race (again this game places  A LOT of faith  on you)

Four hours in. i'm having fun running across three planets i've named after my favourite flat bread. a few UI niggles but no performance issues (970/i5-6600) and i'm going to spend a lot of time just paffing around.

app_name: Call of Duty: Black Ops III
review_text: Campaign Pros: + Fun campaign with a co-op choice + Very good voice acting + Nice weapon and cybernetic upgrade system + Standalone zombies campaign with a better story than the original + Amazing sound track  Cons: - Poor script writing (lots of silly one liners that happen when you are fighting the enemies, and they keep repeating until you move on) - Story was not as great as the black ops 1 and 2 campaigns.  Multiplayer Pros: + Gameplay mechanics feel diffrent than the previous cod games with new learning curves (thrust jumps, wall running). + Weapon balancing is great and there is a great variety in weapon choice in lobbies + The specialist system adds a lot more variety to the game making the multiplayer alot more fun + Paintshop and emblem creators allow for the people who have the skill, to be able to show off their designs and the paint shop lets you make your own camos that you can have in game + All the maps utilize the new mechanics with wall running and thrust jumping opportunities everywhere + Lots of challenges in the game that make you want to continue to play the multiplayer ( prestiging, camos , calling cards) + The free run mode allows for begginers to learn about the new game mechanics  + Lots of the bugs have been hammered out since launch and the game runs perfectly on my pc (you can literally tweet pcdev on twitter with your issues and they will get it fixed asap) + Lots of in game content avaivable in the black market like gestures, camos, calling cards, custom reticles, decals, and weapons (yes, I know right now the supply drops system sucks due to cod points but other than the weapons in supply drops thing, all the gestues and camos make you want to grind the game so you can get it) + Good support for the game post launch like dlc map packs and the release of mod tools soon  Cons: - Due to the small PC player base it is hard to find matches for game modes like demolition or free for all. - Some of the maps have really poor map design with lanes that blatantly tell you this is a wall running spot instead of it being integrated with the map - SUPPLY DROPS   Zombies Pros: + Very interesting setting for a zombies game feeling completly seprate from the multiplayer and the campaign. + Much more complex mechanics (pack a punch rituals, sword upgrade quests and the easter egg in general) + Interesting addition to the zombies storyline + Weapon customizing adds more re play value as one game you could only level up a pistol while another game you can level up your  + Gobble gums allow for more complex gameplay (it just isnt buy all the perks and you are set, you can also buy gobblegums) + Good voice acting from all the characters (The game has Jeff Goldblum in it, need I say more)  Cons: - No cons here.   Final Verdict: This game is definitly worth buying and you should even get the season pass while you are at it, the sheer content available in the game is definitly worth the money and mod tools are releasing soon so this game will definitly still be played for years to come. Don't judge the game by its title, this game is a blast and I enjoyed every minute of it.  9/10

Just finished this game and I admit it made me understand why the 'Arkham' series of Batman games is so popular. The game has very nice and well-balanced mix of combat, puzzle solving and platforming. Lots of effort was put into all mechanics and as a result the player never gets bored with one part or another due to constant variation. Mechanically the game is very solid and it does give you a variety of things to learn in order to be able to progress without getting too overwhelming. All new tricks are introduced at a reasonable pace and most are explained well enough (also thanks to the fact that you are immediately forced to use the new unlocked gadgets in order to progress forward). The game is not entirely open-world and gives you a number of 'hubs' or areas to explore and act in while unlocking them one after another. This makes the experience a bit more linear than some might like. Although, once again, the level design is really good and it will make your exploration enjoyable throughout the whole title.  The voice-acting is very well done and so are the cutscenes. The lore obviously adds a lot of charm to the game with lots of nice little details like interviews (voiced dialogues telling about the characters' past) and biographies to unlock and collect. The in-game graphics still look pretty good although some textures and objects do look at little bit outdated (i.e. low-resolution). Some tweaking with the usage of Steam forums might also be required in order to make the not-entirely-optimal port run at maximum resolution and with maximized visual effects even on reasonably modern systems. The story is reasonably entertaining and holds the game together well to make it interesting for the player. The main questions the game asks (as its name states) are those of sanity and madness and how one can slide from the former into the latter while also asking the player whether the criminals can be considered as 'ill' and/or 'uncurable'. Obviously, the aesthetics of the game is somewhat grim although not without some jokes inbetween (if you like the gags Joker produces). One (non-crucial) problem consists in the fact that one can not access all in-game secrets that one detects immediately (special gadgets that unlock later are required for this). Thus I have spent some time in vain wondering whether I could not find the right approach to the secret before I gave in and Internet told me those secrets can only be accessed later on in the game. The DLCs barely add anything to the main game as they do the same thing as other in-game stealth and arena combat pieces only in new locations. So if you are dying to do more combat/stealth, they can serve as some additional fun, but otherwise they are nothing really interesting. Same goes for the challenges, which basically repeat the in-game locations for arena combat and stealth missions with slight variations (enemy composition and/or additional environmental restrictions). While all of them can serve to improve your in-game stealth and combat skills, the main game learning cruve is so nicely adjusted that it is quite enough to just play the 'Normal' difficulty mode continuously to gradually learn and use everything that might be necessary to beat the game. Overall, if you want to start off with the first Batman game that made the whole series so famous, you will not be disappointed and enjoy your time. I heartily recommend this title.

This is not a finished product. At 5 bucks, it looked cool, so I took a chance. I have played up to 15/84 in the 'Journey' and most of it is trivial tutorial stuff, but some of it is very poorly presented and it takes way too long to figure out what you are supposed to do, even though it should be easy.   Right now, I can't recommend this game. But with some clean up, properly written tutorial, and better controls (or option to set key bindings), it could be interesting. I just don't have the patience to play it further while it is so clunky.  The 'focus' level, for example, was very confusingly presented. Without zoom out or pan screen (which come later in the tutorial), you are bound to lose track of where the cursor is in relation to the 'maze' and you will never figure out where you are. It also wasn't explained why the draw tool did not start working until you got the cursor into the yellow box. I just figured that out with trial and error.  One of the problems is all the poor English in the tutorial. Whoever wrote this obviously has a very poor grasp of the English language, and the phrasing is very awkward. He needs some help from a good English writer.  Now for a few nitpicks. The key bindings are horrible. The arrow keys rotate in 3D space, but the num-pad arrow keys pan the screen. Using the num pad just sucks. Also, the screen pan is WAY too fast. WASD should rotate in space, right click-and-hold should pan screen, and the mouse zoom should zoom in and out (currently done with Z and X). Also, the R key is meant to reset all transforms, but it does not reset the zoom transform.   Oh, I almost forgot. You may wonder, what is the objective in this game? What is the goal? It's not that clear cut. It's stuff like 'connect these two things in space using the tools I gave.' It's usually not as simple as drawing a line because there are built in physics. For example, a cursor will move on it's own in the screen. You can select 'draw' and when you hold down the left mouse button, that cursor will draw. However, you don't control the cursor. It has it's own momentum and is simply 'attracted' to wherever your mouse cursor happens to be when you hold down the left click button. If the cursor gains a lot of momentum in one direction and you move your mouse pointer in the opposite, it will take a lot of time to reverse the momentum. So you have to deal with those physical problems. There is more to it than that, but that gives you an idea.

I guess it comes down mostly on if you like the gameplay or not.  Personally I enjoy it quite a lot, so I can't complain there. Probably haven't played enough yet to rate how fast it gets stale (played both betas and a few hours since launch, prolly ~10hrs in the betas) but still enjoy playing it. You're gonna shoot bad guys, a lot of bad guys, repeatedly. Same as in almost every game. The mission types are pretty straight forward and mostly have you run from A->B while activating things, defending things and shooting bosses.  Stabilitywise the only issue I had so far was one or two server-disconnects and a few lags (on launch-day, really don't think that's bad). While friends had issues like their shots not being registered or being disconnected more often.   Looking back on recent launches of bigger titles I'd say it went pretty fine for us (can't say anything about other regions).  My biggest issue is probably the inventory/interface, which is as usual clearly an issue coming from developing the game for console and pc. They made a UI that can be navigated with a controller but is ♥♥♥♥♥♥ to use with a mouse/kb. Same issue you see with games like FA4/Skyrim/etc.

This game has a great atmosphere but there are a few things that make this game unenjoyable:  - How you save: You make a savegame, die, load a checkpoint (!) and be back somewhere way earlier in the game if you're unlucky... I lost like half an hour of progress due to this - The Menus and how you access inventory is really annoying. You go into a menu klick into the next submenu then into the next submenu and all the way back when going back to game - The combat system has a few good ideas but its not really good at the end. Its hard to skip through targets, there is no real feedback from the healthbar of enemies, positioning is hard since you can't really dodge attacks  This game would be awesome if these things would have been different. But since they are not I can not recommend this game.

PROS: + interesting story for a Solitaire game + nice UI + classic Solitaire experience + relaxing  CONS: - can get boring after a longer while of playing - repetitive gameplay - two achievements that are really tedious to get  Comment: The first 25 hours in this game have been fun. It's the concept of Solitaire combined with a story campaign. Poured into the mix are also RPG elements, challenges and some achievements. While not astounding this game is good. However I gave a point less for the tedious egg gathering which works in a really funky way. As you see above I have played about 68 hours in total, that's becuse of the totally random egg appearance combined with a bad way of generating them. And this game does not add enough fun and gameplay variations to play it for almost 70 hours (or more if you are unlucky with the egg generator) for most people. I would still recommend it as it is relaxing and fun for the most part.

Having played a good amount of Civ 5, I think this game is an excellent addition to the series. It's not Civ 6, if that's what you weer expecting, but it is innovative enough that it feels very different from 5.   Beyond Earth trades 5's diversity for much more customization. 5 launched with 18 Civs, while BE has 8 sponsors. In BE, assuming you specialize in just one affinity, you will only really have 10 or so units (not counting orbitals). However, as you get your level in any affinity higher, you will unlock upgrades for your units. Each unit (with the exception of carriers and unique units) has 1 neutral upgrade followed by 2 upgrades for each affinity. Each upgrade increases the unit type's strength as well as allowing you to choose one of two perks that will be permanently applied to all units of that type AND all it's upgrades.   The perks are different for each affinity, which, in conjunction to the other bonuses aquired from each affinity, means that each of the 8 sponsors plays differently with each affinity. This makes 24 sponsor-affinity combinations, and that's if you pick just one affinity to specialize in. Unlike the idealogies, which affinities seem to replace, you can balance 2 or 3 affinities, or you can have one primary affinity and one or two secondary affinities that you only have a few levels in. You could potentially use affinities differently with every playthrough.   In adition to the vast affinity mechanic, trade is much more emphasized, with two trade routes per city. The victory conditions are innovative and very different from each other. Espionage uses G&K's base but expands on it to the point that it is a much more prominant mechanic. The quests system gives you both minor objectives to work towards while you pursue victory, while also implementing a decision system that allows you to chose a permanent bonus for each building type in your colony or choose different solutions to problems, with various rewards including affinity bonuses.   The game is far from perfect, and I am hoping that an expansion will fix some of the minor problems. However, I can definitely reccomend this to any fans of the Civ series.

This is a terrible PC port of Saints Row 2, it is almost unplayable without the Gentlemen of the Row mod, and even with the mod the driving in this is atrocious. When you accelerate in the car you will spin your tires and shoot off forward incredibly fast which makes it difficult at times, when you hit any other car you will glue to them and be forced to reverse away from them before driving off again, turning feels sluggish with the mod and without it you will be able to turn on a dime, it's that bad.  Driving aside the game has graphics which reflect it's age, I don't think the porters improved them at all, which isn't bad if you have played the game before and you want to play it again, especially at its $10 ($2.50 sale) price. However after hearing nothing but good comments about this game and how it is the best in the series, I just don't see it. The aiming feels off for some reason, it's hard to explain but it feels very floaty.  The Activities, which is what everyone says this game does better than any other Saints Row aren't good in my opinion. The Septic Avenger one is very difficult, mainly due to the drivers erratic actions and the way the game aims and counts up points. Escort is okay though with the driving issues I mentioned it is very easy to fail because you touched another car. Mayhem and Insurance Fraud are very fun, I saw very few issues with them.  The spray painting mini-game is so... urgh. I love games where you can do this but in Saints Row 2 they make it into a draw between the lines mini-game but them make the acceleration on the mouse random at times, I ended up finding it easiest to click than to drag the spray can as when I tried the latter my can would shoot off to the side when i tried dragging the mouse and I would fail because i sprayed too much outside.  I haven't gotten very far into the story so I can't comment much into that, but it seems enjoyable so far. I just find the way the game runs to be so infuriating at times that I don't want to play. I am a Saints Row fan so I will finish it and probably 100% it, however I can't say to others they should buy this game and do the same.  IF you do buy this game, I STRONGLY suggest getting the mod as I don't think the game is enjoyable without it.

Ever thought, Hmmmmmmm I want a modern 2D Sonic game. But I want the same feeling I get from playing older sonic games.  Then this game might be for you. *WIthout the evil chemical plant zone!*  This game is the closest you will ever get to Sonic The Hedgehog being in 2D again! Of course this game is split into 2 Episodes.   Story: Eggman is back at it again! He kidnapped the Flickies/animals of the land, and is using them to create a robotic army again. It's sonic job to stop him and collect the 7 Chaos Emeralds.  Gameplay: You get the basic combo pack with one new move. The Homing Attack! Yes the popular Homing Attack from the newer games. Don't forget new ways of using the environment to get to the end! There is, Ziplines, Bolders, Cogs, And cards. There even the *Annoying* pinball features!  Special Stage: This game got a similar but harder version of the Sonic The Hedgehog *1* Special Stages. And for completing it you get a Chaos Emerald *I never got around to collecting the 7 Chaos Emeralds because I hate 100% Sonic games...* After gathering all 7 Chaos Emeralds you get the ability to transform into Super Sonic I can't really speak too much about this. You also get another ending for gathering all seven emeralds  Visual/Graphics: This games look amazing compare to the Classic Games, But not really for this day and age. It's look nice don't get me wrong but something about it don't feel right.  Still not enough to get me to stop playing this game.   Final Rating: Go Supa Sanic! 6.8/10 Yeah, I know how weird it sound considering I'm not going to 100% this. I'm just not good at Mini-games in games. And this game is rather short, I beaten this in my first 3 hours. If you a die hard sonic fan, This game may be for you. I'm a fan don't get me wrong, But I don't think this is worth the completion for me. I never encountered a game what's worth 100% completing for me, Maybe because I'm lazy but I really get unmotivated to complete a game 100%. But I do replay games often if I like them.     Happy 25th Anniversary, See you in the next review.

PROS  - Unique and interesting playable factions with a robust suite of options for creating your own. - Great art design and graphics that still hold up well. Massive battles between fleets of ships are especially impressive. - So many ways to be 'hands on' with your empire such as building your own custom military units and battle plans. - Very well thought systems for colonization and development of your systems and planets. - An interesting tech tree that doesn't feel copied and pasted from similar games. - A more exciting late game than a lot of similar games.  CONS  - Less than stellar AI. There are mods that make it a little more interesting but for the most part it's not amazing. - High learning curve. Even if you've played games like Civilization before, there's a lot of different systems to learn here.   Overall it's an excellent game that holds up wonderfully against similar games. Endless Legend, the kind-of sequel to this game is also excellent and worth checking out if you want an experience a little closer to tile based games like Civ.

review_text: Everything that is South Park has been condensed into this game. Never has a game made me laugh out loud as much as this one has. This is a great rpg with a rich story and lots of customizable gear & abilities. The best part of this game is feeling like you're really participating in an episode of the series. The worst part of this game unfortunatly is its ease, even on hardcore only a couple boss battles are truly difficult, everything else was a breeze. That said I think it's an issue more for replayability than anything else as I found the pacing of the overall game to be perfect. Any South Park or rpg fan will thoroughly enjoy playing this game.

I really liked Of Orcs and Men. It has a very Dragon Age Origins feel to it and it's great that finally the orcs get a fair chance of being the main characters. The game is well balanced, the story is quite interesting and the gameplay was fun.   I'm afraid many people review this game negatively because they were expecting a real time action game, but as I mentioned, it's more of a Dragon Age Origins style of combat. Graphically speaking the game is not stunning, but it looks nice and the map surroundings are interesting and do not become repetitive or boring. It's a linear game though, so don't expect anything else. However, it also rewards you for exploring and for carefully planning the roles of your fighters, Arkail and Stinx, each with their own mechanics and perks. These characters have well developed personalities and backgrounds and it's a lot of fun seeing them interact with each other. I liked that for once the humans were not the good guys, but the enemy, making us see the now classic conflict from a different perspective.   I really enjoyed the game so I definitely recommend it to anyone interested in this kind of game, as long as they understand that they are not buying a real time action game. I played this game the first time ever when I pirated it. I pirate a lot of games. I've pirated every game I've ever played, and the most important thing to take from this is I only purchase games on Steam that I've aleady pirated and love, so that I can support the developers. The lone exception is when I purchase a game bundle for like $4, and it's usually because one of the games was one I pirated and loved.  Rogue Legacy is not just one of those games, it's the defining example. There are only three other games I've loved as much, the first being Super Metroid, the second being Deus Ex (Original), and the third being LoZOoT. My Steam counter right now only shows 10 hours of playtime, but before I bought this game, I pirated it. And I played it for maybe a hundred hours in total. I can't get over it, it's just so damn great!  First off, the platforming is insane. You 1-3 shot all enemies except Uber enemies, Mini-Bosses, and Main Bosses. On the contrast though, unless you're playing a tank class, they also 1-5 shot you! You can't mess around, they will eat you whole in the beginning! But as you play, you get coins, and when you die, you spend those coins in a massive system that extends its way into infinite New Game+ modes. For example, you can upgrade your HP 75 times! There are like, I don't know exactly... probably 12 classes, plus 12 upgraded forms of those classes, plus 30 stat boosts that can all be upgraded 75 times as well.   The upgrading really feels intuitive and like your choices mean something. Do you keep dying a lot? Buy more health and play as a Barbarian King/Queen! Use your Fus Do Rah (It's spelled that way) to knock projectiles away! Do you wish you could use Time Stop for longer periods? Play as an Archmage and use your huge MP pool to freeze time and whale on bosses!  The platforming is perfect too. It's exactly like what I always wanted in a GOOD MMX1-3 sequel. In fact, the platforming is even better than Megaman. It's better than even Metroid, because every jump feels incredibly responsive thanks to pixel-precision movement. You can slightly tap left or right to move a pixel or two over. It feels RIGHT.  If this game is on any kind of sale, get it. If it's not, get it anyway. Support these developers, and join in the throng of people begging for a fully realized sequel.

If FarCry and LOTR had a baby...  + Genuinely draws on Tolkien for content + Slick basic combat is very simple/console like + Looks great and the world feels alive + Nemesis system is really interesting + Wraith world is a nice mechanic + Humour is well executed and fits + Gollum  ~ Mini challenges are kinda lame (kill 10 of X) ~ Simple upgrade system lacks depth and interesting powers ~ Combat is mostly repetitive grinding and odd key combos ~ Warchief battles are brutally hard ~ Sometimes the cinematics slow the pace down ~ Very bloody  - DLC adds some value, much of it is worthless padding - Later challenges have tough goals and sometimes broken directives  What a hoot! Take out the forces of Sauron while you discover some ancient Mordor history in flashbacks. Aside from some of the later challenges I loved every minute of this open world fantasy epic. It rewards stealth, skill, patience and timing as you plan, execute and escape attacks.  Conclusion: 8/10. Great for Tolkien Fans who like ore and stealth.

What Neverending Nightmare does right: + Amazing black and white style, + Tension filled sounds and monsters that really give a creepy atmosphere. + Great concept with a storyline that will keep you unnerved and guessing.  What Neverending Nightmare does wrong, and is enough of a reason to only watch it played and not buy it. - Gameplay is nothing short of slow walking. - Very little to interact with. - Painfully short, I completed the game in 1.4 hours. - A joke of a pricetag for its limited length. - Little incentive to discover alternate endings.  Neverending Nightmares is a game that had a lot of potentional, and is one of those games you really want to love, until you get bored of the constant slow walking, little development in the characters and storeline, and realise the game is far too short.  This is a great example of blown potential, and I wish I could get a refund.

I played this classic on the GameCube when it originally released. Most dub this as a spiritual successor to Radiant Silvergun, another Treasure classic that was released on Sega's system years ago. Regardless, if you enjoy bullet-hell shooters, then this game will be right up your alley. The implementation of the black/white bullet gameplay is a nice switch-up and the skill/practice needed to chain together kills with the right color shots. You can absorb whatever shots are thrown your way if you're in the correct color mode. This allows you to charge up your super shot also and unleash some heavy damage. The game starts off relatively tame then gets rather hectic, really quick. The soundtrack fits the game pretty well and obviously has a convoluted storyline most shooters like this do. There's the obvious scoring system that perfectionists are going to want to max out out as high as possible. The bosses were also well designed and get progessively harder throughout the course of the game. The length is on par with other shooters, and can be finished rather quick if you aren't in it to master the craft and up your score. Definitely for the niche shooter lover market. I wouldn't recommend the game to casuals.

Alright, so. To start off, this is a game about sharks vs divers. Pretty obvious right?  Moot points  At the start of a match generally the divers are really easy to catch and murder, sharks are squishy. It can roll really easy between power balance of divers or sharks. The clever use of consumables and upgrades on both sides is really crucial, not to mention making full use of all the shark special abilities.  Pros - Very, very fun to play as a shark - Divers can have a lot of fun setting up all kinds of traps and dangerous encounters with sharks - S.T.E.V.E is pretty cute - A few different game modes to choose from including plenty of maps to explore - Scaling unlockable abilities, weapons and upgrades based on amount of time invested in the game - Playing with friends invites delicious murder and funsies.  Cons - If you like having things look pretty, you are going to get jumpscared by a good shark. Very hard to see in game with high end graphics - Queue times. Now this might just be on Aussie Region but a queue generally takes closer to 10 mins + - Everyone, and I mean EVERYONE, wants to play as a shark.   Do I recommend this game? Heck yeah.

My overall score: 9/10  Dragon Dogma has multiple visible flaws that take off from the fun of playing the game and can become quite irritating in the long run. Things such as: - Your AI allies, called pawns in the game, who never wants to shut up. - A lot of time is spent walking around and backtracking until you have multiple playthrought and planned ahead using the stones that create fast travel points around the map. - A lack of difficulty option which makes the game a one size fits all. - The story and characters being absolutely non-memorable and falling quite short when it comes to the Roleplaying side of the game.  But even with all of those rather intimidating flaws, Dragon's Dogma is still one of the best game I've ever played. With 53.5 hours played on the PC and nearly double that amount of the Xbox 360, I can't help but to ignore all of its flaws and see this game as a masterpiece. For it is a one of a kind game. It definitely was worth every god damn penny and more.  The AI may be annoying with their repetitive lines being screamed at the top of their lungs every few seconds, but it is one of the best AI I've had the pleasure of dealing with in any given game beside the few random stupid things they could do. Have you ever seen an AI NPC catch you as you were falling to your death after being dropped from a griffin 200 meters above ground? My pawn did just that. Have you ever seen a pawn jump on your shield to latch on said griffin paws, climb up to it's wing and stab it with the fire enchantment another pawn gave him in order to burn his wings and ground it? Because it happens in Dragon's Dogma.  The lack of difficulty setting isn't that much of an issue as the game gradually increase the difficulty and if content is too hard in the post-game or expansion, you can always run another playthrought, keeping your character level, stats and equipment along the ride and keep on improving your character to make the post-game and expansion a much easier time.  But what it all comes down to is gameplay. This game should be the template for any other open world RPG when it comes down to gameplay. The epic scale of 'boss' battles in this game is throught the roof. Also, the amount of customization in this game for your character is staggering. Considering you will be looking at it a lot with the game 3rd person view, it is very appreciated to have such a wide selection of armor, weapons and a lot of choices when it come down to character creation.  So here is the scores I give to this game:  Gameplay: 10/10 Music and sound: 8.5/10 (Had to remove .5 because of the pawns who never shut up) Graphics: 8/10 Story and characters: 6/10 Customization: 9.5/10  Overall score: 9/10

This game is far from being a full release game which unfortunatly at the moment is the case for a lot of even AAA games. This is at best an early access pickup, so if you are looking for a well polished football game this is defenetly not for you. It does have potetial and if the developers offer it the attention it needs and are not in it just to make a quick money they can do something interesting with it.  Pros: 1) The manual aiming of passes is an interesting idea 2) I absolutly have to mention the names of the teams which have nothing to do with the NFL teams like the Indianapolis Broncos, Seattle warhawks, Kensas City Indians and the fact that the teams are from the exact US cities is just clearly a coinsidence :) 3) It has gamepad support which works fine with my 360 controller  Cons: 1) Totaly unpolished: - the tackles and player movements are not even close to real - there are a lot of rules missing (holdings, interference etc) - but at least they make fun of that in the commentary - some of the available plays can't be selected for some reason - the menu ui and interacton while in a game is horible - do not expect any cutscenes for replays, gamestarts, etc - very limited commentary - at most 20 different lines 2) Graphics with the settings set to maximum is really bad 3) Some plays will never get you yards because the players don't react as they should - my wr was realeasing the corner just as my rb was getting there and I got takled 3 consecutive times in the same way 4) The aimed pass with a controller will defenetly be hard to get used to 5) No options menu and volume contol. I had to lower the volume from the windows mixer.  Summary: Buy this game only to support the devs in the hopes they will continue with it and for some fun from time to time. If you are expecting this to be something to rival the madden games this probably will not be that game
