There have been a lot of negative posts about this game, 90% of which I can understand people frustration - but to me it is still a great game. I love player as SM, and yes at the moment if u r against CSM u will be lucky to win, but its still a laugh. Against eldar is good but maybe balanced against them atm - but here is the crunch point:  Orks. Oh my god have they done a great job so far. Yes there are bugs but hey they have just released them with HUGE pressure to get them released, so potentially they were released while not being as polished as they might be - but one thing they seem to have got spot on it the balancing with them. SM vs Ork games are close and great fun - I really feel that they have brough the 40k universe to life.  People are still down on the melee system, and yes the power fist is still OP but it is no longer a melee wins all game - I personally love the storm bolter! short mag but great as anti melee.  There is a great dev team behind this game that listen and fix (if sometimes it seems slowly) any issues/bugs people have with the game and I cant wait to see all the new things they have in the pipeline.  Personally, who cares when 'launch' is, just keep releasing new stuff :) Keep it up guys.  EDIT:  Post launch - still loving this game. I play this on my laptop, am UK based and have had no/very little problems with lag or animations. Its a shame that some people are having this problem but I've not seen it personally. Hover Junkers is a perfect example of a proper use of room scale and immersion in my opinion. This is such a fun and active game that you have no choice but to be engaged with it at all times.   The vast wastelands provide such a grand scale of depth to your environment that a lot of the times I just want to ride as far out as I'm allowed because I love to explore these world's.   It gives me a borderlands vibe which I love and I love even more because they could of been lazy and just stolen the ideas but they did what any great artist does, and take inspiration from something they enjoyed and used it for their own.  It adds a personal touch that most games lack.   I'm a fit guy who works our frequently so I didn't expect much fatigue, I was wrong, you don't realize how high or low your jumping or ducking until your overheating grasping for a bottle of water. Not only is it physically demanding (in a good way) it is also mentally demanding. You have to have 360 degree awareness at all times, always on edge and newer quite sure what is around the corner.   All in all this is just my personal opinion, it really shows what the vive and stress level zero is capable of. Just the fact that it's so impressive at its infancy is super impressive. Hopefully I'll see you all in the killing fields!

(Spoiler free. If you do intend on playing Undertale, PLEASE try to go into this game blind... it will make the experience all the more enjoyable when you know little to nothing going into it. Even if you want to give the demo a try first, try not to spoil yourself on anything, and just play it like any other RPG)  Out of any Indie Game I have ever played, Undertale has been BY FAR the most enjoyable, and has the most potential for the amount of time you are going to end up puting into it. Day one of release, and I've already sunk 13 hours into the game.  Lovely story, amazing characters, overall great writing, BEAUTIFUL soundtrack, RPG combat that honestly doesn't get repetative like most games of the genre tend to fall ill on. If you enjoy RPG games like Earthbouth/the Mother series, you are going to love Undertale.  The demo is free to play, and has a fair bit of replayability to it too (as does the full game. Almost done my second playthrough, and I'm planning a third and fourth). PLEASE consider buying this game... it is $11 that is VERY much well spent.

If you could bottle childlike innocence, pop culture comedy, and an engaging fairytale story and sell it as an 8 hour long game, you'd get Undertale.  When I first heard about Undertale, before it was actually released, it was getting a lot of negative press. Later, after it was released and some of the vitriol had died down, I heard that it was great, and worthy of Indie Game of the Year nomination. I had to see for myself.  The first thing I have to say is, I love the soundtrack. The mellow synth tunes take me back to the 16-bit console days of my youth, and the frantic and high energy battle music is very pokemon-reminiscent. Needless to say, I've had the whole soundtrack popping in and out of my head all day for the past week and a half.  I had a lot of fun talking to all the NPCs, which is one of my favorite things to do in RPGs - to talk to all the town NPCs and see what kinds of interesting things they have to say. The random humor you find in every interactable always hit me by surprise.  One thing I learned very early on is that Undertale is an RPG that attempts to defy all the tropes that define what an RPG is. Nothing ever seems to be as it is, and I'm hard pressed to say that there are no useless items in the game, even though the game tries to convince you that there are.  The boss battles are challenging. I like how color plays an important role in how the soul controls function. I actually found the game to be quite difficult until I switched from Keyboard/Mouse to an Xbox One controller, at which point I found that fights became significantly easier for me. I didn't find the puzzles terribly challenging, but I was also playing on the normal setting. I don't know if they get any harder as the difficulty rises.  The game really takes you on an emotional roller coaster. One minute you're laughing at the crazy antics of Sans and Papyrus, and the next you are hit with heavy-hitting themes like responsibility, consequences, death, social anxiety, unrequited love, and more. It's way more than just a game about chosing to kill or not kill.  The replayability on the game is high. I've only been through it once, and already I'm considering another playthrough to achieve a different ending, and the chance to find more secret stuff that I undoubtedly missed my first time around.  Final Verdict: Undertale is a work of art. Meant to be experienced and digested within a few hours, it's easy to appreciate the simplicity of the game on surface, but it has enough depth to give it a 'thinker' status in my books. The gameplay is (most of the time) fun and engaging regardless of whether you prefer combat or pacifism. The soundtrack is hands down amazing. The art is well illustrated, and the graphics are what you might expect from a pixel-based RPG, though the game does make excellend use of space in both the foreground and background. In closing, Undertale is anything but Underrated, and it deserves all the praise it gets.  9/10 - Would highly reccomend to others.

It starts easy, and then has a perfectly sensible difficulty curve for about ten levels.  After that, you're on your own.  The game is brutally difficult, and often punishing of even the most minute mistakes. This is exacerbated by a control set that is both incredibly precise and very loose simultaneously, giving you absolute control over your character, but also forcing you to watch him slip around the environment like he's covered in oil.   It's also certainly not lacking in content. With 20 levels per chapter and at least 5 (Or more, but that's spoilers) chapters, plus 'Dark World' versions (Which are the same map, but with the difficulty cranked up a couple notches (Which is saying something)), not to mention quite a few bonus stages and levels, there's more than enough content to keep even the most avid player going for ages, but since most gamers aren't going to make it past Chapter 2, it's a shame that they didn't include a few easier stages so that less capable players couldn't also get their money's worth.    In short, if you want a vast challenge, go ahead. If you want something a bit more relaxed, go elsewhere.

Hrnngh/10  If you already have it, you should give it a punt. You should maybe buy it on sale or something. But it's definitely not a full price purchase, as far as I'm concerned.   It tries do to curious stuff, but fails more often than not. There's some nice verticality to the level design at times, and I like how it discourages close quarter combat by making you vulnerable and letting you carry a pitiful amount of submachine gun ammo. There's also a level where you're caught in a crossfire between the Germans and the Russians, having to fend off waves of soldiers from both sides while you slowly claim ground towards your target. It is The Acest Thing.  At times, Sniper Elite is an amazing powertrip. I once had three enemies in close proximity - one patroling, and two standing guard. I took one of the static ones out, masking my shot with the ringing of a church bell. While the patroling soldier was examining the body, I took the other surviving guy out in a similar way. As the sole survivor turned around, he found another dead body, having no clue what was happening. I like to think he was pretty bewildered as a speeding bullet entered his skull.  Ultimately, I spent a lot of time carefully planning a stealthy infiltration, stealthy assasination and stealthy exfiltration that never was. The checkpoints were too far apart to restart every time I was noticed, and I was noticed far more often than I would've liked. The thing is, I feel like it wasn't really my fault most of the time. The conveniently loud artillery fire would misteriously cease indefinitely as soon as I picked off my first target. Enemy reinforcements would magically appear in places I've already sweeped. Cheap stuff like that.  I had fun for a couple of hours, but I came close to giving up on it several times.

Man! This game... I spent hours playing this on PS3 and I had to get it for PC. One of the most overlooked RPG titles, it's a diamond in the rough. This game was poorly marketed, and released at a weird time. It feels generic, now, but it's a very worthy game.   If you've ever played WoW and thought to yourself, 'What if I could play this just by myself?', this is the answer to that question. Cartoony, yes. Generic storyline? Yes. But, this game offers so much that other contemporary RPGs don't.  This game is nearly flawless in its execution. It's complete - the kinks have been worked out. It's a game in its prime, not like a lot of the new games which release with a ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ of bugs. This game is 100% smooth. Movements are smooth, the quests are smooth, the graphics are very smooth. It runs well on low-spec machines and still looks FANTASTIC.  The only reason this game is overlooked is it feels generic - if you're an RPG player. But, there's only so much you can do with fantasy games, at the end of the day. This game's focus is on fun and exploration. I haven't really ever played anything quite this polished.  The inventory system is great, the leveling system is great, the story is good enough to keep you interested and the combat feels awesome - especially with a controller.  I've played a LOT of games, and I always keep coming back to this game, because it's BIG. But, at the same time, it's one of those games where you don't need to commit hours to a raid, you can just save and quit and pick up where you left off, not feeling like you missed much. Because the story line is so simple, as long as you remember the gist of what's happening, you can hop right back in.  This is the perfect game-between-games. It's not like the storyline sucks you in, but it's just a fun game to play and it feels very well done. As far as VALUE goes, dollar for dollar, this game packs a major punch and is worth every penny - imho.  The ONLY complaint I really have is I wish there was a co-op option, but then again, not every game has to be multiplayer. As far as single-player games go, this one stands out in the crowd of half-finished, half-♥♥♥♥♥ games, at a very approachable price, and with an easy learning curve. 100% recommend.

app_name: Realm of the Mad God Exalt
review_text: I have a love hate relationship with this game.   After dying, you sort of chill for like 3 months or so depending how long you spent building that character. Usually a 0/8 character is equivalent to 1-2 weeks of chill, while dying as a 6/8 would be rage inducing to the point of almost half a year of chill. I know, I know, you're probably thinking it's just a game, you shouldn't even rage, yea, yea 'Git Gud'. But, because it's permadeath, you lose everything, everything you've earnt. I almost had a heart attack, or an aneurism. Let me tell you, when you get attached to one character, you are one with the character, and when you die in the game, it feels like dying in real life. No joke.   It’s not a bad game, proof? Well you wouldn’t see people play this game for hundreds of hours if it was a bad game. In addition I played way more of this game on the internet version. Yea it consumed me (not as much as it did for others though).  Like I said, I have a love hate relationship with this game, and you’ll begin to understand me when you at least clock up 50-100 hours of the game.

XCOM enemy unknown is the best modern turn based strategy game. It has an excellent blend of base management and gritty tactical combat. It's a lethal game, where a tactical blunder means a dead squad member or a mismangement of resources leads to the loss of a country's support. XCOM starts off brutally difficult, with your soldiers clearly mismatched. However, as the game goes on, your soldier's training and equipment clearly outpaces the alien army. The disparity between your progression and the enemies makes the endgame start to drag, and the final battle is anticlimatic.   XCOM enemy unknown (and I daresay it's essential expansion, enemy within) is a fantastic game that captivated me. It took me 3 days to complete the game the first time in 25 hours. If you're looking for a challenge, XCOM is the game to get

Very short, but very entertaining.  It's more of a puzzle game than a stealth/assassination game, and I really think that it does that well.  The controls take a small amount of getting used to, but the pixel graphics ensures you have good precision for your actions. The music was nice, and there were multiple ways of solving the later missions. Sadly the earlier missions are rather linear, but they are more of tutorials than anything else. The game is rather short, but I feel it explores its mechanics well. It could only really done with one or two extra levels near the end at most: any more and I expect it would have begun getting dry.  There were a number of plot points and things that could have happened differently, and I felt, while playing, that had I have done different things I would have gotten a different ending. However, unlike most other 'multiple choice' games, at no point did I feel that I should go back to get '100% completion'. Usually you are choosing between two good – or more frequently, bad – choices. No one is superior over another, only different.  The replayability of the game is actually a little mixed. I feel that I would like to replay the story again to see if I could get a different sort of ending, but replaying the levels would be either boring, or an exercise in speedrunning. These seem at cross purposes.  Overall, it's a great short game, and I think it's well worth your money, even to play through just once.

review_text: I have died a thousand times, and come out stronger. I am shy as a person, often afraid of taking initiatives or chances. app_name: DARK SOULS™: Prepare To Die Edition
review_text: Well, i guess it's that time in every gamer's life. To explain why his game is better then another. After at least 250 hours of gametime on this game, i had to ask myself...was it worth it? My answer is yes. Why you ask? Simple. It's because of all the stories i can talk about with my friends retelling my intense boss fights with strangers. Crazy pvp fights with horrifyingly overpowered opponents. To feel angry, to feel loss and sadness at your eventful demises, but to feel hope, friendship and camaderie with complete strangers there to support you through these tough times in the cursed land of Lordran. So, i urge you to at least try this game, to attempt the impossible and succeed through incredible odds. Too actuallly feel as if you accomplished something important. So remember, if your feeling down, just keep calm and praise the sun!!  Good luck

app_name: The Lord of the Rings Online™
review_text: I just love this Game.  Even though I have the feeling that it was better in the old days, when I just started playing, I still love this game so much. There is no MMO out there that has a nicer community, better character costumization (because of the cosmetic outfit system) and for people who like to role-play this game is just pure awesomeness. The ambience is great, the players are nice and there is allways something to do.  I see a lot of people that complain about this game not truly beeing F2P. If I must be honest, it makes you sound really stingy. Ofcourse they want to make money out of their game! Thats what keeps it alive. And the Lord of the Rings online gives you the opportunity to level to at least level 30 without experiencing big limitations due to beeing F2P. And if you still love the game and want to continue playing, just purchase a few account upgrades and you are ensured of many more hours of fun. Even the smallest purchase gives you quite some premium stats. And also you can earn shop points by playing the game. If you do a lot of deeds you can purchase a new region without even having to pay for one. So please dont be so anoyed about this game beeing not 'entirely free to play'. The amount of restricitons you have for beeing a F2P player is not that significant and if you love this game, dont be cheap and give turbine some of the money they deserve for making this masterpiece of an MMO.

review_text: The full review can be found on  Start 2 Continue  [start2continue.com] . (Originally written 8 Oct 2014)  Excerpt:  Around this time last year, Total War: Rome II stumbled out of the gate seemingly with its pants down. Despite our rather positive review, we felt that the game wasn't completed to the level we expected it to be: it was buggy, the AI was lackluster, and some advertised features were stripped from the game right before launch (I'm looking at you, defense deployment and political system). However, Creative Assembly has blessed us yet again with the game that should have been: Total War: Rome II - Emperor Edition.  To start out, not too much has changed between Rome II and Emperor Edition aside from the inclusion of a brand new Imperator Augustus campaign that puts you in the middle of the Second Triumvirate with Rome split into four factions vying for control. With the same scale of the original grand campaign, Imperator Augustus is bound to keep you on your toes with further threats of splintering (if you're Roman) and opportunistic wars (for everyone else).  Play as either Marc Antony, Lepidus, Octavian, or Pompey and bring peace to Rome. Or, if you'd like something a little less Latin, the Iceni, Marcomanni, Dacia, Egypt, Parthia, and Armenia are also available for you to enjoy (the last of which is now also available in the grand campaign, alongside Syracuse). With the political system now overhauled, civil war has become a significant factor in large empires--especially if you haven't been watching the generals of other houses closely. The more stable your faction's political situation, the better economic and military bonuses you receive. Finally, political intrigue is viable option, both within and without your fledgling country.  ...  Overall, Emperor Edition has reinvigorated my love for Rome II, however much like Empire and Napoleon before it, it stands as a testament to how Creative Assembly should slow down and plug all the holes in their games before launching. I certainly hope they treat the upcoming Attila with the respect it deserves.

I actually dislike most of the action (adventure) games like God Of War and Devil May Cry. They're usually all about the combat, which becomes a boring aspect for me a few hours into the game. And yes, they have great visuals most of the times, but visuals isn't a key aspect for me. Then again; they do help to pull the player into the game world.   Anyway, DmC managed to surprise me because of it's environments, fluent combat and, maybe even more important for me; short campaign. You read correctly, a short campaign. It will take a player somewhere between 7,5-12 hours to fully complete one playthrough, which is a decent length and for me long enough to not become boring or overstay its welcome.  The new Dante starts off as an annoying, run-of-the-mill character. But along the way I started caring for him. He grew more mature it seemed.  Dante will start the game with some basic weaponry and will get new moves and weapons until late in the game, which keeps the combat fresh. Obviously, all weapons and moves can be upgraded. And there really are some amazing moves in there. Using a controller I was able to pull off some of the hardest combos. I'll admit, there was some button mashing involved, but most of the times I actually knew what I was doing.  The environments steal the show though. Rarely have I seen environments this brilliant. The TV show (including boss fight) and nightclub levels are some of the best levels I've ever seen in a game. DmC uses a variety of bright colors, while still having a certain gothic feel to it. It's exceptional.  You can tell they put some real love into the PC version since it will never dip below 60FPS. On Ultra settings things look (mostly) sharp and seeing the game in motion is a real treat, especially during the acrobatic (non-)combat sequences.   The voice acting is top notch, but, unfortunately, the story is a bit on the light side.  Time to wrap things up: I had a great time with this game and I would gladly recommend it to all serious gamers out there.  [Rating: 80/100]

app_name: Brothers - A Tale of Two Sons
review_text: Far from perfect when it comes to the control scheme. Far from perfect when it comes to the graphics. Far from perfect when it comes to the puzzles.  And yet it's one of the best games I've ever played. A heartbreaking story and yet not a word is said. The game is fairly short so just go in blind. Play it. Make sure you have tissues nearby and someone to hold your hand.

The game:  - dripping with promises, intriguing, tempting... - and so poorly executed in some CRUCIAL mechanisms, that one wonders if the developers have ever actually played a management simulation game!  I focus on the main point that make me literally 'scream': manage the speed of the game... Also setting MAX speed, the game is soooooo slow that take ages only to finish a stupid working week...  Oh, ok, I can change it through the settings, right? You mean these incredible and stupid list of about THREE hundred voices? Ok, can I just set time to be more... 'fast'? No problem, but, if you want to change also 'how' the world go fast meanwhile, you have to change these other settings... and this other... and find an improbable balance (yeah, I know it's difficult to understand, but this is HOW it actually works the game!).  And so it's practically impossible to change game speed... because, also if you manage to find an 'ideal' setting, the game is NOT BEING BUILD to change ANY of these settings and make it work, so you'll find innumerable errors, that make this game look as a business simulation game like a Barbie(TM) game would look like!  And why the city are so small and grow so slowly? Why my commuters return at home (from work) at 11 of evening? Why some bugs don't let me set a normal ferry service?  It's a game that brings many 'why'...  But the first, surely is: WHY I have spent 30 euros (game expansion), many restart, and 60 hours of my precious time to play on it, waiting and hoping for the next patch?

Despite the low Metascore, I am going to recommend this game. Yeah, it isn't the tactical game we were promised, but I sure find it fun. The sound design is fantastic. There is no music in the game, which can lead to a lot of tension and enemy gunfire making you jump. A lot of people bag on the graphics being outdated, but they honestly don't bug me. They're nice. One reviewer said that there are N64 games that look more detailed, and that's a flatout lie. There are moments when you start a mission where there will be texture pop in, but that doesn't really bother me. Then again, I'm the guy that still plays MS-DOS games, haha! The AI is a mixed bag. Teammates can sometimes spot enemies I can't and they will pick them off, but there are times where they allow tangos to take us all out like dominoes. Tangos are vicious, which makes the game rather difficult, but I honestly don't mind that. With the few missions that there are, I feel like successfully completing one of them is overcoming a major challenge. I'm sure I will be over joyed when I finally do complete one, haha. The game does fail in terms of being a tactical shooter, the commands you can issue are limited to holding/following and rules of engagement. However, as a regular shooter, I think it's a fun and challenging game. I don't think Takedown: Red Sabre really deserves all of the hate that it gets. Maybe I just have bad taste in games, but I think this game is worth, at least, a try.

This game is positively the laziest AAA game ever to be put into Steam. It's a terrible, terrible console port, you're not even allowed to use a mouse to navigate the menus. In addition to a terrible console port, it has to STUPIDEST control scheme, luckily you can re-bind it, but it still uses the stupid control scheme to navigate the menus. The game also has to change the controls THREE TIMES. The arrow keys and enter to navigate the menu, and once you get into a game it uses the WASD keys and G to select. And then, once you start playing, it swaps to the controls I set so they weren't stupid, which is the arrow keys and ZXCVB as attacks. And this isn't even into the game yet. So, in this game the main way of combat is combos, but the combos are so unresponsive. You have to press the buttons quickly, but if you're too quick one of the keys won't get registered so you just do a basic punch or kick insted of what you tried to do. So they messed up the main way of fighting. The games singleplayer is also very, very dull. I won on expert by just pushing the AI in a corner and button mashing. The only enjoyment I got was from me fighting someone I have over Skype, but even then the enjoyment came us not playing it right. The game is also not noob-friendly. If you're new and are playing as a character you've never played as and don't know any of their combos, guess what? Too bad, the multiplayer community don't give a crap, so you might as well commit suicide. If you like button mashing, crappy console ports, and hostile communities, then pick this game up. Anyone else, avoid this game at all costs.

review_text: A great game with solid game-play. Pleasantly challenging, the stealth aspect of the game makes it reminiscent of Metal Gear Solid for PS. However, if you decide to go all guns blazing (which is, admittedly, really fun), you miss out on one of the best parts of the game. Given the tech tree with the upgrades and the different styles of game play, it has pretty good replayability,

There's two parts to Q.U.B.E. Like most games, there's the story and then there's the gameplay.   The gameplay in Q. U. B. E. Director's Cut is actually quite good. You start the game in a weird space built out of cubes, with a pair of gloves that allows you to interact with a small amount of the world, namely colored bricks.   From this, the game is based on manipulating those bricks, traversing the levels, and exploring the world.   And that's what Q.U.B.E. does exceedingly well. I played this game back before the Director's Cut and remember one or two puzzles I had trouble with. This time around, I only had a single problem with a puzzle (an annoying puzzle, but it's also the last one in the game I believe). However the puzzles are a highlight of the game, and will make most fans of puzzle games or games where the goal is to merely traverse a level (like Portal) enjoy themselves.   On the other hand there's a story, and to be honest, that's where the game falls apart. The original Q.U.B.E. didn't have a story and I remember thinking 'Man I'd love to have some reasoning for me to do this.' Hell I'm sure I suggested a portal-esque story.   Unfortunately I got what I requested, and worse... well it's just bad. The story itself feels disjointed, as if it was placed in the middle of an already complete game (which it was) and sadly the game isn't better for it. The story, tries to be this deep probing or even contemplative thing. But comes across as just manipulative and forgettable. Even the ending is just there. I like the puzzles, not the part where the game tries to talk to and interact with the player.  I actually wish there was a way to not play the story (I still have Q.U.B.E. so I can just play the old version). It's just not a good addition to the original game, luckily they have made it a free upgrade otherwise I would have been unable to recommend this version of the game.   However with that being said, the gameplay is above average, there's some time attack maps that you can play that are actually quite difficult, there's a lot of enjoyment to be had on the first playthrough of the game. Just don't expect an amazing story, or even a game you can play through more than once. You'll spend a couple hours playing the game, and get a fun experience, but it's not going to blow any minds, and since it came out in the same year as Portal 2, it didn't win any awards. That all being said, it's still a solid experience and quite enjoyable for fans of the puzzle genre.

I finished the single-player campaign for this about a week ago. How To Survive is a comedy action RPG where the player chooses one of four characters to play. Each character has different attributes and you weigh up their stamina, agility and strength. You're presented with an array of missions with the eventual goal of escaping a collection of islands filled with the undead. The format is very similar to Diablo or Path of Exile. There's also an online Co-Op mode.  I played through with the agility character, Kenji which has a talent tree geared around marksmanship and hunting game. I usually opt for agility characters in games. I also played the game on normal difficulty, in retrospect I probably should have chosen hard as i found the game started to get a bit predictable and too easy toward the end.  Managing inventory is key. Much like DayZ, your survivability depends on what food, materials and ammunition you carry around. You craft your own weapons out of parts found lying around the island and upgrade them as you find better equipment. Over time your character will become hungry, thirsty and tired. You need to food and water, but it's nowhere near as scarce as DayZ. My god:  'I'm starving' Starving? Again!? I Just fed you ya fat slob! It's the apocalypse for Christ's sake, no more Sizzler buffets for you.  How To Survive has a lot of comedy in it. Kovac's character is pretty funny and the guides you find lying around the place are good for a laugh. The story is done pretty well too. Simple, but it has a good ending. The game is pretty forgiving on normal difficulty when it comes to hunger and thirst. You could get by without even carrying water, just drinking from wells when you find them. I'd recommend people familiar with top-down RPG's to start the game on Hard.  If you want to flay these things, you're gonna want the Triple Sticks Cutting Boomerang. This thing is so OP. Just spamming it will kill anything that stumbles into your field of vision. Towards the end of the game it becomes a bit too easy with this weapon. That said, you can play the game on a harder difficulty which would offer more of a challenge.  Would recommend How To Survive to anyone who enjoys the Diablo style, top-down-view, single character RPG's. A decent 8 to 10 hours of single-player game-play. Usually 15USD, I got it for 5 on those sweet sweet steam sales.

Fantastic RTS. One of the few more modern strategy games that I actually enjoy playing. This game is still buggy to some extent indeed but most of the stuff is cosmetic and doesn't bother me very much. This game employs many real world weapons systems accurately and allows for real world tactics to be utilized because of it. This game is not necesarrilly a simulation by any means, but it is certainly one that you have to actually think about when you play, not just point and click and mindlessly win. I have played all the Wargame games from European Escalation (the first) and I already have more hours logged on this game than the other two (relative to the amount of time I've had them) and this game has only been out for just over a month at this point. The unit variety is enourmous and no single combat unit has an identical counterpart on the opposing faction, making for VERY fun and unique matches that vary player to player.  Overall, this is a FANTASTIC game, and although it could use a little ironing out of the cosmetic bugs such as improper weapon animations and I believe some sound effects that are not that of the weapon being fired on certain AA missiles, is absolutely one of my favorite games that I have played, and that's a lot to say the least...

*Spoiler free*  No Hud. No Inventory. No difficulty. No Map. No Tutorial. No quests list. No colectibles. No acheivments.  Simply put The The vansihing of Ethan Carter is the evolution of the point and click adventure. If you grew up and enjoyed those types of games as i did then prepare yourself for the future. Firstly, lets get the obvious out of the way, the world is absolutely gorgeous. Indoor, outdoor, lighting all looks amazing. When you see all the grass and trees moving in the wind it is very impressive. I could go on and on about the graphics, but thats just one feature.  I really like how there is a small intro at the start and then it's where do you go, what do you want to do as you walk out of a tunnel. You play as Paul Prospero a pyschic detective who has shown up in the town of Red Rock to find a young fan who has dissappeared. The game is about exploration, and discovery. As you explore about you will come upon bodies who you will then try to piece together how they died. If you are a fan of Sherlock, its basically the same concept of finding the obvious and not so obvious clues and then placing events in chronological order and watching the crime unfold. Its a lot of fun, effective and the most important part, tells a cohesive story.  Without going into story spoliers, after you have completed the game i would suggest going to the deveopers discussion  :http://www.theastronauts.com/2014/10/vanishing-ethan-carters-ending-extreme-spoilers/  Super interesting read and will help fill in some holes. My only dissapointment in the game is the length. I would have liked a few more puzzles but sometimes that is not the answer. The value for me is in the experience and i can easily say it was worth the price of admission.  Positives Pushes point and click games forward Graphical powerhouse Solid story and satisfying ending The musical score is amazing Slenderman type level in the mines Coming out of detective vision back into the color of the world No hand holding  Negatives Length Not fully open world  Sound is minimal and charcter models look bad when you comapre to the amazing enviroments Exploring off the path is not rewarding. Thought i found a cave off in the woods, on top of a boulder behind a tree, but no access. Enviromental effects are not utilized. Water dripping in the cave, or rocks falling as an example.

Note: Review will be updated as each episode is released  Sigh... I was dreading doing this, ever since finishing episode 4. Editing this review, that is. How could I go from an updated-as-it-went, previously positive review to a negative one? I've decided to keep the old, positive review of the first three episodes below. Consider that the review of if the game just got cancelled after three episodes. But for now, my thoughts on what unfortunately Telltale saw fit to have the complete game be.  To mirror my former positive remarks in a sadly now more accurate light, with Clementine taking the reigns of the main character, the worst one could hope for was that as she's lost everyone close to her and is an odd character in being young yet needing to be responsible, the writers would have absolutely no idea what to do with her story arc. And... that's exactly what happened.  Stories sometimes take great risks. This is a good thing... but kind of backfires if there's no PLAN to that risk. The risk with The Walking Dead game was having Clementine lose everything she once had, then become the main character. Where do you go from there? How does Clementine, who was just recently OUR purpose, get a purpose of her own? In the first three episodes, it seems like maybe they had some idea of this but ultimately in the latter two episodes, it just got confused and lost in the mess of everything else they tried to cram in, all with no clear direction.  People often say that in The Walking Dead, anyone can die, at any time, for no reason... at least as far as the game goes, however, this is just not true. The latter two episodes provide the ONLY game examples of important characters dying, having achieved nothing. You can challenge me on this if you wish, I've spent plenty of time pointing out how even the most useless seeming characters in the game did have meaning, even if that meaning was only determinant.  Another major problem is the way the characters behave. They have complete 180s, just like that and it's extremely off-putting. It was easy for me not to mind in earlier episodes, when previous hostile characters suddenly became friendly(although I still regarded it as a bit of a problem). However, in latter episodes, previously likable characters become terrible, useless people. This could maybe be forgiven, if not for the fact that, as stated above, people are dying pointlessly left and right. Thus, everyone ends up dead or unlikable.  Just stick with the first season is my recommendation. It's easy to get excited for what lies in store for Clementine's future, as Lee gives her his final advice, but unfortunately Telltale just didn't know themselves what worthwhile things could be in store for her.  Below is my review from before episodes 4 and 5 came out... quite a different tone, huh?  With Clementine now taking the reigns of main character, the best one could hope for was to be given the chance to decide what kind of survivor she would morally be and help her through a world she must now fend for herself in. And of course, the best one could hope for was what we got.  Episode comments (spoiler-free):  EPISODE ONE - All That Remains  This is incredibly gripping and hard-hitting. It's like a metaphor for life, taken up to 11. The world is one unforgiving place. Any kindness and happiness you can find, cherish the living heck out of it, though don't be surrprised if it turns and bites you even then. You're just a little girl, trying to make it by... but the world isn't merciful enough to care. You must live and fight, since now that Lee's out of the picture, who's going to do it for you?  NOT for the faint-hearted. I know that's an easy thing to say but seriously, season 1 in its entirety is tame compared to just the first episode of this season! If you don't want something that's going to smack you in the face with how crappy and evil life can be over and over again, stick with season 1 and appreciate how easy everything is for Clementine.  EPISODE TWO - A House Divided  Another finely crafted episode. The title is wonderfully apt, the story is gripping and filled with clever foreshadowing and the experience as great as ever. Going into detail would mean big spoilers but this is one of the greatest examples in the series of me feeling truly torn as to what action is best to take. One question from a certain character even had me at a loss for words when it came to dialogue options. Oh and I shouted 'DON'T MAKE DO THIS' at the screen. I hadn't done that before.  A House Divided will divide your very soul as to what is best to do, in other words. It's a very character-driven episode and has some of the most emotionally effective scenes and interactions in the series. If the story's making you bond with these characters as much as it does with me when you play it, you'll probably see what I mean when I say all this.  EPISODE THREE - In Harm's Way  Ever wanted to feel like the game you're playing reached through the screen and ♥♥♥♥ing punched you in the gut 10 times? Here you go! You know what? All That Remains was tame in comparison to this! By far the darkest episode so far, that really drives home how harsh people become in the zombie apocalypse and how difficult that is for somebody like Clementine, who is very much still developing. Of course, she isn't just the little girl from season 1 any more. It was by force but she is shaping into more of an adult, quicker than anybody should have to. As such, she is very impressionable and what she's forced to do or believe will make a big impact. With the stakes higher than ever before this season, that's true now more than ever.  I would say this is the second best episode across both seasons thus far, beaten only by No Time Left. It perfectly captures all the fascinating directions Clementine's character development can go in one episode and many of the kinds of difficult choices she will face. Never before have I felt like such a terrible person while playing The Walking Dead. I've felt morally grey, sure but in this episode I actually felt like a straight-up ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥! You know they're doing things right when you try to do the right thing and still end up with that feeling. The fact that everything you do is a part of Clementine, a little girl's character development, makes it even more powerful. How far does your forgiveness go? Your mercy? Your faith? More than ever, this episode pushes those questions to the limit.  ---  I won't write thoughts on episodes 4 and 5 since I already summed them up above the first three episodes' comments. It's amazing how they make everything that happens during those three otherwise great episodes pointless. They are truly great episodes, if one just ignores the existence of 4 and 5... perhaps you could just play those episodes and decide that 4 and 5 never happened and dream of what could have been. At any rate, I don't see myself purchasing the future season 3. The Walking Dead game was a fantastic story that was tragically ruined in the span of episode 4, then buried even deeper during 5. Amid the Ruins of a once good game, there is No Going Back.

Ok the reason I don't reccomend this game has nothing to do with the gameplay. Game play on here is 9/10 in my book and very enjoyable. Great graphics, great graphics optimization, great gameplay, combat, and crafting. The game is great but the thing that ruins it is the energy system.  Crafting, gathering and well doing anything non- combat related costs a facebook style energy points that recharge by 1 every 4 mins and 45 seconds.   When you first start the game there is no energy meter (which is a bit misleading). After you reach a certain level in the game around 13-16 somwhere a yellow bubble mysteriously apears on the bottem of your screen. This bubble has aproximately 130 points in it. From this point on anything you craft or gather takes 1 point from this meter which is easily spent in 10 mins and takes around 11 hours to refill. Unless you cough up some cash and pay thier ingame store for energy potions that refill it.  This completely makes the game linear now you have no option but to fight and do quests or log off till it fills up. It ruins the game. If the store just sold weapon/armor packs (for slight advantage) and costumes (with crit bonuses) They would make more money cause only an idiot would spend real life money to be able to craft, then pay again to get ingrediants, then pay again to craft components to craft the item to level crafting.. Putting a timed energy meter in a game like this is not only stupid but game breaking. This game is 70 levels per skill this would cost 10's of thousands of energy poitions to level them up without interuption. This is a bad business descission on thier part and they will lose player base constantly to it. Great game till the energy meter kicks in and all the nodes in every zone you encounter are untouchable leaving noting but quests and combat, congrats you took an open world game with lots to do and fun at every turn and turned it into a linear hack and slash. Good Job, you should be our next president.

Let's make this an honest review, alright? I will not crack stupid jokes about waifus and pantyshots, and you, dear reader, will take this review not as my scores states, but by taking into account what I said here.   You see, this is not really a bad game. Its problem lies in the fact for WHO this game is. This game is, what I call, a 'You Tube watching' game. The game is very short. A competent player can beat Normal Mode in around an hour or two at best. The enemies are not especially bright in how they fight and most of them can be beaten with one move -> Iai Slash. Once you get this to lvl 3 you can just hold the shoulder button and whack everything with Y. It defeats everything. Some bosses are cheap, but that is until you learn that every attack be Just Guarded. Pretty much till you get to the last Boss, you can dodge all boss attacks. At the last boss, you just Just Guard everything she does.   THAT SAID HOWEVER  This game is pretty good for zoning out. Like, you are watching a You Tube video with a lot of talking and not much going on the screen. Its as if you are playing an MMO, just for people that like Hack and Slash games. The game however is VERY SHORT. Seriously, not much content on. Like an Arcade game. You beat the game and leave it. Beat it again and so on. Like a NES title of the old times.   Can I recommend the game? No, I cannot. I got it personally because I like to fight something while watching long game reviews or when I listen to long talks on YouTube and such. I view a lot of movies that are mainly 30 minutes of someone talking and not showing anything on the screen. And this game, when set to easy, is perfect for this task.   I bought it for 2,50 on a sale. It is not a bad game, just incredibly short and for a niche audience. Requires not much skill nor effort to play. Ideal if this is what you are looking for.   Aside from all this -> During the one playthrough I made I encountered no bugs at all. ...well except for two annoyances, but these are not bugs. The camera will sometimes spaz if during a boss fight you dodge towards the enemy and hit their model, as if trying to pass throught them. Also, if you die and go to Upgrade menu - the game does not stop there. The enemy boss, even after beating you, will continue their assault despite the fact your character is dead and cannot be hit.   Consider this buy if you are looking for an easy, short game. Its fun to slash people. The moves look great and you feel powerful. But that is all.

This has to be very nearly pitch perfection in a game: it knows exactly what it is and plays it to the hilt. From the left-and-right-mouse-button mechanic to the stick figure graphics and the stereotypical Kung Fu Master narration, it is both extremely well-polished where it matters (gameplay) and exactly as well-polished as it needs to be everywhere else, since its simplicity combines with its intrinsic sense of humor to give it a sort of charm.  Eh, who am I kidding. It ain't art, but it'll make you feel like a shaolin tiger after only a few minutes of clicking buttons.

DLC is hit or miss, Anarchy's Children is only costumization, buy it if you like stupid clothing/masks such as short shorts and a welder's mask, Rulers adds comes with fun new weapons, a narrative mission, and new enemies I havent seen yet (Balance is questionable for most, but get moddable ruler turns mod to balance), and Shen's Last Gift, new narrative mission that gets a bit repetitive, but really fun new class. For 4 extra dollars extra the digital delux is worth the sale price. Technical problems are apparent for some rigs, but with FX 6300 and R9 280 I'm running fine, refund if it is excessive.  Hopefully more developers take the mod friendly approach, I can't wait to see how great this game can be later down the line. One of my favorite franchises of all time (yes, I played ufo defence), and this doesn't dissapoint.

The music really goes well with the gameplay, it also let's you add custom music in case it becomes repetitive.

If you like anything to do with cyberpunk, Dragonfall Director's cut does it right. I mean, they really hit a grand slam on every aspect of the game. It's a lot like an interactive novel, except you get to play out the fight scenes. Plot driven Character development is the best I've seen, ever, in any game. And... if that's not your thing... don't talk to your team mates. If you do listen to them, you'll feel for them. I have to say, not once, in any game ever, have I felt an attachment to any character, until now. I get chills playing through most of the missions, reading the deep well written back story for each and every mission. The AI seems semi-intelligent as well... they always go for my shaman, as he's my weakest link, survival-wise. And choices... so many choices. Many skippable runs, some minor, some major. So many different ways to overcome obstacles... locked door? Send a drone throught a vent, or have your 'muscle' break it down, or have your decker hack the security terminal, or have your shaman astral plane and see the keys that have been pressed, etc. If you don't like to read, well, you may not like this game. However, it really is a good mix of reading a compelling story and interactive elements.

I have chosen to not recommend this game due to it’s poor optimization for PC. I should not have this poor frame rate with a 780 Ti when trying to run the game on Medium settings at 1440p. Even with my Gsync monitor the game stutters. All the cut scenes play at over 60FPS with a high reaching the 100s a few times, but when the gameplay resumes so does the choppiness.  The game when you overlook the optimization problems can be a fun quick thrill. I was excited to play this game and did my best to enjoy it rough edges and all. Looking back I don’t think I could have made it through the whole campaign if I knew my issues would persist after two fresh driver installations and tweaking the settings.

I don't usually do this type of stuff but I feel like Toren deserves it because it's weird. I say weird because I don't know why I liked it.   I completed the game in about two hours, two very good hours mind you, and even now that I've finished it I don't know what it was I just played. Toren is supposed to be a Adventure, platform something dead baby, and it is, except for the dead baby, that I can say for certain. You do some simplistic puzzles that aren't to hard to figure out and they get more clever the further you get into the game. Some puzzles I overlooked that made them somewhat harder, like having to draw a symbol on the ground in pitch black darkness, thing was that I didn't notice was that you could get a god damn torch :/ But the platforming and puzzles are solid and fun and you get this very Indie feel as you play the game, character is clipping through everything for one. But aside that I really enjoyed the gameplay and thought it was fun.   And that must be the reason I liked the game because it was the thing I didn't expect to enjoy, thus it surprised me. Why I bought the game was not because of some gameplay video, it was because of the story. I don't ♥♥♥♥ing know what the story was, I really don't. I'm more confident in guessing the contents of my friends balls then I am starting to guess the story of this game. Now, this isn't necessarily a bad thing, it's a good thing, it adds replayability if anything. So I may not have understood what happened in my first run through the game, but maybe the second time will yield some results or the third. It's intresting for a game to add replayability through story rather then unlockables or new gear and the like. It could also get boring as you are forced to to the same exact thing over and over again, but since the game is so short I don't see that as a problem. Last thing I want to mention it that I really liked the character model, or what they did with it. The character is going through 'stages' of human life (Baby, child, teenager, adult) and the model changes when I happens which I find really cool, especially the last one (I have a thing for capes!).  All in all, a very enjoyable experience, I'd rather have two really good hours of gameplay and story then having to play ninety hours of things I don't want to do just to reach the end, I'm looking at you Dragon Age Inquisition! I heartly recommend this game!

I usually value my time too much to write reviews, but I'll break my rule for the first time and write one for this piece of art.  This has been a great adventure, and I don't think that even the next Star Wars will make me reach the level of emotions I felt in this game.   Telltale Games managed to deliver more than a short story in the Borderlands universe, they gave us the opportunity to experience one of the best story since Tiny Tina's Assault on Dragon Keep. I actually laughed in front of my computer for the first time in a while (sad story) and the 5th episode gave me tears of joy at the end. I can only wish and hope that this is not the end & that Telltale will offer us an amazing adventure again.  Universe, soundtrack, humor, characters, fun, tears, everything is in there, just take a few hours of your time to live that.  Thanks Telltale Games, you did an amazing job.

What about some undertanding of what causes changes in oxygen levels in the water?   Any way. It took a few minutes to work out. A few more minutes of watching and hatching baby fish and I had lost interest. I liked the illustration though.

I got sucked into this incredible throwback to the 80s / 90s era of Gauntlet like gaming a few years ago. So much so that I still have a relevant review of the game online if you search for 'Beetlebear Heroes of Loot'.  Back then, I got myself to the healthy level of 240 or so.  I recently learned that OrangePascal was planning on releasing the game into the wilds that we call steam and salivated at the delight that one of my favorite games was not only being released on steam, but that it had been polished and refined to the point that would make me pee my pants trying to achieve the highest of scores.  After playing it on my usual Twitch stream, I found myself playing it for hours AFTER I hung the stream up, trying to get that top HiScore spot.  And I did.  And I will, every day of my life, defend my HiScore in this game.  So bring it challengers, The Beetlebear is waiting for a worthy opponent.  10/10 Would beat your hi score again.

First part of this review is mainly about ''errors and everything bad'' during day one. Bought the game yesterday and preloaded. Today before work downloaded last bits of the game and went to work. Came back from work, had lunch, made coffee and installed Nvidia Game Ready drivers (they weren't available at 6am CET) Game loaded flawlessly. Set everything on high. Game started and so far I had great and beautiful experience in the land of Oros. No crashes. No errors. Nothing.  Don't take those bad reviews to heart. They clearly lack the patience and understanding.   Speaking of setting and Far Cry franchise, I have to admit this is my first encounter. I've heard of the games but never had any interest in them. But game set in Stone age with a little twist of beast mastery? I just couldn't let it pass me by.  Heard melee combat is rudimentary. I really don't mind.  Heard the story is weak. So far I met only Sayla and had few of Udum and wildlife encounters. Scripted stuff is fun and gorgeous looking.  World is gorgeous, I haven't took this many screenshots dunno how long. Will it be your cup of tea? Can't really say but I recommend watching some videos and exploring what game offers (that's what I did) And don't trust Steam user review score (standing at Mixed now, was Mostly negative at 6am CET)  Remember, games are about fun and for me, immersion into another world.  Far Cry Primal delivers on both.

Emily is Away is a beautiful game about a character that you play, but not necessarily have full control over, and a girl named Emily. At its heart the game is about change, as you go year by year, chapter by chapter, you see Emily holding on to the the past, whilst she talks about everyone else changing. Although this game is extremely short, with only about 30 minutes of real gameplay and not much replayabilty, it is sweet, thought-provoking and emotional. Combine this with, what I like to call, the fake typing mechanic, it becomes an interactive experience. The only improvement I could ask for is for your choices to have more of an impact on the ending, but that is one short-coming that can be completely ignored. All in all, this is a short, sweet and powerful game, that definitely has become a must play.

This game is well worth the money; you will sink in at least 100 hours of time to complete everything in this version of the game. The combat is challenging (I get rekt pretty regularly on normal mode) and requires more than simple skill spamming, while the story is complex with a multitude of conversation options. If you like RPGs, you must have this in your library. That being said, there is a steep learning curve and may require some Google research to figure out some of the mechanics, as well as the need to frequently pause in order to carry out commands.

A cute and suprisingly fun game. Although it will nag you regularly about it, the game is playable without creating an EA account. If you already have an Origin account with another game, just furnish your Steam product key to link it. Loading times are much longer than I expected, but everything ran fairly smoothly after that. On my first Sim, I noticed a lot of frequent lags as we were waiting for the Grim Reaper to show up. I'm not sure whether it was some background process on my machine or the game itself, but considering that it didn't start until a few days before Death came knocking, I'm tending to believe it's the game. Actions would lag for a second or so a couple or three times per minute. Really annoying. Well worth the price if you can catch it on sale, but definitely not worth the full price tag, especially considering that Sims4 is due out in a couple of months.  (EDIT)Forgot a couple of things. When you register the game, you'll get 1000 Sim Points (worth about $10), but it will take some hunting to find them. They'll be on your store account page and will need to be redeemed before you can use them. EA is still EA, so expect to have additional content (microtransactions) dangled in front of you anytime you try to build/buy stuff in-game. Annoying, but totally in keeping with EA, so not surprising.

app_name: Unreal Tournament: Game of the Year Edition
review_text: Unreal Tournament is my favorite game of alltime with years of playing prior to steam, over the years since its 2000 release ive proably clocked over 4000 hours of arena based combat with the sheir number and veriety of maps in the tournament alone not to mention the additional maps that are just left for you to play on or off line with bots, it really puts modern games into perspective with their minimal number of maps that come with them.  Mechanically this game will run butter smooth on any computer (even my POS i.3 laptop with intergrated graphics). allowing yout to perform satisfing jumping headshots on Xan gregor in custom bot matches. Furthermore the Addtion of mutators to bot matches allows for some fun quirckie gamplay e.g. instagib fatboys.  Not to mention the Chaos UT Dlc which adds countless new guns and maps to the mix including crossbows, new mega weapons and chainsaws to name a few.   Its fair to say that i always played unreal over Quake however i recoment any of the arena games or live as they also capture the same spark that unreal had as one of the truly best dynamic FPS arena shooters of alltime.

http://www.gamefaqs.com/PC/626629-street-fighter-x-tekken/reviews/review-153085   Easily one of the most unfairly hated games in years. To me it's right up there with the best fighting games of this gen but people loved to hate it because Capcom made it and god forbid there is 100% optional DLC for it. The game itself is a blast to play. It's not SFIV, it plays very differently with new fighting game mechanics. The Tekken roster is what really shines the most, they're the most fun characters to play as, especially their more up close combo heavy style. The only downside is PS3 gets Bad Box Art Mega Man and Pacman so I bought that version too when it was dirt cheap, no regrets. But the PC version is another great modern Capcom port where they went all out in making it the best version out there.

Note that I am writing this review after the game has been down for several months. I am not lying when I say that I had a lot of fun with this game, despite my unimpressive 3 hour play time of the game, as I bought Minimum about a week before it went down. Even though the katanas really need to be balanced, there needs to be more maps for all modes, and having a level 5 upgraded weapon can make some players near invincible, Minimum will most likely be updated in the near future to fix one or all of these issues. Before I forget, I do love the armor system and how you rank it up ingame by collecting. Similarly with the aforementioned weapons, they rank up ingame as well.

Incredible, as someone who knows very little bout the Alien franchise and haven't even seen all the movies and is still so into it throught all that 'it's only nostalgia' ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥t out the window! This is the only game i've ever considered to be genuinely scary, the only horror game to literrly stress me the hell out casue it's so intense and realistic I forget i'm playing a game and my heart is pounding! The only horror game to make me wanna give up because the Alien us such an incredible Apex predetor, never have I felt the Alien is buggin' out or acting outside what it's boundries should be (over powered/over sensetive-wise) I regret ever bothering w/ Resident Evil/Evil Within/ Dead Space/ Outlast/ DOOM, granted there are more and a lot of the games I mentioned are solid games I enjoy but hardly 'Scary' horror elements, sure. BUt hardly close to the experience this game gives. You HAVE to be cunning, swift, fearless, and curious to make it in this game. You're an engineer which is badass and you must use your mechanical knowledge and enginuity to survive which is way more then I can say for all the games I listed to compare- they are all sub-par action games w/ attempts of horror/survival but their lack of realism and surviving left them w/ nothing left to do but cram action and huge gun fights.  Hats of to this wonderful game. It truly is a game of wit and endurance and i've yet to even play the expansions hahah, I totaly recommend this game to anyone but watch Aliens atleast (the second one because it recaps all the story and what goes down and the Marine part of the film is classic it always makes me laugh.)

Well, I did not know who Airtight Games was up until now but doing 2 consecutive walkthroughs of Soul Suspect will certainly make me remember them for a while! This is a third-person view adventure game that employs some stealth elements and lots of crime scene investigation gameplay mechanics.  The story follows Ronan, a con-turned-detective who has just been murdered and whose soul - for some reason - cannot leave this world and proceed to Heaven where his wife awaits. We soon learn his killer is the one he was originally after - a couple of cutscenes later his memory is refreshed and he is certain he needs to solve the mystery of the Bell Killer who has been haunting his residential town for a while so that by solving the murder case he can peacefully enter Paradise.  Now, when playing this game I was constantly thinking of the adventure games released by Telltale Games. Why? Because the puzzles are easy and there is a great emphasis on the story immersion. Unlike those Telltale adventures Murdered allows you to control the protagonist and freely walk around in Salem.   Still, I wouldn't really call this an open-world journey as many places are only accessible if the story demands it - the 'hub area' is mainly for collectibles and a couple of side missions where you help other ghosts finish their business and ascend once and for all.  Then come the demons. Certain sections require you to hide from the beasts and approach them while they turn away - execution is not mandatory but collecting evidence and other 'artifacts' in the areas require you to rid Salem of them for good.   While the game is not difficult, at all, and lacks any real combat it does have a great story. I almost completed it in one sitting as I really wanted to see the antagonist dealt with. Murdered has a great atmosphere which is accompanied by a great score and very well done voice acting.  To summarise: if you are in for a great story and for something new and you do not mind that one playthrough gives you all the experience (a 2nd should only be done for the collectibles) then Soul Suspect is an easy grab - especially with a discount!

7/10    This game is interesting because of it's nemisis system. This is a system where you kill the toughest enemies (warchiefs) and they will be replaced by captins. Normal generic enemies are then promoted to captins. Therefore enemies and bosses get replaced endlessly. This is a feature I really liked about the game.   The game play feels as though someone mixed the Assassins Creed series with the Batman: Arkham series. AC free-running and Arkham-like combat. This gameplay feels smooth and well developed. The upgrades you can earn also make a real effect on the gameplay, it does not feel like you are just upgrading for the sake of it.   The reason I cannot give this game any higher than 7/ 10 is the story. I am a huge LOTR fan, so this was a very important aspect to me, and so was the way that the developers decided to do the combat. From the begining of the game the story builds and builds towards the final boss, that he is the most powerful besides Souron and must be defeated. What an anticlimax. When you finally get to this boss... it is a QTE... and not even a long one, about 5-6 commands. And that is it. All those upgrades you worked for were pointless in the end. No skill is involed in the fight at all. Exceedingly dissappointing.  Overall I have to say that I enjoyed this game right up until the final boss. I felt like collecting all the collectables and doing all the side quests would make me feel like the game was more complete when I killed the boss. Instead I felt as though I had just wasted my time. I would recommend this game if the story does not matter to you. If you are a LOTR fan then I would not recommened as the story just falls flat. The games combat is great and the skills you can earn are fun to use. There is a huge variety of enemies and animals that can be hunted and the nemisis system is wonderful.   Note to any developers: If you are making a game where it's whole story builds and builds toward the final boss, please please do not make that boss a QTE. Some elements are okay, for example, if you have to run up to them and press a command to hurt the enemy fine, but don't make the whole battle a QTE because it's not fun and requires no skill other than that you can read on screen commands.

app_name: Inherit the Earth: Quest for the Orb
review_text: This is a gem from the CD ROM era which was when I entered PC gaming  The sprite animation and character artwork is top notch for it's time and holds up to modern pixel games. It could have been made yesterday but it was published in 1994. It is also fully voice acted with lots of dialog.   The game itself is story driven, original, very innocent and corny to the point of being charming.

JULIA is a point and click sci-fi adventure game about a biologist named Rachel Manners waking up from cryosleep and discovering that she is the last of her scientific expedition on the ship. After saving the ship from an asteroid field she teams up with the ship's AI, Julia, and the team's scouting robot, Mobot, to find out what happened to the rest of the crew. This adventure will take them all over an entire solar system as they explore strange alien worlds. These worlds are beautifully crafted and the quality graphics really make for a strong and immersive atmosphere. The writing is also quite good, Rachel, Julia, and Mobot all have good chemistry together and are likable. All this combined makes JULIA one of those games that make you feel that you're a part of the journey and you want to see it through.  When it comes to the puzzles I'm going against the popular opinion in the Steam reviews so far and say that the puzzles overall suck. They don't suck in the Sierra sense of missing something and making the game unwinnable and there's no pixel hunting thanks to Mobot's hotspot button, but the puzzles are just more obtuse than they need to be because the tutorials for the puzzles tend to be kind of bad. There are several puzzles that actually have easy solutions but good luck figuring that out. The last half of the game is also filled with puzzles that are impossible to solve without trial and error which makes it a chore.   Just play with a guide and save yourself the headache because the really good atmopshere and writing make this game worth playing.

I can't believe I'm recommending this game. But I am.  After enduring the lackluster, ♥♥♥♥♥♥ writing of Borderlands 2, I was skeptical about the pre-sequel. I was ESPECIALLY skeptical about the sixty-dollar price tag. But I'm glad it all turned out well (sort of), in the end.  As it happens, the pre-sequel is a more balanced gameplay experience, and much more challenging - but in a good way - than its predecessors. Gone are the obliterating alpha strikes of badass enemies in BL2. There are far fewer WTF gear gates and gimmicky asshats who one-shot you regardless of spec. Now the ride is smooth, for the most part - the difficulty really spikes once you get into True Vault Hunter Mode, and the game will massacre you if you're ill-prepared. But on the whole, things are way more fun, and ten times more mobile.  The writing blows Borderlands 2 out of the water. I hated the writing in Borderlands 2 for reasons irrelevant to this review. The Pre-Sequel actually reduces the amount of inane nattering in your ears from characters trying far too hard to be clever (or worse, clumsily TELLING you to care about some problem or issue or person or whatever when no one has SHOWN you why you should).   In BLTPS, instead, it presents you with surprisingly dark storytelling, a nuanced world, and incredible pacing. This game is intense, and highly economical. Not a moment is wasted. It's still got some very funny moments, and I find the Scavs to be far more entertaining than the Bandits of BL2. Some of these Australians sound so authentic, it's like they're gargling a wallaby for some of these lines. Just deep, throaty stuff. Delightful.  One of the darkest corners of the plot regards the rarely-trod avenue of artifical intelligences and the ethical treatment thereof. I won't spoil anything, but it's chilling.  The cast of characters is amazing. Claptrap is hilarious, yet surprisingly poignant and tragic, in his own sideways fashion. Wilhelm is a refreshing breath of stoic masculinity in a medium plagued by try-hards and wanna-be ubermenschen alpha-nerd projections. Nisha is at first hateable and seemingly two-dimensional, but unlocking more of her backstory reveals not just a cute 'for funsies' sadist, but an actual born-again psychopath who suffered the kinds of traumas that make modern serial killers.  And then there is my absolute favorite, Athena. In a world starved for powerful, matronly female role models, Athena takes the cake - she is both sexy and professional, modest, yet bold. She doesn't mince words, and she makes her presence known through minimal effort.   In addition to the characters being stronger, there's also a great deal more individuality on the part of the PCs - your character responds to dialogue, to other characters, and there are plenty of in-combat call-outs geared toward specific partnerships (when one player activates their special ability, the other characters call out and notice). Little touches like these are what seperate good games from great games.  On top of all that, the environments are gorgeous and colorful, and the music is a FANTASTIC retro-future ensemble - think EVE Online meets Bladerunner meets When Mars Attacks!  I could go on and on. The game is amazing. Why it has a low metacritic score, I can't imagine. I hated it at first for the difficulty, but once I acclimated, and changed some of my bad habits from Borderlands 2, I loved it.  Give it a try. The price tag is steep, I know. Maybe wait until it goes on sale. But if you can afford it? Go for it - especially if you have friends to play with.

>Microsoft exclusive.  >Triple AAA game.  >Previous titles in series have great PC ports.  >Turns out to be one of the most disappointing PC ports in recent memory.  It doesn't end there, I am a huge fan of the Dead Rising series, owning all the console versions and pouring hundreds of hours into Dead Rising 1 and 2 cumulatively. This game is incredibly westernized and loses most of the depth and imagination of the first two, and is mind-numbingly easy.   Even the psychos are easy. This isn't Dead Rising if the psychos are easy.  The corny acting and story is no longer safeguarded by the fact that it's a Japanese pseudo-western game (a la Metal Gear Solid), it takes itself far too seriously and the narrative falls flat on its face at every oppurtunity. I know I sound like a weeaboo when I say this, but westernization hit Dead Rising 3 in all the wrong places.   If you want to kill zombies, this game's great for that.  If you want to play Dead Rising, this isn't it.  Don't buy at full price, kids.

Card City Nights is a lighthearted and simple card game. It reminds me a bit of the Final Fantasy 8 card mini-game. You and your opponent place cards on a 3x3 grid. You link your cards together via connecting arrows and, depending on the card effects and icons, can either attack your opponent''s health, disable their cards, increase your own health, or revive your own cards (for the most part.) You build your own card deck and unlock new cards as you go. The AI is pretty mediocre. It's enjoyable enough, but really would need another couple game mechanics and a better AI to really shine.  (Minor spoiler) The biggest negative I have against the game comes at the very end - the final boss is deliberately unfair. They even say straight up that they don't have to follow the same rules as you do. Just because the game's AI is ♥♥♥♥ doesn't mean that it's fair to balance it by straight up cheating.
This isn't a bad game to pass the time with, but if you want the legitimate games go and get Counter Strike Global Offfensive or Source.

This Game engine is flawed with performance issues for High-end gaming PC's but according to other reports seems to work best with low end CPU's and Video card combo.'s.  End results For Higher end gaming PC's: (AMD 6-core @ 4GHz; 8GB-RAM + ATI HD R2-290) > The game can become very laggy due to very, and I do mean very low FPS (5-10 FPS) depending on which area you are in. > Player experience is worse when in larger, big Space Ports with very busy traffic from many ships coming and going.  > Player experience is best when in deep space staring into nothingness. > A flurry of patches/fixes are coming in from Egosoft but so far, very few of them have nothing to due with performance gains. > Player experience for Patches/fixes that do address performance gains, range from negligible at worst, to mediocre at best. Still, for many players the game remains unplayable.  Conclusion: > This game is for you if you do not mind viewing this more as a 'turn based strategy game.' > If not; wait for the patches to fix the game 'The Way It's Meant To Be Played'. But lets face it; if it were that simple to fix, it would already have been fixed long ago and real performance gains may only be realized incrementally over a very, very long time. This, maybe even mores so if they have to port this over from Directx-9.0c to Directx 11.0 to fully fix the problem. I believe the reason they chose Directx 9.0c is because they thought they could use their experience with it as leverage for a reduced development time but when the code never gets 'Locked' due to continuing feature adaptations then this just stretched the development even more so. (7-years and counting). And of course Directx 9.0c is probably much cheaper to licence per game sold than Directx-11.

The second coming of Jesus tap-dancing Christ would not pull me away from this awesome game.   It is arguably the single greatest RPG I think I have played in 15+ years. And I have played most of them.  It has 4 minute fetch quests with more compelling story and interesting characters than the main story line of Skyrim.  There is a constant sense of exploration of wonder that actually manages to surprise you over and over again. Not since the days of Baldur's Gate II have I felt so consistently amazed at the places and things I encounter in an RPG.  And best of all, in the most positive sense of the expression: This is not a game for babies. There's racism, sex, cruelty, drugs, moral ambiguity, and more disturbing monsters than you've seen in any other game. You will not be coddled, your actions will have far reaching consequences -- many of them not immediately apparent, and if you walk into the wrong area and attack a the level 20 golem guarding a chest when you're at level 6, you're swiftly reduced to a blob of red paste in the forest floor.   The world design is incredible. It feels like a real world rather than a bunch of sewn-together set pieces. Not to mention the beautiful foliage. Makes Dragon Age III look like a golf course by comparison. The sound design also merits special mention. There's so much carefully crafted ambiance everywhere. Last but not least, the dynamic weather effects are beyond anything I have seen in a game to this date.   The whole game just oozes of the love CDPR put into it.   There are some minor warts, such as the controls which take a moment to get accustomed to, and the inventory interface isn't the most brilliant in the world, but they're barely worth mentioning.  12/10 - Game of the decade.

First of all, if you read reviews, check the time played by the reviewer.
