This game has every C&C fan waiting for. As I played all the C&C games and am a big RTS fan, this game has got me completely. It is hard in the beginning as it is familiar but also makes you think a bit differently and you need some time to learn and adapt to this RTS.  Please do so as it is a gem that will hook you up completely and drag you into the feeling of : more more more !!!  The graphics are eyecandy, and for the tacticians there is the satelite view wich let you look high above the map like a radar grid, but wich makes it very clearly tacticwise. The units are decent thought out and the time to react & counter to enemy units is fair.  for example, at one point i saw planes coming over and lost 2 buildings, i didnt know he had air and i didnt builded AA. the time in between the two bombing runs i quickly made AA infantry, made a stealth spotter recon vehicle to detect them and 2 AA vehicles. Next run he lost few planes and he needed to adapt so he tried coming in from the other side, luckily I had spotted him for a nano sec as he flew past a unit from the east and I made my units going to the east and could took a few out aswell.   It is very adrenaline, rewarding game that every RTS fan should try !!! A MUST BUY FOR THE C&C FANS OUT THERE THAT WANTED A NEW C&C FOR A LONG TIME !!!!!!  9/10

Well what can I say? Phr00t has a unique way of naming his games :) I have the 2 previous games, and when I realised 4089 was released I bought it immediately.  This game is more FPS than RPG, when compared to previous games. Granted I've only run around maybe 10 to 15 rooms altogether (I keep getting lost! I really need to map it out) so far, but I have not seen the same kind of 'RPG-ness' prevalent in previous games 3079 and 3089. At best you only can allocate skill points to your various attributes... and you can pickup and change weapons.  But there's no real 'inventory' (yet?) to speak of, no armour, not crafting etc.  The grappling hook is still there, and it's your starter weapon... so yeah you can swing around the place like Tarzan, reaching weird corners in rooms to find powerups and weapons.  As an FPS, it's fun... it's frantic... and sometimes frustrating cuz I keep going around to visited-rooms. You can do the 'go in guns blazing' thing or you can do the 'sneaking around' thing too... depends on which attributes you've selected to increase. Also, on the whole it has a pretty 'old-school' feel to it too. Many modern FPSes have in-game maps and waypoints to show you where to go... 4089 doesn't. I probably would have to get a pen and paper and map the rooms out and show how they are connected :p  One of the highlights for my games is that I keep hearing how the Overlord keeps trying to justify their actions. Kind of like the, in the movies, the serial killer keeps taunting you as you're running around.  One major new addition is probably the moddability support built into the game - right on the main menu there is a 'Room Viewer' and you can edit and create your own rooms to add into the game. Full instructions are provided at the game's website.

Dark Souls 3 is the third and final entry of FROMSOFT's Dark Souls series. This series is known for its brutal difficulty, beautiful art design, and amazing bosses and DS3 does well in carrying on the legacy and putting it to rest. As the final entry in the series DS3 has been given some pretty major expectations which I believe it does well in meeting. I will be rating the game on it's own merits as well as comparatively to the other games in the Souls Bourne series (being DS1, DS2, Demon Souls, and Bloodbourne)  -PROS- - Amazing art design - 'Souls Brand' Difficulty - Areas really feel like an interconnected world and not just strings of levels - Solid 'Souls Brand' combat - Some of the best Bosses in the whole series (EX: Dragon Slayer Armor, Nameless King, Lord of Cinder) - The best damn tutorial Boss in a video game (Ludex Gundyr) - Beautiful sound track - Feels like a return to the DS1 design formula. - Takes some good notes from DS1 and DS2 while still adding enough to be a unique title - Amazing story if you look hard enough for it  -CONS- - Difficulty sometimes feels cheap (looking at you Farron Swamp and Irathyll Dungeon) - Certain aspects of PvP are a little broken (Invaders have odds stacked against them, Poise is worthless, spamming is pretty much a garunteed win against 75% of players, Bleed builds are just dumb) - Also has some of the worst Bosses in the series (Looking at you Deacons of the Deep and Ancient Wyvern) - 'Good' Dual Weilding is limited to certain weapons and isn't as robust as Power Stances from DS2 - Story is hard to find and feels too similar to DS1 story/ lore - Very heavy on fan service - NPC quests feel dull and uninteresting (with one notable exception) - The Publisher (Bandai Namco) has an absolutely abhorent stance on banning people from the game (You can recieve a softban for interacting with a hacker even if you have no control over the encounter and you did nothing wrong. They have come out and said that people should know better... and they are refusing to reinstate people's accounts)  -Final Notes & Score- Dark Souls 3 does well to end the Souls series on a high note even through its flaws. It feels very similar to DS1 but still manages to have its own identity. The level design is excellent and the bosses are (for the most part) some of the best in the series. It does suffer from 'Rude Publisher Syndrome' and some obnoxious balance issues that will likely not be addressed for quite some time. But overall it is an excellent way to end the Souls series and has become one of my all time favorite games.  Score: 8.5/10

Hack RUN is an old school text style adventure game, under the premise of you being a master hacker. Unfortunately, hacking is not something you do in this game, but rather, you are a script kiddie extraordinaire. Find out what happens the deeper you go and all will be well?  The game is run off the windows console, which I didn't realize at first. That wasn't such a big deal, until the game started running my programs on my machine. It has the ability to open notepad, your default browser and open a url, and I'm not sure what else. I'm not entirely sure how I felt about this. Other applications can do the same, but under the premise of the game, it felt odd. Moving right along, the game consists of you tracing down information and using it to proceed to the darker and darker secrets.  Sounds: There is one sound that I know of. I don't know how to even rate it.  Graphics: It's all text, except for a few external websites that the executable relies on for you to finish the game.  Controls: What controls? It's all about typing.   Game Design: Well the game is extremely linear and achievements unlock as you pass each 'stage' or 'level' though the game. It actually calls them levels. I found that odd. It sort of breaks up the text adventure style concept and breaks the 'narrative' though I'm not sure I can call it that. You're not really hacking in this game, or even doing minor coding as in Hack n' Slash. You're basically an investigator with access to a computer and various scripts/programs are given to you to do more as you proceed further in the game.   Most of the information you need to progress is directly inside the game, and given to you if you look and follow the directions. However, there are a few external sources, where you must access websites outside the game in order to proceed further. This is likely done to enhance the experience and make it more 'real', but for me it made me wonder how long those sites would be up? If those domains aren't renewed or those hosting contracts expire, then the game becomes unplayable.   While there are commands built directly into the game, such as, hints, or even answer, sometimes those tell you that you would have to get the information off the aforementioned site. If the domains ever went down, the developer would likely have to release a patch, but more than likely if the sites go down, the dev is abandoning the game. So, that's something to watch out for.   Story: At first it reads as the typical underbelly of humanity hiding themselves from each other. As you progress further and further, you find out more and more and eventually you will reach a moment where you go, 'That's how you decided to explain it all?' Personally, because it's a rather disjointed story, I didn't really enjoy the game much. I'm familiar with running systems, and some of the restrictions on what I could do felt rather forced. Further, there was no real digging or searching to really find the information I would need to progress to the next level. The dev typically included only the relevant information. Who keeps their computer systems so clean and organized and free of spam, etc. At least if I had to start digging around, I would have felt like I accomplished something as opposed to correctly proceeding through a set of hoops.   Overall: 4/10 - Skip it unless you're really fasciated by fake commands in fake computer systems. Or grab it in a bundle. If you know anything about running a real network, you'll likely laugh at the 'commands' used to manuveur. Maybe that alone is worth it for some people to buy it. If you really like Text Adventures, then maybe you'll enjoy this. Otherwise, I seriously recommend taking a pass on this title. The base price is way too high for what it is.

After recieving a series of flat-out terrible updates over the last few years (except Gun Mettle), I cannot wholeheartedly recommend this game anymore. Valve has no idea what they're doing, and this might just end up killing the game. While this was, and mostly still is, a fantastic game, evenwith the recent course valve has set for it, I just cannot recommend it, not anymore.   Valve servers (arguably the most popular place to play the game) have been removed in favour of the new casual matchmaking mode, which doesn't work very well, and takes absurdly long to match you to a game (I once had to wait 15 minutes only to be matched on a US server).   Competetive is flat out broken, with a broken ranking system, and if someone leaves NOBODY gains experience or loses it, which leads to people just leaving if they lose. The ranking systems is simply grinding where you lose xp by losing and gain by winning. This is ridiculous and leads to people leaving when losing so they dont lose their xp. This broken ranking system would have been acceptable from just about any other dev, but not from Valve. Remember, this is the company that made CS GO and dota.   Valve doesn't seem to play this game, as evidenced by balance changes that just flat out broke many weapons (like the Bison's main feature getting removed because it was a 'glitch', despite the tips on the loading screens talking about it for over 5 years). Or buffing the mediguns to run at the speed of scouts like the quick fix, rendering said weapon useless. Valve doesn't understand the casualness many people love it for, like the sandvich heavies, or the market gardeners (playstyles that are simply impossible in casual).  It's no longer enjoyable to me, updates have ruined that. I cannot wholeheartedly recommend this game anymore. 2 years ago I would've said this was one of the best shooters out there. Now, I can't say that. I have been playing this game since late 2011, and it really saddens me to see where this game is heading.  This overwatchification needs to stop.   Update: November 2016, no improvements. Still cant join ad hoc or switch teams.

As with any Ubisoft game it is tied to Uplay, but this is something I've learned to accept.   I had given this game a negative review long ago because of the Uplay attachment, but mostly because at the time it also had a limit of downloads, making it essentially a rental rather than a purchase. Yesterday when I downloaded it again I saw no mention of this limit. Kudos Uplay.   At it's regular price it is a steal, and I wholeheartedly recommend it if you are looking for an intricate builder/manager game with minimal combat [if you're playing outside of the campaign, combat becomes entirely optional].  Would definitely recommend looking up some tutorials on youtube though.

Ah Tomb Raider Legend, the first game developed by Crystal Dynamics, creators of the famous Legacy of Kain series (e.g. Soul Reaver). I remember I was not concerned that different people would continue the series because I played those games and really enjoyed them. The story involves Lara's mother, Avalon, and the famous King Arthur's sword - excalibur. CDynamics promised that Lara will go back to what she does best - raiding tombs and they kept the word. Game mechanics are easy to learn and the overall game is a lot of fun (there are 3 levels where you ride a motorbike)! What's important to mention is the Croft Manor returns where you can practice movements and uncover few secrets. The graphics are very nice, music is very good just like in every Tomb Raider. I think every fan of the classics was satisfied with what we received. Finally is time to write few words about Lara as she is essential to the series. In my opinion she is great, she's still acrobatic and tough and still beautiful (her original creator was brought back to help). Miss Croft has new voice which I personally like it a lot and think it fits her character perfectly. New developers surely did a great job bringing back Lara from her downfall so that we could once again feel the thrill of her adventures.

Remember when you were a little kid and you had army men, often engaging in crazy battles between transformers, Batman, and even dinosaurs? Those timeless days spent by yourself, picking up tiny pieces of plastic and following along with insane campaigns of your own demented design?  That's sort of what it's like playing Dino D-Day. You might be spraying tiny carnivores with your tommy gun one second, and the next, you'll be ragdolled against a wall, instantly slain by a flying goat. Or you might be courageously defending an ally with your trusty M1 Garand, clips flinging out of your weapon hither and thither, when suddenly you hear a mighty roar behind you, and you are mowed down by the twin chain guns of the Tyrannosaurus Rex. These things have happened to me and will assuredly happen to you.  The game is just great, goofy fun. You'll never feel TOO mad losing in this game, because there's always something dopey to laugh at. Even the loading screens, which are for my taste, a bit long, are still dominated by silly wartime documents detailing the Nazi war machine with their new reptillian additions. I am particularly fond of the newspaper article concerning a dino rendition of a grand Wagnerian opera that goes horribly wrong.  In terms of gameplay, the multiplayer is indeed frantic, and requires you to do some thinking. If you choose the wrong class for your situation/map, then that could spell doom for your team. Alternatively, a well-placed tank dino might be just what you need to hold the point long enough. And although not many people necessarily play games with that much thought, I am confident that the construction of the class system is actually well-balanced and caters to any play style.  Going back to that previous point, some may argue that certain classes are too powerful. To that I say, 'phooey!' Each class has a counter-class, and the sooner you realize this, the better you'll play. As the most specific example, I've heard many people complain about the Allied character with the Garand. Yeah, he's pretty strong, especially in the hands of an expert marksman. But you put him in front of a tank dino and he's toast. Really. My point: if you're sucking, it's your own freakin' fault.  If you don't have the DLC (and I don't, although I'm going to pick it up soon), there are three basic match types: deathmatch, king of the hill, and demolition. The T-Rex spawns on a couple maps, and adds a whole new dynamic to the gameplay. The maps are also well-made and don't allow players to hide anywhere indefinitely, save for the flying raptor, who often needs that upper hand/deception to stalk and make great kills.  Anyway, if you're on the fence, I am all about this game. It's really excellent, and incredibly rewarding to people who play it either casually or otherwise. The only downside is that there is not a huge amount of people playing at the moment, which is really a shame, because this game rocks. I am looking forward to what other games the developers make. Too often I see Terraria dismissed as '2D Minecraft'. In fairness, it starts very similarly. You start by chopping down trees (though with an axe. What kind of idiot chops down a tree with their fist?) and building a wooden house to protect yourself from zombies at night. However, Terraria quickly distinguishes itself from Minecraft, mainly due to the gameplay.  Terraria, like Minecraft, is a sandbox adventure game. You collect items to craft new items, and to protect yourself from the monsters roaming the world. Mining plays an important part as well, most ores and enemies are found below the surface of the world. However, while Minecraft is a much more creative focused game, Terraria focuses on the adventure element.  For example, there are 4 main types of weapon in the game: Melee, Ranged, Magic and Summons. Each of these has several different weapons of varying styles. For example, the Ranged class contains both Bows and Guns, as well as a few other unique weapons. Each weapon may have an effect, such as the Molten Fury, a bow that turns your arrows into Flaming Arrows. Want to shoot flaming arrows with other bows? No worries, you can craft Flaming arrows separately. What's more, certain armour sets can boost certain weapon types, and later in the game, you get armour sets with 3 different head pieces to boost different weapons. You can also reforge your weapons and accessories (which are also plentiful in number and variety) for better buffs, or sometimes even combine them to get better weapons or accessories altogether.  But this weapon system would be useless without an array of enemies to fight, and Terraria has them in spades. Throughout the world, there are many biomes, ranging from the simple Forest biome, to the Dungeon, to the Underground Corruption, to Hell itself. Each biome, as well as having it's own blocks and materials, has its own unique set of enemies. Some biomes are tougher than others, such as the Corruption vs the Forest, or Hell vs the Dungeon. And it is all to easy to die. But the game never feels unfair. If you die, it's because you ventured into an area you weren't ready for, or you didn't pay attention to an enemy, or you simply got cocky. And despite constantly upgrading your equipment, there will always be a new set of enemies in the next biome to push you to your limits.  You never feel without direction either. You start with a single NPC - the Guide. He is invaluable to newer plyaers, as not only does he give you advice on what to do next, but also can show you what you can craft with materials, and what you need to craft with it. Crafting is also very simple. When you open up your inventory, your crafing menu also appears alongside it. Your crafting menu shows what you can craft at thie current time. Simply click on an item to craft it. If you need to use a specific crafting station, all you have to do is stand by it, and the additional crafting recipes will appear.  There are other NPCs you can get aside from the Guide, such as a MErchant, and Arms Dealer, a Demolitionist - and those are just the start of many, many NPCs who will come to your base. All you have to do is prepare a room for them, and fulfil their conditions. They're pretty simple, and for the most part you'll just get them by playing the game. For example, to get the Arms Dealer to appear, you first have to find a gun. What's more, the NPCs can sell different things at different times.  That's another thing Terraria has - time and events. Whilst the progression of the day is nothing spectacular, Terraria has a load of random (or triggered) events that can challenge you if you're not prepared. Such things include the Blood Moon, which causes Zombies to be able to break your doors, or the Goblin Invasion - that one speaks for itself. Several of these events have unique drops from enemies, or other effects in the world.  The bosses are also all epic. Even the first one, which you'll likely encounter randomly, is a sight to behold (fans of the game will aprecciate the pun there). They're all a challenge when you first encounter them in a playthrough, and will have to be refought several times if you want those rare drops. Most of the drops from bosses aren't TOO rare though, that would be cruel. And their music... oh their music is sublime!  In fact, all the music is. It's superb, spectacular! It flows seamlessly from one biome to the next, from a calm forest background music to the tense chords of a boss theme. It's all epic.  And all of your work pays off in the creative department. So many blocks, most of which can be fashioned into background walls, and many havig a furiture set. There's wooden, honey, steampunk, cactus, flesh, gothic... the list goes on. You can place mechanisms in your creations too, with the 3-colour wiring system.  This does bring me to some of the negatives though. First up, the wiring system is not as flexible as Minecraft's, and no way to move blocks with mechanisms. You can 'actuate' them, putting into and out of the background, but that's it. You can also teleport NPCs and mobs around using teleporters, but again, not blocks.  It can also be hard to find specific materials sometimes. Even in a small world, where the materials are in lower quantities but higher conecntrations, it can be difficult to find what you need. That's not to mention how you need at least 2 worlds to find all the ores and unique biome materials.  Farming mobs and bosses is also practically required to get rare drops, particularly if you're going for specific classes. For example, the eaarliest Summoner weapon is the Slime staff, which you get by defeating slimes. However, the staff has a drop rate of 1/10000. Even with an AFK farm, it can take up to half an hour to get. And if you have to farm a boss for a rare item, it can get tedious, particularly since you'll have to farm for the materials to get the boss summoning items.  It can also be difficult for a complete beginner to know how to do things and what's dangerous, since aside from the guide's general advice, there are no in-game instructions, and while basic control is simple, things like opening the housing menu are a bit cryptic to newer players.  That being said, the pros WAY outnumber the cons of this game. In fact, several of the cons are going to be removed in the next update, such as being able to select items that you don't want to deposti in chests with the deposit all command, or being able to use items in chest for crafting - or most importantly of all: Steam integration!  My advice is: get this game, play this game, then play it several more times. It may have one less physical dimension than Minecraft, but it's gameplay has several more to make up for it.

If you like your puzzles difficult, densely packed, and well designed, the Witness is a wonderful game. Be warned though that if you need story, context, or variety in your puzzle format, the Witness can be to some a tiresome slog. The game has beautiful clarity of purpose across every connected component of the location, puzzles, and mechanics, but that purpose is beautiful delivery of puzzles. There's not much in the game but walking around in silence, and dragging lines across your screen.  Its best feature is the difficulty ramp, one of the best I've seen in a long time. It's engagingly difficult, but with variety as well - you at times go from working through a difficult puzzle for 30 minutes to burning through an easy sequence of 5 in under a minute.  Throughout the content I've seen (more than 20 hours worth, about a main-story-and-a-half worth of puzzles), the design in the Witness has so far never felt unfair. While I very much liked both the Talos Principle and Fez, they both felt to me like their difficulty spiked somewhat in the later, optional puzzle content. The Witness never has for a moment to me (though admittedly, I'm not done). In addition, the brilliance of the many independent puzzle sequences is that being temporarily stumped is just an opportunity to go explore and solve puzzles elsewhere.  Again though, if you need a story, context, or a fundamental variety in mechanics to get you through the 20+ hours of difficult puzzles - and I really wouldn't blame you - best hold off.

Aside from giving War of Cybertron a thumbs down, I think this game deserves some mercy. I'm ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥tting after some Jack Daniel's so stay with me for as long as you can.  So I really liked WFC, it had an amazing campaign much better than the usual ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥t games we get. Picking up from rthe last game, robots escaping a dead planet and scavagening any energon they can find. Optimus searching for refuge and Megatron being a general d-bag and not letting them leave. I thought this was a prequel to G1 or Prime but as I've heard it's a game that leads in to the terribly developed, Rise of the Dark Spark, which is a horrendous tie-in with the Bayverse movies. How did that happen? I have no idea.  Campaign was good, the storyline wasnt really much for me to remember as the first game was but the change in enviroment was great. One thing I absolutely loved was Metroplex, how often do we get to see that guy? How they managed to work him into the storyline was awesome. I would have liked to see some more characters though. Yes, playing as Optimus and Megatron is the primary focus but the side characters are just as important.   I did not like how there was no choice in character for mission nor was there any co-op campaign. There seemed to be more fun with two other characters and also how they can help (aside from their meager dps). Focusing on one character seems to make it not only a little more challenging but almost personal even though we did have side conversation all along. As for co-op I was really disappointed. I had a lot of fun in co-op back in WFC so to see I can't enjoy the campaign with a friend really cuts back on a lot.  Now High Moon did ♥♥♥♥ up. Again.  I will give them props for at least being respectful enough for giving us DLC but they failed to even patch the game. I played WFC and FOC on Xbox when both released and did not run into as many problems as I did on this platform. I have run into several instances where I was freefalling out the map, stuck in the floor, had to navigate around invisible walls, and other game breaking glitches. I had to reset my game numerous times or wait for 10 minutes just to continue playing. Not only that but some game interactions don't even work, like when Megatron is in Soundwave's lab.  I will stress this again like I did in my WFC review, don't play multiplayer. Don't play in a public game. The multiplayer community is still around but there are players using infinite shield, FPS unlocker, rapidfire, and other unfair hacks to ruin the game. And just like in the last game, High Moon will do nothing about it. This game was dropped as soon as it was released much like it's last game which is still very disappointing.  Recommended for campaign use only.

An excellent game, though for different reasons than its predecessor. The emphasis here is on extended stealth sequences punctuated by brisk, risky sniping segments rather than the nonstop sniper action of V2. This is a much slower, methodical game with massive open levels (perfect for anyone frustrated by the linearity of V2) and a focus on using the terrain to your advantage. However, the player is given great freedom over how they complete each objective, be it smart and quiet or dumb and loud!  Pros: - Large open levels, with optional side objectives and hidden areas. Exploration is dangerous but often rewarding! - Sniping, whilst not entirely realistic, is incredibly satisfying (when a single bullet gets two headshots and blows up a panzer's fuel tank you'll understand...) - The bullet cam is back and gorier than ever. Muscle and nerve endings are modelled on enemies, giving a greater sense of how damaging a sniper's bullet can be. The camera is now also fully customisable.  - Smart AI, brutal at the higher difficulties (though I do wonder why they don't just sound the alarm as soon as they find a friend's body...) - Greatly improved loadout menu, where you can use weapon upgrades scavenged from the battlefield to fine tune and tweak your rifle. - Beautiful North African setting - sort of an Indiana Jones aesthetic (you know, fighting Nazis in ancient ruins).  Cons: - Story is a little threadbare, often predictable and lacks the intrigue and morally ambiguous edge of V2. However, some may prefer the lighter tone, and the gameplay is so fun that you won't care anyway...  - Enemise sometimes feel too accurate, especially with automatic weapons. - DLC missions are good, but they are hardly standouts that justify their individual price tags. Only buy if you are hungry for more content.  Ultimately a fantastic addition to the franchise that isn't afraid to change up its gameplay. A must for sniping enthusiasts and people who want something different from their WW2 shooters.

Hello!  So I got this game after watching a Let's play of RoosterTeeth like a year ago. I don't play this game with friends and I've only played Escape mode. I enjoy playing because it's challenging. You get a very limited amount of ammo even on normal difficulty so you need to make every  headshot  count. Zombies are a bit buggy sometimes, like staying crouched, suddenly moving at a rapid speed, just wrecking you so you get massive aimpunch and aren't able to recover or in very special occasions simply not dieing. I like the variation in armory like having a Scar 20 or a M4a4 (if I'm not mistaken), revolvers or colts and also crossbows and compound bows!  Some positives and negatives from a solo player perspective:  Pro's: - Challenging - Great maps - Great assortiment of guns - Good voice acting (altough sometimes repetitive) - Maps last between 30mins to 1 and a half hours - Objective rotations (sometimes objectives are different than the last time you played)  Con's: - Buggy AI (zombies stay in a crouched position, don't die or simply demolish you) - Flashlight is too bright! - Objectives can sometimes be hard to spot or hard to get to - Accuracy of weaponry  All in all, great fun game, but if you expect top notch quality it might not be something for you. For €10,- I feel like it was a investment well spent.  Thanks for reading my review! :)

Papers, Please thrives on hard decision-making and emotional attachment. Should you be the most brutal immigration officer possible, in order to insure the wellbeing of your family? Pros >Imrpovement from EU/EW: Lots of customization, different languages and voices to choose from >Classes: They kind of bundled everyone up all nice and neat but they are basically your Heavy, Sniper, Medic, and Trooper classes but each are stylized to fit their needs. Grenadiers blow stuff up, Sharpshooters deal with pistols and sniper rifles, Specialists are Combat Medics/Hackers, and Rangers are your stealthy assault. Psi Troopers are fun, too. >Enemy Variety: From your basic human-like trooper to enemy Avatars there is enough variety to keep you on your toes. Sectoids haven't changed too much as far as tactics go, Vipers will yank you out of cover, MECs launch rockets but can be hacked but with Psi Troopers ANYONE can be hacked. >Character Pool: You can save units from one game to a character pool and when you play through another game you have a chance to get your units in another game or recruit them as Rookies. >Weapons/Armor: There's a lot and combining certain classes with certain armors and loadouts will give you a force that even the most ballsy Berserker will run the other way from. Magnetic Rifles and Exo Suits are fun to play with. It does require a ♥♥♥♥-whack ton of research though. Get as many Engineers and Scientists as you can. >The Avenger: Addons work better, have more upgrading power, it just all fits more seamlessly than a building you can't leave.  Cons >Bugs Gallore: From graphical bugs to game glitches, no game is without them on the first release. I did find some exploits in the game that can help you along the way but yeah, expect bugs >Hit Detection: Ever get a critical hit at 1% or a critical miss at 100%? Both times...with the same sniper... >Multiplayer: It's not that exciting and you can't import your character pool units or customize the ones in your character pool. >AI: Early in the game the AI (From Vet and up difficulties) takes shifts. It might be another bug but I have had enemies just stand there and do nothing while my units unload bullets into them. Didn't happen on Rookie mode and I played it to the end on Rookie and Vet. Currently going Commander and it's happened twice in the beginning levels. Like I said, might be a bug or a minor glitch but it was kind of like entering a pitbull in a ♥♥♥♥ fight.  Final Verdict: If you liked XCOM EU/EW or even if you played the super old XCOM games you'll love this game. I know I did and regardless of what Yahtzee says about it the game is still fun as hell. I just can't wait for the DLC to see what other classes they can add aside from the five they have now. Do yourself a favor and play it to the end and do not skip the final scene of XCOM 2. You'll see what I mean.

review_text: Blops 3 is really not as bad as everyone made it out to be. A solid campagin, of which I am now on my third playthrough, afew twists, and the standard, but not as linear levels, that we come to expect from CoD. Zombies is great, although ZNS is possibly one of the worst zombies maps ever, almost as bad as, dare I say it, TranZit. However the EE's have taken over, and the maps seem more geared to this aspect of zombies, this does detract somewhat from the general playing of some of the maps, Revelations and Gorod leap to mind. Finally, multiplayer. In my opinion, Treyarch took forward the the concept in Sledgehammer's AW multiplayer, and made the exo-suits feel balanced, instead of the constant flying around the map in AW. Plus the spawns are better, but not perfect. The supply drops add new weapons and outfits to the game, but none of these are so OP that your forced into microtransactions to spam out drops to get the new weapons. All in all, the best CoD since BO1 at least, I may even say that it's better than MW2 and BO1, as a complete game

app_name: DARK SOULS™ II: Scholar of the First Sin
review_text: If you played Dark Souls 1 and pretty much know it by the back of your hand, then dark souls 2 has a few surprises for you, new mechanics, stats, enemy locations and types. Scholar of the first sin made dark souls 2 much harder for newer players, so if it's your first time, just get a little used to the 'YOU DIED' appearing.  Invasions and co-op are different in it doesn't matter what your soul level is, it's now about your soul memory tier, which is the amount of total souls you aquired through out the game.  if you feel like you're starting to get the hang of things and want a greater challenge, then you can use the new bonfire ascetics and covenant of champions to raise the game's difficulty.  this game is not perfect, but it is an experience that you must try if you're into the souls series.

This game has given me so much feels no other game has.   At first I didn't like that there wasn't an abundant amount of saves you could do, as you could only save when you found a crow or raven (whatever it was a black bird). So whenever I died I had do approximately 10-25 minutes of game play again which I would then find another bird (so cross your fingers and hope one is near you! *says this like a salesman*) which I found very tedious!   However this game is like a book that you just can't put down and is just amazing each page or episode in this case. The plot just drives you and although you can't save constantly at the very least it does get better as you get further along the game. When it becomes more difficult (ex. killing a boss) you have a save near by so it is manageable if you have the patience and awareness of your characters (meaning if you're character has very low HP/TP/SP you may not want to fight any monsters if you can avoid it and go to the bar to fill up).   After reading and researchng of the game I found out that the game developer Ausin Jorgensen (also known as DINGALING) didn't provide a sufficient amount of saves as he wanted players to understand the feeling of loss in the game, and WOW DID HE ACCOMPLISH THAT! Yet at the same time I had so much to gain from this experience, it's such a beautiful game from the soundtrack, the mechanics of the game, theme, and everything else.   HERE'S THE INTERVIEW IF YOU'D LIKE TO READ IT ->   http://www.gatheryourparty.com/2015/01/20/an-interview-with-austin-jorgensen-lisa/   Also just a heads up this game is actually a sequel from LISA: THE FIRST because when I played this game it never actually mention anything about Lisa which confused me (you don't actually need the first game to play the second as its more indirectly related then directly).   *SPOILER*  *Lisa is actually Brad's sister who committed suicide in the first game. He feels guilty for her death as he did nothing about it and therefore has illusions of her in this game. At first I thought the girl he was hallucinating was Buddy but after doing some researching I found out that it wasn't.   SO ALL IN ALL I VERY MUCH DO RECOMMEND THIS GAME IT'S JUST SADLY DELIGHTFUL AND THE ENDING ONCE YOU REALIZE IT WILL MAKE YOU WANT TO JUMP OFF A CLIFF. =)

Pros: Excellent Story Line  Likeable characters.  Great artwork  Cons: Gets somewhat repetitive after awhile  A few continuity errors.  Time gaps in storyline unfolding can make game feel too long.  I picked this up during the Anime weekend with a heavy discount. Even then I was a little unsure of spending even that much on something of this type. After playing it through all the way on each path, I must say it was well worth the discounted price, and probably the full price even. The story telling is engaging and interesting. In fact, many of the stories are downright fun. So many of these type of visual novels rely solely on explicit content to make it interesting. This story has that aspect (provided you install the patch), but my first play through did not have the patch and I felt the story was excellent even without. The stat management angle makes you have to put some thought into the story too, rather than just mindlessly clicking through.  As mentioned in the cons section, the stats management does become a bit repetitive at times. There are often long period between advancements in the storyline where you just go about your day to day activities. While in gameplay this can be a con, it can also be seen as mirroring real life, though some of the situations you're left questioning why an event that took place can have days go by without consequence or reaction. Keep in mind this game is LONG [9 months made of of 5 weeks, made up of 10 days] . With 4 paths to follow, either make a savepoint before you choose your path (roughly mid month 4 before you reach Pure Day, depending on stats), or be aware that you'll be redoing a lot of repetitive management to fulfill each storyline. Another con mentioned was continuity. I did find that if you did not trigger certain encounters at the proper times, they could show up out of sequence. An example being if you do not meet with a club leader at the right time before the first competition, you may complete the competition, then the next day visit the club leader and have a discussion about preparing for that meet you’d just completed.   After building a connection with these characters, I was pleased to find that a lot of them are in two other games put out by this author. I plan on playing through those as well so I can get more out of these storylines.   Overall 10/10

The Park, a simple name for a simple game that, despite being called a game, is actually more like a 2 hr long interactive novel.  I'm just going to cut right to the meat of the problem I had with this game. It wasn't scary.  Yes, it had some creeepy moments. Yes I was startled by a few of the jump scares (I hate jump scares btw, I feel they are lazy and in poor taste for a truly horrifying experience) but no matter how spooky the atmosphere, or how chilling the sound effects, it all means nothing when you realize in the first 15 minuites or so that you are in no danger. Without that overhanging threat, anything you see on the rides, anything that pops up behind you, anything the game tries to scare you with is just another puppet, dancing in front of you, its strings easily seen. Many will claim this is supposed to be a 'pychological' horror game and so shouldn't need to cause fear with a sense of threat. That would be fine, if they actually bothered to use psychology. I'm not talking about the mother's mental state. That's not scary. It's a predictable and overused trope. I'm talking about using the veiwers own mind against them, I'm talking about creating expectations only to pull aside the curtain and reveal nothing, or something that completely overwhelms. I'm talking about misdirection, not jumpscares, and things that creep around just out of veiw, not something that dances in front of you just to dissapear in the next moment. What I am trying to say is, almost every single scare in this game is predictable, and has been done before in better horror games. Seriously, it got to the point I was simply saying hi to Chuck the chipmunk and his stupid red glowy eyes (seriously, when will people learn that monsters lit up like christmas trees are not scary) everytime I saw him in the park.  Now, the above has nothing to do with the next issue I take with this game, which is it's directly tied to The Secret World MMORPG. That in itself isn't a bad thing. It's fine to use lore your audiance might not be familiar with so long as you explain it throughout the park. The game does this, but not to the extent it should to fully immerse someone who hasn't played TSW. It's as many reviews and the forums have said (in the most non spoilery way possible) 'What does that chipmunk and his freind have to do with the actual story?' If you play TSW, you'd have a better idea, if not, such as myself, it leaves you scratching your head and wondering what was psycholigical out of the the things you saw,and what wasn't. I'll leave my own theories out, but the forums are full of many, which means the story's world and supporting elements should have probably been better explained. Also, before anyone comes on here and says 'play TSW, it will make more sense then,' one shouldn't have to play the supporting title to fully understand what is going on in a stand alone game.  There isn't that much more to talk about really. I bought this game because it fits the theme of the season and for $10 basically saw it as the equivilent of going out and seeing a halloween movie. I don't feel ripped off, and enjoyed my stroll through FunComs haunted amusement park. But I was hoping to be frightened, and not subjected to the same horror tropes I've seen time and again. If you're new to the horror genre and have a high end rig that can run the graphics to the fullest effect, you will likely enjoy The Park. If you're a hardened horror vet like me, whose own nightmares are far more terrifying than anything modern horror tends to throw together, then you may want to give this one a pass.

I want to make this short and simple, as I haven't played much of this port to get into great detail about it, but here's what I've gathered.  Pros: - As a game alone, I prefer the physics of this one over MUA1. - DLC characters included. - Good port outside of menus, gameplay functions great other than one thing I did notice, which I'll mention right below.  Cons: - Dialogue audio cuts off before the end of characters' sentences. Can ruin the story. - Speaking of audio, I did not experience this myself, but I hear lots of people are having crackling issues. - Menus are mostly broken. - PRICE. If this was $15 or at the most, $20, I say it would be close to worth the cost with these issues, so long as they're going to be patched. - Multiplayer is completely broken. Immediately loses sync as soon as you begin your game.  I am really scared that Activision is going to leave us in the dust, but it's only day 1. I haven't heard how the PS4/Xbone versions are, but I just really hope they'll get on patching the PC version immediately, as we deserve better for the price we paid for this game.  I've played about 3 and a half hours of the campaign in the first game, and I go much more into detail with that one. This one didn't give me issues with choosing perks like in MUA1, but I did have problems messing around with the controls and trying to get a friend to join in on split-screen.   Overall, from what I've played on this entry, I like MUA2 as a game maybe a little more than the first, but as a port, I prefer the first. The gameplay mainly seems to be intact and the story doesn't get cut off mid-sentence. Still, you don't get the DLC characters with that one. The other reviews will completely slam this game, and rightfully so. Both titles are a mess right now, but don't let it discourage you. They're still more than playable, and I still have hope that these games will be patched, and once they are, I think the $60 bundle will be worth it. But as it stands, I just can't recommend buying this at full price.  EDIT: 35 of 40 people found the above helpful. Changing my review will wipe that, but I feel the need to keep this updated. The crackling audio stuff seems to have been patched. The speech issue stems from what I believe is having a refresh rate above 60hz. Force it to 60FPS/enable vsync and that problem should be gone. The controller issues seem to be fixed, although it's still not compatible with my Afterglow controllers, which made it a pain to use a third party program in order to have my girlfriend join with said controller via an online game in MUA1. Overall, the state of the game now is playable. Not worth the asking price, but if by some miracle it goes on sale, I recommend it. I do applaud Marvel and Bill Rosemann for listening to us and fixing some of these big problems.

(WARNING this is My first review, so sorry if something is missing...)Star wars Rebellion, now dont let my profile picture discurage you, (and my spelling, its major crap. xD) this game is quite the fun, and challenging one indeed, but i would say it takes quite some time to even get somewhere decent, so if you have no patience, then this is NOT, i repeat, NOT the game for you. Because it took me, at least 3 seperate saves TOTAL, to even get somewhere the computer was at a disadvantage, this is one of those 'Trial and Error' type games, what do i mean? well i simply mean this, if you fail, you must remember what it is you did wrong, and use that knowledge, to go back to an earlier save, and fix whatever the problem was... take it from someone who has 27 hours on the game, not 0.5 seconds, this is a good game, i've had it for about a week now, and i have enjoyed every moment of it, now lets list a few pros and cons. Pro1. Challanging very challanging indeed, as i have said, its quite a hard game to get the hang of, but once you get the hang of it, its really fun. Pro2. It's quite unique, in the sense that normally, in anyother game, your heros which are seen in the movies, CAN'T die, but in this one, they can... which can be either annoying, or interesting. (it also makes for some, very different gameplay, it makes you think about weather you want your hero's to continue a mission or not.) Pro3.The battle box, or the blue box, is a very different, and interesting way of having ship to ship combat. Pro4.Very, introcate Economy, you must manage it carefully, otherwise things like buldings, or ship yards, or even units, will get disbanded, and destroyed, because you just dont have the mantinance time, or money for them. (mantinance is the wrench icon, and your refined materials, which show up as a refinery icon, are basically like your cash, but its not really your cash you should watch, because that you can get TONS of, its how much mantinance you can handle at a time, the mantanince pool, which is indicated by the wrench icon, should have a number next to it, and that SHOULD tell you how much avalible mantinance you have, but be careful, as mentioned earlier, you have to manage that, very closely, why? well each unit and building costs a certant amount of mantinance, which is again, indocaited by the wrench icon, next to, or below the unit, or building you want to build, the other icon, is the refined matierals, which as i said earlier, are quite easy to get.... but in order to get refined materials, you must not only build refinery's, but mines, to get the RAW, matieral... see what i meant when i said its very introacte? xD)  Dont let only 4 pros discourage you! im just out of idea's... i dont know what else to mention.... there is more to mention, i know that for a fact, im just out of ideas, as of this moment. Now for the cons!  Con1.C3p0 is one annoying sonny beach! REALLY! he is, his info in the begining is quite handy, but afterwards, its just like, 'WOULD YOU SHUT UP ALREADY!!!!' well that's just me anyhow..... XD Con2.If you're into the whole, 'Graphics' thing, then this game isn't for you, because its got like the oldest graphics out there, and by that i mean this game was made in the 90's..... yeah...  Con3.The A.I, for some, might be JUST a bit too hard, or 'Cheaty' as my dad puts it, it does like to sneak up on you, and do something like, take one of your planets, or come back with a bigger force then you KNOW, for a fact that they should have for that early on in the game.... even on its easiest difficulty. I personally, think of it as more of a challange, then anything else... (as i have said before, this is a very challenging game, like this has to be the most fun, and challenging game, i've played in a long time.) Con4.Playing as the rebel alliance, gives you a disadvantage, as well as an advatange, the disadvantage, (which makes sense, i just dont like it personally.) is that you have lessor planets then the empire, and are commonly put on the edges of the galaxy to start off with, and the advantage however, is that your influance, (indicated by the red or green bar, just below a planet. Green = empire incluance, red = rebel alliance influance.) is much easier to gain, then the empire's is.. and when they take over a planet, which is heavily influanced by you, the rebel alliance, you get smuggling benifits. (aka the people and smugglers, on the planet steal stuff, and bring it to you, like resources, and sometimes even give you infromation about the planet, or naboring planets.)  since i only listed 4 pros, i will only list 4 cons... i hope my review helped you in some way, and i hope you will buy this amazing game! good luck, and have fun RTS players! app_name: Total War: ROME II - Emperor Edition
review_text: When first released this game was a mess, a giant mess, wasn't finished and should not of been released so quickly. I was hugely dissapointed as I always thought Ancient Rome would make the best Total War games (Rome 1). And whilst I admit, it still has issues, it has improved quite abit since launch, with 7 (yes 7) patches and more to come. I am a massive Total war fan, have been since Shogun 1, and if you love stratergy, or ancient history, then this is a good game for you. I have a mid-tier computer and the game runs fine for me (I have it on medium/high settings, it can get alittle choppy when there is 4000+ units on the screen). There is also a large range of mods to choose from, ranging from texture skins to full on gameplay changes, Radious Total war mod for example is great (and I highly recommend it), it really brings the campaign alive. I am one of the players who bought the game from release and played it since, it has improved, I think alot of people are still rightly mad at the release condition so they review it negatively. Creative Assembly post regularly on the forums letting players know what they have been up to with the incoming patches, and try to take in the feedback they recieve. I can't remember the last developer to patch a game 7 times in 4 months.  On release this game was 6/10 at best, now I give it a 8/10 but still improving. Whilst playing it now, I am starting to get the old feelings of Rome1, which is good. Also, am I the only one who loves the music???

A tragically failure of the Company of Heroes series. Factions are unbalanced and have bugs come out of their ears. And if you check the changelog of the patch of this game, you real found that there is one faction have been weakened every single time and it is very rarely strengthened through the patch. You can go have a look for yourself.  Plus the AI only have an average IQ of 20. Sometime when you ask them to move to certain place, they will deliberately pick the long way around even if there are more enemies to encounter. And sometimes they turn left and right, cant' decide which road to take.  Another AI issue for example if you put your unit near an enemy unit which is not a threat to you, a building for example which can't shoot back. Without giving them order to attack, they will automatically attack that building. There is nothing wrong with that. But after that, if there is an enemy unit that come along which can be a threat to you, a tank for example, your units will NOT turn back an engage the threat and continue to attack the non-threat target unless you give the order, even though you didn't give the order to attack the non-threat to begin with.  Also all AI players will eventually stop their movement if the game last for a while, let's say about half an hour. Making the game unendable. Because your allies will not attack and you will end up as the only player advancing to your enemy. And you will never beat your opponent due to the sheel number of your opponent. So far this bug has not been addressed.  The Sega's approach to bug report is also an issue. Unlike the EA, your bug usually can be solved by a live chat on spot. The Sega support only offers contact through your Sega account by posting an issue directly to Sega technical support staff. They usually only respond you about once per day or 2. And they rarely solve your problem. Sometimes they ask me questions that I have explained twice in my previous posts. It almost feel like some kind of Phone Sex Text. Except they don't get your money based on each text. So why would they ask you questions that you have explained before is a mystery. Probably pretending that they are working on it, or probably they never worked on it and forgot what you have said earlier.  I have been trying to find out a way to overcome issues like unbalance and stupid AI issues. So far, no luck.  And many unethical practics in this game's making. For example, when I pre-ordered this game, they told me that if I pre-order the collector's edition, the future 3 threater of the war DLC will be free for me. But after game was released, they told us that they have changed their minds and only the first threater of the war DLC will be free and we have to pay for the rest like everyone else.    Secondly, this game is a pay to win game to start with. In the earlier stage of this game, it advertise many different commanders who provide you different exotic units to use in the game. After you bought those, they suddenly turn this game into a mod friendly game, which everyone can make mods include 'All units mod'. Once again make you spend your money for nothing.   Just check on the 'only negative review' button and you will see there are clearly way many more players who gave this game a thumbs down than players who gave it a thumbs up. I really enjoyed playing this game, but it does have its faults.  The greatest thing about this is that it is the best zombie sandbox game I have ever had the pleasure of playing. The world is big and there are zombies everywhere. You have to manage resources and players to ensure that your base can withstand all of those hungry freaks from trying to get in the base. I appreciate that there are working cars when the world ends, because it is always a blast to just run zombies over with a car or bash them with a door.   The problem with the game, that I had is that I have to babysit everyone and I mean everyone. The AI in the game could be better, such as the fact that if there is a horde in the area, that the NPC doesn't run into them. Another issue is the annoying tooltips that follow you around for a good 10 to 20 minutes in the game.   If you do get it, I reccommend also getting the DLC Breakdown and Lifeline. I prefer Breakdown for the increase in difficulty that you get when you complete each level, because the vanillia game becomes too easy once you get the hang of it. With Lifeline, it could almost be considered an expansion pack. It introduces a whole mess of new assets, but the AI has become more infuriating. When you play this game, don't expect the AI to save you.

This game is horribly, poisonously addictive.  The idea of the game is to click on certain map hotspots that either advance the plot or give you resources. You can then click on different map hotspots to exchange resources for other resources. This is sometimes puzzle-solving but much more often simply deciding which trade would be more valuable in the long run. I did say 'map,' and I meant it; you're looking at the Kingdom you are the Hero of from an altitude of a few hundred feet.  There's some combat, there are some choices of which path to pursue first, but overall the game is fairly linear and very simple. There's a kind of thin plot involving finding out what happened to your family, but it plays out very slowly. It feels like this was originally a mobile game, and seems especially suited to a touch screen ... though I wouldn't want to read this much text on a screen that size. (Also, there are irritating hidden objects, like mushrooms, scattered around, which are barely visible even on a full-size monitor.)  Although it never actually happened to me, it certainly seems like it's possible to get stuck without the resources you need to continue and without any ability to accomplish anything. So there's an element of resource management. Otherwise, though, this is a decidedly mechanical point-and-click game, distinguished only by the UI's bird's-eye view.

A good game. The puzzles vary from easy to impressively challenging. You can skip any level you get stuck on and come back for it later. Basically, you're moving blocks and skating across ice/conveyor belts to make it to the end of the maze.  Chuck Summerville is a renown puzzle designer and this is a reskin of his game from 1989 called Chips Challenge.  I will say that the interface -- which was created for iPhones and Android devices -- leaves a lot to be desired. If you die too many times, you'll get a message that for the most part implies that you're an idiot and asks you to skip that level and try again later. Also, there are cutscenes that are activated by stepping on a shirt that is contained in the level -- also ragingly annoying.  But if you can get past the handful of interface issues and just focus on the puzzles, this title is a ton of fun.

At first glance it looks like the usual garbage that usually come out related to Warhammer 40K as of late (Dawn of War series excluded), however it is much more than that. The graphical design holds up well on low end systems and runs rather smoothly. The port itself onto PC is rather good aswell, however it does take a few deaths until the game decides to get you a tip on how to use a grenade right.  Levels can be repetitive but the 4 classes you can play as does give it a bit of life. But overall I believe the design of the game to be very good and presents a good challenge to the player. . . but not inn the sense you would expect.  Here is the big problem with this game: the camera. I don't know who programmed the camera in this game but whoever it is should take a refresher course. Level after level, I would reach the next section only to be attacked from the left side by a wave of Orks I didn't see due to the camera doing 'look the fancy scenery' thingy. Getting the camera into a good position is a bit like a hungry child, wearing oven gloves, trying to get beetroot out of a jar.  While this game is basically a close up version of Dawn of War 2, I hesitate to say that as it just doesn't hold up in comparison, it just feels too much like a cash in on the license. I do recommend this game but with great caution as the camera makes the game much harder and frustrating than what it should be.

It's been mentionned on a lot of gaming websites and on Capcom's site too so I don't get the complaints I read here. app_name: STAR WARS™: The Force Unleashed™ II
review_text: This game was a horrible addition to The Force Unleashed, although the graphics gameplay where significantly better the story,plot,voice acting, and enemies were just ungodly pathetic. The game just ended up becoming another revengeful hack and slash game with little to no boss fights, and the boss fights that did occur were just too simple. Even though the first Forced Unleashed looked terrible and had it's share of bugs (oh did it have it's share of bugs) it felt like you were actually playing a star wars game.

Great style. Fun mechanic. Neat levels. The pacing was a little slow for my tastes. And I wish the game saved progress in between levels. I liked the bosses at the end of each world. Having played for a few hours I would say the game is more 'beat 'em up' than 'think long and hard'. Biggest problem? Feeling like I have to bump against every wall in order to find secret areas!

UNFINISHED GAME.... READ BEFORE YOU BUY I wanted to love this game I wanted to give it a big thumbs up I wanted to dig my teeth into this.....  BUT OMG ...there is alot missing from this game, After the terrible release of ROME 2 i would have expected them to release a game which they have played themselves and tested to fix all the minor details... This game is like taking your car to the panel beaters to get the dents and scratches out of your car, A week later you get a call from the panel beaters to notify that your car is ready, You get dropped off at the workshop and as soon as you SEE your car your in love with the result. But when you walk closer you notice the amount of dents and scratches still left untouched, You talk to the panel beater and ask him to fix the dents and scratches he has missed, He replies that would be extra and would cost 3 payments of $9.99 and one of $3.59.  Here are a few points to sum up a few more DENTS AND SCRATCHES The non-existent Campaign AI. No unit collision. Lacking the intricacies in detail of the strategy map. Lack of Depth in pop-up random events. Very little diversity in faction units and buiildings . My personal opinion is the interface was terrible to use and far too messy to find what you need in the campaign map.  In conclusion.... fix the DAMN dents and scratches in the car (game) before you sell anymore DLC's.....

I'm not usually one to play roguelike games very much, but WASTED is one of the few I've been following for a while, and it's totally RIGHTEOUS, man.  OVERVIEW:  WASTED is a Procedurally Generated Dungeon Crawler Roguelike. (Yeah that's right, a 'PGDCR') It takes place in a post-apocolyptic future where buzzed out wasters scavenge for Booze. Unfortunately any remaining hooch lies deep within these futuristic dungeons the wasters call 'Coolers' Every bad guy in the Western Wastes is out for the sauce...and so are you.  GAMEPLAY THOUGHTS:  The gunplay in the game is pretty alright, though sometimes I feel like the hit detection can be a little iffy. I feel like I've seen my bullets go straight through an enemy without them taking any damage, but that might be a combo of low level weapons, aim debuffs and stats in general.   Melee weapons on the other hand are odd. I swear I was knocking an enemy around with a crowbar and they were taking zero damage. It wasn't until I gave them a moment to run around again was I able to finish them off. So just be careful when using a melee weapon, because that would be a most unsavory way to bite the dust.  The hangover buff/debuff is pretty reminicent of The Binding of Isaac's pill items, where you find nameless hipflasks of liquid courage to quaff down. It can be good or bad, mostly the latter, but I think that's what gives the game it's spice. There are bottles of labed Booze you'll find rarely around and at the end of coolers that give a very specific and character permanent buff/debuff. Again, can be good or bad, or can even be a mix!  Of course, one more thing I have to mention is the S.O.B Purifier. Y'see, every level of the Cooler there's a timer. I'm not sure how much time or if it changes the deeper you go, but at the end of the timer an S.O.B Purifier will show up and he'll basically hunt you down and shred you to pieces with a minigun. Think of the ghost from Spelunky, but unlike the ghost from spelunky, I hear you can kill him! He's pretty easy to escape from though, so not bad.   ART & MUSIC:  The graphics are really nice and stylized. Of course the first thought that comes to mind is a Fallout game mixed with Borderlands and that's not too far off I guess. Everyone in the game always has a funny expression, though I will admit that in the heat of a gun battle inside a Cooler, having the enemy's huge melon head get all up close and personal with you is a little jarring...At least for me.  The music is really, really good. The music when you're deep in the Cooler is nice and isn't unpleasant when hearing it over and over again. Basically just think of Spelunky HD, those nice synthy tunes. The music you hear passing by radios really compliments the game too.  ENDING THOUGHTS:  This game is really challenging and at times pretty brutal, but that's just the nature of these types of randomized games. Though the humor in the game really helps counterbalance the difficulty, plenty of the cheesetastic jokes I dig.   Overall I totally recommend this game, excellent purchase. Especially at the price it's at now! C'mon, Go for it!

It's hard to achieve good puzzle mechanics in first person, and credit needs to be given to the creators of Magnetic: Case Closed for creating a solid, tactile gameplay with few (but not zero) shortcomings. However, good level design is even harder to come by (both in 2D and 3D), and the game fares much worse in this respect. Finally, there's absolutely no excuse for using the shortcomings of the game mechanics as a tool to increase difficulty. I could live with the often pedestrian level design and the large amount of unskippable intermissions, but the level where you have to move the cube from top to bottom in a maze blew my fuse as the cube kept getting stuck and I kept falling down from the narrow ledges due to the mutual attraction. It could be such a superb game, but alas...

7/10  its a pretty good game. the story is good from the start and the side missons are awsome. but there is some issues.  the building texture goes all over the place. they are probably going to fix this soon, but its really annoying. i am playing it alone, and i know for a fact that this game whould be awsome with a friend. but there is no doubt that the game is good playing alone to.

Combat. you get an initial choice of 3 basic classes: fighter (sword and shield) strider (daggers and bow) mage (uh magic)  the combat consists of light attacks and strong attacks, as well as up to 6 abilities that consume stamina when used. many abilities have much higher potential for damage than either of your basic attacks, but many of them are also more defense/utility focused.  abilities are important. initially i had issues with the combat, as i was trying to get by just blocking and attacking as a fighter. this works on basic monsters, but anything tougher will break your guard and take little damage if you use this strategy. you need offensive abilities to deal significant damage and break guard, and you need defensive abilities to effectively block or tank or dodge anything substantial.  Pawns. you create a NPC character (pawn) in addition to your own character, and you customize his/her class/abilities/gear as well. you can also take on up to 2 other pawns for your party. these additional pawns are taken from a database that stores every player's main pawn. you're probably gonna want at least one pawn to heal you (tho you could just wharf down curitives like a madman instead).  Fast travel? mentioning this here because it took me a bit too long to realize this myself. there is fast travel, but you need to place the travel points (portcrystals) yourself. it also apparently required a consumable items (ferrystones) in order to fast travel to these locations, but this updated version of the game grants you an 'eternal ferrystone' (that can be reused indefinitely) very early on.  So. this wasn't really much of a review, just an overview of what you're getting yourself into. the game's pretty good. I hope they make a second (and that it's on pc). seems like a perfect game for co-op, but it doesnt have any. oh well.

app_name: Sound of Drop - fall into poison -
review_text: Upon first glance I thought that it was going to be a Never17 rip-off, but once I had played it I was very surprised with how good this game was. The story was interesting, the characters were adequately developed, and the horror aspects were well done without going for the over the top fear factor garbage most games try to employ.   The game is relatively short, but has a high replay value due to the numerous ending that you can get. Many of the unanswered questions in my first playthrough were pleasantly answered in my second playthrough through the use of NG+. Overall I highly recommend this to both new and veteran visual novel fans. While there are a lot of terrible games claiming to be “visual novels” on Steam this one is quite a gem.

Besides the obvious tabboo inbreeding going though-out the game, there isn't really that much to complain about. Me personally, i had a bit of trouble with the FPS, and it seems the game has a 40 - 50 FPS cap, but a few times it will drop to 10 or less and was fixed after a re-start. Besides that, its a game that even with the problems, is playable, and is worth the time taken to play it. Im looking forward to playing the expansion Arcania: Fall of Setarrif. 6/10

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=skFdSHQi8pw  The year 2017 will mark the second decade since the landmark, cult-classic game POSTAL was released into the world. Controversy sparked from every corner of the dark title, especially due to its gruesome ending and remorseless killing. It took Running With Scissors a few years, but they finally latched on to the success of the series and eventually birthed POSTAL 2 and 3 into the world; those are reviews for another time, however.   POSTAL Redux is not just a simple graphical overhaul of the original title, instead it's a complete revamp of the game done in Unreal Engine 4. The makeover definitely shows, not only in the aesthetic presentation of the game, but also the fluid, up-to-date controls that make the Redux so much easier to play over its predecessor. There are many notable differences between POSTAL Redux and the first POSTAL, which will be taken a look at within this review.  For those who have played the first POSTAL, feel free to skip this paragraph. Those of you questioning what the game is all about, stick around. In POSTAL, you play as an antagonist who's simply known as The Postal Dude; some say he's schizophrenic, some say he's a madman, some like to believe he's a pretty nice guy. Either way, The Postal Dude has a theory that his hometown of Paradise, Arizona is being flooded with mind-altering substances; he starts believing that he is the only individual who is unaffected. Once he gets evicted from his home, he starts a murderous rampage of blood-lust, and doesn't stop until he reaches the Military Air Base; where he believes the source of the noxious gas is stemming from.  For long-time fans of the series, you may be upset to know that Running With Scissors have changed the iconic ending in the Redux. There's no longer   an elementary school with children running around at the end of the game, but the laughter of the children can still be heard as you wander along a cemetery  . The interpretation of this is up to the individual, but the same end cutscene is still present after the last 'level' plays out. In my experience, the new Redux ending is much less impacting than the original, and the game loses some of its overall morbidity due to it.  The original POSTAL had 21 levels, plus four extra levels from the Special Delivery expansion pack, the Redux only has 17 levels in total with one of them being the newly created Carnival map; so it's safe to say that Running With Scissors trimmed the fat a little bit. As well, the difficulty of this title is nowhere near that of the original, and seasoned players will find even the hard difficulty severely lacking in comparison to POSTAL's '11' difficulty setting. However, this could be something that is fixed in the future, as the Redux has been receiving regular updates since its release in May, 2016.  The developer claims that the visuals are a 'faithful' recreation of the original, and quite honestly I haven't seen a difference in them other than the fact that they're of higher resolution and thus much more clear. This is a good thing, as it genuinely looks as though the original hand-painted backgrounds have been re-scanned in a superior format; if someone redid all of the backgrounds, kudos to them as they look like the real deal. As for the character models, they're noticably upgraded from the Playstation 1-era models from the original game, and you can even change The Postal Dude's trench coat color.  It's safe to say that the Redux functions like any other modern top-down shooter now. In the original, it was difficult to control The Postal Dude with both the keyboard and mouse, due to the games age and engine limitations. The Redux sees to it that the mouse control is extremely fluid, as is the aiming/targeting system and ability to switch between weapons with the scroll wheel. Not to mention, the Redux can also be played with a console controller, for those who are not fond of keyboard and mouse controls.  Other than the upgraded visuals and lack of levels, POSTAL Redux has some other notable changes. One of the biggest additions is the new executions. In the original game, no matter what gun you were using The Postal Dude would switch back to his AR-16 for the execution of downed enemies, now each gun has its own unique animation. Another fantastic feature is when playing on Hard mode, the journal entries that show up between levels read as they appeared in the original manual of POSTAL; on Easy and Medium they simply stay the same as what is shown in the first game. There's a new revolver weapon, the machine gun (or SMG in this version) has a higher rate of fire and range, the shotgun and spray cannons do not share the same ammo any longer, the journal entry dates all read 2017 as opposed to 1997, and the newly introduced Rampage mode is a bit like the Gauntlet Challenge from the original game.  Sadly, POSTAL Redux does have a major downfall; the game likes to crash... a lot. The amount of crashing that still occurs despite the numerous updates to the title is unacceptable. Running With Scissors just released an update that implemented 4 player co-op, yet they cannot find the time to rectify crashes that skew the players experience with the game? That's just hard for me to believe. The game will crash during level transitions, during start up, but most of all during the Rampage mode.  With that being said, if you can live with the amount of crashing that this game likes to do, then POSTAL Redux will be a great addition to your collection of violent, twisted, dark humored games. The complete engine overhaul has really brought the classic POSTAL title up to speed in the modern world, although it would be nice if Running With Scissors left all of the levels intact instead of picking which ones to keep in and leave out; it would've also been nice to have the original ending. The audio is also completely remastered, so The Postal Dude's witty and sarcastic comments can be heard crystal clear now. As well, I'm sure the newly introduced 4 player online co-op will appeal to a large range of gamers. If you've never played POSTAL before, this is an excellent starting point. If you're a long-time fan of the series, you won't be disappointed.   Rating:  4.5/5.0  - Excellent, highly worth playing.

global gameplay stats show that less than 15% of people who have bought this have acheived a colony of decent ongoing status.   you will fail at least your first colony. i failed 2 before getting the third going well.   its a very tight game and you have to understand whats going on in the colony to keep it alive. for example, trading a tonne of goods at once through one airlock means your entire colony sorta shuts down to fulfill the trader. then you might have an unrecoverable starvation wave shortly thereafter.   the AI feels dumb but ive only experienced 2 actual dumb instances (probably a bug). if your colonist has a yellow icon over its head, it wont work. if its not going to get food.... its because someone else is already going to get that particular meal and you dont have enough in storage.   Stick with it, get past the learning curve (short curve if youve played city builders before) and its an awesome game.
