Umm.....No. I don't recommend this. Maybe for free achievements, but was the experience something enjoyable? No. This game essentially proves that statements like 'I'll always be there for you', 'You'll always be my best friend', and 'We're still friends' mean absolutely nothing.   Two teenage friends talk, but you essentially watch the end of a friendship.    Your crush gets a BF, you play the jealousy game, you visit, you grow distant, and you essentially end your friendship by growing distant from one another    Sure, the game seems like an interactive where you get to choose your route, but you'll always come back to the same ending. Now, Emily does 'remember' options you choose and will bring them up later, but it doesn't change what ending you get.  I've seen similar stories played many times throughout my life and they're people I realize were never people I should have trusted. People I should have never even met. People that I feel my life would have been better if I had never heard of these people....but those are stories for another time. This simply reminds me too much of my own past, showing how little you can rely on somebody...   PROS: Achievements AIM nostalgia of Windows XP days No bugs and programmed decently Free  CONS: Boring A friend who doesn't know what she wants Death of a friendship Depressing ending as you essentially watch it all rot away   Despite it being a free game, I only recommend this for the free, easy achievements. The bonus icon achievements have some nice in-jokes, but that's about it. As far as the story itself goes, you might want to pass. Nostalgic and well-programmed text game, but not something that I enjoyed playing. Sometimes the past should stay burried.   2/10. (Emily, just go away and stay away. Best friend my *ss... 'I'll always be there for you' means nothing.)

Compared to Blue Shift and Half-Life, Opposing Force is fairly unique. You play as Adrian Shephard, a soldier sent to Black Mesa to cover up the catastrophe of Black Mesa. In Opposing Force, you encounter new aliens and acquire new weapons (plus, you have nightvision goggles instead of a flashlight).   Honestly, Opposing Force is one of my favorite games in the Half-Life series. The gunplay is fun, you can actually get decent allies (the soldiers use rifles and shotguns, unlike the wimpy security guards), and the ending makes me hope that Shephard is featured in the next Half-Life game.

Knew this game would be awesome as soon as I played the, all be it glitched but fun, demo on the PS3.  Bought it on sale for 70% off, including all the DLC, and I've been having a blast ever since. There are the usual, boring, MMO kind of quests, but there are also some fun ones that take skill with your abilities.  The moves, whether it be with a sword, magic, or a combination of the two together, are amazing. Combos seem to never end and you're constantly learning new ones with combat experience.  The map is MASSIVE! I can't believe how big it is. Surprisingly though, travelling the map is quite easy. Thankfully they put in fast travel via the world map.  Graphically, the game is stellar. Very detailed but not hard on resources. I can almost max out everything with my PC and I'm very happy with that.  The best part about this game? There seems to not be a lot of grinding involved. That's one thing that I've always hated about my other favorite RPG games - Dark Souls and Demon's Souls.  The only downfall I have with this game is that there's no 'wait' option. Sometimes completing a quest is based upon waiting for a certain time of day in a specific spot. Time moves 1000x faster in game than in real life but it's still boring to wait from 6pm to midnight in the same spot.  All in all, I recommend this game to all who enjoy RPG games, fantasy, or MMORPGs without the online aspect. I can only hope that EA make the next instalment of this game (if they plan to. Fingers crossed they do!) with an online or co-op aspect.

If you added a map to this game, it would actually be very good. Not BRILLIANT, perhaps...certainly not 'perfect'...but very good. As it is, you require an almost-photographic memory to work out where you're supposed to go next, even though you've probably been there a few times before. Could this have anything to do with the fact that the first half of the game takes place on a cargo ship and the second half on an oil rig, so y'know, much of the scenery looks much the same? Forgive me for not remembering where the 'upper corridor next to the secondary science lab' is, 'cause I've seen a few of those of late, I swear to God. Yessiree, a map would have be nice. Or some of those more modern, new-fangled, Bioshock-y arrows pointing you in precisely the direction you need to go, failing that. But a map...even better. Heck, the very first Resident Evil had one of those, and this game certainly doesn't go out of it's way to not rip off virtually every OTHER aspect of that classic game, so...again, a map? Too much to ask?!   There's a few other problems, too...though ones which are perhaps SLIGHTLY more forgivable, given the era this is from. The save points are kinda crap (it chooses them for you, sometimes miles apart from each other), some of the aiming with the laser sight on your guns is kinda abominable (gotta love them stairs), and the plot is as bizarre as bat guano. Having said all this, though...it's a defiantly 'old-school' survival horror game, and anyone who enjoyed Resident Evil 1 through 4 is sure to find it a virtual must-play, if for no reason other than the fact that games like this are pretty hard to find, and how many times CAN you play those first four Resident Evils before you tire of them completely (the answer in my case being MANY times, but there's still a limit, even for yours truly).    Sincerely, there's a lot to love about this game - the atmospheric rain/wind/rocking boat effects are particularly impressive - and, unfortunately, a few things to truly detest. But I got past them. And so will you, if you're the kind of person I just described. JUST DON'T EXPECT A ♥♥♥♥ING MAP, AND YOU'LL BE FINE!! AAARGH!!!  Verdict: 8/10.

This is probably one of the best games I have ever played… you know, after fixing it so my computer doesn’t melt whilst playing it. (That isn’t a stab at the game at all!)  I’m gonna do a simple Pros and Cons list for this game because I’d end up writing an essay about it. TL;DR located at the bottom. PROS: - Genius ideas. It takes on an FPS style obviously but it keeps your interest for the entire duration of the game. The enemies are so creative and original that I was honestly curious as to what the game would throw at me next. - Graphics. Are. Godly. There’s detail where detail wouldn’t even be necessary, helping to complete the atmosphere the game wants to create. - Music. This is more of a personal thing but I personally loved the music. At certain parts too, it just knows how to get your adrenaline pumping. - The STORY. I loved the story so much. I grew a love for characters and I grew an unrelenting hatred for characters too. If you want to have your heart cycle between loving the characters and experiencing extreme bloodlust, this game is for you. - Weapons are pretty awesome too. I loved using the missile launchers, in the few instances that I could. - The game knows how to balance between explosions, chaos, fire and death and the calm “just go around and explore” levels. It makes you appreciate both of them if you’re someone that gets annoyed when there’s too much of one or the other.  CONS: - If your computer isn’t the absolute best, this game will decimate it. Initially when launching the game (I can’t remember if it detects your computers specs and sets graphics accordingly beforehand) it was very de-synced. The video was slow and the audio seemed to had to repeat itself because the video was so slow. But after fixing the graphics so my computer can tolerate it, it ran pretty well. - I don’t know if it was just me being bad at video games in general, but it’s pretty easy to get lost. The path isn’t given to you with a giant neon green arrow screaming “GO HERE.” The good thing about this though is that it sort of encourages you to explore more. Though when you explore everything you might get a bit frustrated. All the more satisfying when you make progress, though. - Some of the robots can be a bit of a pain to figure out how to kill. Honestly though I can’t come up with many Cons. The Pros definitely dominated in my opinion.  TL;DR:  Would definitely recommend this game for anyone looking for something that will give you a satisfying feeling when you launch rockets and grenades and Nazis and see them explode all over the floor. The game bombards your senses with the most detailed atmosphere I’ve ever seen in a video game, and takes a pretty frightening and nightmarish idea and makes a badass game out of it. A bit expensive though. I bought it when it was on sale for $20, mainly since I don’t want to spend too much on games. A truly spectacular game. Took me out of reality for the time I played it.

app_name: Total War: ROME II - Emperor Edition
review_text: Hmm I see still no neutral way to comment on game, so guess Ill have to use the thumbs down.  Ok first off, this isn't so much a bad game. This game could very well have the best graphics of any Total War game Ive ever played. Ive also been very impressed with Creative assembly and their Total War sries for providing great strategy games, historical notes, and a realness to the campaigns that just isn't on other RTS games. Sadly, this game has alot of problems that other TW games dont have, thus the thumbs down.  The cons of this game:  Soldier cards while in battle. They're black shapes of men, like ancient artwork. Which is really annoying in battle. Cause, while Im trying to move my troops around to engage, and hopefully, get the best results from the engagement, I got to keep hovering over the troop cards to ensure Im not sending my slingers to the front and my spearmen to a slingers position. The fact that, 2-4 versions of the spearmen are available, and only difference is how they are holding the spear, only adds to the confusion.  The Grand Capmaign. Where to begin with that? Ill start off with, the map. Feels like the Ice Age is over and the cavemen are now starting to form tribes, despite the fact the starting year is listed as 272 BC. Next, the map is only so big. Yet, there is SO many Ai tribes, with strange, unheard of names and every shield emblem imaginable. Its hard to believe the map is even big enough for all of them. The campaign also needs help explaining the different researchs, how to increase food production, etc. Felt like the more I researched, the less production! And, didn't seem to be any farming building to build. Yet, my soldiers were dieing of food shortage.  Battlefield camera controls. They stink. As you go left and right, it swings you in a wide arc, making it hard to be sure your even moving towards the units you wished to move/command. Which then makes it tough to do your battle plan and ensure you dont take heavy loss's.  The units seem to not be numbered. On medieval TW or Empires TW, you might recruit a unit of 45 calvary. On Rome, you dont seem to have a set number of soldiers in any unit.  The few pros I did find:  The battles are a bit realistic. I saw a horse riding away from the battle, riderless. Empires and Medieval dont have that.  Each general character you recruit, his army is given a name and can choose an emblem, which does not happen on Medieval or Empires.  Overall, not worth the $60 price it has. $20 is the highest I would ever pay for this, if I ever chose to buy this game. Its not a bad game and at least worth a try. But its not the best Total War game and I wouldn't recommend it to new Total War fans. I'd recommend Medieval Total War 2 for swords, spears, and archer warfare or Empires Total War for much better experiences.  Note: I bought Medieval Total War through Amazon, not Steam, thus why Medieval TW aint on my Steam library.

app_name: Total War: ROME II - Emperor Edition
review_text: Basically a massive, complex, and awesome game of risk, in which you can choose to resolve battles yourself on the battlefield. It is somewhat complicated, with a steep learning curve, as the game requires military and civil management, building and upgrading cities, managing politics within your own faction, plus diplomacy with other nations, But, it does capture the feeling of building and maintaining an empire. If you're all about the battles, this game is not for you. But if you like battles on the battlefield, plus managing a long-term campaign of world domination (which, while mostly military, also contains civic and economical goals) then I highly recommend this game.

A metroidvania with solid combat that misses a few basics of the genre.  I've reached a point with Dust where I don't really care to complete the game. I've had fun with it for the ~10 hours I've played, but at this point I've kind of realized that the game doesn't have anything else to reveal, and so I'm not really compelled to keep playing.  The basic combat is fun, but the fact that your gear and upgrades don't actually affect how you fight enemies means that it gets stale after a while. You have the same weapon throughout, and gear upgrades for the most part are just that: straight upgrades, with little to make the player think, 'would a little more of x or y be better for how I play.' Unlike the game that I consider to be the gold standard of the genre, SotN, where finding a new weapon might make you more powerful, but also can change how you approach combat.  The controls seem good at first, but as you advance you find yourself needing to platform more and more, and the controls are too sloppy for really precise platforming controls. They animations and control are set up to create a flashy, exciting combat experience, with lots of extraneous movements by your character that look cool, however these extraneous movements create moments of real frustration when you're trying to precisely jump on platforms or navigate areas with other environmental hazards such as ground and wall spikes.  The story is bad, basically a crappy Western interpretation of crappy anime/JRPG tropes, but it's not too intrusive.  The art is a mixed bag: the environments are incredibly gorgeous, but the character and enemy sprites look like a high schooler who might be good with a lot more practice drew them. Again, there is a heavy influence from mediocre anime in all of the character designs.   So finally, I'm going to give this a tentative recommendation, but only if you can buy it on sale.

This game is endlessly fun for me. No matter how many times I play in a day or a week, I never got bored of it. The achievements give you something to constantly work towards or improve upon, and the same goes for the Monster Logs. While some achievements are of average difficulty, there are a few that even I don't have. Those few provide a challenge that will take quite some time to overcome. Also, I love the new relic system. It's fun to mess around with. The game on its regular difficulty (Rainstorm) is quite challenging, and its hardest difficulty (Monsoon) is... well... intimidating, to say the least. I'm not sure what they were thinking when they made the achievements to 'not take damage' or 'beat the game on Monsoon', but I like it. The one problem I have with the game is the multiplayer right now. It's not impossible to make it work, but my friend did have to spend a decent amount of time getting a server up. The single-player alone is amazing, and multiplayer is great for small groups. If they could fix the in-game hosting, Risk of Rain would be 10/10 for me.

For every crap run you get - like an item synergy that ends up drawing enemies into you - you will eventually get an overpowered run to balance it out, such as a missle that can be shot anywhere in the room that shoots death lasers when it lands. Pass or Play - Towerfall Ascension Review  Check out a full review here:  http://tyrrexx.tumblr.com/post/96204954380/pass-or-play-towerfall-ascension-review   Towerfall: Ascension by Matt Thorsen is a combat platformer that places archers in an arena to fight for glory and bragging rights. Developed primarily with local multiplayer in mind, it reminds me of playing Goldeneye for the N64 with friends when I was younger. Towerfall makes me wish local multiplayer wasn’t something almost forgotten in today’s industry. If you have the local player-base, the game combines a competitive atmosphere, zany gameplay, and retro graphics to create a great experience.  The aesthetics of Towerfall are very good. The graphics in the game are done in a modern pixel art style. The sound design applies simple sound effects and a solid soundtrack to ensure the game is appealing from an audio perspective. I think it is a good example of marrying sound, graphics, and style so the sum is greater than the whole. This unification gives Towerfall a unique personality.  This personality spills into the character and level design. The various archers feel different, despite having no actual gameplay difference or dialogue. I feel each character was given a persona purely by their title and appearance. The levels are well done and provide a good variety of environments. Each level has different layout and graphics. I felt most levels were thematically different and allowed for different strategies to be applied. Enemy design and aesthetics are also cohesive with each level. Some levels contain specific enemies that further drive the theme of each level. Many monsters have unique behavior and weaknesses, making them feel believable and different. There are some enemy re-skins and reuse, but overall the variety is enough.  There are 3 main modes in Towerfall: quest, multiplayer, and trials. Quest mode is a single player/co-op mode that I found myself primarily playing. In this mode, you go through the various levels of the game defeating waves of monsters. This mode is decent for learning the game and features a “hardcore” difficulty, but I didn’t feel the need to play through it again. Trials mode is similar to the target run in Super Smash Bros. This mode was fun to play through once but like the quest mode, I personally wouldn’t go back. Overall I found the single player modes somewhat boring. I only really found myself playing through the levels to just see the new enemies and gather some unlockables.  The game is marketed at couch multiplayer and the arena is where the designer’s dream is realized. It allows 2-4 people to test their skills and has a full array of options to modify the game such as changing win conditions, items available, and level variables. There is even a system to give losing or inexperienced players a handicap. The mode is well polished and is something I can see playing on game nights for a long time.  The actual gameplay is what makes Towerfall great. It is crisp and simple enough for anyone to learn. Characters can simply only shoot arrows, move, jump, and dash. Compounding upon these basic actions, the designer added more features to enhance gameplay. For example, the arrows have slight homing properties. This allows players to not have completely accurate aim(which can be hard with only 8 way aim) but still be competitive. There are also a lot of various power ups. Things like shields, bomb arrows, wings, and drill arrows all work to create dynamic scenarios and open up strategic options. I found myself starting to focus on one type of power up to fit my play style. This all comes together to give a strong sense of flow. I always felt like a badass, dodging two arrows, catching the third, and firing it midair for a kill. Win or lose the gameplay is fast, fun, and furious.  My praise for the game is overshadowed a bit by some things though. The controls and movement system take a bit of getting used too. I was pretty frustrated until I learned the nuances. As I said above, the single player content is somewhat boring. Maybe leaderboards or something could have helped. This game hinges on being able to play with friends, without others the experience is extremely short. This is a game I’d highly recommend everyone to try out. I’d only warrant a purchase if multiplayer sessions could be set up.  Play if you:  - Like games that are easy to learn but hard to master.  - Reminisce about local gameplay.  - Want to dive into a theme that is whimsical and fun.  Pass if you:  - Don’t have friends to play the game with.  - Are tired of the pixel art look in indie games.  - Prefer games with a story or more developed single player mode.  My recommendation: Pass. The game is great, but I can’t really recommend buying unless you have the friends to play with. Score: 7/10.  Follow me:   Twitter:  twitter.com/Tyrrexx   Tumblr:  tyrrexx.tumblr.com   Follow the developer here:  twitter.com/MattThorson

(long review -but its not a negative rant about item shop prices I promise! Saw so many negative reviews and I just thought Id pop my opinion on this game I love so much at the moment)  A very unique game - I have no interest in real life hunting either!  Yet... I find it almost impossible to spend less than 4-6 hours in this game at a time... it just draws me in... kinda lucky each hunt day ends and you run out of ammo eventually or I dont know how I would get out of this game... I would probably hunt with my bare hands when outta ammo if let me - its a vortex. Weird too because I can fire only 1 bullet/arrow every 10 mins or more/less and still be satisfied. (sometimes long times just wandering and tracking/waiting/chasing).  The atmosphere and creature AI are fantastic - the weapons feel satisfying, and kills dont feel too hard or too easy. Any bad things I have to say about this game are related to some items being too expensive, and draw distances being too low - but these things make sense when put into context I suppose, not the item shop so much (but I guess it is keeping them afloat). I doubt these guys are making these game updates inside mansions.  Its good value, unless you expect free to play in traditional sense, where you are the product, there to fill groups/be easy targets of paid members. You can pay a very small amount to custom tailor your experience if you want. This is mostly a single player session game, with multiplayer contests/leaderboards, with the option of multiplayer hunting with (not as many) multiplayer contests. So all the content is there for you to enjoy personally - and you dont get to play good games for free for the most part. Addictive and way too easy to spend money though - warning about that.  This game has been out for over 5 years but has had many graphics updates etc with new zones and animals (all for free) and the zones etc are very nice. AI/sound/weapons/contests etc are all outstanding also.  Consider the free to play a demo that lasts forever and lets you get a taste and a teaser. Then pay the $50 and get the best package deal and be just about set. Yes you can pay more, (or less) and yes if you dont you wont get all the items, but you dont need all the items. I am pretty sure a lot of the bad reviews for this game are people without money, or unable to understand that simple concept of games costing money, if they are good, and if people are prepared to pay. That said I think they went overboard on microtransactions in some ways.  Its a unique business model, but one that obviously works for them and their customers, not everyone understandably - but you cannot please everyone. Its nowhere near as pay to play as people make out - they just exaggerate... sure you can spend a LOT in the item shop, but you dont need most of it, and most of it just makes the game a bit too easy anyway...   Just buy one of the packs and you can kill almost anything (except bison with the most basic pack) with the guns provided - with few limitations - but spend another 10-20$ and u can get a decent rifle like a 7mm that can shoot almost anything you cant already shoot - and a backpack maybe some other items you wanted also. I recommend the $50 pack tho as it fills most things you will want to get anyway once you are hooked, lasts a year subscription and is by FAR the best value.  (I dont understand why people complain so much about these guys trying to make money from their game, yet the massive game market seem happy to pay $110 for a crappy annually released sequel and then a $60-70 season pass for games like battlefield, COD and fifa etc.) IF the hunter charged what those games did for the time it has been out, to pay for the current content you would be looking at $250-500 dollars EASILY. You can buy $15/3 month subscription to this and hunt on 10 reserves for 25 species. Pay another $10 and you can hunt anything.. game over for the animals but so much fun for you.   7/10 - even hippies can like this game...

You start off with a group of survivors, stuck in a dirt hole of a house  barely held together by wood and cloth. Gunfire and explosions can be heard close by, but that's the least of your worries. You have a handful of tools, some wood and some food that might last you for the day, and there seems to be no end in sight to the war.   A major milestone, It's more of an experience rather than a game.  This War of Mine drops every single money-making aspect of AAA games in the market and provides a totally new perspective in war. The graphics are dark and gritty without being boring, the music is sad and slow yet hopeful. The gameplay is slow and, on paper, is simple.. but the way everything is packaged, provides the most unique gaming experiences ever.

After playing it for just under 2 hours, having spent just £3.99 on it, I have to say that, so far, it has been money well spent. Considering it's a tablet port, the controls feel fluid and precise (although the sight on the most expensive assault rifle sways horribly when you move your mouse), the zombies are well animated, the weapons are literally 'This gun is far better than this gun. Use this.', and when you get the minigun, you've practically won the game.   It looks good, there are plenty of secrets to find, the music seems a little out of place at times, but the sound design is rather well done. They've done a good job of making sure you know you're on a broken down, abandoned, zombie filled spaceship, with the hisses of broken oxygen tanks and hoses, the gargling and moaning zombies are easy to locate due to the fact that, even with just speakers, you can hear exactly how far they are from you, AND where they're likely located.   For £3.99, I genuinely cannot complain. It's very reminiscent of games like Rise of the Triad, Painkiller, and in survival mode, maybe even Serious Sam, gameplay-wise. I just got nearly two hours of non-stop fun out of it, but I will say this..  If you're looking for a perfectly polished, difficult, long, drawn out experience, this isn't the game for you.   If you want a few hours of mindless zombie slaying with some quite decent story (albeit grammatically incorrect, at times) told through the dialogue over the communicator you're wearing, and through text-based PDA logs (a-la Doom 3 without them actually being read out to you) that you find throughout the game (some are hidden fairly well, so check EVERYWHERE in the room you're in before moving on to the next one! If you stand still for a sec and look around, you'll catch them glinting slightly, making them a little easier to find!), where you can just unload stupid amounts of ammo in to stupid amounts of zombies, whether you play the story, biohazard mode, or survival mode, this is the game for you.   I strongly recommend looking up the first level of the story on youtube before you buy, as this game COULD be either the best £3.99 you've spent in months, or the worst, based on what your tastes are. I enjoyed what little I played of it, and if you're anything like me, and sometimes just like to blow off some steam (hurr hurr), killing zombies mindlessly, you'll have a riot. Do not, however expect any semblance of multiplayer. I mean that. There is none. At all. This is a solely single-player experience.  Graphics: 7/10 Sound: 8/10 Gameplay: 7/10  All in all, a fun, yet very average-at-times experience.

Do I recommend this game?! - Does a leshen hate dimeritium bombs, relict oil, silver swords and igni? Hell yes!  I rarely get 'hyped' for games, or anything else really, but I had a feeling that the guys at CD PROJEKT RED would not let me down with this one, so around 9.5 months ago, I pre-ordered this game and patiently waited for my steam account to give birth to a rugged, emotionless, gravel voiced, monster hunting baby, and boy is it a beautiful baby!  This game lived up to all my expectations and then some. This isn't your dad's open world rpg game with nothing to do but run for miles and miles in snow and then occasionally whack a monster or two with your monotonous one button combat, no... this game is something completely different, the combat, the quest structure, the dialogue choices that actually impact the outcome of the game, the graphics, the music, the optimization, everything is just perfect and makes this game incredibly fun to play.  So, like in the past two games, you play a witcher/an adventurer... a monster slayer enthusiast, if you will, named Geralt, who is looking for his protege Cirilla (he refers to her as his daughter, so you know they're tight!) who is being pursued by the wild hunt. But even though he has to find her as quickly as possible, along his journey Geralt will find time to stray off the main course, as you do in open world games, and pick up an occasional secondary quest, a witcher contract, a gear set scavenger hunt map, play some magi...err hearthsto... I mean gwent! he'll play gwent... (a card game that took me some time to appreciate, but once I won a few cards from random merchants, I really got into it).  The game world consists of 3 major separate maps (you can freely travel between them) White Orchard, Velen/Novigrad and Skellige, and while White Orchard and Velen are somewhat similar, and are mostly woodland maps, well, Velen has some swamp areas and a giant city in it (it's so big and full of quests that it might as well be a separate area), Skellige is a snow covered, viking inspired map which consist of one big island and a few smaller ones. If you're wondering if there is much to do in such a large game world, hell yeah there is! You'll be done with White Orchard in about 5-7 hours if you 100% it, but the other two maps are just chock-full of quests, and it'll take you days to play through them. I have 110 hours game time as I'm writing this, and I'm not done with either of them yet.  Onto the 'goods' and the 'bads'. It's a pretty game, so there are no uglies, well...  + The graphics are just amazing for an open world game, sharp textures on most objects and most main characters (Geralt and his friends especially), light effects, shadows, grass density, facial expressions, view distance... everything is just jaw dropping gorgeous.  + Sound design is pretty good, nothing you wouldn't expect from an AAA title, good medieval tunes while running around outside of combat, dynamic and intense music when you're in combat, no complaints there. Voice acting is a step up from Witcher 2, which itself was a huge step up from the first game, and while not perfect it's still good, and the important thing is, story related characters are voiced really, really well.  + Gameplay! While the combat is somewhat hindered by the controls, it's still pretty fun. The sign system gives it variety and a tactical approach to each fight (especially at the two hardest game difficulties). The witcher contract quests are especially a blast to play (they are my favorite part of the game), where you have to detective your way to the monster you have to kill, or not kill, you never know...  + Optimization is pretty good, I locked my game to 30fps on my i7 920 @3Ghz, gtx 660ti OC and 8gb of ram, and I can play it on ultra at 1200p, with shadows and foliage draw distance at medium, ambient occlusion turned off and without the nvidia hair thing...  - Not everything is flower petals and fancy chocolates and cute baby unicorns, sadly... the controls and combat are somewhat clunky, and are the only bad thing about this game... controls especially. Sometimes Geralt acts like he can't turn around in place, and instead he makes a wide circle which makes movement in tight indoor areas, and under water a controller dropping/face palming/deep sighing pain. Roach, Geralt's trustry horsey, suffers from the same leg disease as Geralt, the horse has a mind of her own, sometimes a twig can stop Roach at full gallop, sometimes she'll go left if you tell her to go right, well you get the idea, no point in beating a dead horse (ha!). The combat, while it can be very fun and tactical, also suffers from bad controls from time to time, but not as much as the above examples. Sometimes Geralt starts combat with his sword sheathed and starts fistfighting a boss, in most of those cases it'll take you more than a few button presses till the game figures out you're trying to take out your sword. Also, the control scheme changes when you enter combat and wipes the jump button from existence, so if you're stuck in a small space surrounded by a knee high fence, prepare to die!  - some characters have bad textures which really stands out when you talk to them.  All in all, this game definitely lived up to my expectations, it's my new favorite open world rpg (sorry Oblivion), and while not entirely perfect, with the controls and whatnot... in my opinion, it's as perfect as any game can get in this day and age. I really can't give it less than a perfect score - 10/10.

I was going to type up a full fledged review, and then I realized: Only a absolute idiot wouldn't see that this game has great gameplay, great graphics, great story, and great sound. There's a plethora of reviews for those people, and well, just look at the game! Anyways, instead of a actual review, this will be more of a love letter to the developers, please enjoy.  Dear CD Projekt Red, I think I might love you. What you do, is just incredible! Bethesda tries to sell us horse armor and mods, Ubisoft DRM's the living f*** out of us and sells us half finished games, (abitrary f*** you EA), and you... You take their mistakes, learn from them, and do the exact opposite. Sure, there was a bit of a graphics controversey, your game crashes for some people, but I know you're hard at work trying to fix our complaints. Instead of giving us cheap, one off pieces of DLC, you promised us full on expansion packs. And I believe you even though they aren't out yet. Why? Simply because you have given me every reason to trust you. You did exactly what you said you would on this game, and then a little bit more. You have great buisness practices, and you've gotten a lot of respect in my book after this release. You are in a way, a AAA Indie company. First review I've ever written for anything in my life. And for a good reason.  -----This game is RIDICULOUS in how good it is.-----  With the lack of quality games recently, I'm becoming the type of person that picks a game up and is bored of it within an hour, tops, because I usually can already tell what the game is, where it's going, and feel like I've already experienced the best it has to offer. For this reason, I typically don't spend money on games and end up playing mindless flash games on the internet.  This game is just wow. I downloaded it and just played it for over 5 hours straight. It sucked me in. It's perfectly challenging.  I cannot recommend this game enough if you are a fan of RPGs in ANY facet. This could end up being one of my favorite RPGs of all time, and that's saying a lot as I've played and beaten well over 50.  I feel like I STOLE the game only paying 20 bucks. I would have paid 50. And I've already only played it for 5 hours.  Well done, Numantian Games. I cannot believe this is your first published game. Un-freaking-believeable.  I don't think you can top it. I hope you keep putting more time into this game; it could seriously be one of the best ever.

app_name: Game of Thrones - A Telltale Games Series
review_text: Absolutely great game, perfect for someone who's disabled such as myself. When I'm feeling bad or my medications have me a bit too discombobulated to play other games, I can still play Telltale games to my statisfaction. It's like watching the series, but being a part of it too. And each chapter is easily and hour in playtime. Have friends who just watch me play for the story itself. A must buy for any fan of the Game of Thrones series. And with four (4) save slots, you can let some friends playthrough while you watch how their storylines unfold.

This is easily one of the best Visual Novel's i've seen on steam in all my years. I've heard people giving it a hard time because the graphic style is a bit different, and the setting is out of the ordinary.  I found it to be refreshing, something new to add to the Genre itself.  The endings were interesting, but only three stood out above the others, and that was Miku, Harem, and Lena's endings.  Had i worked with the group i would of suggested more endings like that of Lena's.  I had a blast playing it and going through the gallery again, I can safely say i'll likely play through it and wrap it 100% again in the near future.  The mod community is quite active and my only nit picks about the game are fixed with that.  Creative, imaginitive, only a few short comings and you can't beat the price (free).  Knowing what i do now about the whole thing, i wish i had paid for it, as its definitely the best deal i've ever had on steam.  Music was perfect for the story, I think i vaguely remember crying for one of the endings like a little ♥♥♥♥♥. The characters were the best part and in the end i felt like i wished there was more... so I'm going to see if i can help with the community to make more for it.  I'd say check out the various mods and if you enjoyed Alisa, check out 'It not a dream' which i helped with the translation of!  Oh... Right... I rate it 10 Motherland's!  PS. Because of this game I'm actually trying to learn Russian so i can be active in the workshop mods to translate some of them to english. Already worked on one as best i could and made some good friends because of it.

This is the most reactive rpg I have played.   Everything is so tightly done regarding the dialog system and how it REACTS to previous choices. It is just a shame that the gameplay it is attached to is pretty mediocre, and fairly bad in some of the minigames.  The game's easy mode is to max up pistol and brilliance. That is the best.   You get 3 'moods' for every response. From memory, left is cold and professional, up is AGGRESSIVE, and right is suave.  It is meant to be 3 spy archetypes, Jason Bourne, Jack Bauer, and James Bond, but the 'Bond' option instead ends up being the Sterling Archer option. Needless to say, if you are fond of Archer, doing at least ONE playthrough with THAT set is a treat.   This is a title that seems pretty bleh or mediocre on the first playthrough, but just the sheer amount of detail and change that can happen with apparently MINOR decisions makes it shine above pretty much everything else before and after it.  Play it. Then try it again. If you can stand its (now for me very minor) issues of gameplay being rather mediocre, it is a ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ amazing set of experiences. It is a game that no matter if you play it 10 times, you will find new details on each playthrough. You CANNOT experience all of it in one single playthrough.   Definitely recommended.

Time has slowed down to the point of almost standing completely still, or rather: movement has. The world has lost its force, in the physical sense of the word, and it has halted any progression save for the player, and a rather major antagonist. This is where TIMEframe sets off.  There is this direct punch to the game that is very powerful. It hits the right notes with its initial choice of music and spawn-point. Before you there is a giant glaring sun, signifying cleverly the player's path forward. This in tandem with the music that is constituted by this bright powerful guitar strums and finger-picking with some nice alternative tunings (I assume). And once you step out of the edifice you spawned in, it becomes overwhelmingly beautiful. Direct comparisons would lead to Journey, only without the fluidity and particle effects, which makes sense due to the time-flux problematic we have at hand. But aesthetically there is this similar orange hew filling the screen, but in a darker, more abrasive way. It is not as soft as Journey, and following the lines of this jaggedness, there are bare polygons, but they themselves create a mosaic image of different shades of dust and desert. It is not as soft, but still beautiful enough to warrant a response of some kind. Slightly reminiscent of something like Shelter, although a bit more subtle.   Following the Journey comparison, there is also a vastness of space, seemingly unimportant considering time is stood still, but for the player the vast is still vast. Journey combated this with an incredible movement system, with sand being sweetly pushed aside by the fluidity of your garment. In TIMEframe we have a slightly less satisfying way to move forward, making the vast feel like a hindrance, instead of a joyous sign of there being a lot to explore. Even with the slight drudgery that accompanies moving through the spatial, the atmosphere makes up for it. You start feeling like you're not that far away, and there are always things to look at that distract you from the menial process of getting there. The sky seems like a dome of fire that, even though static, feels vibrant.   TIMEframe gives you a time-frame within which you are supposed to explore. The idea is to explore. The game gives you a lot more to explore than mere buildings or statues. It accompanies each finding with a text, each one filling the game with more and more – not only content, that I imagine you're interested in – but with a sort of depth. Ruins do not merely mean tourist attraction, or “exploration”, as the game had written it is much more: “Memories lost to death streams can sometimes be revealed through stone”. It is rare I feel a strong need to quote a game. Or a game that with its mechanics and aesthetic, with its concept, signify an idea much greater than its medium. Everything is connected in TIMEframe, you are playing through a thought out game, a concept realized.   For what TIMEframe sets out to do is fantastic. Its selected writings and setting symbolizes something very special. Something I've had trouble with articulating succinctly. Annihilation is inevitable, impermanence is a fact, and before its imminent strike, there are things to explore, a world to live through and with, and a past world all the same. This game reads like a love-letter to the history of ideas and lost civilizations that with a direct causal link built ours. There are multiple philosophical ideas, mostly existential, that are tackled in this game, all that are bound to at least evoke some type of reaction in the player. Attempts have been made to create a philosophically inscribed game, but it has often failed due to either being far too pretentious or far too insecure in its elucidation of the concepts themselves, either through text or aesthetics – or mechanics! TIMEframe hits a balance, despite not being the most graphically advanced or aesthetically out there, it follows a theme and it does it very well.   I expected a minimalist exploration game, but I found something almost incredible. This is a game that actually deals with the looming threat of death. It deals with surrendering, with acquiescence, with the rationalist defeat against the overwhelming cruelty of nature. Mind does not defeat matter. Memory and mind, dies with the death of the stream, mind and memory dies with you. The they-self, as Heidegger put it, and the equivalence of Flesh, as Francis Bacon put it, both realize, along with this game, the sadness and destructive force of the world. Stones lives on, memories do not. Artifacts are preserved, humans wither away, humans become dust. And that dust is you. The message is strong enough for revivalists to abandon their practice.   It's an effective game. The musical cues are on point, soft acoustic goes into orchestral at the right moments, and uses ambient atmospheric music equally as effectively. This adds to making new places and new texts more digestible, more evocative. Musically, the game has a backbone, something that props up everything and elevates it to what it is, as compared to what it otherwise could have been without its context: unimportant, ambiguous or pointless. It is manipulative, sure, but not without merit, not without substituting the being manipulated part with being given actual substance. Nothing is unimportant, nor pointless, albeit slightly ambiguous, but in a mysterious way that stimulates the mind to associate and grasp the abstract and drag it down to the concrete.   In a way this is an anti-game. It slows down time, it removes the ADHD-like propensity towards fast action and movement, it slows the player down as well, and it gives room to think and reflect. At the same time, however, there is an immense agency to the game, a nagging stress, much akin to the one we feel in real life before death, that begs the player to accomplish, to accomplish and accomplish, before his or her doom. Will you have time to do everything you want to do in life? If the game is the ruler: no. But within the theme of the game things remain, topological and intellectual traces exist and carry on. Here I part ways with the game philosophically, but it does not mean I do not appreciate what it attempts to confer upon the rest of us.  A part of me feels that TIMEframe is open enough for people, both in the way they play and think, to create their own meaning. In minor ways, perhaps, as the general themes are glaringly obvious, but still with a great deal of satisfaction.   I would wholeheartedly recommend TIMEframe. It is humbling, as you weep before your great annihilation, that you are painfully aware of, to be faced with ideas like this, in a medium like this: it makes me happy. It is a good experience, with wonderful expression of concepts and with an intriguing story to boot. Not everything is clear, but it becomes clear as you envelope yourself in this world. You will not regret trying it, buying it; at least I didn't.

I've sunk many hundreds of hours into Paradox games over the years, particularly HOI 3 and Vic 2. HOI 4, unfortunately, tried to streamline and automate so many things that this game ends up a boring, frustrating mess. The production system, however, is really cool. Although there are some major realism problems with resources and unit upkeep, it's a big step forward from HOI 3.  tl;dr -- if you care at all about realism and history or even just playing a functional wargame, play something else. If you want to conquer the world as Poland or whatever it is that 14 year olds like nowadays by drawing arrows on a map and waiting for a time counter to click down, then this might be the game for you!   -- Air  Air was tedious in HOI 3, no doubt. The solution, replacing province-level air with 'air regions,' is not much better. Besides lacking flavor, you lose a tremendous amount of tactical felxibility with your air due to these zones. Say you're invading France. 'Northern France' is one air zone, and your fighters can only reach about half of it, so they get a -50% air superiority malus, so you need, say, 4000 planes to be on equal terms on the frontline against 2,000 French planes. Apparently your planes just go sightseeing around Brittany instead of fighting on the frontline. Why can't I concentrate my fighters just over where the battles are being fought? Why can't I focus my bombers on the breakthrough point instead of having them bomb random 1 division vs. 1 division battles that.  The air war is also horribly bloody, largely due to the 'accident' system. As a baseline, you lose 2% of your planes every time they fly a mission, and they fly 2 missions a day.   There's also no officer/pilot pool in the game at all, so you never run low of pilots until you run out of manpower. (This also means every country in the world has an equally plentiful and well-trained officer corps, which is obviously beyond silly.)  Ground AA, despite being incredibly important IRL, is once again useless/a joke.  -- Naval  I admittedly haven't played much with the naval system, but there are a couple dumb oversights. For example, you can't force your navies to retreat, your admirals don't retreat even if horribly overmatched, and if you send a fleet back for repairs, it will sail right back out unless you CONTROL clicked on repair. I didn't notice this and lost my entire fleet because I thought the ships would stay in port unless I ordered otherwise. Nope, they repaired and immediately wandered out, got attacked by the entire British fleet, and sat there for weeks getting massacred and I couldn't make them retreat.   Also, the AI isn't smart enough to move its ships if they're being port striked. In one game, I sank the entire RN by getting air superiority and then port-striking for months, while the computer just sat its ships there getting bombed instead of rebasing them to say, Scapa Flow or Gibraltar or ANYWHERE.  -- Land  The 'battle planner' system is an abortion, and the game forces you to either use it or (essentially) take a significant combat malus because of the massive 'planning' bonus it gives you. Apparently, unless a 4-star takes weeks 'planning' an offensive (or defensive), your lower officers just march randomly into the enemy's fire. Of course, the battle planning AI, even after the recent fixes, still shifts units around seemingly at random and generally leads its troops like an ADHD 5 year old with a shiny new toy. Good thing they've got that planning bonus!  The supply and weather/terrain system is also a disaster. Supply basically doesn't matter, you can jam zillions of divisions in the middle of the desert and, if you even DO get low supply, they don't run out of fuel/lose org/move slower, they just get a combat malus. You can encircle divisions and it can take weeks to de-org them if they're well-entrenched and in decent terrain because they only get a -33% penalty for not having any food or bullets.  Want to attack Moscow in January? No problem, you've got plenty of supplies and your frostbite casualties are instantly replaced by troops teleporting straight in from Berlin. Worried about mud or snow bogging down your panzers? Don't be silly, little things like that won't slow you down! No oil? Don't worry, your tanks never run out of gas, it just takes a little longer to build new ones!

Superb version of a classic boardgame although, as usual, 'free to play' means 'limited demo' unless you want to pay extra. Games only take about 15 minutes, it's easy to learn, the manual's only a few pages, but gameplay's excellent... the sign of a great game. All you need to know about is movement, terrain restrictions and range modifiers and you're away. The visuals may put some people off (it's hexey, same as the boxed real-life version) but behind that it's a gem. Roll the dice, get symbols that match your target type to hit, destroy units or capture a location to get a victory point medal. Simple but effective.  The only trouble is the pay to play model, imo one of the nastiest money leaching and off-putting things to happen to gaming in the last 20 years. Once the 50 free gold runs out you're stuck with only 2 free maps. It may only work out as pennies to play a match but if they'd sell it as a complete game, drop the 'free' tag and the extra payments, it would've been great (and maybe had a larger number of long term players). It's happened with other boardgame conversions such as Ticket to Ride, Smallworld, Catan, Space Hulk, Talisman, so why not this too?  Use the free bits then buy the boardgame until they come to their senses and release it as a full game where you can play to your heart's content without paying extra for the pleasure.

Disturbingly enteraining is the best adjective I can come up with. Binding of Isaac is a morbid game filled with more morbid symbolism, such as a coat hanger through your head which gives you a attack boost (symbolizing a back alley abortion). Despite the disturbing enemies that you kill with your tears and fetuses that give you extra health bars, this game keeps you playing and exploring rooms to find more items to upgrade your character. When I bought the game during the sale, I was going to 'try it out' for 5 minutes. 45 minutes later I was doing the final boss battle (which I lost). This game constantly changes, which is nice since it only has 6 short stages. But each new play through is a completey different expierence. Rooms change, items change, boss battles change, everytime you play. But with no checkpoints through out the game (meaning, when you die you start from the very beginning) it defiantly keeps on you toes and gives you a choice to make sacrifices (being health or money) for a nice (or not so nice) surprise.This game is excellent (and cheap) entertainment!   I give it 5 coat hangers out of 5.

The story is a little genertic as the aliens invade and there is a special task force dedicated to fighting them off. You build a base, where resouce managment is important, and it adds a nice home feeling to the game where you are sent off to missions from base and return back with tales of battle. In battles you control soldiers in a turn based combat system. Its hard and thrilling, as you try and gun down aliens only to have them flank you and take out your whole squad. This forces you to learn tactics and think ahead, making the game all the more enjoyable. Get this game, just get it and save the world fromt he xenos.

Dishonored is a unique stealth game that allows you to progress through the main story in two fashions: killing a lot or killing nobody at all. The outcome of the game is the same in both paths, but the events that lead up to it vary depending on the choice.  The powers you obtain are interesting and beneficial to a typical stealth game ranging from teleportation to mind-possession. I found myself using them more often than the actual weapons you are given including a crossbow and foldable blade.   The art style is also sweet and unique. The texts and imagery presented to you in the UI and objectives tabs look like straight-up paintings popping out. The graphics aren't overly intensive, but still pleasing to the eye.   The story felt a little predicatable, but maybe that is just my own opinion.   Overall, Dishonored is well worth the money.

Crimesfet 2015 was promised to provide free content. On the first day of this community event, weapon 'rebalancing' turned everything to $hit. Some of the guns are overpowered now, but most of them can be thrown out. But wait, there is more! They give us CS:GO-ripoff case mechanic with gun skins. Not only the keys (which cost 2.50$ by the way) cannot be obtained by in-game methods, those skins have weapon stats bonuses on them. Which you need to turn now crappy weapons to pre-update state.  Think of it. Retail cooperative game, with ~100$ cost of all DLC now features microtransactions to access optimal weapon performance. Not to mention that overkill repeatedly said in the past that no microtransactions would ever take place in payday. That surpasses even EA level of bull$hit. That precedent sets new standard for cash grabbing by publishers/developers.  Aside from that, this update broke any modding of the game which done more than simple texture replacement. So all of the huds enhancing crappy vanilla game experience were rendered obsolete overnight. And they won't return. Because they need game engine lua hooking to function. And as that functonality can be used to basically cheat, say instantly finish a heist, god mode, etc., no one would ever allow to do that in a game which is tied to steam inventory and market.  6 hours after beginning of Crimefest 2015 reddit's /r/paydaytheheist most upvoted post OF ALL TIME is literally titled 'Fu©k you overkill'.  FBI files, released one year after promised time, was simple robbery of  pd2stats.com  community page. Which was shut down after first day of Crimefest 2015. See  pd2stats.com  page for details.  Many veteran community members are just walking out. Wikia payday admins are stepping down. Steam discussions and reviews are rioting.  Not buying anything from overkill/starbreeze/505 ever again.  --------------------------  June 2016: Okay, update 100 and subsequent sdtatements have done a very nice damage control. This is probably the only game I have ever played that requires a lot of combat skill to play. Everything else is simply, point and click, when you get done playing Chivalry.  I have been playing this game since it's inception as a half-life mod, Age of Chivalry. No other game comes close to satisfying that medieval desire that streams through my DNA for some reason.  If you have that same itchy feeling, that nagging urge to lop someone's head off, there is no where else you need to go. This is it. The meaty sound of a javelin piercing someone's chest cannot be duplicated anywhere else I can find.  However, as with most gaming communities these days, don't expect a warm welcome. Even to this day, I still play with in-game chat turned off. I don't need to read all the ♥♥♥♥♥ing about archers, dragging weapons, feinting, etc. I have no problems with any of the mechanics that are currently in the game. I personally do not spin attack, and even after many hours of game-play, there are still many players I just cannot beat. What's my solution? I don't know, maybe a fire pot to soften them up, or a heavy xbow bolt from cover.   This game can even be fun for the family on a LAN with bots playing Team Objective. Imagine paying $3.74 for a thousand hours of fun? What a steal.  Buy the game and tough it out rookie. Is that chainmail? Or are you wearing a skirt?

Even after more than a decade I still find myself gravitating back to Morrowind. The depth of the story was always compelling to me, the savior but not actually the savior prophesy, all the ways the game played with familiar tropes and advanced the Elder Scrolls games. While Daggerfall held my heart for so long its bugs made it unplayable, Morrowind was not perfect but it presented a huge play space, lots of discovery and interesting opportunities and a game system that let you play the way you wanted, and rewarded you for doing so.  Even with the graphical enhancements and system refinements of the later games, Morrowind for me hits just the right balance of a Role-playing experience and an action game.

Divinity: Original Sin is a single-player and co-operative multiplayer fantasy role-playing game by Larian Studios. This game was partially funded via Kickstarter, and is a sequel to Divine Divinity (which if I'm being honest I have never heard of, or played). This game was released on 30th June 2014 for PC and Mac. I played this entire game on Steam with my husband using the co-operative multiplayer mode.  The customizable protagonists of the game are known as Source Hunters. In single-player the player controls them both, while in the co-operative multiplayer each player takes control over one of them. Gameplay begins in a town called Cyseal in southern Rivellon, and the Source Hunters receive orders to investigate the murder of a town councilor. The plot goes on a journey to investigate cults, relationships, sacrifice, and more.  This game ships with the editor that it was created with, and this allows players to create their own single-player and multiplayer adventures, and then publish them online.  Divinity: Original Sin is a 3D, isometic, party based, turn based, choice and consequence, strategy RPG. You will need to make optimal use of action points, skills, spells, equipment, and objects within the environment to get the most out of your experience.  There are 11 preset classes to choose from at the start of the game, however each can be customised to suit your own personal style of play.    There are 6 attributes to select which are common throughout any RPG genre, such as strength, dexterity, intelligence, etc.    There are 6 abilities to choose from which are weapons (i.e. single-handed, two-handed, etc), defence (i.e. armour specialist, shield specialist, etc.), skills (i.e. expert marksman, pyrokinetic, witchcraft, etc.), personality (i.e. bartering, leadership, etc.), craftsmanship (i.e. blacksmithing, crafting, etc.), and nasty deeds (i.e. lockpicking, etc.).   There are 45 possible talents to choose from, some of which unlock as your character gets stronger and makes specific choices throughout the game. Examples of talents are gaining additional ability points, damage boosts, immunity from spells, increased spell range, etc.   There are 9 different skills that you can take, and you can either have spells in each skill, or you can specialise in specific skills. Examples of skills are Expert Marksman for rangers, Man-At-Arms for melee combat, etc.   My husband played as the Battlemage, and I played as a Cleric. We used supporting NPC's Madora and Jahan. I felt as though it took me a little time to really get into the combat, but our four characters complimented each other well. My husband and Jahan (specialising in Hydrosophist and Aerotheurge skills) were the casters; I was a single-handed, shield bearing tank (specialising in Man-At-Arms and Hydrosophist skills); and Madora was a two-handed damage dealing tank.  Party relationships feature heavily throughout the game, for example if you damage one of the NPC's in your party whilst trying to attack an enemy they can get angry and leave the party; likewise when playing co-operative multiplayer there are specific dialogues that take place between the two 'real' players that affect their in-game relationship.  The game plays out in real-time, and only the battles are turn-based and are dependent on Action Points that your character has. The number of Action Points you can use in combat is dependent on your attributes and abilities. These stats can also be affected by your gear. Combat can be influenced heavily by elements within the game, i.e. if one of your characters casts a fireball at enemies, and then poison is cast over the top of the fire, this can generate an explosion; likewise if a fireball is cast at an enemy and it then begins to rain, this can generate a steam cloud; water can be electrified by using lightning which can stun enemies (and friendly characters). All of these combinations lead to a higher level of strategy needed to finish off some of the stronger enemies that you come across.  There is an entire crafting and blacksmithing element to this game, however as we were not actively role-playing throughout the game, we didn't actually find these abilities particularly useful. I had my NPC put a couple of points into crafting and blacksmithing so we could repair gear in an emergency if we were unable to teleport back to a town, but we never found any need to make potions, make our own armour, or use any food available throughout the game. Early on in the game we did use health potions a fair bit, but once all four of our characters were able to use a healing spell, they eventually became redundant too.  As well as the main story in the game, there are also over 70 other side quests available. Side quests can be the best way in which to find new armour and weapons, as well as getting valuable experience to further improve your characters attributes.  I did find parts of this game frustrating as the combat at times can go on forever in a big fight, you can at times get insta-killed by lava when you weren't expecting it, and we also found a few glitches here are there where we had a the game crash completely, or quests that didn't quite work as they should causing us to have to reload. Despite these issues this didn't detract from my overall enjoyment of the game, though I would definitely recommend auto-saving on a regular basis.  Having played the entire game using the co-operative multiplayer, our total play time came to just over 80hrs (each) logged on Steam. This game is most certainly excellent value for money, although I do think that we could have finished the game a little quicker than we did as we did miss some important quests early on which meant leveling up took us a bit longer than it should, but overall that was all part of the experience, and we would know better if we ever decide to do a second play through.  For any fans of Diablo, Torchlight, Titan Quest, etc., I urge you to get a copy of Divinity: Original Sin. It is excellent value for money, and is a great strategy RPG with a well thought out, well explained story, with excellent longevity and replayability.

First things first guys. If you want a game with no bugs, or P2W, then pay a monthly fee. As you can see I like this game for many reasons, but I will be unbias in my review. Let's prove it by talking about the downfalls:  The three worse parts about RO2 are the leveling and the [lack of] PvP, and the release of new content. In my thousands of hours of WoW, it is fair to say, that a much better Quest line system from lvl 1 to max level is available in other games. The leveling is very, very grind-till-you-drop style. There are three kinds of Quests: 1. Kill this monster twenty times (some times for an item it drops to give to the quest giving npc) 2. Talk to this npc 3. Gather this item.   Poor planning of Patch release dates that bring new content to the game. There have been three, no wait, four new dungeons in six months added to the game. I have the best gear in the game (^^) but to be honest, a lot of people are either skipping new raid dungeons to go to the newest one, or moving to the most recent raid dungeons half geared from the one before it (my point is a 'new' dungeon shouldn't become redundant after a month an a half). They would be better off releasing 4-8 dungeons a year all at once, or six months at a time, and letting players work through them tier by tier.  Pvp is entirely under developed, but they did add a new PvP map, which is actually pretty awesome, if you have a DPS cahracter. Healers are not really needed unless they are a hybrid (DPS mixed with Support). There are three maps: 1. Colo (it is a big open circle so not really a map) on this map you free for all with 30 other players for 5 rounds and people are eliminated each round 2. WOE (don't even get me started) this is a poorly designed map with too many choke points and you can only enter with your guild (fyi: I am in a very large, strong guild) and the big, strong guilds always win (my guild is PVE based) 3. Jehoon Training Site (nice map and game play) uses a lane and tower mechanic like DOTA or LoL with merchants to buy buff scrolls and monsters in the 'jungle' which you kill for a quest to obtain items and points to win the match or buff your team (also debuffs other team).  PVE: The Good Stuff : D  The best PVE content in my opinion is end-game. Like anything there are good and bad sides. Let's get one thing straight, I have done every raid a bunch of times on my own and in a guild, and they are all doable with Pugs (random people) aside from AoD (unless you are over-geared, but for review sake, let's assume you have gear from the tier below the raid you are doing). The bosses in the ten-man raids are all fun. They aren't too hard that you rage out and quit the game, but aren't too easy that you get bored and quit the game. There are two bosses though, that are extremely difficult to most people (Rat Master and Zoda). I did Rat Master with only pugged raids and killed it almost every time, but never the first try (Rat Master: two tries, four tries, nine tries.) in order to enter the Chaos Dungeon (newest raid dungeon). People say it is impossible and cry, and cry, and cry. Put simply, it takes chatting software, strategy, practice, and skill. Not every one is meant to make it to the furthest endgame content in any MMO, and those people, usually have trouble accepting it. For me, PVE is the best part of the game, and reason I have played so much.  Interesting Content: There are a lot of unique aspects of the game that I hadn't seen in others. 1. Costumes: these are either 'grind your face off' or P2W items, but overall it is an interesting and 'fun' aspect of the game. The amount of power you get from costumes depends on how many Runes you put in it to give you bonus stats. You can also combine costumes with a chance to get 'rare' costumes. I have only gotten one 'rare' from thirty combines total over a span of months of game play, but lucky people in my guild have gotten 'rare' costumes with only one fusion. 2. Cards: cards are only dropped from monsters and bosses. They give you bonus stats, and you can combine them to get rare cards, with great stat bonuses. 3. Phantom Pyramid: this is a P2W mini-game that you play in order to win content. It is based on RNG and can provide great rewards, or waste money (in-game or real life money) and time.   Overall this is a great F2P *once you hit max level*. There are bugs, and the only real issue I have is the lack of attention put towards fixing those bugs faster. If you are a talented PVE player in any MMO game, be prepared to be surrounded by the most idiotic players you have ever seen (mainly Tier 1 raids and early game five man dungeons). You have to find people that are awesome players to raid with from the pugs you do, or join a great guild. There is a decent job system, and class system (with a nicely set up talent tree for each class). And even though I say to myself, 'why would the developers do that?' more than I would like to, I ffind myself logging back in to play (way too often).   PS: Thanks for reading my book.

Event though I finished the game with all the cards and *almost* all the achievements it left me feeling empty. The 8 / 16 bits graphic style is great. The music is ok for the most part. The levels are all fun and somewhat frustraiting, but not enough to make you rage quit in the middle of the game. However none of that is enough to counter the biggest down of this piece: its complete lack of story, or should I say, its completly lack of sense.  Don't get me wrong, a game doesn't require to have any profound meaning to be entertaining, but this one is just too much. The zones or levels have no relation between them. It's as if they were a bunch of levels from different games put together. Young (the main character) just appears in this word for some unknown reason. It seems that he might be dreaming but you have nothing to expand this theory. In most parts of the game the NPCs present you with filosophical issues, but all of the dialogs are void and empty and provide nothing that your mind can 'munch' into, failing to give any 'deepness' to game. Often times I was at a crossroad, picked one direction and then left the level area in the process - never being able to discover what was on the other side of the crossroads.  The story seemed shady and ... I honestly didn't get it. Had to look for a synopsis only to find that apparently noone really knows for sure what the hell is going on (even tho people seem to have a pretty clear GENERAL idea of what's going on)  As it appears to be somewhat random which abilities you get on your first playthrough (?) I ended up with a combination that only involved 2 dmg abilities for the bigger part of the game, making me have to mostly spam one ability most of the time to get an enemy down. Later on I actually got to taste the sweetnes of combos and having several useful abilities that deal damage. But without several damage abilities the 'stop time and plan your moves' ability seems ... pretty stupid.  I had high hopes because of bastion. The game has a good feel to it but I really wasn't awestruck by neither the actual gameplay nor the way the story was delivered. It's an OK game but not nearly as great as it's pedecessor.

If you liked Diablo 2, you will probably love this game.   It is similar in many aspects, but introduces mechanics which work out great: they are similar to the Materia System and the Sphere Grid from Final Fantasy: the result is a huge variety of possible builds.  While the passive skill tree is difficult to change afterwards, skills can be (un)equipped at will: they are found, rewarded and traded throughout the game. And they level up individually and independend of each other. In the end, this makes it much easier to try something new and experiment with different skill combinations. You can also combine skills with support skills to add/increase a specific aspect, usually at the price of increased mana cost.   There are many more minor/mediocre changes too, e.g. gold has been replaced by various currency items which can all be consumed for personal use, mostly to enhance items.   It's free to play and people cannot buy any significant gameplay advantages, which is probably the most benefical commercial concept for us players. The donations-for-cosmetic-stuff keep things going and the game is being actively improved e.g. an expansion pack is on the way in ~20 days as of this writing.   Some downsides (mostly minor): The amount of items dropped seems quite excessive, but that's quite common in this genre I guess.  Vendor recipes: you better look up the most important ones before playing, otherwise you might waste quite some currency in the long run.  Upgrading skills shows the requirement for the next level, but not the granted benefit: you have to remember the values to see what has changed (or look it up online).    Overall, the game turned out great: 9/10.

This rather simple yet addictive, often hectic game packs a lot of punch despite it's somewhat downgraded graphics (although the retro look in my opinion suits this game and the background is usually quite colourful and good-looking) the gameplay itself varies greatly thanks to a wide selection of characters with multiple abilities both melee and ranged, various items with different effects, most of which can stack (some infinitely as far as I'm aware) there's a decent selection of monsters (although I hope a few more are released in future updates) and added extras in the game known as 'Artifacts' which you usually aquire through obscure methods, these extras are activated before starting a game and persist throughout the game and can change the style of play completely (item drops not being random, items not spawning in chests but instead dropping from monsters etc) the bosses are generally quite epic, my first encounter with a Magma Worm actually shocked me as I wasn't expecting something quite so collosal, most of the bosses have a decent degree of difficulty as well with some of them clearly built to be effective against either a ranged or a melee character (e.g. Collosus being easy on a ranged character.)  Onto downsides/potential improvements: First thing to mention is a rather persistent glitch I've noticed, I'm not sure if it's a combination of circumstances which causes this but sometimes you can spawn into the first level and either be falling infinitely because the ground isnt solid or you might find an area that isnt solid ground, this is obviously a bit game breaking but unfortunately not too bad an issue as starting a new game usually fixes this and its only on the first level as far as I'm aware so you wont have made much progression, this should hopefully be patched soon but this has been happening since I started playing about a month ago and there's been an update since then.  The local multiplayer is a nice feature, with a controller and a keyboard two people can play on the same system, the xbox controller feels responsive and is easy to map and I'd recommend a controller for anyone playing this game however the issue I find with the local multiplayer is that you have to play zoomed out making it difficult to see things and sometimes distinguish between your characters, the screen seems to try and follow both players simultaneously and doesn't go into split screen once youve gone out of range of each other.  Online multiplayer doesn't seem to work for me, not sure why, I can get it working with virtual local area network software but me and my housemate couldn't seem to connect over the same network, many people seem to have similar issues so I think this is something that might need fixing or at the very least explained how to host a game within the game itself if there isn't an issue with it, having said all of this, the multiplayer doesn't rely on servers hosted by the games publisher, instead you host the server (which can cause issues depending on internet connection/system specs) but this means there's never a risk of (rain) the servers being closed down.  Some of the items require being unlocked before they will drop and the achievements to unlock the items vary greatly with some having, in my opinion, far too high a difficulty to achieve and the achievement for aquiring the Soldiers Syringe doesn't seem to work, the item is unlocked and it says the achievement is unlocked ingame but it doesn't appear in your achievement log ingame or on Steam - quite an annoying bug for any perfectionists.  Overall I think this game is good and worth playing, a few tweaks here and there, a bit more new content (maybe even some sort of open sourcing so people can create there own content?) and I think this would be a great indie game.
