This game is an experience, sort of like the Stanley Experiment or Portal, so I reccomend to not read about it or watch videos on it until you've played or if the demo hadn't peaked your interest. If you've read anything on it already but haven't played it yet, don't read more until you've played it. I consider it to be one of the best games of all time, but that claim could cloud one's judgement of it. If you like a game that can play around with your expectations, and if you like quirky comedic adventures with dark undertones like Adventure Time and EarthBound, this game will be up your alley. Spellforce is a hybrid RTS with strong RPG components. It's good but has its flaws.  Good: Long campaign well worth the money; innovative mix of RTS and RPG; some nice touches in combat interface (eg select an enemy and icons show up under your heroes indicating which spells you can throw at target); a big world worth exploring; decent scenery; excellent music.  Not so good: Storyline a bit boring; voice-acting somewhat weak; RTS component a little weak - overly complex resource management (six resources?), big battles hard to micro properly (this is no Starcraft . . .); as far as it goes as an RPG - very limited/no real choices. RPG heroes not well-balanced vs the RTS army (on some maps it can be easier to NOT trigger the base-building part and clear out some enemy bases with the heroes, as enemy spawn rate increases once you claim a base).  I would still recommend it for the novelty value of a reasonably-done RPG/RTS hybrid.

Amazing and adventurous! I have discovered that computer games, no matter how much you like them, can sometimes be rather stressful. What I mean is in hardcore games, like Dragon Age you are to be in tension every single second and from time to time I feel like playing some relaxing game, without waiting for one more enemy to appear any moment. That’s why I bought several indie games on Steam winter sale, one of them being Giana Sisters: Twisted Dreams, which I like greatly. My present day favorite is a small labyrinth game called “Hamilton’s Great Adventure”. Among tons of indie projects it is really hard to find one that is thrilling and addictive indeed. Nevertheless Hamilton’s game is very nice allowing both to get relaxed and amused at the same time. As I have already mentioned it is a labyrinth game in which you must walk through about 60 levels, collecting keys and coins.  But it is not so simple as it may sound. Very often the game turns out a pure strategy puzzle and you have to think over what to do, where to go, which method to choose. The puzzles are sometimes rather tough, but the balance is extremely good – you do not have time to get bored, too annoyed or angry at the process, they make you wish to find the solution and beat all the traps and enemies. read more  http://www.viewforum.net/gaming-dept-171/hamilton’s-great-adventure-2960/

A very intricate city builder/resource management game. It may seem simple at first glance, but it is far more subtle than it lets on. Maintaining the proper balance between things is far more difficult than you might expect and growing too fast can easily screw you over in the endgame.  It takes place across islands and underwater (with the dlc). If I had to compare it with sim city or your average city builder, it is less about building things and more about managing production chains and maintaining the very delicate balance between resources, inhabitants and the ecosystem. Very pleasant and can be as challenging or as relaxing as you'd like. It also looks rather great and has fairly elaborate multiplayer features, including coop.  Disclaimer: The game works through uplay, which is Literally Hitler. Aside from that and the odd minor glitch, it is fairly great. Would definitely recommend picking this up when on sale, especially if you're into this kind of game. You will definitely get your money worth of fun.

Honestly, this game has too many flaws to recommend. After one playthrough the game has nothing else to offer, in fact, it really doesn't have anything to offer from any other generic hallway shooter besides a change of scenery.  Problem 1, The Gameplay: It's boring. Bioshock was great because plasmids were a novel thing, and there was a variety of ways you could take out enemies, from freezing, to fire, to ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ BEES. Infinite drops the ball here, you have multiple generic stuns, a fireball, and a shield. they really aren't functionally different from one another enough to warrant including them.  The enemies aren't any better. Yeah, we saw the Motorized Patriot, the Boys of Silence, The Handymen and Firemen from the previews, but most of the time you face waves of boring guys with guns. And instead of facing off with Songbird (as was foreshadowed) you get a few waves of generic enemies as an anticlimax.  On top of that, you're made to believe your choices matter throughout the game. If you believed that, you were suckered. After scrapping the game and delaying it a few times, they decided that multiple endings (in a game with an ending involving multiple universes) was too much time. A two-gun carrying limit (wholly unnecessary) means that you won't get to try out a variety of weapons (half of which are useless), it means that you'll carry around the most common weapons so you won't run out of ammo.  Oh, and Elizabeth is pointless. Resident Evil 4 at least made protecting Ashley matter, Infinite just removes that part of the equation. All she does is dispense money or ammo you might have missed (removing the reason to explore), and pickpockets you after she revives you. She might be able to open tears, but that's only in certain situations and is useless 90% of the time. She can only pick locks if you find her a lockpick (which you can't re-use, despite it being a HAIR PIN), just making them glorified keys.  Problem 2, The Story: Possibly the most overrated part of this game, the story shows promise through the game. It shows themes of lampooning American Exceptionalism and Americna Ideals, and condenses the Hypocrices of American history in a little can and delivers it neatly. Fink, entrepenur extrordinare and representing freedom of enterprise, stole most of his inventions, treats his workers like ♥♥♥♥, and snuffs out competition. The society and government of Columbia, representative of American ideas of civic nationalism, free expression, and separation of powers espouses xenophobia and racism, is blatantly theocratic, places all the power in a single man, and supresses any political dissent with force. All of this could have made an excellent game.  Instead we got some ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥t about 'constants and variables' to justify some alternate-worlds time-travel plot that doesn't make sense under scrutiny, pointless ♥♥♥♥♥♥y over how special Elizabeth is, constant brooding by Booker in-between gleefully slaughtering dozens, and scripted events that are supposed to make us care about other characters that don't stay in the story for very long. Then it ends after going through Rapture (reminding you that you're NOT playing an altogether better game), with the biggest anticlimax ever (desparately trying to create a 'mind♥♥♥♥').  The gmae blatantly takes you through a place like Finktown, showing the state of working conditions in 1912 and the attitude of management towards them (They allude to the Pinkertons firing on striking wokers), they say Booker was a retired Pinkerton for god's sake, they show the beginnings of Socialist movements in the U.S., and even give a taste of totalitarian rule brought about by some of the revolutions that followed (like in the U.S.S.R.). The game then blueballs you by going back to the 'Elizabeth is speshul' plot.  The game drags you through multiple places where blacks are segregated and treated like ♥♥♥♥, then takes you through some abolitionists house (included with an actual historical abolitionist poster), then goes back to 'Elizabeth is speshul'  It drags you through various technological laboratories and shows how Handymen and their counterparts are created, then goes back to 'Elizabeth is speshul' No mention of ethical concerns, apparently too high-brow for the consumer.  All of the potential of the story was wasted, and coupled with the gameplay which feels like a dumbed-down version of Bioshock, just means I can't recommend this game to anyone.   Save your money, buy Bioshock.
